The Ripple Rebellion

Citizens' Toolkit · East Cork

Cyber Strategy submission closes 21 Aug 2026

Ireland's Data Centre Investigation

This is the KAIROS moment.

The opportune moment for action. Ireland's water. Ireland's harbours. Ireland's children's future. The architecture of impunity has been mapped — now it must be challenged.

22%
of Ireland's electricity went to data centres in 2024 (CSO)
~121
data centres — only 16 EPA-licensed (Oireachtas L&RS)
~30%
of all EU data hosted in Ireland
€5k
EPA's maximum environmental fine

Do one thing today

A live Government consultation closes 21 August 2026 — the draft National Cyber Security Strategy. A submission in your own words is the single most useful thing you can do right now.

Take action →

Eight months. Five instruments. No public debate worthy of the name.

Each one individually defensible. Together — a layered architecture that makes meaningful environmental accountability almost impossible to trigger.

In 1961, President Eisenhower warned: "We must never let the weight of this combination endanger our liberties or democratic processes." He was speaking about the military-industrial complex. In Ireland 2026, replace military with digital. The warning stands.

Investigated by Bríd

Former teacher. Mother of three. Midleton, Co. Cork. Doing this voluntarily because someone had to. Everything here is free to copy, adapt and share — every load-bearing claim traced to a named source, every open question marked as one. Clean hands, in both directions.

Find your way around: The Investigation is the full dossier. The Five Acts shows how the machine works. Data Sovereignty is the submission toolkit. Water Bill App is a tool in the making. Creative & Shop holds the poems, films and audio. Connect is where it lives day to day.

The citizen dossier · full text

Chapter Map — The Whole Book at a Glance

A one-line guide to every chapter, so you can read straight through or go directly to what you need. The book is long by design — it is meant to be a reference as much as a read.

Part 1 — The Architecture of Capture. This chapter sets out the engine of the whole book: the five-act sequence by which a system arrives as a gift, reverses the flow, embeds itself in law, rewrites the host’s reality, and locks the terms in.

Part 2 — The All Too Many Sites (That We Know Of): Cork, Clare, Louth and Beyond. Here the pattern becomes concrete and local.

Part 3 — Who Pays, Who Benefits. This is the chapter that follows the money and the resources.

Part 4A — The Planning Contrast. This chapter holds two planning decisions side by side: what an ordinary citizen or small builder is put through, and what a hyperscale data centre is waved past.

Part 4B — The Revolving Door: Who Came From Where, Who Went Where. This chapter traces the movement of people between the bodies that regulate and the industries they regulate — the revolving door.

Part 4C — The Global Extraction Map: Sixteen Countries, One Pattern. This chapter lifts the eyes from Ireland to fourteen countries and asks whether the same shape recurs.

Part 4D — What They Said, and What They Do: Mission Statements vs Reality. This chapter sets corporate and consultancy promises against the documented record — including the reports commissioned to justify the build-out, and what those reports leave out.

Part 4E — The FDI Trap: Venture Capital, Dependency Scenarios. This chapter examines the deeper financial machinery — venture capital, private equity, and the dependency that foreign direct investment can quietly build.

Part 4F — The Camouflage: How a Data Centre Hides in the Planning System. This chapter is a practical field guide: how a data centre can enter the planning system under other names and categories, and how an ordinary person can learn to recognise one before the concrete is poured.

Part 4G — The Global Playbook: The Same Architecture, Country by Country. Where the map showed the pattern, this chapter shows the playbook being written into law abroad — and, crucially, where it is being stopped.

Part 4H — The Good Actors: Proof That This Is a Choice. This chapter is the antidote to despair.

Part 5 — Surveillance & Centralisation: One Pattern, Not Three Issues. This chapter argues that surveillance powers, health-data centralisation, media concentration and the new AI law are not separate stories but one — the steady expansion of how clearly the powerful can see us, as our ability to see them narrows.

Mask of Virtue — A Snapshot of Recent News (June 2026). A short interlude: a snapshot of how the story was being told in the news of a single month, and how the language of virtue and progress is used to dress the same old mechanism.

Part 6 — What You Can Do: FOI, AIE, Legal Routes & Templates. This is the toolkit.

Part 6B — Constitutional Leverage: What the President Can Do, and What Citizens Can Ask. This chapter looks at the formal levers that already exist within the State’s own architecture — what the President can do, and what citizens are entitled to ask — and how to use them.

Part 7 — Schools, Youth & All Citizens: Mobilise. This chapter is the call to organise — in schools, among young people, and across the wider public.

Part 8 — Every County: Find Out What’s Planned Near You. A practical, county-by-county orientation: how to find out what is planned near you, and how to act on it locally.

Part 9 — A Call to Those Inside the Industry. This chapter speaks directly and without hostility to the people who work inside the industry — engineers, planners, professionals — who may see what is wrong and feel unable to say so.

Part 10 — Allies, Media & Coalition. This chapter maps the allies — the TDs, MEPs, unions, journalists and organisations already doing the work — and how to build the coalition that turns isolated objections into a force.

Part 11 — Alternatives: Diversifying Our Internet, Measuring What Matters. This chapter is constructive: it sets out the alternatives — to a single extractive model of the internet, and to measuring a country’s worth by GDP alone.

Part 12 — What Was Taken, and What ‘Free’ Really Costs. This chapter is about the largest uncredited borrowing in history: the human knowledge, art and writing the AI models were trained on, taken without asking and sold back as a service.

An Bata Scóir: The Tally Stick, the Hedge School, and the New Empire. The historical heart of the book.

Part 13 — KAIROS: The Educational Framework. This chapter offers an educational framework — a way of teaching the next generation to recognise the pattern and read the map, so it is never handed to them undrawn.

Part 14 — When People Took Agency, and It Worked. A chapter of evidence for hope: documented cases, here and abroad, where ordinary people took agency against an extractive system and won.

Part 15 — The Moment: Schools, Tipping Point, Murmuration. This chapter is about how change actually tips — the murmuration, the moment a scattered public becomes a single turning flock.

Part 15B — Sliding Doors: The Harbour in 2035. A pair of imagined futures: the harbour in 2035 if nothing changes, and if it does.

Case Study — The Agents Are Already Here: The Facts Behind the Hype. A careful, sourced case study separating what AI ‘agents’ actually are and do from the marketing around them.

Case Study — The Brain in the Dish: Biocomputing and the Next Frontier. A case study on the genuinely strange next frontier: computing with living brain cells.

Reasons for Hope: It Has Been Done Before. A gathering of hope, grounded in fact: it has been refused before, and can be again.

The Reframe: One Lot, One Shadow, One Mother. This chapter reframes the whole argument away from tribe and flag and toward kinship — the recognition that the dividing line was never between peoples, but between those a system treats as owners and those it treats as resources.

Part 16 — Your Action Tracker. A practical tracker to turn intention into sustained action — a way to keep account of what you have done and what comes next, so momentum is not lost.

Part 17 — Use Your Agency: Support Those Already Fighting. This chapter points to the people and groups already in the fight and shows concretely how to back them.

Part 18 — The Irish Diaspora Sovereign Fund: A Call to the Global Family. A constructive proposal: a sovereign fund drawing on the global Irish family, modelled on what other small nations have built, to fund a different kind of future.

Part 19 — We Don’t Wait: Starting Our Own Permanent Citizens’ Assembly. This chapter argues for not waiting for permission — for citizens convening their own permanent assembly to deliberate and decide.

Part 20 — The Unifying Thread: Kinship, Commonality, and the Mitochondrion. The book’s closing argument, drawn from biology: the mitochondrion, once a stranger, became part of every cell through symbiosis, not extraction.

Closing — The Nitrogen Fixers. The final word, and the book’s central image: the white clover, the nitrogen fixer, working underground without permission to enrich the whole field.

Appendix A — AIE & FOI Request Pack: Ready-to-Send Templates. The action pack: every Freedom of Information and Access to Information on the Environment request, ready to copy and send, each shown in a green box.

Appendix — The Action Index: Every Action, What It Tackles, and Where to Find It. An index of every action in the book — what it tackles and where to find it — so nothing is lost in the length and every reader can find the step that fits them.

Appendix B — Open Letter to Professor David Farrell. A closing open letter, included in full, as an example of the kind of direct, reasoned, public approach this campaign believes in — addressed to a named academic in the spirit of genuine dialogue.

Why This Booklet Exists

Some saw it coming. They shouted. They weren’t heard. They kept going anyway.

This booklet is a citizen investigation into how data centre infrastructure is being approved, connected and brought online in Ireland — and beyond — with no binding water disclosure, no thermal discharge limits, and renewable-energy requirements that the biggest projects are exempt from.

It is for ordinary people. Students. Teachers. Anglers. Parents. Graduates. Environmentalists. Anyone who wants to understand what is happening in their own area and do something about it.

Every claim that can be sourced is sourced. Where the figures are estimates, they are labelled as estimates — because the companies hold the real numbers and choose not to publish them.

It is free. Add to it. Share it. Pass it on.

A note on honesty: clean hands

Some claims floating around online cannot be independently verified. They are left out on purpose. The strength of this investigation is that it does not need them. The documented facts are damning enough.

Three rules hold the whole thing together: facts are kept separate from interpretation; estimates are marked as estimates and given as ranges; and quotations are kept short and attributed.

Where a figure cannot be confirmed because the operator will not publish it, that gap is named for what it is — a disclosure failure, not a guess.

Put plainly, everything in this booklet falls into one of three grades, and the booklet tries always to make clear which. Documented fact — a figure or statement tied to a primary source: an official record, a court decision, a named regulatory document, a company’s own filing. Reported estimate — a figure from credible secondary reporting or modelling, useful but approximate, and given as a range where possible. Open question — a plausible concern not yet proven, included only as a question to investigate, never as an accusation to repeat. This is not a weakness. It is the reason the booklet can be defended: a campaign that separates fact from inference is far harder to dismiss than one that blurs the line.

Check the sources yourself, and verify any figure against its primary document before you repeat it in public.

A note on using AI to write about AI

This booklet was produced with the help of Claude (Anthropic). The irony is named openly: we are using the infrastructure we are investigating to hold it to account.

That is not hypocrisy. It is the nitrogen fixer’s method. Use what is available. Be open about it. Keep your hands clean.

What this booklet is doing

This is not only a report. It is a map, a warning, a toolkit, and a call to action.

It joins data centres, water, energy, planning, surveillance, media concentration, academic capture and democratic reform, because those are not separate issues. They are one pattern.

The pattern is simple to say and hard to unsee: public things are being treated as private opportunity, while the public is handed the cost, the risk, and the silence.

This booklet is here to make that visible.

Start Here: How to Read This

A companion website is on the way. This booklet has a living counterpart: a GitHub page that will hold the clickable, copy-and-send versions of every template here — the AIE and FOI letters, the consultation links, the citizen noise and water protocols, the Ripple Register, and a short plain-language summary. The print pages in this book and the web page are meant to stay in step: paper for the kitchen table and the council meeting, web for the link you send on. Earmarked here so you know to look for it — start at theripplerebellion.github.io.

This is a big document, and it is meant to be. The people building all of this are counting on it being too much — too long, too technical, too tangled across too many subjects for any ordinary person to hold in their head. That is not a side-effect of how these systems work. It is part of how they work. Complexity is somewhere to hide. So before you go any further, here is the whole thing in plain language.

The scale, in one breath

Every number below is sourced and explained later in this booklet. They are gathered here for one reason: you are never shown them together. They reach you separately — a line in a CSO release, a paragraph in a UN report, a figure in a regulator’s ruling — in different language, from different bodies, on different days. Apart, each is a technical detail you can file away and forget. Together, they are the shape of the thing. So, in one breath:

  • Data centres now take 22% of all the electricity in the country — more than every home in Ireland combined — and are heading for a third.

  • More than a third of all our treated water is lost to leaks before it reaches a tap — while households are rationed and put under hosepipe bans, and the data centre on the same network is asked to disclose nothing.

  • Globally, the AI build-out is on course to drink 9.3 trillion litres of water a year by 2030 — the basic domestic water of 1.3 billion people — and to use more electricity than most nations on earth.

  • The 1% training levy paid by every small employer, tradesperson and farmer — a surplus of about €1.8 billion — was redirected, without consulting a single one of them, into the research arm of the very AI and chip industry that is draining the grid.

  • Our national wealth is an illusion of roughly 60%: Ireland’s headline GDP runs to about 162% of the income that actually stays in the country — the widest such gap in the developed world.

  • A new generation of always-on AI “agents” is projected to multiply computing demand a thousandfold — and no operator will tell you how many litres a single one of them drinks.

  • And the men who own the machines can change country to sidestep one tax vote, while the household cannot move to escape the substation built on the hill above it.

Hold those seven facts in your hand at once. That is the scale. Everything that follows is simply the evidence for each one — and what can still be done about them.

If you only have five minutes

Here is the argument, start to finish. Public things — our water, our electricity grid, our land, our planning system, even our own information — are being quietly handed to private hands that answer to no one here, and the cost is handed back to us. Data centres are the clearest example, because you can stand on a road in Little Island and look at one. But they are only the sharpest edge of a wider pattern, and the same pattern turns up in housing, in nursing homes, in childcare, in the price of your electricity.

None of it needed a conspiracy. Every door was opened by an ordinary policy choice, made quietly, while most of us were busy. And because they were opened by choice, they can be closed by choice — but only while there is still time, and on some of these the time is shorter than people think.

That is the whole of it. Everything after this is the evidence for it, the proof that it can be turned, and the means of turning it.

If you are young, or new to all this

You do not need a degree in planning law to follow this, because at heart it is simple, and you already know it. If something belongs to everyone — a river, the air, the water out of the tap — then everyone should get a say in what happens to it, and everyone should be able to see what is being done with it. That is the whole idea. It is the same idea as asking a question in class instead of taking the teacher’s word for it.

Right now that is not how it works. Big projects can use enormous amounts of water and electricity without ever telling the public how much. The rules a family must follow to build an extension are often lighter for a project a thousand times the size. And communities often find out what is happening only after the real decisions are made. That is not fairness, and you are allowed to say so.

The most useful thing you can do is the simplest: keep asking why, and refuse to accept “that’s just how it is” as an answer. Ask at home. Ask at school. Look up what is planned near where you live — this booklet shows you how. Write to your councillor or TD; they are obliged to answer you. And tell other people what you find, because the one thing this machine cannot survive is being seen clearly by enough people at once. Curiosity is not a small thing here. It is the whole mechanism.

How the rest is laid out

You do not have to read this front to back, and most people won’t. It is built so you can come in at whatever depth suits you and leave with something useful.

The evidence. The full case, sourced — the planning contrast, the water and energy mechanism, the sites, the global pattern. Read it straight through, or dip into one part when you need the facts for a meeting, a submission, or an argument at the dinner table.

What you can do. The practical tools — how to file an information request you were told was pointless, how to find out what is planned in your own county, who is already fighting and how to stand with them, and what real alternatives look like.

Why it is worth it. The wider frame and the long view — history that proves ordinary people have turned things that looked unturnable, the imagined choice of where this leads, and the reasons for hope that are earned rather than wishful.

The appendices. Ready-to-send templates and an open letter you can adapt. Lift them, change the names, send them. That is what they are for.

One last thing before you start. Nothing in here asks you to be a hero, and nothing in here waits on a single great leader arriving to fix it. It is built on the opposite — on a great many ordinary people each doing one small, stubborn thing, the way clover changes a whole field from underneath without anyone’s permission. If that sounds too modest to matter, that is exactly the belief this booklet is written to undo. Read on.

How to read this — including if you have ten minutes

This is a long document, and deliberately so: the case it makes is cumulative, each part one more panel of a single picture that only convinces when the panels are seen together. But it is built to be entered at any point, and read in any order.

If you have the time, read it through. If you do not — if you are a journalist, an official, an MEP or a Commission staffer with ten minutes — read this spine: Part 1 (the architecture of capture, and how each objection is intercepted) and its two-track table; Part 3 (who pays — water, energy, the leaking pipe and the rationed household); Part 4F (how a data centre now hides in the planning system); Part 6B (what the UN Special Rapporteur already concluded about Ireland); and the toolkit at the head of Part 16. Everything else is the evidence beneath one of those claims.

Every part opens with a one-line summary in italics, so the whole shape can be taken in by scanning the summaries alone. And nothing here asks you to take the campaign’s word for anything: factual claims are footnoted to primary sources, estimates are given as ranges, repeated figures are stated identically throughout, and where something cannot yet be proved it is marked as an open question, not asserted. That is the standard the document is built to — clean hands, in both directions.

Part 1 — The Architecture of Capture

Five acts. The same sequence, every time. Not an oversight — a pattern.

What this chapter is about. This chapter sets out the engine of the whole book: the five-act sequence by which a system arrives as a gift, reverses the flow, embeds itself in law, rewrites the host’s reality, and locks the terms in. It shows that the data-centre build-out is not a series of accidents but a recognisable architecture — and names the documents, the bodies and the mechanisms that hold it together.

“Power concedes nothing without a demand. It never did and it never will.” — Frederick Douglass

The five-act sequence

What the Ennis case showed, in real time, is how the machine works — the same sequence, repeating across every contested development.

  • The application — lodged quietly, framed as progress, jobs, investment.

  • The objection — local people, ecologists and community groups raise the questions the process is built not to answer.

  • The permission — granted. Conditions vague. Disclosures absent. The hardest questions deferred or ignored.

  • The legal challenge — years of community time, money and energy spent trying to make the system do what it claims to do.

  • The door closes — leave to appeal refused. The machine moves on.

Two tracks, one town

The rules do not treat everyone the same. A householder and a café owner carry the full weight of scrutiny; the strategic development moves through a faster, lighter, less challengeable track. Laid out side by side, the contrast is the system itself.

Planning factor Ordinary householder Small business / SME Data centre (strategic infrastructure)
Who decides Local authority Local authority An Coimisiún Pleanála — bypasses local authority
Process route Standard planning application Standard planning application Strategic Infrastructure Development — fast-tracked nationally
Public notification Newspaper notice; neighbours notified; any member of public can object Newspaper notice; neighbours notified National oral hearing; local objections carry less weight
Decision timeframe 8 weeks 8 weeks 24 weeks (An Coimisiún Pleanála)
Right to appeal Yes — to An Coimisiún Pleanála within 4 weeks Yes — within 4 weeks Limited — Futureproof Clare fought 6 years and lost, June 2026
Water impact assessment Standard drainage conditions may apply Standard conditions may apply No mandatory water-consumption disclosure; no thermal discharge limits
Energy impact assessment N/A for most residential Basic energy-efficiency standards No mandatory assessment; 80% renewable only for post-Dec 2025 applicants
Community benefit required None None None required; no waste-heat obligation; no water disclosure; no local-employment conditionality
Planning extension New application; full scrutiny New application; full scrutiny Extension granted without fresh environmental assessment (EPark, March 2025)
Max fine for environmental breach Standard enforcement Standard enforcement EPA maximum: €5,000 (Blackwater fish-kill investigation, Aug 2025)

The point is not that data centres face no rules. It is that the rules bend for scale.

CRU/2025/236 — the grandfather clause

In December 2025 the Commission for Regulation of Utilities lifted the Dublin data centre moratorium and announced new rules — including an 80% renewable-electricity requirement. It looked like accountability.

It wasn’t. The new rule applies only to applications lodged after 12 December 2025. Anything submitted before that date proceeds under the old regime — no 80% requirement, no catch-up.

The trap is neat. EPark DC1 can be called “the first grid connection under the new CRU rules” — proof, supposedly, that the system works — while being exempt from the most important rule in it. The new rules did not stop the old regime. They laundered it. That is not a loophole. It is architecture.

LEAP — the Large Energy User Action Plan (January 2026)

LEAP reframes data centre expansion as national economic strategy. It was created by Cabinet decision, with no democratic mandate. It ends the moratorium and introduces “Green Energy Parks.” It carries no mandatory curtailment, no binding water disclosure, and no excess-heat obligation. The tap stays fully open.

The KPMG report

DETE commissioned KPMG to produce “The Value of Data Centres to Ireland,” published 2 June 2026 — at a cost to the taxpayer of €104,938 (confirmed by Minister Peter Burke in a Dáil reply to Deputy Aidan Farrelly). Ministers used its headline figures — some 876,000 jobs “enabled,” over €100 billion “underpinned,” 94,000 jobs “at risk” — to argue the build-out must continue. Read closely, the report gives itself away: a survey base of five respondents; a benefits-only analysis that, by its authors’ own admission, is not a full cost-benefit analysis. And the most telling detail of all: the tender document (obtained by The Journal Investigates) explicitly asked KPMG to assess the “potential for increases in electricity prices” from further data-centre development — yet rising electricity prices were absent from the final report. The single cost most likely to land on ordinary households — the one the State itself commissioned — went missing. The full anatomy of this report, and the wider pattern of consultancy failure behind it, is set out in Part 4D.

The EU secrecy clause

Microsoft and the lobby group DigitalEurope pressed the European Commission to insert a confidentiality clause into the EU Energy Efficiency Delegated Act — and their suggested wording was adopted almost word for word. The effect is more precise, and more damning, than simple silence: the Commission still gathers each facility's energy and water figures under the 2023 Energy Efficiency Directive, but only aggregated national totals ever reach the public — the individual numbers are sealed, beyond the reach even of a freedom-of-information request. Ten legal scholars told the cross-border journalists who exposed the clause that this may itself breach the Aarhus Convention's guarantee of public access to environmental information; member states were, on the reporting, urged to refuse such requests outright. In a letter to Environment Commissioner Jessika Roswall, 35 MEPs including Lynn Boylan asked the Commission to delete the Microsoft amendment and restore transparency. Ireland has also missed multiple transposition deadlines for EU Directive 2023/1791.

Brussels — the lobbying behind the pattern

The Microsoft/DigitalEurope confidentiality clause isn't a one-off. It's a single visible instance of a much larger, well-documented lobbying operation, and it's worth naming the scale of it plainly.

The money. The digital industry's annual lobbying spend in Brussels grew from €113 million in 2023 to €151 million in 2026 — a 33.6% increase in two years, according to Corporate Europe Observatory and LobbyControl. Meta alone spends roughly €10 million a year, making it the single largest corporate lobbyist in the EU; Amazon spent €7 million in a single year. Together, Amazon, Apple, Google, Meta and Microsoft spent more than €35 million in 2025 specifically to influence EU tech regulation — a research group, SOMO, found Big Tech's Brussels lobbying footprint now exceeds finance, energy and chemicals combined. On average, Big Tech companies hold more than one lobbying meeting a day with European Commission officials.

Where it landed. In November 2025, the Commission proposed the "Digital Omnibus" — a package weakening both the GDPR and the AI Act, framed as "simplification." Corporate Europe Observatory and LobbyControl compared the Commission's actual proposed text against Big Tech's lobbying submissions and found the two matched closely, clause by clause, including new provisions that would make using personal data to train AI systems a default "legitimate interest" rather than something requiring separate justification. Politico described the episode as marking the end of the "Brussels effect" — the idea that EU rules set the global standard — writing that Washington was now setting the pace on deregulation in Europe instead.

The astroturf layer. During earlier negotiations over the Digital Markets Act, a group calling itself the Connected Commerce Council (3C) sent EU lawmakers voting recommendations on behalf of a list of small-business signatories. When journalists contacted those signatories, most had no memory of signing anything. The group was being run by a lobbying firm, funded by Google.

The alliance with the far right. The same Corporate Europe Observatory/LobbyControl analysis found Big Tech companies increased their meetings with far-right MEP groups in the European Parliament ahead of the Digital Omnibus push, and that groups including Patriots for Europe publicly called on the Commission to go further still. This campaign takes no position on any political party as such — but a documented pattern of a foreign industry courting the political fringe to weaken environmental and privacy protection is exactly the kind of fact this booklet exists to put on the record.

And the conflict of interest closest to home. In September 2025, the Irish government appointed Niamh Sweeney — who spent nearly eight years at Meta, including as head of public policy for Facebook Ireland and then as WhatsApp's director of public policy for Europe — as one of three commissioners at Ireland's Data Protection Commission. This is not a minor domestic appointment. Because Meta, Google, Microsoft and TikTok all have their EU headquarters in Ireland, the DPC is the lead EU privacy regulator for most of Big Tech — and from 2026 it also gained enforcement powers over high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act. Max Schrems of the privacy group noyb called it "mind-blowing" that a "former lobbyist of Meta" had come to the DPC. Sweeney has said she recuses herself from matters touching Meta or Stripe (where she later worked) from her employment years there. Worth sitting with regardless: the DPC is now owed more than €4 billion in fines that have never been collected. Over the past six years it has levied €4.04 billion, almost all of it against multinational technology firms — and of that, just €20 million has actually been paid, according to the Irish Times. The rest sits in appeal, tied up in the Circuit Court confirmation process the law requires before a single euro can be collected. None of it, the DPC says, has ever been written off as uncollectable. It is simply, indefinitely, owed. The pattern in Part 4B of this booklet — the people who move between regulating an industry and working for it — is not a national quirk. It is the same architecture, replicated at the EU's most consequential privacy and AI regulator, staffed from Dublin.

Sit with what the appointment actually says, past the individual involved. Nobody had to bribe anyone, break a law, or hide a payment. The industry did not need to capture the regulator by force — it simply became close enough, for long enough, that a senior figure from inside it was the obvious, qualified, unremarkable choice to help run it. That is what embedding looks like when it succeeds: not a scandal that gets caught, but an appointment that barely raises an eyebrow domestically, because the distance between "the industry" and "the state" has already quietly collapsed. The EU's own AI Act enforcement, and the privacy rights of hundreds of millions of Europeans, now run partly through a Dublin office one insider-appointment away from the companies it is meant to hold to account. That is not a reason to move on. It is a reason to ask, loudly and often, why this was ever allowed to look normal — and to keep asking until it stops.

The acceleration timeline

When What happened
2016 EPark, Little Island earmarked for data-centre use. A 60MW grid connection to the Little Island substation reserved.
2017 Grid connection pathway reserved — a decade before the CRU rules that would have required 80% renewable.
August 2025 Blackwater fish kill — 42,000 fish dead. Maximum EPA fine: €5,000. Zero accountability.
July–Sept 2025 Cork County hosepipe ban. East Cork communities including Ballincurrig and Lisgoold still receiving tankered water after it lifted.
December 2025 CRU/2025/236: Dublin moratorium lifted. Grandfather clause locks in exemption from the 80% renewable requirement for all pre-December 2025 applicants.
January 2026 LEAP created by Cabinet — no democratic mandate. No mandatory water or waste-heat obligations.
February 2026 AI Advisory Council advice paper: facial recognition is “high-risk” and needs independent evaluation. Government proceeds with the Garda Recording Devices Bill anyway.
March 2026 CRU transparency deadline missed. No consequences, no enforcement.
30 April 2026 Health Information Act 2026 signed. Framework for secondary use of citizens’ health data — research, AI training, “innovation.”
April 2026 AI Advisory Council disbanded — including OpenAI Ireland and AWS representatives. FOI pending.
27 May 2026 Garda Recording Devices Amendment Bill 2025 at Second Stage. Facial recognition. ANPR. No judicial oversight.
4 June 2026 High Court refuses leave to appeal in the Ennis / Art Data Centres case. Six-year community fight ends. 200MW on the Shannon catchment proceeds.
7 June 2026 Ireland misses the EU Pay Transparency Directive deadline — a delay lobbied for by IBEC.
2026 DC1 EPark live — 12MW. First grid connection under the new CRU rules. Exempt from the 80% renewable requirement via the grandfather clause.
1 July 2026 Ireland takes the EU Presidency. Eight Council configurations to chair at once. No independent AI advisory body.
July–August 2026 Open consultations close: National Nature Restoration Plan (1 July), National Bioresources Strategy (22 July), Galway Wastewater Strategy (24 July).

The timeline is not destiny. Every line above is a domestic decision — Irish fast-tracking, Irish silence. None of it touches the law that sits above the national planning system. EU environmental law — the Habitats Directive, the binding requirement for an Appropriate Assessment in a protected site, the right to environmental information under the Aarhus Convention — cannot be fast-tracked away by any Irish bill, the Critical Infrastructure legislation included. National acceleration meets a European floor it is not free to lower. That floor is where the leverage lies, and Parts 2, 6 and 6B show how to stand on it.

Compliant on paper, slow exactly where it counts

First, refuse the easy version — because the easy version is wrong, and a wrong claim is a gift to the other side. It would be satisfying to say Ireland is a serial law-breaker, drowning Brussels in infringements and about to be fined into the sea. It would also be false, and this campaign does not trade in claims that collapse the moment a hostile reader checks them. On the aggregate measures the European Commission actually publishes, Ireland is unremarkable. The EU's own Single Market and Competitiveness Scoreboard put the average transposition deficit — the share of directives a member state is late writing into national law — at around 1.1% across the Union in 2025, with the conformity deficit about the same; both sit above the 0.5% target the EU set itself, which is a polite way of saying that being late with EU directives is not an Irish vice but a European habit. Ireland sits roughly where the pack sits — neither the star of the class nor its dunce, an ordinary middling performer on the numbers. Anyone who tells you the sky is about to fall in fines has not read the scoreboard.

But the average is not where the story is. The story is in the selection. A state's aggregate compliance score tells you how much law it is late on. It does not tell you which law — and once you ask which, the pattern stops looking neutral. Lay the two columns side by side. Ireland moved smartly to stand up the machinery that serves the industry: the AI Office of Ireland, built to be operational by its EU deadline of 1 August 2026, the domestic plumbing that lets the compute economy run. In the very same window it was more than twenty months late transposing the NIS2 Directive — the cybersecurity law that would oblige hospitals, energy networks and public bodies to secure themselves — so late that on 8 July 2026 the Commission referred Ireland to the Court of Justice over it, one week to the day after Ireland took up the Presidency of the Council of the EU. It missed the Pay Transparency Directive deadline of 7 June 2026, a delay IBEC had openly lobbied for and which this booklet documents earlier. It missed, more than once, the reporting deadlines under the Energy Efficiency Directive 2023/1791 — the very law that would have made data-centre consumption visible. And whether it transposes the Environmental Crime Directive (2024/1203), due on 21 May 2026, robustly or grudgingly is a live and pressing question taken up later in these pages.

Name the pattern plainly, because it is the whole thesis in miniature. The laws Ireland races to implement are the ones that build and legitimise the compute economy. The laws it lets drift are, with a consistency that is hard to read as coincidence, the ones that would secure the public, expose the industry's resource use, or make an employer disclose what it pays a woman. That is a distinction no scoreboard can capture, because on the scoreboard a late cybersecurity law and a late fishing-quota regulation count exactly the same. Selective speed is a political choice wearing the costume of administrative delay. The tell is not how much Ireland is late on. It is what it is never late on.

The fines, tallied honestly — and the offset nobody models

So how frightened should a citizen actually be of European fines? Less than the urgency-merchants would like — and the honest arithmetic is worth doing, because fear of Brussels is one of the levers used to justify hurrying the build and deferring to investors. Here is how the money works. When the Commission takes a member state to the Court of Justice under Article 260 of the Treaty, it asks for two things: a one-off lump sum to punish the breach that has already run, and a daily penalty that keeps accruing until the state complies. The Commission's own 2026 figures set the dials — a flat rate of €3,560 a day for the penalty, a reference lump sum of about €3.3 million — each then scaled to the country's capacity to pay and adjusted by the Court for how serious and how long-running the breach is. These are not astronomical sums. They are built to sting a treasury, not to bankrupt one.

What does it look like when it actually lands on Ireland? There is a real, recent example, and it rhymes with this whole booklet. In November 2019 the Court of Justice fined Ireland €5 million, plus €15,000 for every further day of non-compliance, in the Derrybrien case — because the State had let a wind farm be built in the Slieve Aughty mountains in Co. Galway without the environmental impact assessment EU law required, a failure that in 2003 sent half a million cubic metres of peat sliding down the mountainside and killed an estimated 50,000 fish in the Owendalulleegh river. The daily penalties ran for years; the total passed €17 million before the file was closed in 2023. Read that twice, because it is the argument in a single case: the largest EU environmental fine in the history of the State was imposed not for building too little, but for skipping the assessment that would have caught the harm — the exact assessment this booklet keeps asking whether anyone actually carried out.

Now weigh the live exposure honestly — including the part the headlines leave out. The cases open against Ireland today — NIS2 chief among them — are non-transposition cases, the least-punished kind. And here is the fact that quietly dismantles the fear: these fines are very often never paid at all. The overwhelming pattern is that a member state, once hauled toward the Court, finally passes the missing law while the case is still live, and the Commission withdraws before any penalty is imposed — it dropped more than a dozen such cases in a single year for exactly that reason. The infringement procedure is, in practice, less a fine than a countdown: escalating political pressure whose real message is not "pay up" but "the clock has run out on our patience." So the realistic financial exposure from the current crop of Irish breaches, if the State does what it almost always does and legislates before judgment, is plausibly close to nothing in penalties, and at the outside a lump sum in the low single-digit millions. Even a deliberately pessimistic tally — every live case running to judgment, daily penalties ticking for a year — struggles to clear a few tens of millions. That is a real number. It is not a frightening one. It is a fraction of what the State has already spent merely planning the Dublin Metro, before a single sod was turned.

And that comparison is the door to the argument that actually matters. Set the fines — a few million, quite possibly nothing — beside the scale of public value moving in the opposite direction, uncounted and uncaptured, and the asymmetry becomes the whole point. Take MetroLink, since it is the largest thing the State is about to build: an official 2021 estimate of €9.5 billion, with credible ranges already running past €15 billion, sixteen stations threaded through a capital with an acute shortage of land. A metro does not turn a farebox profit — it never will, and this booklet will not pretend otherwise — but it creates enormous value all the same, in the price of every site, every rezoning, every development within walking distance of a new station. On the present arrangements almost all of that uplift is captured privately, by whoever happens to own the land the public's railway has just made valuable. The purse that pays for the line keeps almost none of the value the line creates. It is the same shape, precisely, as the grid finding already in Part 3, where Irish households pay something like ten times what the data centres pay toward the very grid upgrades the data centres require. Public money builds the asset; private interests bank the appreciation.

It does not have to be arranged this way, and in other places it is not. The tools for capturing publicly-created value have names and working examples. Hong Kong's metro funds a large share of the railway itself by developing and capturing the land value its own stations generate — the "rail-plus-property" model — so the network is paid for by the uplift it creates rather than by the fare and the taxpayer alone. Land value capture, public equity in the revenue-generating assets the State underwrites, a sovereign-commons fund that holds a share of what public investment makes valuable — these are the mechanisms Part 4E and Part 18 of this booklet already set out, and Elinor Ostrom's Nobel work on the commons is their academic spine. The idea is not exotic. It is the plainest question a competent steward asks: if the public is paying to create the value, why is the public not keeping a share of it?

So here is the ledger, kept the way a household keeps one — and treat these as illustrative figures, not invoices. On one side, the European fines a citizen is taught to dread: realistically a few million euro, and frequently nothing at all once the missing law is finally passed. On the other, the public value being generated and given away: not millions but billions — in the land uplift around a single metro line, in the grid appreciation the public subsidises for private users, in every strategic asset the State builds and then declines to hold a stake in. A single-digit percentage of the value MetroLink alone will create, captured for the public, would cover every environmental fine Ireland has ever paid to Brussels several times over — and go on paying, year after year, long after the fine is a closed file. The fine is not the threat to the public finances. The giveaway is. We are urged to hurry, to defer, to keep the investors sweet lest Europe punish us — while the thing actually draining the commons is not the punishment we fear but the value we surrender without ever writing it down. Write it down. That is all this section asks. Once the ledger is honest, the fear does the precise opposite of what it was recruited to do.

Sources: Article 260 TFEU financial-sanction mechanism and 2026 figures (penalty flat rate €3,560/day; lump-sum flat rate €1,190/day; reference lump sum €3,323,508, scaled per Member State by a capacity-to-pay "n factor") — European Commission, Communication C/2026/1653, "Annual update of data used for calculation of financial sanctions" (12 March 2026), and the 2023 Communication on financial sanctions in infringement proceedings; the Court retains discretion over the final amount and, in non-transposition cases, cannot exceed the sum the Commission proposes. Derrybrien — Court of Justice of the EU, Commission v Ireland, C-261/18 (12 November 2019): €5 million lump sum plus €15,000/day; total exceeding €17 million; file closed 2023 following An Bord Pleanála's February 2022 refusal of substitute consent; landslide and fish-kill details as reported by RTÉ, The Journal and the Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage. NIS2 referral — European Commission press release IP/26/1499 (8 July 2026), referring Ireland, Spain, France and the Netherlands to the Court of Justice for failing to transpose Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (transposition deadline 17 October 2024); Ireland's National Cyber Security Bill remained in pre-legislative scrutiny at the time of writing, with the responsible minister indicating transposition by end-2026. The pattern of the Commission withdrawing non-transposition actions once the state legislates mid-proceedings is documented by the European Institute of Public Administration (13 such withdrawals in 2018). EU-wide transposition and conformity deficits (~1.1%, both above the 0.5% target) — European Commission, Single Market and Competitiveness Scoreboard, 2025. MetroLink cost estimates (€9.5 billion 2021 midpoint, credible range €7.16–12.25 billion, later projections beyond €15 billion; over €360 million spent pre-construction) — Transport Infrastructure Ireland, the Department of Transport and the Committee of Public Accounts, as reported by The Irish Times and others (2023–2026). The grid cost-share comparison is sourced in Part 3. Hong Kong "rail-plus-property" value capture — MTR Corporation model, widely documented. Land value capture, public equity and commons governance — see Part 4E and Part 18; Ostrom, E., Governing the Commons: The Evolution of Institutions for Collective Action (Cambridge University Press, 1990). The offset is presented as an illustrative model of value capture, not as a projection that MetroLink will generate distributable profit; every figure is a range drawn from a named public source.

Putting a number on it: an illustrative value-capture model

The claim above deserves more than assertion, so here is the arithmetic — built entirely from public figures, with every assumption on the table and stated as a range. Treat it as the campaign's own model, not a forecast: an honest sketch of an order of magnitude, not a prediction to the euro. Three things are needed to size the prize, and all three are on the public record.

First, how much land value a metro actually creates. The best-documented comparator is London's Jubilee Line Extension, opened in 1999 at a cost of £3.5 billion. A study for Transport for London by Jones Lang LaSalle found that two stations alone — Canary Wharf and Southwark — lifted land values in their immediate vicinity by around £2.8 billion. Estimates for the whole route run far higher: a residential-land study put the uplift near five stations at roughly £9 billion, and the property investor Don Riley, who owned land near Southwark, calculated the total land and property uplift along the line at about £13 billion — comfortably more than three times what the line cost to build. The lesson repeated across the value-capture literature is consistent: a new metro reliably generates land-value uplift of at least the same order as its construction cost, and often several times it.

Second, how much of that uplift the public currently keeps — almost none. On the Jubilee Line Extension, the developers' total contribution to the £3.5 billion cost came to less than 5%. The public built the railway; private landowners banked the appreciation. This is not a London peculiarity — it is exactly what Ireland's own State documents describe. The Department of Housing, introducing its Land Value Sharing measure, conceded plainly that the majority of the benefit from a zoning decision goes to the original landowner rather than to the State, and that the problem had been named as far back as the Kenny Report of 1973 and left unaddressed for the half-century since.

Third, what share it is reasonable for the public to capture — and here Ireland has already answered its own question. When the State sat down to decide how much publicly-created land value it was fair for the public to keep, it did not reach for a radical number. Its Land Value Sharing measure sets a charge of 25% of the uplift from a zoning decision — reduced from an originally proposed 30%, and fixed by law within a band of 20% to 30%; combined with existing development levies and Part V housing obligations, the Department estimated that "greater than 50%" of the combined uplift could, on occasion, flow back to the public. Twenty-five to fifty per cent, in other words, is not the campaign's figure. It is the State's own.

Put the three together — conservatively — and the order of magnitude is unavoidable. MetroLink is a €9.5 billion line (plausibly €15 billion or more) with sixteen stations threaded through a capital starved of housing land. Take the most cautious reading of the comparator evidence — that the line generates land-value uplift merely equal to its own cost, rather than the three-times multiple the Jubilee route managed — and apply the State's own most modest capture rate of 25%. The illustrative ledger looks like this:

Illustrative ledger — one metro line vs the fines Modelled range
MetroLink construction cost €9.5bn (official 2021 midpoint); €15bn+ on later estimates
Land-value uplift created (≈1× to ≈3× cost, per Jubilee Line evidence) ~€9.5bn to €28bn+
Public share at Ireland's own 25% rate ~€2.4bn to €7bn+
Public share at the Department's "greater than 50%" combined figure ~€4.75bn to €14bn+
— set against —
Every EU environmental fine Ireland has ever paid of the order of €20m (Derrybrien, ~€17m, was the largest single one)
Realistic exposure from all current EU cases (NIS2 etc.) a few million; frequently €0

Read the two halves of that table against each other and the whole fear inverts. The captured public value, even on the meanest assumptions in the model, is measured in thousands of millions. The fines the citizen is urged to dread are measured in single millions, and often come to nothing. The public share of one metro line's land uplift, taken at the State's own most conservative rate, would cover every environmental fine Ireland has ever paid to Europe not a handful of times but on the order of a hundred times over — and several hundredfold at the model's upper end. And unlike the fine, which is a closed file the day it is paid, the captured value keeps arriving: site after site, station after station, year after year.

Now say what the model is not, because clean hands require it. These are illustrative ranges, not a costing. MetroLink runs largely through already-developed urban Dublin rather than open country, so its uplift will fall differently from a greenfield rezoning, and the Land Value Sharing charge as drafted targets newly-zoned residential and commercial land, not transit uplift as such — it is used here as the State's own benchmark of a fair public share, not as a statute that would mechanically apply to the metro. The comparator figures are gross, context-specific and methodologically contested. Every one of those caveats moves the precise number around. Not one of them touches the conclusion, because the conclusion does not rest on precision — it rests on the gap between millions and billions, and that gap is fully three to four orders of magnitude wide. The honest headline is not "MetroLink will earn the State €4 billion." It is this: the money the public is taught to fear losing to Brussels is a rounding error beside the money it hands to private landowners, without a murmur, every time it builds something. The fine is the distraction. The uncaptured uplift is the story — and the tools to keep it, from the Kenny Report to the State's own Land Value Sharing measure, have been sitting on the shelf for fifty years.

Sources: Jubilee Line Extension — cost £3.5 billion; approximately £2.8 billion land-value uplift around Canary Wharf and Southwark stations, Jones Lang LaSalle study for Transport for London (2004); approximately £9 billion residential-land uplift across five stations, Lincoln Institute of Land Policy working paper; approximately £13 billion total route uplift, Don Riley, Taken for a Ride (2001); developer contribution under 5% of cost — Transport for London and the historical record. Value-capture precedent: E-Rail / Northumberland County Council rail scheme, reported as the first UK line to fund 25–30% of its capital from land value capture (2021). Ireland's Land Value Sharing measure — the Land (Zoning Value Sharing) Bill 2024 and the preceding General Schemes (December 2021; April 2023): a 25% charge on zoning uplift (reduced from 30%; statutory band of 20–30%), with the Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage estimating that, combined with Part V and development levies, "greater than 50%" of the combined uplift could be captured; the Kenny Report (1973) on active land management, as summarised in that Department's own materials and The Irish Times (April 2023). MetroLink cost estimates as sourced in the preceding section. This model is the campaign's own illustrative construction: it applies the State's own capture rate to comparator-based uplift ranges to indicate an order of magnitude only. It is expressly not a projection that the Land Value Sharing charge would apply to MetroLink, nor that the line will generate distributable profit. All inputs are ranges drawn from named public sources.

The single-minister problem — how each step intercepts the objection

One hand on a lot of levers. A recurring unease in this story is how much can be changed, and how fast, by very few people — sometimes by a single minister — in ways that land on the whole population. It is worth being precise about the machinery, because the precise version is the damning one. A minister does not need to win a long public argument to reshape the ground. A great deal moves below the line of full Oireachtas scrutiny: powers exercised by ministerial regulation or statutory instrument rather than primary legislation (the Miscellaneous Provisions Bill, for instance, leaves the EPA’s decision timelines to be set “by regulation… to make it easier to amend them”); commencement orders, where a minister decides when, or whether, parts of an enacted law actually take effect; and pure Cabinet-decision instruments with no legislative mandate at all — the Large Energy User Action Plan was created this way in January 2026, ending the moratorium without a vote. None of these is illegal. Each is a normal tool. The question is what they add up to.

The objection, intercepted at every stage. Read the sequence in Part 1 not as a list of separate events but as a series of points at which a citizen could have objected — and notice that something closes each one in turn. Pre-legislative scrutiny, the stage where a bill is examined before it is locked, can be waived (as it was for the Critical Infrastructure legislation). Floor debate can be guillotined — cut short by motion — as the International Protection Bill debate was in April 2026. The Strategic Infrastructure and fast-track routes shorten or remove the local objection window that an ordinary applicant would face. A grandfather clause (CRU/2025/236, December 2025) protects everyone who applied before the new standard, so the standard never reaches them. A confidentiality clause — the Microsoft amendment to the EU Delegated Act — removes the very information you would need to frame an objection. The new power to review only part of a licence (Part 4F) lets a project grow in stages without a fresh, whole-site assessment. And when an objection does make it all the way to an appeal, it can be ruled invalid on a technicality — as the appeal against the €1bn Red Admiral approval was in June 2026, over a single missing acknowledgement. By the time the public is invited to engage, the decision is substantially made and the grounds left to stand on have been narrowed one clause at a time.

Say only what can be stood over. It is tempting to call this a plan, and there are people who would profit if you overstated it, because an overstatement is easy to dismiss. So here is the disciplined version, which is worse: none of this requires a single coordinating hand or a corrupt intent to produce its effect. Each step can be defended on its own as “efficiency,” “certainty for investors,” or “emergency.” Their cumulative result — fewer and fewer moments at which an ordinary person can be heard — is real whether or not anyone designed it as a whole. When Justice Minister Jim O’Callaghan, pressed in June 2026 about rushing legislation before the Dáil break, said he would not apologise for enacting legislation of public benefit, he was not confessing to a conspiracy. He was describing, candidly, a settled habit of governing by acceleration. That habit is the thing to name, and it is nameable without alleging more than the record holds.

Where it cannot reach, and how to close the gap. The acceleration runs out at the European floor — Appropriate Assessment, the Habitats Directive, the Aarhus right to environmental information — which no domestic minister can lower (Parts 6 and 6B). And the democratic remedies are unglamorous and specific: make pre-legislative scrutiny mandatory and un-waivable for any bill touching strategic resources; put sunset clauses and Oireachtas confirmation on ministerial regulation-making powers; require that emergency and “exceptional circumstances” exemptions be reported and reviewable. The fix for governing-by-shortcut is not outrage. It is restoring the stages at which a citizen is allowed to say no.

*Sources: Ministerial regulation-making power over EPA decision timelines — Environment (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2025 and accompanying legal analysis (A&L Goodbody; MHC), as cited in Part 4F. LEAP created by Cabinet decision, January 2026 — Department of Enterprise / gov.ie. Critical Infrastructure legislation exempted from pre-legislative scrutiny; International Protection Bill debate guillotined (April 2026); O’Callaghan remarks (“would not apologise for enacting legislation of public benefit”) — RTÉ News, 20 June 2026. CRU/2025/236 grandfather clause (December 2025); Microsoft / DigitalEurope confidentiality amendment to the EU Energy Efficiency Delegated Act; Red Admiral appeal ruled invalid (June 2026) — as sourced in Parts 1 and 4F. The “interception” reading is offered as an analysis of cumulative effect, explicitly not as a claim of coordinated intent by any named person; each mechanism is individually lawful and separately defensible, which is precisely why the cumulative effect deserves naming.

How hidden it is — and how total

Stand back from the five acts and see the size of the thing. The single most important fact about this architecture is the one hardest to photograph: it is hidden by design. Not in a vault — in plain sight, behind dull names and routine procedure, scattered across so many separate decisions, departments and documents that no ordinary person could ever assemble it in one evening. That dispersal is not a side-effect; it is the camouflage. A confidentiality clause here, a grandfather clause there, a “miscellaneous provisions” bill that quietly amends half a dozen unrelated Acts at once, a strategic-infrastructure designation that lifts a decision up out of the county, a planning application filed under a shell company and a generic label — each one is lawful, each one is boring, and each one is invisible on its own. Only laid side by side do they resolve into a single structure. The whole of this booklet is the act of laying them side by side.

The people who did everything right, and the ground that moved under them

Lay one more thing beside the five acts: the calendar of a single, real six-year fight, against the calendar of the rules that governed it. The two do not match, and the mismatch is not an accident of timing. It caught good-faith objectors exactly the way a trapdoor is meant to.

A Clare resident who objected to the Ennis data centre rezoning in January 2019 did everything this booklet asks of its reader. They raised the concern early, before a single brick was planned. They made a submission during the statutory consultation window. They joined 17 others in doing the same. When the planning application came, they were one of nearly 50 formal submissions, backed by 250 signatures gathered from their own neighbours. When permission was granted, they pursued the appeal. When the appeal failed, they pursued judicial review — and won an admission from An Coimisiún Pleanála itself that it had broken the law in granting permission.

That objector began in 2019. The rules that decided whether their persistence would matter did not exist yet. Section 22 of the 2022 Planning Act made appeal, not judicial review, the required first step — commenced in October 2022, three years into their fight. The Planning and Development Act 2024 — a government source told journalists its explicit purpose was to make judicial reviews "a waste of time" — introduced the standing test and the paused-clock rule, and its judicial review provisions commenced in August 2025, six years in. The cost regulations, S.I. No. 200 of 2026, capping what a winning objector could recover, were signed in May 2026 — the same month the final leave-to-appeal judgment in this exact case was being prepared, and one month before it was delivered. The judge who dismissed that final appeal did not merely apply the old rules to an old fight. He applied language — "litigation-by-snippet," "un-compensatable... prejudice" to the developer — that reads as if six years of methodical, escalating, good-faith citizen participation had become, by the rules now in force, indistinguishable from bad-faith obstruction.

No individual step in that sequence targeted this objector by name, and that is exactly the point. Each law was passed for a general reason — efficiency, cost predictability, housing delivery — argued on its own terms, defensible in isolation. But a person who began a lawful fight in 2019, using every tool available to them at the time, finished that fight in 2026 governed by a different, harsher set of tools they never agreed to and had no opportunity to plan around. The state does not need to announce that it is moving the goalposts mid-match. It only needs to keep amending the rulebook while the match is still being played, and let the final whistle fall wherever it falls. A minister does not need the public to notice this happening. The complexity is the strategy — and the wager, every time, is that most people will not trace the dates closely enough to see it. This booklet traces them. The dates are not in dispute; they are on the record, in the Acts themselves and the commencement orders that bring them into force. The reader is invited to check every one.

And it is worth naming plainly what does, and does not, stop this. No constitutional protection does — Article 15.5 bars retrospective effect only for criminal law, not civil or administrative procedure. "Legitimate expectation," the closest doctrine Irish administrative law offers, essentially never succeeds against a general rule change; a citizen has no recognised right to have their case governed by the rules that existed when they started it. The Aarhus Convention's "non-regression" principle — the idea that a state should not quietly weaken access to justice once granted — is the one real hook, and it is exactly what the UN Special Rapporteur invoked in February 2026. But it is a soft, political tool. No Irish court will strike down a costs regulation because a UN official called it regressive. This is not an oversight in the architecture. It is why the architecture works.

Two things do function, and neither one requires waiting for a court to agree.

The first is citizen science and public naming — the very thing this booklet is. A ministerial regulation that is never voted on cannot be defeated in the Dáil chamber, but it can be read, dated, and put next to the calendar of a real person's real fight, in public, under a name and a face. That is not a legal remedy. It is the only remedy available when the legal system itself has been the thing narrowed — and it is available to anyone with the patience to trace the dates, which is precisely the patience this booklet has modelled from its first page.

The second is a straightforward, nameable legislative fix — one any future government or any TD could commit to tomorrow, at no cost and with no plausible objection that survives being said aloud. A transitional saving clause, guaranteeing that any person or group with an open legal proceeding retains the benefit of the procedural rules — standing, costs, timelines — that applied on the date their proceeding began. This is not a novel legal idea; saving clauses of exactly this kind already exist in Irish law for other transitions. Its absence here was a choice, not a gap. Any candidate, in any election, can be asked directly: will you commit to a transitional saving clause protecting pending litigants from retrospective procedural change? A yes or no answer is possible. There is no honest version of "it's complicated."

And there is a moment to use both, now, in daylight, that will not come again for years. Ireland holds the Presidency of the Council of the European Union from 1 July to 31 December 2026 — six months in which European press, European Parliament committees, and counterpart governments are all, unusually, paying attention to Ireland specifically. A Presidency wants a clean story: competent, fair, a good European citizen setting the agenda. It is the single worst six months in a decade for this state to be shown, with dates and documents, quietly narrowing its own citizens' access to justice while hosting the very institutions built to prevent exactly that. Every MEP, every foreign correspondent based in Brussels, every counterpart environment ministry official who reads this booklet's timeline during those six months is a person positioned to ask the question publicly that Ireland would most prefer stayed domestic and unnoticed. Shame, deployed with the same discipline as everything else in this booklet — dated, sourced, undeniable — is not a lesser tool than a courtroom. During a Presidency, it may be the sharper one.

And it is almost certainly not only here. The mechanisms in these pages — the quiet ministerial instrument, the renaming, the public body that will not disclose, the lobby that drafts the “certainty” it then receives — are not uniquely Irish. In every country that has not declared a moratorium, the same incentives are pulling in the same direction, and it would be naïve to assume the same obfuscation of intent is not happening there too. Offer that as what it is — a hypothesis, not a proven claim: this is most likely a global pattern, and Ireland is a case study in it rather than an exception to it. Wherever you are reading this, the right next question is whether it is happening on your own ground, under names you have not yet learned to read.

But say plainly what is exceptional about Ireland, because it is. Few states have rolled the carpet out this far. Here the accommodation has gone beyond welcoming the industry to rebuilding the State around it — contorting planning law, threading enabling provisions through unrelated legislation, lifting the moratorium, designating the build-out as strategic, and channelling the training levy of small employers into its research arm. The consequence is that data-centre and hyperscale-compute interests are now braided into nearly every domain of public infrastructure and public decision — energy and the grid, water, planning, education and research funding, even the words we are given to describe it, as “data centre” quietly becomes “digital hub,” “AI factory,” “campus,” “private wire,” “battery storage.” The same financial logic, mapped in Part 4’s account of asset-manager infiltration, reaches on into housing, nursing homes, childcare, farmland and pensions. This is what it means to say a State has set down its stewardship of its citizens: not that anyone was bribed, but that the public interest quietly stopped being the thing the rules were built to protect, and the investment thesis took its place — renamed, rezoned and woven in so thoroughly that pulling on one thread now tugs at all the others. That is the true scale of it. Naming it is the first act of taking the stewardship back.

Part 2 — The All Too Many Sites (That We Know Of): Cork, Clare, Louth and Beyond

Your harbour. Your investigation. Documented.

What this chapter is about. Here the pattern becomes concrete and local. This chapter walks the actual sites — in Cork, in Clare, in Louth, and beyond — where the infrastructure is being built, and shows how to read a development from its planning footprint outward. It turns the abstract argument into specific places you can stand in front of.

“Think globally, act locally.” — commonly attributed to René Dubos

This is where the argument stops being abstract and lands on a road you can stand on. No national story is honest if it stays in the air. So: the places, the projects, the consequences.

And a necessary caveat in the very title: these are the sites we know of. There is no single public register of data centres in Ireland — no one list, held by one body, that a citizen can consult to see every facility built, permitted, or proposed, with its power load and water draw beside it. What exists instead is scattered across separate county planning files, grid-connection data the CRU holds but does not fully publish, and commercial announcements that appear or don't. The sites documented below are the ones that surfaced — through a planning objection, a press release, a local campaign, an appeal. How many more sit below that waterline, consented quietly on already-zoned industrial land under a name that does not say "data centre," nobody outside the industry and the regulator can currently say. That absence of a complete public picture is not a gap in this booklet's research. It is a feature of the system this booklet describes — and it is the first thing a serious transparency reform would fix.

How to read the sites that follow — because the "how it happened" is the same each time. These are not scattered coincidences. Read across them and a single method appears, repeated county by county, that anyone can now recognise before the concrete is poured. First, a site is chosen that lowers resistance: former industrial land, already zoned, often State-linked, where there is no rezoning fight and little ecological objection — EPark on the old Mitsui Denman site, Premier Periclase on a former magnesia plant. Second, a grid connection is secured early, sometimes years ahead of the rules that would later constrain it, so the project can arrive "grandfathered" and exempt — EPark's 60MW Little Island pathway was reserved as far back as 2017. Third, where the word "data centre" now draws objection, the application leads with something else — an "energy centre," a "sustainably powered enterprise campus" — and lets the computing sit inside it. Fourth, the decision is lifted where possible above the county, to An Coimisiún Pleanála, as Strategic Infrastructure, thinning the local say. And fifth, when someone does object and even wins a point — as Futureproof Clare did, extracting an admission of legal error — the timeline and the costs are arranged so that being right, and being early, and doing everything correctly, still may not be enough. Each site below is a specific instance of that same five-step method. Once you have seen it twice, you can read the next one coming.

EPark, Little Island, Cork (JCD Group / T5 Data Centers)

EPark, at Evergreen Business Park, Wallingstown, holds full planning permission for 508,000 sq ft of data-centre space across three buildings on the former Mitsui Denman site — earmarked for data-centre use since 2016.

  • DC1 — 86,000 sq ft, 12MW grid connection, going live 2026. The first grid connection under the new CRU rules anywhere in Ireland — and exempt from them, via the grandfather clause.

  • DC2 — 99,000 sq ft, about 10MW, cleared.

  • DC3 — 323,000 sq ft, 60MW connection pending. The largest single data-centre connection in Munster — exempt from the 80% renewable requirement because permission predates December 2025.

  • Total planned campus — approximately 92MW if all phases are delivered as described.

The March 2025 extension to the 2016 permission was processed as a technical extension, not a fresh application — no new public notice. EPark is developed by JCD Group (John Cleary Developments), a Cork commercial property company; the campus is a roughly €200m project, with T5 Data Centers as the original partner — a US operator entering Europe through this joint venture, its first facility outside the United States.

And here is the detail that explains the rest. The developers had access to a 60MW grid connection to the Little Island substation, earmarked for delivery as far back as 2017 — years before the CRU’s December 2025 rules. The power pathway was reserved nearly a decade in advance. That is precisely why the project can be brought online now without meeting the new renewable requirement: it was always positioned to arrive ahead of the rules.

Hoffmann Park, Inchera, Little Island

A second Little Island development, with reported phased capacity of up to 35MW. Same harbour, same estuary, same watershed — and the same absences: no renewable requirement, no thermal outfall limits, no water-disclosure obligation. Nobody has been required to assess the cumulative water draw or the combined thermal load of these developments together, on a single Natura 2000 site.

Ennis, Co. Clare (Art Data Centres Ltd)

Two hundred megawatts. Six halls. One hundred and forty-five acres. Futureproof Clare fought it for six years; the High Court refused leave to appeal in June 2026. The same absences apply — no mandatory renewable energy, no water outfall requirements, no thermal discharge conditions. That is not a local planning dispute in the ordinary sense. It is two hundred megawatts on the Shannon catchment, under the same regulatory silence. Ennis’s own domestic route is now exhausted — but the lesson it leaves for every site still in the pipeline is the opposite of despair: national fast-tracking cannot reach EU environmental law. Appropriate Assessment and the Habitats Directive sit above the planning system; Parts 6 and 6B show how to use them.

Drogheda, Co. Louth — the energy centre that carries a data centre (Premier Periclase / “CORE”)

Look north, to Drogheda, for the clearest live example in the country of the disguise this booklet describes — and of a community honestly divided about it.

On the site of the former Premier Periclase magnesia plant at Newtownstalaban, a developer proposes a “Sustainably Powered Enterprise Campus” branded CORE. Read the name and the marketing and you would think the project was an energy-and-regeneration scheme. Read the planning file and the shape inverts. According to the development’s own Environmental Impact Assessment Report, “Phase 1 CORE” consists of an Energy Centre housing nine gas engines, a large battery storage system, and 3 MW of solar panels — and a 25.5 MW data centre building. The energy plant is the headline; the data centre is what the energy plant exists to feed. This is precisely the reclassification traced in the chapter on camouflage: as the words “data centre” became a flashpoint, the application leads with the power infrastructure and lets the computing sit inside it. Nobody hid anything — it is all in the file — but you have to read past the name to see what is being built.

Louth County Council granted conditional permission in June 2025. In July 2025, An Taisce — the National Trust for Ireland, a statutory consultee, not a fringe objector — appealed the decision to An Coimisiún Pleanála, and the appeal was still live as this was written. Its grounds are the argument of this entire booklet, made by a national heritage body: that permission “would facilitate a breach of the emissions reduction obligations under the Climate Act”; that local water supplies and abstraction are a real concern for a water-hungry use; and — most importantly — that authorities are granting data-centre permissions “on a case-by-case basis without adequately addressing the cumulative impacts of energy use,” which An Taisce calls a systemic infringement of the Environmental Impact Assessment process. That is the cumulative-impact failure named at the start of Part 3, identified here in a specific Irish file by the body whose job is to see it.

What makes Drogheda especially honest as a case study is that the community is genuinely split, and this booklet will not pretend otherwise. Local councillors reacted to the appeal with real anger — describing it as “reckless,” “irresponsible” and “counterproductive,” one saying his “blood is up,” another “disgusted” — and their reasons are not foolish: the Premier Periclase site is a long-idle industrial parcel, gas production having been scaled back after the price shocks of 2022, and they see the campus as regeneration, jobs, and the cleaning-up of what one called an “environmental dump.” That case deserves to be heard plainly, because it is the case made in every town the build-out reaches: investment now, on land that is doing nothing.
