Against it, others in the same town — including a People Before Profit representative — backed the appeal, making the point this booklet documents elsewhere: that Irish households pay roughly twice what data centres pay for electricity, and that another large energy user lands its costs on the ordinary bill-payer while the returns go elsewhere. Energy for whom? was the campaign’s question.

The reason Drogheda belongs in this book is not that the objectors are right and the councillors wrong — that is for An Coimisiún Pleanála to weigh, and for the town to argue out. It is that the case shows, in one place, every mechanism the rest of these pages describe: the energy-centre framing that carries the data centre; the cumulative-impact gap that a national body had to appeal to raise; the water and emissions questions; and the genuine, painful local bind between the promise of jobs and the long cost to grid, climate and water. Watch this appeal. However it is decided, the shape of it — what was proposed, how it was named, who had to object to force the whole picture into view — is the shape coming to every county next.

Sources: Premier Periclase Ltd “CORE” development, Newtownstalaban/Boyne Avenue, Drogheda — planning file and Environmental Impact Assessment Report (component breakdown: Energy Centre with nine gas engines, BESS, 3 MW solar PV, 25.5 MW data centre), as lodged with An Coimisiún Pleanála (case file 323134). Louth County Council conditional permission, June 2025; An Taisce third-party appeal lodged 24 July 2025 (Louth Live / Dundalk Democrat; The Irish Times, Dec 2024 on the emissions/Climate Act grounds; Drogheda Life). Councillors’ reactions and the People Before Profit response — Dundalk Democrat and Drogheda Life (Feb–Apr 2026). Household-vs-data-centre electricity-cost comparison — People Before Profit Louth, drawing on the grid-share and price figures sourced in Part 3.

The Cork Harbour context

A single average Irish data centre uses up to 500,000 litres of water a day, rising to 5 million litres during a heatwave (NUI Maynooth, 2024).

Cork Harbour is a designated Natura 2000 Special Protection Area and Special Area of Conservation — a flyway for migratory birds, a nursery for salmon, an engine of phytoplankton. The absence of Appropriate Assessment is not a detail. It is a concrete legal vulnerability.

All of this unfolds one year after the Blackwater fish kill of August 2025 — 42,000 fish dead, maximum EPA fine €5,000, zero accountability — and while a fifty-year wastewater strategy for the harbour is still being drawn up.

When the next one comes, spare us the bewilderment

Say this now, before it happens again, so that it cannot be said innocently afterwards: there will be another fish kill. The conditions that produce them have not changed, and on the present course they are worsening — strained catchments, stressed wastewater systems, abstraction climbing in dry weather, a harbour whose fifty-year wastewater strategy is still only being drawn up. So when the next river turns, let us refuse the ritual that follows every one of these events: the furrowed brow, the statement of concern, the question asked as though it were a riddle — how could this have happened?

It is not a riddle, and that is the whole point. The pressures on these waters are named in the State’s own reports. They have been flagged, again and again, by anglers, ecologists and community groups, who are told each time that the process will look into it. And when the looking is done — if it is done at all — the result is the one already on the record in these pages: forty-two thousand fish dead in the Blackwater in August 2025, a maximum available penalty of five thousand euro, and no accountability anyone can point to. A regime in which the cause is rarely established in public, and the consequence, where there is one, is set so low as to be a rounding error, is not a regime baffled by fish kills. It is one that has decided in advance not to look too hard. A penalty that small is not a deterrent. It is a price — and a cheap one.

So we will not accept the bewilderment, and we are saying so in advance. When the next kill comes, the only honest questions are the ones the facade exists to avoid: what was the cause, who established it, by what independent process, and why did it take a catastrophe to ask? Was there an independent investigation, or only an internal one? What enforcement followed — and if none, why not? This is not a demand for outrage. It is a demand for the thing withheld every previous time: a transparent, independent investigation, a cause placed on the public record, and a penalty that means something. Until those exist, “how could this have happened?” is not a question at all. It is an alibi — and we have heard it before.

A note on this section: it makes no claim as to the cause of any specific fish kill, and names no party as responsible — to do so without an established, published finding would be both unfair and unverifiable. The argument is about the documented pattern of recurring fish kills, the inadequacy of current environmental penalties, and the absence of transparent, independent investigation; the demand is for exactly that investigation and accountability. The recurrence of fish kills and the low statutory ceiling on penalties are matters of public record — the establishing of cause and responsibility in any given case is precisely what the campaign asks be done properly, not what it presumes to assert.

The commons principle — locals first

Cork Harbour is not a backdrop. It is a national asset — one of the oldest natural harbours in the world, with public water, public foreshore, and publicly designated land. Much of the ground now being turned to data-centre use is former industrial and State-linked land, served by grid and water capacity that the public built and paid for.

Strategic national resources — land, water, foreshore, grid capacity — are a commons. Local communities and citizens should have first claim on how they are used, before they are handed to foreign corporations for private, extractive purposes.

A small, concrete illustration of the opposite principle sits on one of the most spectacular headlands in the country. Old Head of Kinsale — a dramatic promontory of genuine natural and historical significance — is home to one of the world's most exclusive golf resorts, operated by Ashbourne Holdings, which recorded pre-tax profits of €14.93m on revenues of €5m in 2024. In 2026 it lodged plans with Cork County Council for a major expansion: more luxury guest accommodation, a spa, additional bedrooms, and clubhouse alterations, following earlier permissions in 2020 and 2024. There is nothing unlawful here, and a successful business employing local people is not, in itself, a grievance. The point is narrower and structural: this is what the enclosure of a commons looks like in practice — a headland of exceptional public value progressively organised around access for "wealthy golfers from around the globe," expansion by expansion, while the ordinary public's relationship to one of Ireland's great coastal places is quietly, legally, narrowed. Every commons is enclosed the same way: not by a single dramatic seizure, but by a sequence of individually reasonable permissions, each one small, each one granted, until the sum is a place that used to be everyone's and is now effectively someone's. The commons principle is the demand that someone, somewhere in that sequence, be empowered to ask the question the process never asks: not "does this expansion meet the planning conditions?" but "who is this place ultimately for?"

*Sources: Old Head Golf Links / Ashbourne Holdings 2026 expansion application to Cork County Council, as reported by the Irish Examiner (David Forsythe), July 2026; company revenue and pre-tax profit figures (€5m revenue, €14.93m pre-tax profit, 2024) as reported in the same coverage; prior permissions granted 2020 and 2024.

The ask is simple: a statutory community-and-public-benefit-first test for the disposal or major reuse of strategic State and harbour land, and for the allocation of large grid and water connections — so locals get first claim on national property before it is committed to private extraction.

Part 3 — Who Pays, Who Benefits

Every major project has a hidden ledger. The story is told as growth, investment, innovation. But every decision has a cost, and the question is always the same: who pays it, who takes the benefit, and who was never asked?

What this chapter is about. This is the chapter that follows the money and the resources. Line by line, it lays out the hidden ledger behind the boom — the electricity drawn from the grid, the drinking water poured through cooling systems, the nitrogen the farmer is restricted on while the server hall is waved through, and the land quietly changing hands. The throughline is one question asked of every figure: who carries the cost, who pockets the benefit, and who was never consulted at all.

“The opposite of poverty is not wealth; the opposite of poverty is justice.” — Bryan Stevenson

A note on cumulative impact — why the whole is the point

Before the figures, one idea that the rest of this chapter depends on. A single project can be assessed on paper and still be harmful in reality, if it is only ever judged in isolation. Water draw, heat load, grid demand, traffic, emissions and pressure on a habitat do not arrive separately in the living world — a river does not experience one abstraction licence at a time — so they should not be assessed separately on the page. The real question is never whether one application, taken alone, can be made to look manageable. It is whether the combined burden on a catchment, a grid, and a community has been measured as a single whole.

This is not a campaigner’s invention; it is the established legal and scientific standard. Environmental assessment law explicitly requires that a project be considered “in combination” with other plans and projects. Yet the present system too often does the opposite: it breaks the burden into pieces — this substation, that abstraction, the next phase as a separate application — until no single assessment, and no single regulator, is responsible for the total. That fragmentation is the recurring mechanism this whole booklet documents. Hold it in mind as you read the ledger that follows: each line is troubling on its own, but it is the sum that tells the truth.

Electricity

Irish households pay among the highest electricity prices in the EU — around 40c/kWh, well above the EU average. Data centres accounted for the overwhelming majority of increased demand since 2015 and are forecast to reach roughly 31–33% of the national grid by 2030. Households pay a higher unit rate than data centres. And the PSO levy — paid by every household and SME — funds the renewable infrastructure that grandfathered data centres then benefit from without having to match it.

By 2026 the figures are no longer abstract. Eurostat’s data for the second half of 2025 puts Irish household electricity at 40.42 cent per kilowatt-hour — the highest in the European Union, almost 40% above the EU average, around €480 a year more than the typical European home. And it is still climbing: Flogas raised standard variable electricity by 10.9% from 20 July 2026, Electric Ireland by 8% from 1 July, with more than 300,000 households already behind on their bills.

Why the highest in Europe? Not one villain, and it is worth saying so plainly. Gas still generates over 40% of our electricity, so the gas price largely sets ours; the grid is islanded, with only two interconnectors and the French link not due until 2028; a dispersed population makes the network dear to run. Stacked on top of all that is something uniquely Irish: data centres now draw over a fifth of the State’s electricity — against roughly 1.5% globally, the highest share of any country reported in the world — and that is what drives the grid upgrades and the high-cost emergency generation bought in to plug the gap.

Here is the part that belongs on a poster. Lynn Boylan has pointed out that households pay roughly ten times what data centres pay toward those grid upgrades — the very upgrades the data centres require. And a Friends of the Earth and Beyond Fossil Fuels report by Dr Seán Fearon modelled the drain: around €715 million pulled from the Irish economy between 2015 and 2023, and, in another energy shock, as much as €1.6 billion over the coming decade. Those are modelled figures, not invoices — but they are sober ones.

The water tells the same story in a different key. Uisce Éireann spent the summer of 2026 telling households their usage was “unsustainable” — shorter showers, watering cans, fix the dripping tap — while its treatment plants, by its own account, have run at full capacity for years, and roughly a third of treated water still leaks back into the ground before it reaches a tap. In November 2025 the regulator fined the utility €20 million for missing its leakage-reduction target. Read that twice: the State’s own regulator fined the State’s own water company for losing the water, in the same season the public was told to ration it.

That is the pattern under all of it. The costs are socialised onto the kitchen table — the highest bills in Europe, the water you are asked to do without — while the use, and the benefit, accrue to a fast-growing industry that pays a fraction toward the grid it strains. We do not have a shortage. We have a question of who the system is run for. Trevelyan’s corn, 2026.

Water

Irish operators face no legal obligation to publicly disclose water-consumption volumes. There is no Water Usage Effectiveness reporting requirement. Thermal discharge returning to waterways, and any chemical content of that discharge, is unmonitored and undisclosed.

Why no one can tell you — the blind spot, assembled one lawful move at a time

Ask the simplest question — how much water does the data centre near me use? — and you will be passed in a circle, not because anyone is lying, but because the answer was taken apart and the pieces were put in rooms that do not talk to each other. Walk the circle once, slowly, because the design only becomes visible when you see the whole of it at once — which is precisely the thing it was built to prevent.

Start at home, with the utility. Uisce Éireann can only see water it supplies through the public mains. Ask it, and it will give you that slice honestly — the roughly 810 megalitres a year it once put on the record was based on just twenty-four connected sites. But water drawn straight from the ground through a private borehole, or from a river, is not the utility's business at all; it is the Environmental Protection Agency's. The utility's own words are that private sources lie "outside its remit." So the instant a site sinks its own well, it falls off the utility's map — and no dishonesty was needed to make it disappear.

Move to the register, which looks like the cure and is only half of one. Since August 2024 any abstraction of twenty-five cubic metres a day must be registered with the EPA, and two thousand or more needs a full licence; the register has been public only since March 2025. A data centre cooling from an aquifer would sit far above both thresholds, so in principle the trail exists. In practice the rule is barely a year old, and no data centre currently appears on it for groundwater — an absence that could mean several things, is itself worth a formal question, but is not an answer.

Now climb to Brussels, where the real move was made — and made in the part of the law almost nobody reads. The 2023 Energy Efficiency Directive looked like transparency arriving: it obliges data centres to report their energy and water use, and it holds that such information should, as a rule, be public. But a directive only sets the aim; the detail is filled in afterwards, in a technical instrument few citizens ever open — here, the Commission's Delegated Regulation of March 2024 on the data-centre rating scheme. That is where the tweak went in. During consultation, Microsoft and the lobby group DigitalEurope submitted near-identical wording to classify every individual facility's figures as commercially confidential — pushing, in their own submissions, beyond the Commission's draft, so that the data could not even be reached by a freedom-of-information request — and the Commission adopted it almost word for word. The duty to report survived intact. The numbers were simply sealed on arrival.

Then the two side-doors were shut. Only aggregated national totals are published, so you may learn what every Irish data centre drew together, but never what the one in your townland drew alone — and facility level is the only level at which a community can hold a particular site to account. And member states were quietly instructed to keep the individual figures confidential and to refuse public requests for them, closing the national route before anyone walked down it. Ireland, for its part, missed the deadline to bring the directive into domestic law more than once. Six moves, each lawful, each dull, each defensible on its own: wrong body, thin register, a directive that promised openness, a delegated act that quietly revoked it, an aggregation that blurs the site, and a standing instruction to say no. Stack them, and the citizen who asked a one-sentence question meets a wall with no single builder.

Which is the real lesson, and the real remedy. The reason this works is that no one is charged with reading across the instruments. The TD votes on the directive and hears "transparency," and never sees the delegated act that unmakes it. The councillor approves the mains connection, and never sees the borehole. The committee waves through a Miscellaneous Provisions Bill — three of the most quietly dangerous words in the Oireachtas, a single bill that amends a dozen unrelated Acts at once — and no one traces where each amendment lands two years downstream. This booklet has already argued, in the language of network medicine, that harm in a complex system is invisible if you only ever inspect one part; the governance version is the same illness. So the single most valuable thing the State could commission is not another strategy document but a standing systems-analysis function — a small, well-briefed unit whose entire job is to read every bill, every delegated act, every "technical" tweak and every miscellaneous-provisions clause against all the others, and to ask the one question no individual desk is ever asked: where does this lead, once you add it to everything already in force? Until someone is paid to connect the dots, the dots will go on being drawn, deliberately, in separate rooms — and the people's representatives will keep voting for the aim while missing the mechanism. It is not that the politicians are stupid. It is that no one has been handed the whole map, and the whole map is the one thing this design exists to withhold.

Sources: division of responsibility between Uisce Éireann (mains) and the EPA (abstraction), and the ~810 ML / 24-site mains figure — as sourced earlier in this Part, via the Dublin Inquirer (2026). EPA abstraction register — Water Environment (Abstractions and Associated Impoundments) Act 2022 and 2024 Regulations (registration ≥25 m³/day; licence ≥2,000 m³/day; register public since March 2025). The EU chain — Energy Efficiency Directive 2023/1791; Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/1364 of 14 March 2024 (data-centre rating scheme); the Microsoft/DigitalEurope confidentiality amendment adopted almost verbatim; only aggregated national data published; member states instructed to withhold — as sourced in Part 1, from the Investigate Europe cross-border investigation (with the Guardian, Le Monde, El País, Die Zeit, The Journal, EU Observer and TechPolicy.Press) and the Corporate Europe Observatory / AlgorithmWatch analysis (April 2026). Named legal assessments that the clause breaches the Aarhus Convention — Prof. Luc Lavrysen (former President of the Belgian Constitutional Court), Prof. Kristina Irion (University of Amsterdam) and Prof. Jerzy Jendrośka (nineteen years on the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee), via Investigate Europe. The standing systems-analysis proposal is offered as a constructive institutional remedy, consistent with the network-medicine argument made earlier in this Part.

The Shannon pipeline — a nation’s third-largest lake, on a 172km steel spine to Dublin

In December 2025, Uisce Éireann submitted the largest planning application in the history of the State: the Water Supply Project, Eastern and Midlands Region. Strip away the bureaucratic name and the plan is this — abstract water from the Parteen Basin on the Lower Shannon, immediately downstream of Lough Derg, Ireland’s third-largest lake; treat it at Birdhill, Co. Tipperary; and pipe it 172 kilometres through Tipperary, Offaly and Kildare to a new reservoir at Peamount, Co. Dublin, feeding straight into the Greater Dublin Area network. The stated capacity is up to 300 million litres a day. The stated cost is €4.58–5.96 billion. Preliminary works are expected to begin in 2027.

Uisce Éireann’s own justification is housing and population growth — Dublin’s over-reliance on a single source, the Liffey, and a projected 34% rise in demand by 2044. That justification should be stated plainly and fairly: this is not, on the public record, a project Uisce Éireann has said exists to serve data centres. But the same public record contains an adjacent, undisputed fact this booklet has already established: data centres and the wider large-energy-user category already draw a fifth to a third of the State’s electricity, and Irish operators face no obligation to disclose their water draw at all. Two facts, sitting next to each other, neither one proving the other: a 172km pipeline is being built to solve a real and growing water deficit, in a country that is simultaneously handing out grid connections and water allocations to an industry that will not say how much it takes. Readers can draw their own line between them. This booklet draws none it cannot support.

What is beyond dispute is the scale of what is being moved, and the speed with which the decision is being taken out of local hands. This is a Strategic Infrastructure Development, meaning it bypasses the individual county councils along its route — Tipperary, Offaly, Kildare — and goes directly to An Coimisiún Pleanála for a single, binding national decision. It is the same fast-tracking architecture this booklet documents everywhere else: the decision moved above the heads of the people who will live with it.

Sources: Uisce Éireann, Water Supply Project Eastern and Midlands Region — planning application submitted to An Coimisiún Pleanála, December 2025 (water.ie); Uisce Éireann news release, 19 December 2025; Irish Construction News, 12 December 2025; Eolas Magazine, 3 March 2026. Readers pursuing this through FOI or AIE should confirm the live An Coimisiún Pleanála case reference directly on pleanala.ie before submitting — case numbering for Strategic Infrastructure Development applications of this kind changes as a project moves through stages.

Reclaiming the waterways: stewardship, not scarcity

The water we are told to ration is also water we are letting degrade. In June 2026 the EPA reported little overall improvement in water quality, with just over half of monitored rivers and lakes in good or better biological condition. The cause it names is not a mystery: excess nutrients — nitrogen and phosphorus — from agriculture, wastewater discharges and run-off from land, which feed algal blooms that strip oxygen from the water and choke the habitat for everything else. Forty-three per cent of river sites carry too much nitrogen, worst in the east and south-east. Be precise about the source, because precision is the campaign’s armour: this is farming and wastewater, named by the State’s own agency — a separate question from how much water the data centres draw, which is its own undisclosed scandal.

The other side of the nitrogen ledger: farmers restricted, data centres accommodated

One sector measured and squeezed; another waved through. There is an asymmetry in this story that East Cork, of all places, feels in its bones. Irish farming is being held to account on exactly the environmental grounds this booklet cares about — and in part rightly, because agriculture is named by the EPA as the single largest pressure on water quality, and excess nitrogen and phosphorus are real harms. But look at how differently the two extractors are treated. Farmers face binding, enforced limits: Ireland is one of only three EU states with a nitrates derogation, and when the EU tightened it in 2023 — dropping thousands of dairy farmers from 250 to 220 kilos of nitrogen per hectare — stocking rates had to fall, with farm organisations including Macra na Feirme warning it would push already-dear land further out of reach of the next generation. Government modelling has floated cutting the dairy herd by around a tenth; chemical-nitrogen use is to come down by a quarter or more. These are measured, mandated, consequential restrictions on a traditional livelihood, imposed in the name of water and climate. Meanwhile the data-centre sector grew to more than a fifth of the national grid — 22% of all metered electricity in 2024, more than every household in the country combined, and still climbing — with no binding carbon limit, no mandatory water disclosure and no enforced cap, accommodated precisely where farming is constrained. Farm organisations have put the contrast as a choice the State seems already to have made: dairy cows or data centres.

And the answer the land itself already knows. There is a deeper rhyme here, and it is not decoration. The thing Irish dairy farmers are turning to in order to cut their chemical nitrogen is white clover — sown into the swards, pulling nitrogen out of the air and fixing it into the soil, feeding the grass without the bag of fertiliser. The nitrogen fixer is not a metaphor this campaign borrowed from somewhere else; it is in the fields of Coachford and Limerick right now, doing the patient, unglamorous, underground work the whole campaign is named for. The point is not that farming is blameless — it is not, and the rivers say so. The point is fairness in how the State applies its measuring stick. If agriculture can be held to a nitrogen budget, enforced to the hectare, then a data-centre sector drawing the country’s water and more than a fifth of its power can be held to a water-and-carbon budget too. Measure both. Restrict both. Or admit that the rule was never really about the river.

The measuring stick, and where it never points

They will not measure both — and you can already see which way the stick swings instead. Watch where regulatory attention travels, because it tells you who the system is built to manage. It is not swinging toward the data centre's water. It is swinging toward the vitamin bottle in your own kitchen. In 2026 the European Commission moved at last to set harmonised maximum permitted levels for the vitamins and minerals in food supplements — dose ceilings that the 2002 Food Supplements Directive always envisaged but never delivered. The Commission's own "Have Your Say" register puts the public consultation in the third quarter of 2026 and a final regulation in the first quarter of 2028, with the ceilings anchored to the European Food Safety Authority's tolerable upper intake levels. A leaked working model, described by regulatory specialists who have seen it, would fix each nutrient's ceiling by taking its safe upper limit, subtracting what an ordinary diet already supplies at the ninety-fifth percentile, and splitting the small remainder between supplements and fortified foods — nutrient by nutrient, down to the milligram. The precision is already visible in the positions member states have tabled: some countries today permit up to 1,000 mg of vitamin C, while France has proposed a ceiling of 208; Germany has pushed a vitamin D limit of 20 micrograms against EFSA's own upper level of 100; EFSA itself has recommended roughly halving the tolerable ceiling for vitamin B6, from 25 milligrams a day to 12. Be fair about the reason, because fairness is the whole method of this campaign: the safety logic is genuine. Some nutrients — vitamin A, vitamin D, iron, B6 — really can harm at sustained high doses, and a single EU rulebook really would end an incoherent patchwork in which a supplement sold freely in Dublin is treated as contraband in Berlin. This is not, whatever the louder campaigns insist, a pharmaceutical plot, and this booklet will not pretend that it is. There is even a legitimate argument the other way — that ceilings set too low could deepen Europe's real micronutrient shortfalls, a concern raised by nutrition bodies and by Irish health-food retailers pointing to the Directive's own Article 5, which requires the limits to weigh dietary need and not merely the risk of excess. But that, too, is an argument being had in daylight, on the record, about a jar of tablets.

But hold that precision up against the silence next door, and the asymmetry is the whole story. The same machinery that can move to fix an adult's daily magnesium to the milligram — a low-risk decision, made in a person's own kitchen, about their own body — cannot tell you how many litres of water the data centre in the next townland draws from the ground, from which catchment, or what chemistry sits in the coolant it may return to the river. That is not an accusation; it is the State's own position, already set out in this Part. Ireland's water utility has said it holds no information on data centres' private borehole use because it lies outside its remit; the environmental regulator's abstraction register lists none of them; operators face no legal duty to disclose their water-consumption volumes at all, and the chemical content of any thermal discharge goes unmonitored. One draw is metered to the drop and rationed by public notice in a dry summer. The other is taken on trust, and reported — where it is reported at all — by the company itself, on a sustainability page of its own design.

This is what sovereignty actually turns on, and it is not the flag on the building. Sovereignty is the direction and the grain of governance — whose choices are made legible and managed, and whose are left in a comfortable, profitable shadow. On the present arrangement a citizen's body is rendered transparent to the State to the last microgram, while the multinational's infrastructure — its water, its power, the forever-chemicals that can sit in industrial coolants and fire-suppression systems — stays opaque even to the regulators nominally charged with it. That inversion, the powerful rendered unaccountable and the ordinary rendered fully legible, is not a glitch in the system this booklet documents. It is the system's signature.

And to be scrupulous, because the standard demands it: the answer is not less regulation. The European Union is, on chemicals, among the most serious regulators on earth — its move to restrict the entire PFAS family of forever-chemicals across the whole economy is one of the largest such actions ever attempted anywhere. So the honest complaint is not that Brussels regulates too much; the machinery to compel disclosure plainly exists, and on chemicals it is being wielded. The complaint is that this same resolve is not pointed at the campus with anything like the force it brings to the pill bottle. The campaign's request is therefore the opposite of the "cut the red tape" cry, and it is a modest one: bring to the water-hungry multinational at least the seriousness the system already brings to a jar of magnesium. Measure the powerful with the same instrument you have calibrated so finely for the powerless. That is not over-regulation. It is the bare minimum of a rule that means what it says.

Sources: EU harmonised Maximum Permitted Levels for vitamins and minerals — Food Supplements Directive 2002/46/EC; public consultation planned for Q3 2026 and a final regulation targeted for Q1 2028, limits to be anchored to EFSA tolerable upper intake levels (European Commission "Have Your Say" initiative 12750, "Food safety — vitamins and minerals added to food (minimum and maximum levels)"; AGRI-FISH Council conclusions, December 2025). The "residual amount" working model (safe upper limit minus 95th-percentile dietary intake, split between supplements and fortified foods) is described from a leaked draft by regulatory specialists and is not adopted Commission policy — reported by FoodChain ID (J. Le Bloch) via Nutrition Insight and by NutraIngredients, June 2026. Member-state positions cited (vitamin C permitted up to 1,000 mg vs a proposed 208 mg; a proposed vitamin D limit of 20 µg vs EFSA's upper level of 100 µg) and EFSA's recommended reduction of the vitamin B6 upper level from 25 mg to 12 mg/day — NutraIngredients (June 2026) and EFSA scientific opinions (2023), as compiled by Eurofins. Proportionality counter-argument (Article 5 of Directive 2002/46/EC requires weighing dietary need, not only excess) — Alliance for Natural Health Europe and Health Stores Ireland's "Save Our Supplements" campaign, 2026. Irish data-centre water non-disclosure — as sourced earlier in this Part, drawing on the EPA, Uisce Éireann and reporting by the Dublin Inquirer (2026): no abstraction-register entries for data centres, no utility visibility of private borehole use, no mandatory water-consumption or Water Usage Effectiveness disclosure, and thermal-discharge chemistry unmonitored. PFAS in data-centre cooling, semiconductors and fire suppression, and the EU's universal PFAS restriction proposal under REACH — Environmental & Energy Study Institute, Sierra Club and trade/industry reporting (2025–2026); noted here to show that EU chemical-accountability machinery exists and is being used, so the argument is one of even application, not of deregulation. This section states the vitamin-safety rationale as legitimate and explicitly excludes the "pharmaceutical conspiracy" framing advanced by some supplement-sector campaigns as unsupported.

The bigger sovereignty: our data, and who holds the keys

Widen the lens from the pill bottle to the whole arrangement, and the same inversion is everywhere. If sovereignty is the grain of governance — whose choices are made legible and whose are left in shadow — then the deepest version of the question is not about water at all. It is about who owns the infrastructure the country is being wired into, where its control sits, and where its profits go. The answer is not comfortable. In 2023, eighty-eight per cent of all Irish corporation tax was paid by foreign multinationals, and fifty-seven per cent of it by just ten companies. The digital estate these firms run on Irish soil is directed from elsewhere and its returns flow elsewhere, and every new dependency ties the knot a little tighter. That is a sovereign decision being made by drift rather than by choice, and it deserves to be named as one and made in daylight — before we deepen it, not after.

And the promises that are supposed to protect us rest on ground that is quietly shifting. Citizens are told their data is "anonymised," as though the word were a wall. But under EU law there is a moving line between data that is merely pseudonymised — still yours, still re-identifiable with a key, still protected — and data deemed anonymous, which falls outside data-protection law altogether. Recent European court rulings have made that line depend on who is holding the data, opening new pathways for it to be pooled and reused, precisely as the Union builds large frameworks for exactly that reuse. Researchers who have studied re-identification are blunt that stripping out every means of tracing a person back is close to impossible at big-data scale. This is not a claim that a promise was knowingly broken. It is that the promise leans on a classification the law is loosening at the very moment the machinery for pooling is being switched on.

Here is the trap that a cyber-security strategy could walk us straight into. Ask what it means to "secure" our public services if the securing is done by binding them ever more tightly to concentrated, privately owned, foreign-controlled infrastructure. You would have hardened the walls of a house you no longer hold the keys to. Resilience that deepens lock-in is not resilience; it is a more comfortable cage — a surveillance-and-dependency economy in which the State cannot walk away from its own systems because it no longer controls them. The thing meant to keep us safe becomes the thing that owns us. That is the risk that must be put on the table before the strategy is signed, not discovered after.

And it matters, for all of this, who is doing the guarding. For a sector that has to be held at arm's length, the arm's length is the whole game. Ireland already knows this: it wrote a cooling-off period into the Regulation of Lobbying Act, barring senior officials from lobbying for a year after they leave office, and it keeps a public register of who lobbies whom. The tell is in what happened next — the original rule had no teeth, no way to enforce the cooling-off at all, and the State had to come back in 2023 to bolt on real sanctions. A guardrail only gets reinforced when something has been leaning on it. This booklet names no individual and imputes nothing to anyone; it says only that where the same faces move between the industry and the offices that must regulate it, the measured, discerning distance that good governance requires is exactly what erodes — and that any citizen can go to the register and see the traffic for themselves.

But none of this is an argument against a digital state. It is an argument for owning one — and there is proof it can be done. After it was hit by one of the first nation-scale cyber-attacks in 2007, Estonia did not retreat from technology; it built a digital government around sovereignty instead of dependency. Its backbone, X-Road, is open-source and published for anyone to inspect; the data stays with whoever it belongs to rather than being pooled; it is shared only with consent; and every single access is logged, so an Estonian can see who looked at their records and when. The principle that the State may ask for a piece of your data only once is written into law. The core runs on a state-owned cloud. And a "data embassy" in Luxembourg holds a backup of the nation's most critical data under the same inviolability as an embassy building, so that even invasion could not erase the country's ability to function. It is not paradise — the ID system is run by private contractors under state watch, and scholars argue about how far the model travels — but it settles the only question that matters here. The choice was never data or no data. It was ours or theirs. Estonia kept the keys. The standard to hold ourselves to is not whether we host the most data in Europe, but whether we still control the infrastructure that holds it — and right now, on water and on so much else, the honest answer is that we have handed the keys across the counter and called it investment.

Sources: Irish corporation-tax concentration (88% from foreign multinationals; 57% from ten firms, 2023) — CSO/Revenue figures as reported by TechPolicy.Press (2025), and Central Bank of Ireland analysis of ICT-firm tax revenue. Anonymisation/pseudonymisation and the recipient-relative test — EU General Court ruling of 26 April 2026 (SRB case) as analysed by Freshfields and Pinsent Masons; Article 29 Working Party guidance on re-identification risk; European Health Data Space secondary-use provisions. Vendor lock-in and public-service autonomy are stated here as a governance risk to be guarded against, not as an accomplished fact. Revolving door — Regulation of Lobbying Act 2015 and the Regulation of Lobbying and Oireachtas (Allowances to Members) (Amendment) Act 2023 (one-year cooling-off for Designated Public Officials; sanctions of up to €25,000 and lobbying bans commencing June 2024); public register at lobbying.ie; regulated by the Standards in Public Office Commission. This passage names no individual. Estonia — X-Road (open-source, published under the MIT licence), the "once-only" principle in law, the state-owned G-Cloud, and the Luxembourg data embassy (authorised 2015): e-Estonia, Nortal, LOTI, Privacy International, and Google Cloud's account of data embassies; caveats on private operation of the e-ID and on replicability from Privacy International and policyreview.info. The judicial-review narrowing that runs parallel to all of this is documented earlier in this booklet in the account of the Clare objector and the Planning and Development Act 2024.

The worked example: Meta, in three moves

If the pattern in this Part sounds abstract, one company draws it in hard lines — and lawfully, in the open, which is the whole point. Take Meta, whose European operations are headquartered in Dublin and whose lead data-protection regulator, for the entire European Union, is therefore Irish. Watch it do, inside a single year, the three things this booklet keeps warning about: reach deeper into ordinary people's private lives, move to silence the insider who objected, and outspend everyone in shaping the rules meant to hold it. None of it is secret. All of it is on the record. That is exactly why it repays a close reading.

First, the reach into the camera roll. Through 2025 and into 2026 Meta rolled out a feature — tested first in the United States and Canada, then offered in the EU and UK — that asks Facebook users to let it "cloud process" the photos on their phone, including pictures they have never posted anywhere, uploading them to Meta's servers and analysing them down to the facial features. Meta already trains its AI on users' public posts and photos going back to 2007; in Europe you may opt out of that, in the United States you may not. On the private camera-roll photos, Meta said they were "not used to improve AI models in this test" — but, pressed, declined to rule out training on them in future. That refusal is the tell. The line between what you chose to make public and what you kept to yourself is precisely the line being dissolved, one helpful "suggestion" at a time.

Second, the silencing of the one who spoke. Sarah Wynn-Williams ran Facebook's global public policy from 2011 to 2017. In 2025 she published a bestselling memoir, filed whistleblower complaints with the US Securities and Exchange Commission and Department of Justice, and testified before the Senate. Meta's answer was not to sue her for libel — it pointedly did not — but to obtain an emergency order from a private arbitrator barring her from promoting the book or saying anything "disparaging" about the company, true or not. By 2026 that order had been stretched so far that when she sat on a festival stage in Wales in total silence for an hour, on her lawyer's advice, Meta moved to have her sanctioned for it, while pursuing penalties of $50,000 for each alleged breach — counting each book sold as a separate one. In June 2026 she sued to have the gag struck down as an unconstitutional restraint on speech. Meta's position belongs here too: that she signed a non-disparagement clause in exchange for a large severance, that an arbitrator has already found she breached it, and that her book is false and misleading. But strip the dispute to its mechanism and it is stark — a company using a private, closed process to silence a person who had already gone to Congress and the regulators. Set that beside the Irish law this booklet describes elsewhere, the Protected Disclosures Act, which exists precisely to shield the worker who reports wrongdoing, and you are looking at two opposite theories of what a whistleblower is for.

Third, the spending that shapes the rules. Meta is the single largest technology lobbyist in the European Union, declaring around €10 million a year on the EU Transparency Register — the top spender in a digital-sector lobbying effort that now runs to some €151 million a year and fields more full-time lobbyists than there are Members of the European Parliament, meeting Commission officials on average three times every working day (Corporate Europe Observatory and LobbyControl, 2025). This is neither illegal nor hidden; it is registered — which is the booklet's argument in miniature, because the influence that matters most is the kind that never has to hide. And its fingerprints are on this exact subject: the lobby group DigitalEurope, whose members include Meta, was found by a cross-border investigation to have led the push to classify data centres' own energy and water figures as confidential — the very secrecy clause set out earlier in this Part.

And in Ireland the circle closes, in a way that should trouble anyone who wants a fair referee. In September 2025 a person who had led Meta's Facebook lobbying in its European headquarters from 2015 to 2019, and who had worked until shortly beforehand at a firm whose registered speciality was lobbying on EU digital rules, was appointed Ireland's Data Protection Commissioner — the office that regulates Meta for the whole of the EU — with no cooling-off period between the two roles. The Irish Council for Civil Liberties has lodged a formal complaint against Ireland with the European Commission, arguing the State failed to secure the independence and impartiality that EU law demands of such a regulator, which must be "above any suspicion of partiality." This booklet names no individual and asserts no actual bias: the appointee came through an official process and holds an office bound by duties of independence. It says only what the record says — that the door between the industry and the body meant to hold it to account was, in this instance, walked through without so much as a pause, and that a system which permits that has confused the appearance of independence for the substance of it. The register where you can read Meta's Irish lobbying for yourself is public, at lobbying.ie. Use it.

Sources: Meta camera-roll "cloud processing" (tested US/Canada 2025; opt-in EU/UK rollout 2026; access to unpublished photos, cloud upload, facial-feature analysis; training on public posts since 2007; EU opt-out; "not used to improve AI models in this test," with future use not ruled out) — The Verge and TechCrunch reporting, 2025–2026. Sarah Wynn-Williams — Careless People (Macmillan/Flatiron, 2025); SEC complaint (2024), DOJ complaint (2025), Senate Judiciary Committee testimony (9 April 2025); emergency arbitration gag order (March 2025) and its enforcement; federal suit filed 25 June 2026 (N.D. Cal.) to vacate the order; damages sought of $50,000 per alleged violation — AWO Agency, Associated Press, The Guardian, KQED and Wall Street Journal reporting. Meta's response (non-disparagement clause signed for severance; arbitrator's finding of breach; Meta's contention that the book is false and misleading; Meta declined to bring a defamation claim) is stated as reported; her underlying allegations are her contested account and are not asserted here as fact. Meta EU lobbying (~€10m/year, top digital-sector spender; ~€151m sector total; lobbyists outnumbering MEPs; roughly three Commission meetings per working day) — Corporate Europe Observatory and LobbyControl analysis of the EU Transparency Register, October 2025; LobbyFacts. Data-centre confidentiality lobbying by DigitalEurope (Meta a member) — Investigate Europe / The Journal Investigates, April 2026, as also treated in this Part's secrecy-clause section. Irish Data Protection Commissioner appointment and cooling-off concern — Irish Times (17 September 2025) and Irish Examiner (24 October 2025); formal complaint by the Irish Council for Civil Liberties to the European Commission (October 2025); Irish lobbying returns at lobbying.ie. No individual is named in this passage; the concern stated is one of process and the appearance of independence, per the ICCL, not a finding of actual partiality.

The science under the clover: a living system is never the sum of its parts

Before the supermarket shelf, one idea worth earning properly — because the whole book leans on it. The clover is not a poetic flourish this campaign reached for because it sounded nice. It rests on what is now among the most firmly established ideas in modern biology: that a living whole cannot be understood, or governed, or healed by isolating one part of it and holding everything else still. A field, a body, a cell, a catchment, a society — each behaves as an integrated system, and the thing that actually drives it is rarely the single variable you were staring at. Pull one thread and the whole web moves. The nitrogen fixer only makes sense as part of that web; so, it turns out, does the harm this booklet spends its pages documenting.

For most of a century, science tried the opposite, and it is worth being fair about why. The method that built modern medicine and physics was reduction: break the system into parts, isolate one variable, control for the rest, find the single cause. For simple problems it was miraculous — one microbe, one disease; one gene, one disorder. For living systems it kept coming up short, because living systems rarely have single causes. The correction, when it came, came from inside hard science, not from outside it. The field now called network medicine — set out by the physicist Albert-László Barabási and the cardiologist Joseph Loscalzo in Nature Reviews Genetics in 2011, and in their Harvard textbook in 2017 — begins from a plain observation: most disease is not the failure of one broken gene but the perturbation of the entire network that links genes, proteins, tissues and organs. Their founding admission is the one that matters here — that reductionism on its own is inadequate to complex disease. The lineage is older than the label — and, as it happens, partly Irish. Erwin Schrödinger built one of the field's founding texts, What Is Life?, out of a course of public lectures he delivered at Trinity College Dublin in February 1943, published the year after; the systems view of the living cell was, in a real sense, first spoken aloud on this island. Ludwig von Bertalanffy set out his General System Theory in 1968; the Oxford physiologist Denis Noble argued in The Music of Life (2006) that there is no privileged "gene for" the organism, only a system playing together. None of this is mysticism. It is the exact opposite — the laboratory, at last, catching up with the whole.

And the whole is what the oldest traditions were always reaching for. Long before anyone had a phrase like "network medicine," many of the world's oldest medical systems — the systematised holism of traditional Chinese medicine among them, and the older Irish ways of tending body and land this booklet honours elsewhere — treated a person as an interconnected whole rather than a bag of separable parts. Be exact about the claim, because the standard of this booklet demands it: this is not an endorsement of their mechanisms. It is not a claim that qi is a literal energy field, or that any particular ancient remedy works. It is the narrower, sturdier point — that these traditions reached for the method, the systems view, millennia before instruments existed to confirm the principle. The metaphysics can be wrong and the instinct still right. The clover, in its wordless way, had been running the same method underground the whole time.

Now carry that one idea across from the cell to the country, because it is the same idea. What network medicine diagnosed in biology — that you cannot see the harm by staring at one isolated part — is precisely the failure of method this booklet documents in governance. A river does not experience one abstraction licence at a time; a grid does not feel one connection agreement at a time. Yet the system assesses them in exactly that way: this substation alone, that water draw alone, the next phase as a separate application, until no single regulator is responsible for the sum. That is not an accident of paperwork. It is reductionism turned into an administrative method — and its effect, as the note on cumulative impact earlier in this Part sets out, and as environmental law's own "in combination" requirement already admits, is that the total harm becomes invisible to everyone, because no one is charged with looking at the whole. Fragmentation is not a side-effect of the extraction model. On this evidence it is closer to the point of it.

Which is, in the end, the difference between the plantation and the field. A plantation is reductionism made into an economy: optimise one variable — yield, shareholder return, compute — and push the cost of everything you ignored onto the web that has to carry it, the soil, the water, the people, the generation after next. It looks productive for a season and dies over a generation, because a monoculture cannot feed itself. The nitrogen fixer is the other answer to the same question: the clover enriches soil it will never individually own, because it was never operating as a single isolated part in the first place. This is why Elinor Ostrom's commons research, set out later in this booklet, keeps arriving at the same finding from a different direction — that a shared resource governed as a whole system, by the people inside it, outlasts the same resource sliced into parts and optimised by a distant owner. The science says the whole is where the truth lives. The plantation's entire method is to stop you seeing it. The campaign's entire method is to add it back up.

Sources: network medicine — Barabási, A.-L., Gulbahce, N. & Loscalzo, J., "Network medicine: a network-based approach to human disease," Nature Reviews Genetics 12 (2011), 56–68 (doi:10.1038/nrg2918); textbook treatment in Loscalzo, J., Barabási, A.-L. & Silverman, E.K. (eds.), Network Medicine: Complex Systems in Human Disease and Therapeutics (Harvard University Press, 2017). Intellectual lineage: Schrödinger, E., What Is Life? The Physical Aspect of the Living Cell (Cambridge University Press, 1944), based on public lectures delivered at Trinity College Dublin in February 1943 under the auspices of the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies; von Bertalanffy, L., General System Theory: Foundations, Development, Applications (George Braziller, New York, 1968); Noble, D., The Music of Life: Biology Beyond Genes (Oxford University Press, 2006). The reference to older holistic traditions, including traditional Chinese medicine, is deliberately confined to their systems-thinking method: no claim is made that qi or any similar concept denotes a literal physical energy field, or that specific traditional remedies are effective. Cumulative-impact and the "in combination" assessment standard — see the note earlier in this Part and the environmental-assessment law cited there. Commons governance — Ostrom, E., Governing the Commons: The Evolution of Institutions for Collective Action (Cambridge University Press, 1990), discussed later in this booklet.

The same shape on the supermarket shelf

The asymmetry above — one sector measured and squeezed, another waved through — has a near neighbour, and the State’s own regulator has now documented it. In June 2026 the Agri-Food Regulator (An Rialálaí Agraibhia) published its second annual supplier survey, a window into relationships it called “relatively secret”: those between the people who grow and make our food and the handful of large retailers and wholesalers who buy it. It drew on more than 1,300 trading relationships between nearly 500 suppliers and eight of the biggest buyers — Aldi, BWG, Dunnes, Lidl, Marks & Spencer, Musgrave, Sysco and Tesco.

Its central finding could be lifted straight from the opening of this booklet. The relationships, it said, “tend to work well on the surface… beneath this, there are wider system-level pressures that shape how these relationships actually function.” Beneath the surface, buyers control “if, when, and how prices change.” Larger players are “more cushioned”; smaller suppliers “absorb greater risk and are less able to pass on inflation.” The risk flows downward, to those least able to carry it — the host arrangement again, described this time by the regulator of the State rather than by any campaigner.

Two details will be familiar to anyone who has read this far, because they are the same two signatures seen in water and in data. The first is the fear of speaking. The regulator believes a “fear factor” stops suppliers reporting unfair practices, so many go unrecorded; suppliers told it, anonymously, that they feared being delisted, and feared that even an anonymised complaint could be “easily guessed” and traced back. “We can’t risk losing the business at this point,” one wrote. The second is the regulator that cannot see. The Agri-Food Regulator has had to rely on data businesses volunteer — and when it tried to report on the egg supply chain, several retailers did not co-operate; when it planned a report on horticulture, it could not produce it, because businesses did not provide the data. Its board recorded “concern and frustration” at its lack of powers. Only from 31 December 2026 will it gain the power to compel commercially sensitive information. Until then — as with the water no one will measure and the data-centre consumption no one adds up — a regulator exists, but the thing it is meant to oversee is shielded behind commercial sensitivity and simply withheld.

In fairness, the picture is not one of collapse: the regulator found a high overall level of compliance, and the share of suppliers reporting an unfair practice fell from roughly one in seven to one in nine year on year. The rules can also sit awkwardly — by design they can treat a billion-euro multinational as a “smaller supplier” if its buyer is larger still — so not every case is David and Goliath. None of that changes the structural finding, which is the point: the power to set the terms sits with the buyer, the risk sits with the producer, and the body meant to watch it spent its first years unable to compel the data it needed. The same shape that runs through the data centre and the water main runs through the trolley you push on a Tuesday.

*Sources: An Rialálaí Agraibhia (Agri-Food Regulator) second annual supplier survey, June 2026, as reported by RTÉ (28 June 2026); compel-powers from 31 December 2026 under the Agri-Food (Price and Marketing Information) Regulations 2025, and the board’s “concern and frustration” over the egg and horticulture reports — The Irish Times (Jan/Mar 2026).

An open question: who is buying the land, and what do they know?

A question worth asking plainly — and not over-claiming. There is a worry, raised often in farming communities, that runs further than anything yet documented: that financial interests use aerial and satellite imagery, soil and yield data to identify struggling farms, and then move in — selling them inputs on poor terms, or buying them out cheaply when they fail. It has to be said clearly that this campaign has found no evidence of such a coordinated practice in Ireland, and will not assert one; to allege it without proof would be both unfair and self-defeating, and it is exactly the kind of unprovable claim that lets opponents discredit everything around it. But three things adjacent to it are real and documented, and together they make it a legitimate thing to watch rather than a fantasy to wave away. First, farmland is increasingly an investment asset: institutional and private-equity money has been moving into agricultural land across Europe, treating it the way Part 4E describes the biggest funds treating housing, grid and water — as a yield, not a place. Second, the data genuinely exists: precision agriculture, satellite imagery and farm-financial information are a real and growing commercial market, and the information gap between a fund and a tired farmer is a real advantage. Third, the vulnerability is real: as the nitrogen ledger above shows, Irish farmers are under genuine financial and regulatory pressure — and pressure is what makes land change hands.

So the honest form is a question and an instrument. Put together, the fair version is not an accusation but an open question — and the booklet’s usual instrument answers it. Who is actually buying Irish farmland, and through what vehicles? The records held by Tailte Éireann, which now keeps the land registry, and by the Companies Registration Office can be read; patterns of purchase by funds, trusts and holding companies can be traced exactly as a data-centre special-purpose vehicle can. If there is nothing there, the records will show it. If there is a pattern, the records will show that too. The nitrogen fixer’s rule holds here as everywhere: do not assert what you cannot yet prove — but do go and look.

The academic term for what this passage describes carefully is "farmland financialisation," and it has its own body of research. Madeleine Fairbairn's Fields of Gold: Financing the Global Land Rush (Cornell University Press, 2020) is the standard scholarly account of exactly the shift this section flags as a documented, watchable trend rather than a proven scheme: institutional and financial capital, since the 2008 crisis, increasingly treating farmland not as a place to grow something but as a yield-bearing asset class to be held. Fairbairn's own focus — the people and institutions doing the acquiring, rather than only the farmers affected — is a useful discipline for this booklet too: the pattern is provable and worth documenting even where, as here, the sharpest specific allegation about it cannot yet be.

*Sources: framed deliberately as an open question. The specific allegation — that firms use imagery to identify and prey on vulnerable farmers — is stated as unproven and is not asserted. The documented surrounding context (institutional and private-equity investment in agricultural land; the growth of precision-agriculture and satellite-data markets; the financial and regulatory pressure on Irish farms) is well reported; see Part 4E on the financialisation of essential assets and the nitrates/derogation sourcing earlier in this Part. Land-ownership tracing routes: Tailte Éireann (land registry) and the Companies Registration Office. Farmland financialisation as an academic field: Fairbairn, M. (2020) Fields of Gold: Financing the Global Land Rush, Cornell University Press.

*Sources: nitrates derogation tightened 2023 (Ireland one of three EU derogation states; ~3,000 of 7,000 derogation farmers moved from 250 to 220 kgN/ha) and farm-organisation reaction — The Irish Times (Sept 2023), quoting Macra na Feirme president Elaine Houlihan, ICMSA and IFA. Proposed ~10% dairy-herd reduction — Farmers Weekly / Irish Department of Agriculture modelling (2023). Chemical-nitrogen reduction of 25–30% — CAP 2023–2027 analysis (UCD Earth Institute). White clover sown to fix nitrogen and reduce fertiliser — National Dairy Council / Teagasc practice, as reported (Lancaster Farming, 2024). Data-centre electricity share — 22% of all metered electricity in 2024 (more than all households combined), the wider large-energy-user category at 31%, and projected toward ~30% by the early 2030s — CSO, Data Centres Metered Electricity Consumption 2024 (10 June 2025) and EirGrid; the CRU self-generation requirement — Oireachtas Library & Research Service (2026). The “dairy cows or data centres” framing reflects documented farm-organisation reactions to the regulatory asymmetry; agriculture’s own role as the EPA-named primary water-quality pressure is stated plainly in Part 3 and is not minimised here.

Two truths sit side by side: the State is failing to protect the water, and a community does not have to wait for it to start. Restoration is something people can do — locally, lawfully, and with public money already on the table. The effective, community-doable measures are well established: plant riparian buffers, the trees and shrubs along a bank that shade the water and trap run-off before it reaches the channel; build or restore wetlands that filter nutrients out of surface water; re-meander and re-naturalise straightened streams so they slow, settle and clean themselves; remove invasive species; and run citizen-science monitoring so the data is yours, not only the State’s. The freshwater pearl mussel — once abundant, now critically endangered in Irish rivers — is the measure of all of it: where the water runs clean enough for the mussel, it runs clean enough for us. Its decline is the warning; its return would be the proof.

And there is money for exactly this. LAWPRO — the Local Authority Waters Programme, working on behalf of all 31 local authorities — runs the Community Water Development Fund, funded by the Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage under the Water Action Plan 2024–2027. Open to community and voluntary groups since 2017, it awards grants (recently in the €5,000–€40,000 range, more than €1 million in total last year) for precisely these nature-based projects. The 2026 round closed in January, so the move now is to ready a project and apply in the next cycle — details at lawaters.ie/cwdf. This is the cleanest kind of action there is: it improves the water, it builds the local group, and it is paid for by the State’s own programme, on the record, with nothing to hide.

We do not, in the end, have a scarcity crisis. We have a stewardship crisis — and stewardship is a thing a community can simply take up.

How the secrecy actually works

This is not an accident. It is architecture. Under the EPA Act 1992, operators can claim commercial confidentiality within their Integrated Pollution Control (IPC) licence applications. Where an IPC licence applies, it is not possible to seek separate air, water or fisheries licences in relation to the same matter — so the IPC licence is the only accountability mechanism. If commercial confidentiality is claimed within it, the public has no alternative route.

Water abstraction licence applications have only been required to be made public since March 2025. That means years of data-centre water abstraction in Ireland preceded any public transparency requirement at all.

The aquifer question — and why the dots stay unconnected

A reader asked the question that unlocks the whole pattern: is this happening because it is so fragmented — are the dots simply not being connected? That is exactly it, and naming it matters, because it is the truth that needs no conspiracy. No secret room is required to take a nation’s water and power. Fragmentation does the work on its own.

Here is how, in plain terms. Responsibility for what a data centre consumes is split across separate bodies, and each sees only its own slice:

  • Mains water is Uisce Éireann’s. They supply it and meter it — but routinely withhold site-level figures as commercially confidential.

  • Groundwater — water taken straight from an aquifer through a private borehole — is not Uisce Éireann’s at all. It is regulated by the Environmental Protection Agency, under a separate law. So a citizen who rings Uisce Éireann to ask about a borehole will, quite reasonably, be told it is not theirs — and may conclude, wrongly, that no one is tracking it.

  • Electricity and grid connections sit with the CRU and EirGrid.

  • Planning permission sits with the local authority and An Bord Pleanála.

  • Industrial strategy sits with the Department of Enterprise.

Each body discharges its own narrow function and assumes the next one is watching the rest. None is charged with adding up the total — with saying out loud that, across all of it, data centres now take a fifth of the national grid while a third of the treated water is lost and households are rationed. The cumulative figure is everyone’s concern and no one’s job. That is why the dots stay unconnected: not because they are hidden in a vault, but because the structure was never built to join them — and, in places, has been quietly rebuilt to keep them apart. The grandfather clause, the confidentiality clause, the consultation timed to fail, the single ministerial signature, the “miscellaneous provisions” bill: each widens a seam that responsibility can fall through. Some of the fragmentation is accident. Some is maintained on purpose — because a total nobody can see is a total nobody can fight.

Which is also the good news, and the whole point of this booklet: connecting the dots is something a citizen can actually do. The system’s power depends on the picture staying in pieces. Assemble the picture, and you have already done the one thing it is built to prevent.

So, to the aquifer specifically. Is groundwater being drawn? The honest answer is that this is checkable, not something to assume. Since 28 August 2024, under the Water Environment (Abstractions and Associated Impoundments) Act 2022 and its 2024 Regulations, any abstraction of 25 cubic metres a day or more (about 150 baths) must be registered with the EPA, and 2,000 cubic metres a day or more requires a full EPA licence with environmental assessment. A data centre cooling itself from an aquifer would be far above both thresholds. The EPA keeps a public, searchable water abstraction register. So the question answers itself through a paper trail: if a site is drawing groundwater at scale, it should appear there — and if a large self-supplying site does not appear, that absence is itself worth a formal question to the EPA (an exemption? a pending application? an unregistered abstraction?).

The clean-hands rule holds, and it makes the argument stronger, not weaker: do not claim a particular data centre is drawing from an aquifer unless you have found its register entry or licence file. Claim instead the thing that is unarguable — that if it is, there must be a traceable EPA record, and the public is entitled to see it. The step-by-step, and a ready-to-send template, are in Appendix A.

Sources: Water Environment (Abstractions and Associated Impoundments) Act 2022 and Regulations 2024, commenced 28 August 2024; registration threshold 25 m³/day, licensing threshold 2,000 m³/day; public abstraction register and licensing files — Environmental Protection Agency (epa.ie). Uisce Éireann regulates only mains supply; self-supplied groundwater abstraction is an EPA matter. Clean-hands boundary: no claim is made that any specific data centre abstracts groundwater — the point is that any such abstraction at scale should be traceable on the EPA register unless exempted, and that the register is public. A third lost before it arrives. And hold the rationing against this number. While East Cork was tankered and the county sat under a hosepipe ban, Uisce Éireann’s own figures show that about 36% of all treated water — more than a third — is lost to leaks before it reaches a single tap, from a network of 65,000 kilometres of pipe aged, on average, between 75 and 95 years: more than double the European norm. The rate has fallen from 49% in 2014, but the regulator still fined Uisce Éireann €20 million in late 2025 for missing its leakage-reduction targets, and Ireland is projected to need 40% more treated water by 2044. Set the facts side by side and the asymmetry is complete: the household is told to save every drop and is rationed when it is dry; a third of the supply runs into the ground from pipes the State left to age; and the data centre, drawing on the same system, is required to disclose nothing. The citizen is squeezed at one end of a pipe that is leaking at the other.

Sources: national leakage of about 36% of treated water (other Uisce Éireann pages state 38–40%), down from 48–49% in 2014, with a 2030 target of 25%; network of ~65,000km, pipes 75–95 years old; €20m CRU penalty (November 2025) for missed leakage targets; projected need for ~40% more treated water by 2044 — Uisce Éireann (water.ie) and the Commission for Regulation of Utilities (RTÉ, November 2025).

The global meter: what the UN now puts on the record

The Irish picture is one node in a global one — and in June 2026 the United Nations put hard numbers on it.

The figures are no longer fringe; they now carry a UN imprint. In June 2026 the United Nations University Institute for Water, Environment and Health published Environmental Cost of AI’s Energy Use: Carbon, Water and Land Footprints — the first UN assessment to look past carbon alone to artificial intelligence’s water and land demands. Its headline findings are these. Global data centres consumed an estimated 448 terawatt-hours of electricity in 2025 — enough that, treated as a country, they would have ranked as the world’s eleventh-largest electricity consumer, behind France and ahead of Saudi Arabia. By 2030 the report projects that to reach 945 terawatt-hours, close to triple the combined annual electricity use of Pakistan, Bangladesh and Nigeria. And the dimension Ireland feels most directly — water — is projected to reach 9.3 trillion litres a year by 2030, enough to meet the basic domestic water needs of all 1.3 billion people living in Sub-Saharan Africa.

Be precise about the per-query figures, because this is exactly where bad numbers breed. The report does not say that one chatbot prompt “equals” some exact cupful of water, and any viral graphic handing you a tidy litres-per-query or litres-per-token figure should be treated with suspicion — because the honest answer depends entirely on the model, the task and the cooling system used. What the UN report does say is comparative and aggregate, and that is firm ground: a single conversational AI query is on the order of 200 times more energy-intensive than a basic text-classification task; generating one AI image can take roughly 1,450 times more; and ChatGPT alone, at an estimated 2.5 billion prompts a day, is reckoned to draw around 383 gigawatt-hours of electricity a year — for one product. The report’s central warning is the one this booklet has been making about Ireland in miniature: that “low-carbon” is not automatically “low-water” or “low-land,” and that judging this industry by a single metric lets it quietly shift its real costs onto the regions — and the water tables — least able to carry them. Its core recommendation is the one the campaign keeps returning to: mandatory, standardised disclosure of carbon, water and land footprints. Measure it, and make the measurement public. And read every figure in this section as a floor, not a ceiling: the report’s own authors warn that AI’s environmental cost is being significantly underestimated, because the public debate fixates on carbon while largely ignoring water and land, and because operators do not disclose site-level water use at all. The honest position is not that these numbers are too high — it is that the real ones are larger, and we are simply not permitted to see by how much.

Sources: M. Aczel, S. Chamanara, M. Matin, A. Farsi, T. Marwala and K. Madani, “Environmental Cost of AI’s Energy Use: Carbon, Water and Land Footprints,” United Nations University Institute for Water, Environment and Health (UNU-INWEH), Richmond Hill, Ontario, 3 June 2026 (doi: 10.53328/INR26RMA002); UNU-INWEH press materials; Business Standard, Moneylife and EurekAlert! reporting (June 2026). The relative figures (~200× and ~1,450×) and the ChatGPT estimate (~2.5bn prompts/day, ~383 GWh/year) are the report’s own. This booklet deliberately does not state a single “litres per query” or “per token” figure: no reliable universal conversion exists — the per-query water-and-energy cost varies by model, task and cooling — and the precise tokens-to-litres conversions circulating online could not be traced to a primary source, so they are excluded under the clean-hands standard. What is cited here is what the UN report itself puts on the record.

The same method, abroad: Memphis

What it looks like when nobody makes them ask. To see where a “build first, account later” road leads when it is followed without a brake, look at Memphis, Tennessee. To power its Colossus supercomputer — which runs Elon Musk’s Grok AI — xAI installed as many as thirty-five gas turbines and ran them, by environmental lawyers’ account, without the air permits the Clean Air Act requires, effectively standing up a private power station in a residential area. It then trucked twenty-seven more unpermitted turbines across the state line into Southaven, Mississippi, to feed a second data centre. The NAACP sued. The plant has the potential to emit more than 1,700 tons of smog-forming nitrogen oxides a year — likely the single largest industrial source in a city already failing federal air-quality standards — within half a mile of homes and a mile of an elementary school, in a community that is disproportionately Black and already carries a cancer risk well above the national average. An xAI manager said the plan for the next site was simply to “copy and paste” the same approach.

And watch the water, because it is the same tell as everywhere else in this booklet. xAI publicly promised an $80 million water-recycling plant as a community benefit — the sustainability story, the mask of virtue. Then local reporting found the project quietly placed on indefinite pause and its staff redirected to finish the data centre instead; Musk’s own answer was that it would be built once the data centre was stable. The benefit was the press release; the data centre was the point. And hold that beside the brand. This is the man whose fortune and public image were built on saving the planet — Tesla, the electric car, a company whose stated mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy. The same man, to power a chatbot, ran dozens of unpermitted methane turbines in a neighbourhood already sick from industrial air, and shelved the clean-water plant he had promised it. The green saviour and the gas-burner are the same person. That is not an aside: it is the mask-of-virtue pattern named earlier in this booklet wearing its most expensive costume — and it is the plainest possible warning against ever taking an operator’s sustainability story on trust rather than on disclosure. It is the Irish pattern exactly — the promised gain announced loudly, the cost imposed quietly on whoever has least power to refuse — playing out at American scale, and accelerated by a federal push to weaken the very clean-air and clean-water rules that are the only brake. The lesson for Ireland is not that we are Memphis. It is that the method is identical, and that the only thing standing between the two places is whether the rules here are enforced or hollowed out. That is a choice — and it is still ours to make.
