Sources: xAI’s unpermitted gas turbines at Colossus 1 (South Memphis) and the 27 turbines at Southaven, Mississippi feeding Colossus 2; the NAACP and Mississippi NAACP lawsuit (represented by Earthjustice and the Southern Environmental Law Center, April 2026); the potential emissions of 1,700+ tons of NOx per year (with particulate matter, carbon monoxide and formaldehyde); the half-mile/one-mile siting near homes and a school; the disproportionate impact on a Black community; Senator Sheldon Whitehouse’s April 2026 Senate Environment & Public Works investigation; and the “copy and paste” remark — Senate EPW Committee, Earthjustice, SELC, NAACP, CNBC, TIME, Inside Climate News (2025–2026). The $80m water-recycling plant and its indefinite pause — Daily Memphian, reported via Data Center Dynamics (2026). The deregulation context — the 2025 US “AI Action Plan.” These are United States facts, offered as a documented parallel; no claim is made that xAI operates in Ireland.

The air, and who breathes it

Memphis is the warning; here is the instrument that measures it. In April 2026 the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, working with the Piedmont Environmental Council, analysed the air-pollution health damage from a single data centre — the Vantage facility in Loudoun County, Virginia, which uses on-site gas turbines in one of the densest data-centre corridors on earth. Their estimate: between fifty-three and ninety-nine million dollars in health damage every year from that one site, driven by an estimated three-and-a-half to six-and-a-half additional premature deaths annually, alongside hospital admissions, asthma attacks and lost work — among the largest health-damage figures ever produced for a single facility. The same Harvard group went on to examine xAI’s gas plant near Memphis. This is not an activist estimate; it is causal-inference epidemiology from the laboratory whose founder, Francesca Dominici, established through decades of Medicare data that there is no safe level of fine-particle air pollution — that the harm rises in a near-straight line from the very first microgram.

Why this reaches Ireland, even though those are American turbines. The mechanism is the same here. Data centres rely on large diesel backup generators — each typically several megawatts, sized well above the connected load — and Ireland’s own CRU connection policy, set in December 2025, permits up to a fifth of a site’s power to come from on-site fossil generation, gas or diesel. Those generators are tested regularly and can run during grid stress. Diesel combustion releases nitrogen oxides and fine particulate matter — PM2.5 — at far higher rates per unit of energy than a modern gas plant. And the health science on those pollutants is not contested: PM2.5 and NOx are causally tied to asthma, COPD, heart disease and stroke, and the World Health Organization’s cancer agency classifies outdoor air pollution and particulate matter as Group 1 carcinogens — the same category as tobacco smoke and asbestos. Hold that still for a moment: the exhaust is not merely an irritant. On the WHO’s own classification, it is carcinogenic.

And Ireland is, of all places, badly exposed. This is the country with the fourth-highest prevalence of childhood asthma symptoms in the world — behind only the UK, Australia and New Zealand — and the fewest respiratory consultants in Europe, around 1.3 per hundred thousand people, behind only North Macedonia. The data-centre clusters sit beside residential Dublin, at Coolock and Clonshaugh, where the generators are closest to homes and schools. We are proposing to add a new, intermittent source of asthma-relevant pollutants in the EU country least equipped, medically, to absorb them.

The smell, named accurately. Residents near these sites do report a smell — and it is worth being precise, because precision is what makes the complaint stick. It is not “methane,” which is odourless; it is the exhaust of diesel generators and, where they exist, gas turbines — the same combustion fumes that carry the NOx and the particulates. A community that notices the generators kicking in is not imagining things. It is smelling the thing the health studies measure.

Now set that beside the sales pitch — because this is the cruellest mask of all. The industry’s headline justification, repeated in every ministerial speech and investor deck, is health: that artificial intelligence will cure cancer, design new drugs, revolutionise medicine. Set that promise against the Harvard arithmetic. The infrastructure being built to deliver the cure emits, into the air of the communities made to host it, pollutants the WHO classifies as causes of the very disease. In Memphis those turbines sit within a mile of an elementary school, in a community already carrying a cancer risk above the national average and disproportionately Black — because pollution, here as everywhere, is sited where there is least power to refuse it. A machine that promises to cure cancer in the abstract while measurably raising the risk of it in the concrete — next to a school, for the people with the least say — is not a contribution to human health. It is the mask of virtue wearing a white coat.

The clean ask. It is modest, and it is exactly what the consultants’ reports leave out: require a local air-quality assessment — NOx and PM2.5 — and ongoing monitoring near data-centre generators as a condition of planning, especially near homes and schools, in the country with among the highest childhood asthma rates in the world. The KPMG report assessed those generators only for carbon; it never once looked at the air the neighbours breathe. A citizen can begin closing that gap with a single AIE request to a named facility: how many generators, of what size, burning what fuel, permitted to run for how many hours — and with what emissions reported to anyone at all? And the deeper answer is not only to refuse the harm but to redirect the spend: the money and power poured into infrastructure that adds to the asthma load could instead fund the respiratory care this country conspicuously lacks. That is the argument this booklet keeps returning to — investment in community is not the soft alternative to growth. It is the better investment, full stop.

Sources: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health analysis (with the Piedmont Environmental Council) of the Vantage data centre, Loudoun County, Virginia — an estimated $53–99 million per year in health damages and 3.4–6.5 additional premature deaths per year — and its examination of the xAI gas plant near Memphis: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health (April 2026). The “no safe level” finding: Francesca Dominici et al., Harvard, on Medicare data (2017 and subsequent). PM2.5 and NOx health effects, and the WHO/IARC Group 1 carcinogen classification of outdoor air pollution and particulate matter: IARC (2013). Ireland’s fourth-highest childhood-asthma-symptom prevalence (after the UK, Australia and New Zealand) and ~470,000 people with asthma: Asthma Society of Ireland; GINA/WHO ISAAC studies; Irish Times reporting. Fewest respiratory consultants in Europe (~1.3 per 100,000, after North Macedonia): Asthma Society of Ireland. CRU December 2025 connection policy permitting up to 20% on-site fossil generation: Oireachtas Library & Research Service, “The future of data centres in Ireland” (20 March 2026); CRU. Clean-hands boundary: the pollutants and Ireland’s underlying vulnerability are established; the specific local health impact of any individual Irish data centre’s generators has not been measured, and is framed here as a serious, uncosted risk and a monitoring demand — never as proven harm to any named community or child. The Virginia and Memphis figures are US-modelled and offered as a documented parallel, not as Irish measurements.

The mechanism, stated plainly: it is not NDAs in the traditional sense — it is commercial-confidentiality claims made within licences that the EPA grants and enforces, which keep outfall chemistry and abstraction volumes out of public view.

Sources: EPA Act 1992; EPA IPC licensing framework (epa.ie); water abstraction licensing publication requirement confirmed March 2025 (Money Guide Ireland / epa.ie). Verify current publication status at epa.ie before formal quotation.

LAWPRO, and the gap

The Local Authority Waters Programme’s work at catchment level is careful, methodical, and real — the citizen science, the river monitoring, the community engagement, all of it matters. But knowing what is happening upstream, it can feel like watching people patch a crack in a dam wall while a far bigger burst goes unaddressed further up the river.

That is not LAWPRO’s failure. It is the failure of a regulatory architecture that hands commercial confidentiality to operators and then asks citizen scientists to fill the gap with test kits and goodwill.

Clean evidence, not guesswork

Where this campaign uses estimates, they are labelled as estimates, given as ranges, and based on peer-reviewed research and published infrastructure data. The point is simple: if any figure here is wrong, the correction exists on servers the companies own. They choose not to publish it.

Under the doctrine of unclean hands, a party cannot seek remedy for a situation created by its own conduct. Companies that demand public trust while refusing public accountability cannot then turn around and contest the estimates their own non-disclosure made necessary.

Declare your water

To every data-centre company operating or building in Ireland: declare it. All of it. Publish, per site and per month —

  • how many litres of water you take in;

  • how much you discharge;

  • the temperature and content of that discharge;

  • and exactly how much you recycle and reuse.

Households already get a water bill and a meter reading. The largest water users in the country should be no different.

The employers’ levy, redirected — and the research money that followed the same tilt

A note before the numbers: this section began as a lead that had to be almost entirely rebuilt. The claim that reached this booklet — that Ireland’s universities have been “completely captured” by the data-centre industry — came with real figures wrapped in a verdict the figures do not support. The figures checked out, almost to the euro. The verdict did not. What follows is only the part the primary sources will stand over, which turns out to be sharper than the accusation it replaces.

This booklet’s whole argument is that capture works through ordinary process, not conspiracy — and the research-funding version is a textbook case, provided you resist the urge to over-tell it. Two movements of public money in 2025 and 2026 are worth setting side by side. Neither is secret; both were announced with pride by the same department; and the honest question they raise together is narrower, and harder to wave away, than any claim that the colleges have been bought.

Start with who actually pays for “workforce training” in Ireland, because most people get it wrong. The National Training Fund, set up by the National Training Fund Act 2000, is built from a levy on employers — one per cent of reckonable pay, raised from an original 0.7 per cent in steps between 2018 and 2020. Employees pay nothing into it. Its statutory purpose is narrow and plain: training for people in work, help for jobseekers, and identifying the skills the economy will need. Over the boom years the levy took in far more than the Fund spent, and a surplus piled up — roughly €1.8 billion by the end of 2024, a figure the Comptroller and Auditor General had already flagged as well beyond any prudent reserve. Money sitting still is money that attracts a plan.

In 2025 the plan arrived, and it was lawful, deliberate, and — depending on who you ask — either overdue or a quiet raid. The National Training Fund (Amendment) Act 2025, signed by the President on 23 December 2025 and commenced that January, unlocked a package of about €1.485 billion from that surplus for the tertiary-education and research sector across 2025 to 2030 — current funding for higher and further education and apprenticeships, plus a capital uplift explicitly earmarked for healthcare, veterinary and further-education skills facilities. The responsible minister was careful, and correct, to stress that the Fund’s statutory purpose was not being changed. This matters, and it is why the lurid version fails: the capital money genuinely is going to nurse and vet and apprenticeship training, not laundered into server halls. Anyone who tells you the healthcare places are a data-centre Trojan horse is telling you a story, not a fact.

The real grievance is narrower than “capture,” and you don’t have to invent it — the employers made it themselves, on the record. ISME, which represents small and medium businesses, publicly opposed the move. Its case was simple and pointed: employers put roughly a billion euro a year into this levy and see only a fraction of it back as co-funded training, and — in its own words — “SMEs are not paying a levy to provide funding support for universities.” They said they had not been properly consulted on the redirection. And the Bill that made it possible was granted a waiver from pre-legislative scrutiny by the relevant Oireachtas committee — the ordinary step at which such a change would be examined before it becomes law, skipped. That is the defensible point, and it is the booklet’s recurring shape exactly: not that a billion euro was stolen, but that a charge levied on every employer in the country for one stated purpose was pointed at another, with the levy-payers saying they were not asked and the scrutiny stage set aside. Consent and process — not villainy — is where the argument lives.

Now set the research money beside it, because the tilt is the same and this half is where the caution has to be strongest. On 10 June 2026 the State announced €460 million for seven new national research centres under a network called Rinn, to run to 2034. The single largest award — €121,752,497, more than a quarter of the whole — went to a national centre for artificial intelligence and data science. A further €500 million is to be leveraged from more than 200 industry partners, over 100 of them multinationals. In the government’s own framing, the investment is tied to attracting foreign direct investment and to the National Digital and AI Strategy, and Research Ireland’s strategy is candid that commercialisation, spin-outs and enterprise partnership are among its aims. Put plainly: the biggest single slice of a national research push is placed on AI and data science, heavily co-funded by the same multinationals whose physical infrastructure — the data centres — this booklet spends its length examining.

And here is the brake, because without it this becomes the very thing it is criticising. This is not proof that Irish universities have been “captured,” and this booklet will not say that they have. The Rinn network spans energy-system decarbonisation, quantum, medical devices, pharma and semiconductors as well as AI — it is not an AI programme wearing six disguises. The AI centre’s own director describes it as ethical, responsible, interdisciplinary work, explicitly weaving arts, humanities and social science into the technical core, and it succeeds two long-established, internationally respected research centres rather than appearing from nowhere. Industry co-funding of university research is entirely ordinary and frequently valuable; a state backing strategically important science is doing its job, not betraying it. Casting the minister as the “architect” of a “capture” would be precisely the defamation-by-framing this booklet refuses on every other page, and it is refused here too. What survives all of that is a fair and pointed question, not a verdict: when the largest single research bet a state makes is placed on the very sector whose footprint the same state is meant to be regulating, and when the levy that helps fund the wider pipeline was redirected with the levy-payers saying they were never consulted, that is an alignment of incentives worth naming out loud and worth watching closely. A question, on the public record — not an accusation dressed as one.

The honest way to press it is the boring, durable one, not a lawsuit drafted in advance. The temptation — and the thing the machines will happily generate for you — is a ready-made court case with a confident win-rate attached. Resist it; at least one of the precedents offered up alongside this material appears not to exist, which is the whole reason every lead in this booklet is checked before it is built on. The real leverage is quieter and cannot be waved away: request, under Freedom of Information, the full sectoral breakdown of National Training Fund allocations from 2020 to date; ask what consultation the levy-payers were actually given before the redirection; and ask Research Ireland, in writing, for the industry-partnership, intellectual-property and co-funding terms attached to the Rinn centres. Answers on the record settle this far more firmly than any conclusion asserted before the evidence is in hand. The templates are in Part 6. Use them, and let the file speak.

Sources: National Training Fund — established by the National Training Fund Act 2000; 1% employer levy (raised from 0.7% between 2018 and 2020); accumulated surplus of approximately €1.8 billion at end-2024, flagged by the Comptroller & Auditor General (Report on the Accounts of the Public Services 2022, Ch. 19). The National Training Fund (Amendment) Act 2025 — signed 23 December 2025, commenced 19 January 2026 — unlocking a ~€1.485 billion package for the tertiary sector 2025–2030: gov.ie / Department of Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science press releases (April, June, October, December 2025) and the Oireachtas Library & Research Service briefing on the General Scheme (September 2025), which records the pre-legislative-scrutiny waiver. ISME’s objection and the ~€1bn-in / ~€60m-back Skillnet figures: ISME statement (August 2025) and The Irish Times, “Isme pushes back on plans to raid National Training Fund” (28 August 2025). The seven Rinn Research Ireland centres — €460m total, individual awards (Rinn AI €121,752,497; Energy €51,785,397; Medical Devices €64,488,000; Pharma & Biopharma €60,324,450; Quantum €39,621,376; Advanced Therapies €51,057,588; Semiconductors €70,979,936), €500m industry leverage from 200+ partners (100+ multinationals), 577 research positions, 800+ PhDs, 17 institutions, commencing 1 July 2026: Research Ireland, UCD, UCC, DCU announcements and RTÉ / The Irish Times / Irish Tech News coverage (10 June 2026). Rinn AI director and interdisciplinary framing, and its succession of the Insight and ADAPT centres: Dublin City University (June 2026). This section deliberately excludes, as unsupported by the primary record, the claims that Ireland’s universities have been “completely captured,” that non-AI training allocations are camouflage for data-centre expansion, that any named minister is the “architect” of a capture, and a purported litigation precedent that could not be verified to exist; it states the redirection-of-levy and alignment-of-incentives concerns as questions and requestable facts rather than as findings.

What the Defenders Say — and Why It Does Not Settle the Matter

Before the contrasts and the case studies, a fair hearing for the other side — because an argument that cannot state its opponent’s case honestly does not deserve to win.

What this chapter is about. This short chapter sets out the strongest arguments made in favour of the data-centre build-out, states them as fairly as their own advocates would, and then shows why — even granting them — they do not answer the questions this booklet raises.

“The test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two opposed ideas in mind at the same time and still retain the ability to function.” — F. Scott Fitzgerald

Defenders of the build-out generally make three arguments, and they are not foolish ones. First, that data centres bring jobs and investment. Second, that hosting the world’s largest technology firms protects Ireland’s reputation as a place they will keep investing. Third, that the sector is getting greener, and will be cleaner in time. Each deserves to be taken seriously rather than waved away.

But notice what none of the three actually answers. Jobs can be real and still be temporary, concentrated, and — as the commissioned figures in this booklet show — heavily overstated; a headline of hundreds of thousands of jobs “enabled” is not the same as jobs that exist because of data centres here. Investment can be genuinely valuable and still leave the public carrying the costs it generates — the grid strain, the water demand, the higher bills. And a promise of future efficiency discloses nothing about present consumption: it does not tell you how much water is drawn today, how much power is taken from the grid today, or how much of the public’s capacity is committed before anyone was asked. A good claim about economic benefit does not erase the need for disclosure, for limits, and for democratic consent.

So the disagreement in this booklet is not with the existence of data centres, or with the idea that digital infrastructure matters — it plainly does. The disagreement is narrower and harder to dodge: whether this industry should be allowed to expand faster than the public can measure, contest, and control it, on terms written so that the benefits are counted loudly and the costs are left off the page. That question is not settled by pointing at jobs and growth. It is only settled by answering it — with numbers, with limits, and in the open.

Part 4A — The Planning Contrast

The rules are not applied equally. Two tracks, one town — and the gap between them is the system itself.

What this chapter is about. This chapter holds two planning decisions side by side: what an ordinary citizen or small builder is put through, and what a hyperscale data centre is waved past. The contrast is the argument — the rules are not absent, they are applied unevenly, and the unevenness is the point.

“The law, in its majestic equality, forbids rich and poor alike to sleep under bridges.” — Anatole France

Two pictures are worth more than a page of argument — and you have already seen them, in Part 1. The two-track table there is the foundation of everything that follows: the householder and the café owner carry the full weight of scrutiny; the strategic development moves through a faster, lighter, less challengeable track. The point is not that data centres face no rules. It is that the rules bend for scale. Keep that contrast in mind for the rest of Part 4 — every section that follows is another face of the same asymmetry.

Part 4B — The Revolving Door: Who Came From Where, Who Went Where

Follow the people, not just the policy. The door between government and industry swings both ways — and it never stops.

What this chapter is about. This chapter traces the movement of people between the bodies that regulate and the industries they regulate — the revolving door. It names what is on the public record, frames what is not yet known as questions for FOI, and asks how independent oversight can be when careers flow both ways across it.

This is not a new concern, and it is not a fringe one. The phrase "revolving door" has described this exact worry since at least the late 1800s, and it has been a serious subject of empirical political science for decades — not because critics assumed the worst of every official, but because researchers kept finding the pattern held up under scrutiny. A landmark study by Jeffrey Cohen (American Journal of Political Science, 1986) tracked the movement of commissioners through the U.S. Federal Communications Commission and found the door swung in a direction that mattered: officials' decisions while in office correlated with the private-sector jobs many of them went on to hold afterward. The field has since refined the concept usefully. Political scientists now distinguish a regulator's cultural capture — where an official comes to see the world through the industry's own framing simply from years spent among its people, with no bribe or backroom deal required — from straightforward financial capture. There is even a term for the quieter version of the behaviour this produces: bureaucratic capital, meaning the specific, insider knowledge of a system's loopholes that a regulator accumulates while in office and that becomes valuable to a future private employer the moment they leave. None of this requires assuming wrongdoing by any individual named in this chapter. It requires only what the FOI templates below exist to establish: the record, and an honest account of who moved where, and when.

And where researchers have actually measured it, in industry after industry, the pattern is real — though honesty requires reporting it exactly as mixed as it is. At the FDA, a 2016 study in The BMJ tracked 55 medical officers who reviewed cancer and blood-disorder drugs between 2001 and 2010: of the 26 who left the agency, 15 — more than half, and more than a quarter of the original 55 — went on to work or consult for the pharmaceutical industry within five years, in many documented cases for the very companies whose drugs they had just approved. A later academic study found FDA reviewers who later moved to industry approved measurably lower-quality drugs while still at the agency, consistent with leniency rather than mere expertise. It is also worth knowing, because it is the least visible and most structural version of capture there is: the FDA now receives roughly 65% of its drug-review budget directly from the pharmaceutical industry itself, through user fees — meaning the dependency this chapter worries about is not only about where individuals go afterward, but about who pays the regulator's wages while they are still deciding.

At the SEC, the picture is genuinely more mixed, and it is worth reporting that honestly rather than only the damning half: the main academic study of SEC trial lawyers (deHaan et al., 2015) actually found evidence against simple capture on average — lawyers eyeing a future in private practice tended to enforce more aggressively, not less, consistent with wanting to prove their expertise first. But the same study found the opposite — real evidence of leniency — specifically among Washington-based lawyers and at firms that had hired more former SEC staff. And separately, high-profile enforcement failures including the collapse of Bear Stearns and the Allen Stanford Ponzi scheme have been directly linked by researchers and Congress to lax SEC oversight traceable to the revolving door. The lesson is not that every revolving-door career is corrupt — the evidence does not support that blanket claim, and this chapter will not make it. The lesson is narrower and, this booklet believes, more useful: the effect is real, it is measurable, it clusters in specific, identifiable conditions, and it should be checked case by case rather than assumed or dismissed wholesale.

Why the lawful, tacit version of this is more corrosive than an outright bribe, not less. A bribe has a face and a crime attached to it — someone can be caught, charged, and removed, and the system can tell itself it worked. The revolving door, done entirely within the rules, offers no such relief. Nobody breaks a law when a regulator moves from overseeing an industry to being paid by it, or when a report is commissioned from the very firm whose advice will shape the market it operates in. That is precisely what makes tacit acceptance of it one of the deeper threats to fairness in both politics and industry: it launders an ordinary, human, career-advancing incentive into something that looks like impartial public service, and it does so with no individual villain for the system to expel and no scandal sharp enough to force reform. Carpenter and Moss's own conclusion, cited earlier in this chapter, is worth returning to here: capture is not a conspiracy, and it rarely needs one. It is what an incentive structure produces by default, quietly, for as long as nobody is required to explain it.

“It is difficult to get a man to understand something when his salary depends on his not understanding it.” — Upton Sinclair

The revolving door is how regulatory capture works in practice. Officials who regulated the industry move into it. Industry representatives sit on the bodies that advise on their own regulation. Consultancy firms paid to assess the value of data centres hold commercial interests in their expansion.

What is documented here is a matter of public record. What is not yet known — because the FOI and SIPO searches have not come back — is flagged as a question, not asserted as a conclusion. Where a named individual appears, only what can be stood over is stated; the inference is left to you. That distinction is the whole method.

Person / role From To Why it matters
Patricia Scanlon — Chair, AI Advisory Council Founder & CEO, SoapBox Labs (AI speech-tech firm, since acquired by Curriculum Associates) Chair, Government AI Advisory Council, under DETE Ireland’s AI policy advice was chaired by a career AI-industry founder — an industry-background appointment, not a neutral one. (SoapBox was sold before the chairing, so this is proximity, not a company run at the same time.) Council disbanded April 2026.
Ibec presidency Head of Meta Ireland (Anne O’Leary, 2024–25 term) President of Ibec, Ireland’s largest employer lobby — role passed to AIB’s Colin Hunt, Sept 2025 Ireland’s biggest business lobby was led by the head of Meta Ireland as recently as 2024–25. Ibec lobbied for a one-year delay to the EU Pay Transparency Directive; Ireland missed the deadline in June 2026.
AI Advisory Council members (multiple) OpenAI Ireland; Amazon Web Services (industry representatives) AI Advisory Council (government advisory body) The companies being regulated sat on the body advising on their own regulation. Council disbanded April 2026 — FOI pending.
KPMG Consulting firm (private sector) Authors of the DETE-commissioned “Value of Data Centres to Ireland” report Five survey respondents. One water sentence in 181 pages. A steering group with conflict-of-interest exposure and no community voice.
DigitalEurope members (Microsoft, Apple, Google, Meta, Amazon) US tech corporations EU Commission policy — the Microsoft/DigitalEurope confidentiality wording adopted almost word-for-word into the EU Energy Efficiency Delegated Act Corporate lobbying text became EU law, blocking public access to individual data-centre environmental data. 35 MEPs including Lynn Boylan are fighting to reverse it.
CRU officials (to confirm by FOI) Commission for Regulation of Utilities Private-sector energy / data-centre roles? (FOI required) SIPO revolving-door register to be checked. CRU authored CRU/2025/236 — the grandfather-clause decision.
DETE officials (to confirm by FOI) Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment Data-centre industry / tech sector? (FOI required) DETE commissioned the KPMG report, houses the National AI Office, and is the target of FOI requests on the Council’s disbandment.
Local-authority planners (to confirm by FOI) Cork County Council / Clare County Council planning departments Private-sector planning consultancy? (FOI/SIPO required) Planners who processed the EPark and Ennis permissions. Standard revolving-door risk — check SIPO.

These are the unanswered questions. Read the rows marked “to confirm by FOI” again: nowhere do we assert that a named official moved from regulator to regulated. We state only that the public architecture is opaque enough that a citizen has to file an FOI or a SIPO request to find out — and the requests below are exactly that. Asking on the record is the clean-hands move. A clear answer closes the question; a refusal to answer is itself the evidence.

How to investigate the revolving door yourself

  • lobbying.ie — enter company names: “data centre,” “IBEC energy,” “DigitalEurope,” “Microsoft Ireland,” “CRU data centre.” It shows who lobbied which minister, on what date, about what.

  • SIPO.ie — the revolving-door register. Public officials who move to private-sector roles in areas they previously regulated must notify the Standards in Public Office Commission.

  • An Coimisiún Pleanála — cross-reference board membership and senior staff against their prior employment history.

  • EirGrid and CRU — check the board compositions against industry backgrounds.

FOI requests for revolving-door information

Each of these is a specific, answerable question. Filing them is the campaign — the answers, or the refusals, become the evidence.

To DETE (foi@enterprise.gov.ie): “The names, prior employment histories and subsequent employment declarations of all members of the AI Advisory Council, and of all officials involved in commissioning the KPMG data centre report — including all conflict-of-interest declarations made.”

To CRU: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by CRU board members and senior officials in relation to CRU/2025/236? What post-employment notifications have former CRU officials made to SIPO in the last five years?”

To DETE: “What post-employment notifications have former DETE officials involved in data centre policy made to SIPO in the last five years?”

To Cork County Council: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by planning officials in relation to the EPark applications, including the March 2025 extension? What post-employment notifications have former planning officials on these files made to SIPO?”

To An Coimisiún Pleanála: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by inspectors and board members in relation to the Ennis / Art Data Centres application?”

Remember: the disbandment of the AI Advisory Council should be established by the FOI answer, not assumed. Asking the question on the record is stronger than asserting the conclusion.

Part 4C — The Global Extraction Map: Sixteen Countries, One Pattern

The infrastructure is new. The logic is old: take the resource where the people have least power to refuse, leave the cost behind, carry the profit elsewhere.

What this chapter is about. This chapter lifts the eyes from Ireland to fourteen countries and asks whether the same shape recurs. It does — but not as a single hidden hand: as the same set of incentives producing the same outcome, sometimes captured, sometimes refused. The chapter is built on a clean-hands discipline that separates what is verified to primary sources from what remains a lead to check.

This pattern has a name and a body of scholarship behind it, not invented for this book. Nick Couldry and Ulises Mejias's The Costs of Connection (Stanford University Press, 2019) and Michael Kwet's work on "digital colonialism" (Race & Class, 2019) both argue, independently and with different case studies, that today's data and infrastructure extraction by large technology companies follows the same structural logic historical colonialism did: appropriate a resource — territory and labour then, data and water now — from people with the least power to refuse, using an ideology of benevolence to make the appropriation look like a gift. Couldry and Mejias's own words apply almost exactly to the sixteen countries below: historical colonialism took land and called it civilisation; today's version takes data, water and grid capacity, and calls it connectivity, progress, digital transformation. Recognising the pattern as named and studied, rather than as this booklet's own metaphor, matters for exactly the reason every other academic citation in this book matters: it means a skeptical reader has somewhere else to check the claim.

“The infrastructure is new. The logic is old.” — (this book)

The map overleaf reads across sixteen countries. Risk ratings run from LOW to CRITICAL; where a water column reads UNKNOWN, that gap is itself the finding — a disclosure failure, not an absence of impact.

Sources: UN INWEH Environmental Cost of AI report (June 2026); EirGrid; CRU/2025/236; Democracy Now! (April 2026); Axios (June 2026); Mongabay (November 2025); Daily Maverick; Australian Government; Irish Times.

The global extraction map

Country Capacity & growth Water situation Energy % grid Water stress Colonial legacy Government response / community voice
IRELAND (UN cautionary tale) 82 operational DCs · 22% national electricity (2024) — highest in EU · 40 with planning approval UNKNOWN — no mandatory disclosure · 58% water zones at supply risk · Cork Harbour Natura 2000 SAC/SPA 31–33% of grid by 2030 · households pay 2× DC rate MODERATE–HIGH · 58% supply-risk zones · Blackwater fish kill Aug 2025 ~800 yrs British colonial presence · 12.5% corporate-tax FDI model Failing: moratorium lifted Dec 2025 · permanent grandfather clause · no water disclosure · zero community voice on KPMG steering group
DENMARK (model) ~400MW · Microsoft, Meta, Apple · 80%+ renewable · moratorium imposed Mar 2026 Minimised via district heating · Meta Odense heats 11,000 homes 80%+ renewable · moratorium protecting grid capacity LOW–MODERATE Strong social-democratic institutions Model: all-party waste-heat agreement 2022 · grid moratorium imposed · communities benefit directly from server heat
FINLAND (model) Growing hyperscale · 88%+ renewable grid · targeted growth Minimised · Microsoft/Fortum: 250,000 people served with waste heat 88%+ renewable · waste heat replaces fossil-fuel heating LOW · abundant freshwater Swedish, then Russian rule until 1917 · strong welfare state Model: state partnership · Fortum waste-heat programme · 250,000 residents benefit
AUSTRALIA (progressive) NSW: 44 DCs in pipeline · 11.4GW · capacity to triple · A$51.9bn investment Sydney Water: DCs could be 15–20% of Sydney supply by 2035 · Day Zero risk in drought Significant grid pressure · NSW = 4 Eraring coal stations HIGH · drought-prone · recurring Day Zero risk British colonial rule 1788–1901 · Aboriginal peoples dispossessed Partial: AEMC grid-connection standards Mar 2026 explicitly benchmark Ireland, Texas & Finland — and cite Ireland’s moratorium as a cautionary case · Senate inquiry active · First Nations peoples required in framework
INDIA (critical risk) Mumbai & Chennai = 70% absorption · 4% national electricity · $167.5bn incoming · 18% annual growth ~150bn litres (2025) → ~358bn litres by 2030 · Chennai Day Zero 2019 · 18% world population, 4% freshwater Growing rapidly from 4% baseline · massive build-out planned CRITICAL · Chennai Day Zero 2019 · acute water scarcity British East India Company 1757 · Crown rule 1858–1947 Failing: Karnataka guarantees uninterrupted water to DCs · tax holidays to 2047 · Human Rights Forum objecting · no community consultation framework
SOUTH AFRICA (opacity) 56 DCs · largest African market · all hyperscalers expanding UNKNOWN · city refused electricity data as “private customer data” · Cape Town Day Zero 2018 Significant · Eskom load-shedding forces diesel backup HIGH · Cape Town Day Zero 2018 · recurring drought British & Dutch colonial rule · apartheid 1948–1994 Failing: refused electricity data · minimal regulatory framework · no documented community consultation
BRAZIL (conditional model) São Paulo dominant · all hyperscalers present · capacity to triple by 2030 · TikTok Ceará above planned water use Community concerns documented · São Paulo 2015 water crisis precedent · Indigenous water fears in Ceará (Pecém) 85% renewable (hydro) but growing DC demand MODERATE–HIGH · São Paulo crisis history Portuguese colonial rule 1500–1822 · extraction economy Contested: ReData (MP 1.318/2025) set renewable-only conditions (incl. tight water-efficiency targets) but LAPSED in late Feb 2026 when Congress failed to pass it in time — reportedly amid gas-industry lobbying to broaden it beyond renewables (Valor Econômico via Dialogue Earth); govt says it will revive it · meanwhile grid-access requests hit 38 GW (Min. Silveira) vs ~61 MW largest live DC · $38bn ByteDance project at Pecém, Ceará, where Indigenous leader Chief Roberto Anacé fears local water/power shortages
SPAIN (pushback) Madrid/Aragón boom · major hyperscaler pipeline Mixed · new powers to impose sustainable-water conditions (2026) Significant grid demand · speculative reservation a known problem MODERATE Not a colonised state; imperial power historically Pushback: Royal Decree-Law 7/2026 (Mar 2026) introduced a capacity-reservation charge to curb speculative “megawatt hoarding” — forcing projects to build or release grid capacity — and empowered sustainability/water conditions on data centres. (Critics note it also created a fast-track “strategic project” lane.)
CHILE (resistance win) Santiago (Cerrillos) · Google ~$200m project Aquifer-critical · 228 litres/second (over 7 billion litres/year) potable draw authorised from the Central Santiago Aquifer Growing CRITICAL · 15-year mega-drought · rationing since 2022 Spanish colonial rule 1540s–1818 WIN: environmental court partially reversed Google’s permit (Feb 2024) on the precautionary principle; by Sep 2024 Google took the project “back to square one” and redesigned with AIR cooling, dropping the groundwater draw. A court and community forced the change.
NEW ZEALAND (nuanced) ~80MW added in 5 yrs (market tripled) · Microsoft & AWS cloud regions · proposed $3.5bn 280MW Datagrid campus, Southland Datagrid permitted to draw up to 220 million litres of groundwater/yr (~7 dairy farms); 49 ha of farmland ~85% renewable grid · Datagrid matched with new renewable generation MODERATE · localised, near concrete projects British colonial rule; Māori (Te Tiriti o Waitangi 1840) Mixed: BCG report frames DCs as a NZ$70bn “strategic opportunity” & urges streamlined consenting; the Resource Management Act is being replaced to speed consents (mid-2026). Genuinely double-edged — renewable advantage vs a 220M-litre groundwater draw on farmland. Note: NZ’s “golden visa” residency-by-investment scheme has made it a noted bolt-hole for the ultra-wealthy (Thiel granted citizenship 2011 after ~12 days; his Wānaka bunker-lodge plan was refused by the council in 2022).
ARGENTINA (proposed) Proposed $25bn / up to 500MW OpenAI–Sur Energy “Stargate Argentina” data centre, Patagonia (Letter of Intent; 1st 100MW phase targeted 2027) Environmental questions raised re: Patagonia ecosystems & wind-energy scale (not yet assessed) Renewable (Patagonian wind) intended MODERATE · proposal stage Spanish colonial rule 1500s–1816 Proposed: structured under RIGI (Régimen de Incentivo para Grandes Inversiones) — 30-yr fiscal & customs benefits for large foreign investment under Milei. Cabinet chief Manuel Adorni told Congress billionaires “fleeing” higher-tax countries are “welcome in the Argentine republic, the new land of freedom.” At Letter-of-Intent stage — a proposal, not yet a built project.
INDONESIA (critical) Jakarta metro hub · Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Alibaba, Tencent · $37bn+ SE Asian investment UNKNOWN · will compound critically overexploited groundwater · Jakarta sinking 25cm/yr Growing significantly · unreliable grid CRITICAL · Jakarta sinking 25cm/yr · capital being relocated Dutch colonial rule ~350 yrs (1602–1949) Failing: no DC water-governance framework · expansion without adequate regulation · communities facing displacement
MALAYSIA (improving) Johor Bahru major hub · significant pipeline · ~30% of proposals rejected WUE requirements being introduced · 85% efficiency rule 85% efficiency rule introduced MODERATE British colonial rule 1786–1957 Partial: comprehensive DC framework Oct 2025 · 30% of proposals rejected · partial consultation via regulatory requirements
KENYA (extraction risk) 19 DCs · Microsoft, Google, Amazon present · Microsoft/G42 $1bn DC · 10-year tax exemption UNKNOWN · farmers documenting falling groundwater · drought-prone Growing · 10-yr tax holiday reduces state revenue HIGH · drought-prone · water scarcity British colonial rule 1895–1963 · land dispossession Failing: 10-year tax exemption · minimal conditions on water or community benefit · no documented consultation
NIGERIA (emerging risk) 17 DCs · all major tech companies expanding · West African hub UNKNOWN · water-access crisis · oil-extraction legacy Growing · unreliable grid · diesel backup generators HIGH · water-access crisis British colonial rule 1884–1960 · oil-extraction legacy Failing: limited regulatory framework · no documented community consultation
MEXICO (Querétaro) 32 new DCs announced during worst drought in a century · major US nearshoring hub Worst drought in a century · groundwater crisis · farmers vs tech companies Growing rapidly · nearshoring boom CRITICAL · historic drought · groundwater collapse Spanish colonial rule 1521–1821 · extraction-economy legacy Failing: “colonial legacy of resource extractivism” (local critics) · undersea cables follow former slave-trade routes · no community consultation

A clean-hands note on this table

The strength of this table is also its discipline. Some entries here are verified to named primary or top-tier sources and can be stated with confidence: Ireland’s 22% grid figure (CSO); Spain’s Royal Decree-Law 7/2026 (Spanish government, confirmed by multiple international law firms); Italy’s Law Decree 21/2026; Australia’s March 2026 AEMC grid-connection standards, which explicitly benchmark Ireland and cite our moratorium as a cautionary case; Mexico’s Querétaro crisis (Thomson Reuters Foundation, named academics); Chile’s environmental-court ruling (court records); Brazil’s ReData and the 38 GW of grid-access requests (Ministry of Mines & Energy / Minister Silveira, BNamericas, Dialogue Earth — note ReData lapsed in Feb 2026 rather than passing, a correction this table now reflects); New Zealand’s $70bn BCG framing and the 280MW Datagrid consent (BCG, The Spinoff, Environment Southland); Argentina’s RIGI regime and the proposed Stargate data centre (Buenos Aires Herald, DataCenterDynamics — a Letter of Intent, framed here as proposed); South Africa’s grid and secrecy figures (Daily Maverick). Other entries — among them some of the water projections for India, the groundwater claims for Kenya, Indonesia and Nigeria, and the undersea-cable observation — rest on secondary reporting (UN INWEH, Mongabay, Democracy Now! and others) and are presented as such: strong leads, reported in good faith, that a citizen investigator in each country should verify against a primary source before building a case on them. That distinction is not a weakness in the argument. It is the argument: the pattern is real and verifiable, and it does not need exaggeration to indict the system. Where a figure here is not yet nailed to a primary source, treat it as a question to be answered, not a fact to be repeated.

Where “UNKNOWN” means something

Where a water column reads UNKNOWN, that is not an absence of impact — it is a disclosure failure. The countries that protect their communities, Denmark and Finland, are the ones that consulted them and built waste-heat reciprocity into the deal. Ireland sits in the amber band for a reason.

Who gets targeted

Read the colonial-legacy column down the table and the pattern is hard to miss. The places absorbing the heaviest, least-regulated, least-consulted expansion are very often the ones that already carried the worst of earlier extraction — India under the East India Company; Indonesia under three and a half centuries of Dutch rule; Kenya and Nigeria under British administration; South Africa under colonialism and apartheid; Brazil under the Portuguese extraction economy. Mexico: thirty-two new data centres announced during the worst drought in a century, which local critics call a repeat of a colonial legacy of resource extractivism — noting that undersea data cables often follow the very routes once plied by slave ships. And Ireland: roughly eight hundred years of colonial presence, then reinvented as a low-tax conduit for foreign capital.

What the critics are saying

Krystal Two Bulls (Oglala Lakota / Northern Cheyenne) of Honor the Earth, tracking more than a hundred proposed projects on tribal and rural lands, calls the boom a “modern-day iteration of settler colonialism,” warning that her communities are repeatedly the ones forced to sacrifice their relationship to land, air and water. Nick Couldry, co-author of Data Grab: The New Colonialism of Big Tech, argues that taking data and resources as if they were simply there for the claiming is “a deeply colonial act.”

What Erin Brockovich is doing — and what every country needs

In spring 2026, Erin Brockovich — the activist whose investigation forced a US$333 million settlement against PG&E over contaminated drinking water — launched a public, citizen-reported map of AI data centres across the United States, at brockovichdatacenter.com. Residents upload locations, photos and firsthand reports; within weeks it had logged thousands of submissions from dozens of states. Her legal team is preparing civil actions built on the right to environmental information — aimed squarely at the confidentiality clauses that hide water and electricity use.

Two things to take from it. First, the method works: a citizen-built map turns scattered local worry into a national pattern that journalists and courts can use. Second, the legal strategy mirrors ours exactly — attack the secrecy, not just the building.

Every country in that table needs its own version. The right to environmental information is a universal instrument — grounded in the Aarhus Convention, in EU law, in national FOI and AIE frameworks. The Microsoft secrecy clause, the “private customer data” refusal in South Africa, the undisclosed groundwater depletion in Kenya and Indonesia — all the same move. And the counter-move is always the same: citizens with the legal right to information, using it. A citizen map of Irish data centres, county by county, is well within reach — and this booklet’s step-by-step is how to feed it.

The water numbers behind the pattern

  • India — ~150 billion litres consumed by data centres in 2025, projected to reach ~358 billion by 2030, in a country with 18% of the world’s population and 4% of its freshwater. Chennai hit Day Zero — complete municipal water failure — in 2019. Karnataka now guarantees uninterrupted supply to data centres, with tax holidays to 2047.

  • Indonesia — Jakarta is sinking 25cm a year from groundwater over-extraction; the capital is being relocated. Data centres are expanding with no water-governance framework, compounding critically overexploited groundwater. Communities face displacement.

  • South Africa — Cape Town came within weeks of Day Zero in 2018, and the risk returns with every drought. When journalists asked the city for data-centre electricity data, they were told it was “private customer data.” The secrecy is the policy.

  • Mexico (Querétaro) — thirty-two new data centres announced during the worst drought in a century. Farmers are in direct competition with data centres for groundwater. Local critics call it a repeat of colonial extraction.

  • Australia — Sydney Water estimates data centres could take 15–20% of Sydney’s entire supply by 2035. A Senate inquiry is active. Australia is doing more than Ireland — but it started from a worse regulatory position.

None of these are coincidences. They are the predictable output of an industry that treats water as a free input, operates under voluntary disclosure, and targets jurisdictions with weak oversight, colonial governance legacies, or both.

The same law, country after country

The map above shows where the water and power are being taken. There is a second map, just as telling: the laws being rewritten, almost in lockstep, to lift these decisions out of local hands. The fragmentation and fast-tracking documented in Ireland are not an Irish quirk. They are a template.

In February 2026, Italy enacted Decree-Law 21, creating a single national authorisation for data centres and providing that any project of “strategic national interest” worth a billion euro or more can be signed off by a special commissioner appointed by the Prime Minister — lifting the decision clean out of the municipality that has to live with it, and halving the timelines for environmental assessment. The United Kingdom reached the same destination by another road, designating data centres as Critical National Infrastructure — the tier of power stations and water networks — so that a local refusal can be overridden in the name of national security. Ireland has its own strategic-infrastructure route, direct to the national board. And over all of them sits the EU confidentiality clause — inserted, an Investigate Europe investigation found in April 2026, in wording almost identical to what Microsoft and the lobby group DigitalEurope requested — which seals the water and energy figures for any individual data centre across the entire bloc.

Read those together and the architecture is unmistakable. “Strategic infrastructure” in Dublin, “single authorisation” in Rome, “critical national infrastructure” in London, “commercially confidential” in Brussels: four phrases, one function — to move the decision above the heads of the people it lands on, and to keep them from seeing the numbers while it happens. The playbook is not being improvised in each country. It is being copied.

The cruelty of taking it from those already bled

There is a particular injustice in where the heaviest, least-regulated version of this lands — and it is the same injustice this whole booklet is about, written at world scale. It is one thing to take the water of a wealthy, well-watered state. It is another to take it from nations that spent centuries being extracted from under empire, and are now asked to surrender the scarcest thing they have so that machines elsewhere can think.

India is the clearest case. Its data-centre water use is projected to more than double — from roughly 150 billion litres in 2025 to 358 billion by 2030 — and independent analysis finds the majority of its data centres already sit in places under high water stress: Mumbai, Chennai, Hyderabad, Bengaluru, cities that have known Day Zero and metre-deep falls in the water table inside a single season. In at least two Indian states, data centres have been classified as “essential services” — which means that when the wells run low, the servers are entitled to water ahead of households. A country where some forecasts put two in five cities short of groundwater by 2030 is being asked to cool the world’s computers first and drink second.

And the promised return is thin. A finished data centre employs very few people — a handful of security and maintenance staff, not the workforce the “investment” headline implies. Worse, the very technology these centres exist to run threatens the work the country does have: an economy that leans heavily on its information-technology and business-services sector now faces the real prospect that automation could hollow out exactly that employment base. So the bargain offered to much of the world is the old colonial bargain in modern dress — surrender the resource now, for a prosperity that arrives, if it arrives at all, in someone else’s accounts.

We, of all people, should recognise it, because we lived an earlier draft of it — grain leaving a starving country under the law of the day. We have been here before. That is exactly why we do not get to look away while it is done to others. The same right-to-information tools, the same registers, the same escalation routes set out in this book work in every one of these countries, with the local names swapped in: county by county in Ireland, country by country everywhere else. And one principle, won anywhere, becomes a template everywhere — that no data centre may be granted a community’s water ahead of its households, and none waved through in a water-stressed place without a published, site-level account of what it will take.

Sources: Italy Decree-Law 21/2026, single authorisation and strategic-national-interest special-commissioner procedure — A&O Shearman, Latham & Watkins, Bird & Bird (February 2026); UK Critical National Infrastructure designation for data centres (2024); EU Energy Efficiency Directive (2023/1791) confidentiality clause — Investigate Europe with the Guardian, Le Monde, El País and The Journal (April 2026); India water projections, stress, “essential services” classification and employment profile — BBC/S&P Global, WRI India, Down to Earth, Deccan Chronicle (2025–2026). Clean-hands boundary: these are documented policies and reported findings; the point about automation and employment is framed as a foreseeable risk, not a certainty; unverified country-specific corporate-ownership and case-law claims circulating in campaign material are deliberately excluded.

Part 4D — What They Said, and What They Do: Mission Statements vs Reality

A public commitment, made in writing in support of a planning application or a licence, is not a mission statement any more. It is a representation. And representations can be held to account.

What this chapter is about. This chapter sets corporate and consultancy promises against the documented record — including the reports commissioned to justify the build-out, and what those reports leave out. It is about the gap between the mission statement and the meter reading.

The gap itself has an academic name and a real research field behind it. "Greenwashing" is not a slur invented by campaigners — it is a defined term in management scholarship, most influentially set out by Magali Delmas and Nicholas Burbano's 2011 study "The Drivers of Greenwashing" (California Management Review), and refined since as "a gap between symbolic and substantive corporate... actions" (Walker and Wan, 2012): a company adopts the language and appearance of environmental responsibility — a "clean energy centre," a "sustainability commitment" — without a matching, measurable change in what it actually does. Delmas and Burbano's own finding is worth having in this chapter specifically: greenwashing is not typically the act of a single dishonest executive. It is a predictable organisational response to two pressures arriving at once — real financial pressure to keep costs down, and real stakeholder pressure to appear responsible — which makes the symbolic commitment cheaper to produce than the substantive one nearly every time. That is precisely the gap this chapter exists to measure, case by case, against the meter reading rather than the press release.

“By their fruits ye shall know them.” — Matthew 7:16

Why this matters legally

Corporate sustainability commitments sit on a spectrum. At one end, aspirational branding — legally unenforceable. At the other, representations made to public bodies in planning applications, licence submissions or investor filings, where they become material facts that can be challenged.

The EU Green Claims Directive, now being finalised, will make greenwashing far more enforceable under consumer-protection law. The Competition and Consumer Protection Commission has powers already. And under securities law, listed companies that make materially false ESG commitments in their annual reports carry real exposure.

The prize for this campaign is specific: find where a company’s own sustainability language was submitted to An Coimisiún Pleanála, the CRU or Cork County Council as part of a planning application. That is the point where aspiration becomes representation — and representation can be held to account.

The commitments on the record

Microsoft. Public commitment: by 2030 it will “replenish more water than we consume across our direct operations,” alongside pledges to protect the environmental health of the communities hosting its data centres. What it discloses in Ireland: nothing site-specific. It reported total global water use in its 2025 sustainability report, but not by site; investors pressed for site-level transparency at the 2026 AGM and were told it was coming. It has not arrived. The gap is stark — Microsoft commits publicly to water stewardship while, through DigitalEurope, it lobbied to insert the very confidentiality clause into EU law that blocks disclosure of individual data-centre water and energy use. Both positions cannot be honoured at once. The public is entitled to know which one is.

Google / Alphabet. Public commitment: net-zero emissions across all operations and value chain by 2030, and carbon-free energy on every grid, every hour, by 2030. Reality: Google’s own climate reports show emissions up since its baseline year, and its Irish data centres sit under the same grandfather exemption as EPark — no mandatory 80% renewable requirement for pre-December 2025 applicants.

How “100% green” is claimed without a single green electron reaching the server

The mechanism behind claims like Google’s is worth naming plainly, because it is not a lie so much as an accounting trick every hyperscaler in Ireland relies on. A data centre signs a Corporate Power Purchase Agreement (CPPA) with a wind or solar farm — usually a virtual or synthetic CPPA, a financial contract for difference settled against the wholesale market, not a physical wire running from turbine to server. The renewable developer feeds its power into the general national grid, exactly as it would anyway. The data centre, at its actual physical site, pulls ordinary grid electricity — whatever mix of gas, wind and imports happens to be running at that moment — and pays the developer a fixed price under the contract. On the data centre’s global balance sheet, this is booked as “100% renewable energy matching.” On the ground, in Cork or Dublin or Clare, not one electron the server uses has ever touched the turbine it claims to be powered by.

This is not a fringe workaround; it is the dominant format hyperscalers use in Ireland, and it is entirely legal. It is also precisely why a company can report global carbon-free energy targets while its own site-level Irish disclosures — as the entries above show, company after company — remain silent on the numbers that would let anyone check the claim against the meter.

The second green fig-leaf: "biomethane-ready" gas that runs on gas

There is a twin to the CPPA trick, and it works on the fuel side rather than the electricity side. Its name is biomethane, and understanding it requires first being fair about what biomethane genuinely is. Biomethane is real, and in the right place it is genuinely useful: it is gas captured from decomposing organic matter — slurry, food waste, sewage — in an anaerobic digester, then upgraded until it is molecularly identical to fossil gas. Because that carbon was pulled from the atmosphere recently rather than dug up from millions of years ago, and because it intercepts methane that would otherwise leak straight into the sky (methane being roughly eighty times more potent than CO₂ over twenty years), capturing slurry gas from farms is a real climate good. Professor Hannah Daly of University College Cork — no friend of greenwashing — supports genuine anaerobic digestion for exactly this reason. So the word itself is not the con.

The con is what happens when "biomethane" is bolted onto a data centre's gas turbines as a promise about the future, to justify building fossil-gas infrastructure in the present. The clearest documented case in Ireland is the Pure Data Centres microgrid in west Dublin — owned by the US private-equity firm Oaktree Capital Management — announced to considerable fanfare as "Europe's first" biomethane-capable, "biofuel-ready" data centre campus, engineered to power the equivalent of 100,000 homes. Read past the announcement to the technical detail and the fig-leaf falls away: beyond its battery component, the microgrid features no renewable energy at all. Its three energy centres run on natural gas, backed by a 200MW thermal gas connection. The biomethane is a capability the plant is built to accept someday, not a fuel it actually burns. Pure DC's own words describe renewable gas as "a transitional measure rather than an ultimate outcome," and say the company is merely "actively evaluating" biomethane purchase agreements. A fossil-gas power station is thereby marketed on the strength of a green fuel it does not use.

And the reason it does not use it — the reason almost none of them can — is arithmetic, stated by the most authoritative voice available. Ireland's entire national target is for biomethane to replace about 10% of the country's fossil gas demand by 2030. Data centre demand alone, Professor Daly warns, "would exceed sustainable production" of biomethane entirely, and diverting the limited supply to server farms would simply rob other sectors that need it more. On Bord na Móna's proposed 600MW gas plant — which "claims it will later switch to hydrogen or biomethane," according to its own environmental impact assessment — Daly's verdict is flat: "No credible pathway exists to supply these fuels at this scale." This is the whole mechanism in one sentence. A gas plant is approved today on the promise of a green fuel that cannot physically be supplied in the quantities required, ever — so the "transition" never arrives, and what has actually been built and locked in for its thirty-year operating life is fossil-gas infrastructure wearing a renewable label. Social Democrats TD Jennifer Whitmore named it precisely when the Cabinet approved the new data-centre rules: "there might be an 80% renewable requirement, but developers can still rely on on-site fossil fuel backup which could lead to locking in carbon-heavy infrastructure." Biomethane, honestly used to capture farm slurry, is a nitrogen-fixer. Biomethane, invoked to license a gas turbine it will never fuel, is the plantation calling itself a meadow.

*Sources: Pure Data Centres / AVK microgrid, west Dublin — RTÉ (12 March 2026), Solar Power Portal (23 March 2026, confirming the microgrid runs on natural gas with a 200MW thermal gas connection and "no renewable energy" beyond battery storage; Pure DC's "transitional measure" and "actively evaluating" quotes); ownership by Oaktree Capital Management per Bloomberg reporting. Professor Hannah Daly (UCC) on biomethane scale and the Bord na Móna 600MW plant — AlgorithmWatch (18 December 2025) and The Irish Times (5 March 2026). National biomethane target (~10% of gas demand / 5.7 TWh by 2030) — Gas Networks Ireland; Government Climate Action Plan. Jennifer Whitmore TD greenwashing quote — TheJournal.ie (13 January 2026).

Meta. Public commitment: net-zero across its value chain by 2030, and “designing, building and operating sustainable and innovative data centres.” Reality: Meta did not respond to investors pressing for site-level water disclosure ahead of the 2026 AGM. Its Odense facility in Denmark heats 11,000 homes with waste heat — proof the model is possible. No equivalent commitment exists for its Irish operations.

Amazon / AWS. Public commitment: the Climate Pledge — net-zero carbon by 2040 — and water-replenishment targets. Reality: Amazon did not report total water use in its 2025 sustainability report, only usage per unit of power; pressed by investors, it said it was “increasingly disclosing site-level consumption data.” For Irish operations, none is published. And AWS representatives sat on the AI Advisory Council disbanded in April 2026 — the body meant to provide independent oversight of the industry AWS operates in.

KPMG. Public commitment: to deliver “high-quality professional services in the public interest,” with stated values of integrity and responsible corporate citizenship. Reality: KPMG was commissioned by DETE, with taxpayer money, to produce “The Value of Data Centres to Ireland” — five survey respondents, one sentence on water across 181 pages, a steering group with conflict-of-interest exposure and no community representatives. A benefits-only analysis with the costs left out. KPMG also advises data-centre operators on tax and regulatory strategy. Whether a firm with commercial ties to the industry it was assessing can produce an objective report “in the public interest” is a question the public is entitled to ask. And how much taxpayers paid for it is not publicly available — FOI required, below.

The IBEC / pay-transparency pattern — transparency for thee, not for me

The same lobby body that resists water transparency for data centres also moved to delay salary transparency for workers.

IBEC — Ireland’s largest employer lobby — formally called for a one-year delay to implementation of the EU Pay Transparency Directive in February 2026, despite a draft bill having been published in January 2025. Ireland missed the 7 June 2026 deadline, and ICTU branded the delay “unacceptable.”

The proximity is worth noting, accurately: the head of Meta Ireland, Anne O’Leary, held the IBEC presidency as recently as the 2024–25 term — though by the time of this lobbying the role had passed to AIB’s Colin Hunt.

The directive requires employers to disclose salary information to job applicants, to act where gender pay gaps exceed 5%, and to provide gender-based pay information to staff on request; under it, employers cannot ask candidates about their earnings history.

The pattern is the point. IBEC lobbied to delay a law requiring employers to be transparent about what they pay their workers. IBEC’s data-centre members lobby against laws requiring them to be transparent about what they take from public water supplies. The common thread is not complexity or timing. It is a systematic resistance to disclosure — same lobby body, same arguments.

The question — framed as a question, per the clean-hands standard, not an assertion: did IBEC lobby to delay pay transparency because disclosure of salary structures at major tech and consultancy firms would reveal what those firms would prefer to keep private? The lobbying record is public. The answer should be too.

A note on rights: ICTU has warned that even without enacted legislation, employees with valid claims under the directive may be entitled to seek compensation backdated to 7 June 2026. The missed deadline does not extinguish employee rights — it delays the enforcement mechanism.

The same man, the same year, asking for the opposite kind of speed. On 5 July 2026, weeks after his own organisation secured that delay, Colin Hunt — AIB’s chief executive and Ibec’s president since September 2025 — told the Business Post that Ireland’s pace on infrastructure was too slow, in his own words: “the danger now is not that nothing is happening. It is that we settle for incrementalism at the very moment the challenge demands more.” Two positions, held by the one office, in the one year: too slow when the ask is planning and grid connections for industry; not too slow at all, six months is worth asking for, when the ask is telling a worker what their male colleague earns. No inference is required to notice that both positions serve the same interest — investor certainty over public disclosure — and simply point in different directions depending on who is being asked to move faster.

It is also worth placing beside AIB’s own year. The bank quadrupled Mr Hunt’s pay to €2.7 million in 2026, the same year it returned fully to private ownership after the state’s bailout, and the same year its president’s organisation was arguing that Irish workers should wait longer to learn what their employer pays. None of this is asserted as wrongdoing — pay awards and lobbying positions are each, individually, unremarkable and legal. What is remarkable, and what the clean-hands standard permits stating plainly, is the pattern: transparency urged for the state’s planning system, resisted for the lobby’s own member firms’ payrolls, in the same twelve months, from the same desk. A ready-made FOI template on the Department of Finance’s own decision to lift the pay restriction that made this possible sits in Part 9, alongside the rest of this booklet’s FOI templates — the record is public; the reasoning behind it should be too.

Case Study — One Man, Three Doors: The Colin Hunt Pattern

The individual sections above are scattered through this booklet because each fact landed where it was verified. Gathered in one place, the pattern they form is worth seeing whole — not because Colin Hunt is uniquely culpable among the figures in these pages, but because his case happens to touch three of this booklet’s central mechanisms at once: the revolving door, the double standard, and the closed loop of unaccountable pay.

The door. In the mid-2000s, Colin Hunt served as a Special Policy Adviser inside the Departments of Transport and Finance — shaping public policy from within the state. He then moved to Macquarie Capital, an investment bank, to build its Irish operation, before joining AIB in 2016 and becoming its chief executive in 2019. This is the same structural pattern Part 4B documents for Patricia Scanlon, for the AI Advisory Council’s industry members, for Anne O’Leary before him: a person moves from writing the rules to working the system the rules govern, or back again. No law was broken. That is precisely the point this booklet makes throughout — the revolving door does not need to be illegal to be corrosive. It needs only to be routine.

The double standard. As Ibec president since September 2025, Hunt leads the lobby group that formally sought a one-year delay to the EU Pay Transparency Directive — a law requiring employers to disclose what they pay their staff. Ireland missed the transposition deadline. Months later, the same man told the Business Post that Ireland’s pace on infrastructure delivery was dangerously slow, warning against “incrementalism at the very moment the challenge demands more.” Transparency, in other words, can wait when workers are asking for it; urgency is essential when investors are.

The closed loop. In 2026, AIB — newly returned to full private ownership after the state’s costliest bailout — quadrupled Hunt’s pay to €2.7 million, made possible by the Department of Finance’s own decision to lift the fixed-salary restriction imposed after that bailout. The department that once capped his pay to protect the public purse that rescued his bank chose, in the same year his lobby group was resisting pay disclosure for ordinary workers, to remove the cap on his own.

What this case study does not say. It does not allege that any of this is unlawful — nothing here is. It does not allege coordination between these three threads — no evidence of that exists, and none is claimed. What it says is narrower and, this booklet believes, more durable: that a single public figure, entirely within the law, can move through the revolving door, argue for speed when it serves one interest and delay when it serves another, and benefit from a public decision that a public body has not been asked to explain. The FOI template above exists so that the explanation can be asked for. The record is public. The reasoning behind it should be too.

Sources: Ibec, “President Colin Hunt” biography page and 3 September 2025 appointment announcement; Institute of Bankers in Ireland, “Meet the IOB President” interview; Business Post, “The window is narrowing: AIB chief warns investors losing patience with Ireland,” 5 July 2026; Irish Times, 4 March 2026 (AIB pay award); ICTU statement on the EU Pay Transparency Directive transposition deadline, June 2026.

Case Study — The Language of Urgency: Three Figures, One Rhetorical Pattern

Colin Hunt did not invent the seat he occupies, and he is not the only person to have spoken from it in this particular voice. Widen the lens by one step, to the person he succeeded and to a completely different figure in a completely different field, and a pattern emerges that has nothing to do with any one of them personally — and everything to do with what that kind of seat trains a person to say.

Anne O'Leary, Head of Meta Ireland, became Ibec president in September 2024, with Colin Hunt serving as her deputy. Her presidential theme, announced at her inaugural address: "openness" — openness "to flows of trade, capital, innovation and people." She called on government to close "continued gaps in our critical and social infrastructure," warning that such gaps were "posing significant obstacles to the next phase of our economic growth," and that Ireland must "remain agile and responsive to new investment."

Colin Hunt succeeded her a year later. By July 2026: "the danger now is not that nothing is happening. It is that we settle for incrementalism at the very moment the challenge demands more."

Patricia Scanlon, founder of the AI company SoapBox Labs, chairs Ireland's Government AI Advisory Council. In a June 2025 opening statement to the Oireachtas Joint Committee on AI: "Ireland's window to lead this transformation, rather than be shaped by it, is rapidly closing... We cannot wait for perfect clarity. We need to move decisively and urgently... Hesitation and half-measures will leave us behind."

To be fair to all three, plainly and on the record: none of this is illegitimate advocacy, and it would be wrong to flatten it into simple industry capture. Scanlon in particular is on record saying she is "not an AI advocate at any cost," has explicitly called for AI regulation "because anything that powerful needs to be regulated," and has warned of AI's risks to jobs, bias and privacy in the same breath as she warns of the risk of moving too slowly. Her sincerity is not in question here, and this booklet does not question it.

What is worth noticing is not any one person's honesty. It is the shape of the sentence itself, recurring across three unconnected fields. The window is closing. We cannot afford to wait. Hesitation will cost us. It is the same rhetorical structure, deployed by an industry lobbyist, a bank chief executive, and a state AI advisor, none of them coordinating with one another, none of them insincere — and in every recorded instance, the urgency argues for the same thing: less delay, less friction, more speed for the institution the argument comes from. It is worth asking, the next time this sentence appears in a headline, urging a different kind of urgency: who is being asked to move fast, and who is quietly being asked, in the same breath, to wait.

Sources: Irish Times, 5 September 2024 (O'Leary's appointment and inaugural address); Ibec, "Meta's Anne O'Leary appointed Ibec president," 5 September 2024; Business Post, 5 July 2026 (Hunt); Oireachtas Joint Committee on Artificial Intelligence, "Opening Statement — Chair of the AI Advisory Council," Dr Patricia Scanlon, 24 June 2025; Irish Times, 9 February 2024 ("I'm a realist" interview, Scanlon on AI regulation).

The pattern, live: SIPTU and the same asymmetry, in real time

This booklet went to print in the same week the pattern above showed itself again, in public, without any need for interpretation. On 6 July 2026, SIPTU and all 19 unions affiliated to the Irish Congress of Trade Unions' Public Services Committee announced they would begin balloting more than 80,000 public service workers for strike action, after the previous public service pay agreement expired on 30 June with no successor talks even begun.
