SIPTU General Secretary John King did not need this booklet to name the asymmetry — he named it himself: he pointed directly to the Government's swift response to "business concerns," citing the recent hospitality VAT reduction and support packages for the farming and haulage sectors, and contrasted it with the silence public servants had been met with on their own pay. "Our members deliver essential services every day," King said. "Their patience is now being tested."

Worth noting for readers following the AI thread throughout this booklet: the same union statement listed, as one of its core demands for any new agreement, "agreement on the management of AI in the public service" — meaning the labour movement's own live industrial dispute and this booklet's central argument are not parallel concerns being forced together. They are, this week, the same argument, made independently, from two different directions, at the same time.

Sources: RTÉ, "SIPTU to ballot public service members for strike action over pay talks delay," 6 July 2026; TheJournal.ie, "SIPTU to ballot more than 80,000 public service workers on strike action over pay talks," 6 July 2026; SIPTU, "Public service unions to prepare ballots as pay talks process stalls," July 2026.

My Future Fund — 800,000 people's savings, no factsheet in sight

On 1 January 2026, Ireland's first mandatory pension auto-enrolment scheme went live. Roughly 800,000 workers who had no existing pension were automatically enrolled — not asked, enrolled, with the right to opt out only after six months. Their contributions, matched by employer and State, are managed by three companies: Irish Life Investment Managers, Amundi (Europe's largest asset manager, €2.24 trillion under management), and BlackRock (the world's largest, at $11.6 trillion). These names were confirmed in May 2025, eight months before a single euro was invested.

A month after launch, an independent financial commentary podcast made a simple, damning observation: there were still no detailed fund factsheets available to participants. Not obscure trading data — the basic public document any investor is normally entitled to before their money goes anywhere, showing what a fund actually holds. Employees were being auto-enrolled into "lifestyle" funds that shift risk with age, with no advice offered and, at launch, no clear published breakdown of what their money was actually buying.

This matters beyond bureaucratic tidiness. Global funds run by firms this size are, as a matter of ordinary index construction, heavily weighted toward a small cluster of US technology companies — the same companies building the data centres and AI infrastructure this booklet documents throughout. An 800,000-person mandatory scheme without published factsheets means a very large number of Irish workers cannot currently answer a simple question about their own retirement savings: how exposed am I to a single sector, should the AI investment boom this booklet has also examined turn out to be overbuilt?

Nobody has to guess. This is answerable, and there is a right to ask. Participants and citizens can request the fund factsheets directly from NAERSA (the National Automatic Enrolment Retirement Savings Authority) and from the Pensions Authority, which supervises the scheme. If factsheets exist but are not proactively published, that is itself worth naming. If they do not yet exist eighteen months after the managers were named, that is worth naming more.

Sources: Irish Times, "Irish Life, Amundi and BlackRock picked to run Ireland's auto enrolment pot," 20 May 2025; European Pensions, "Three asset managers selected as preferred bidders for Irish AE scheme"; gov.ie, Department of Social Protection, "My Future Fund" press releases; Moneycube, "MyFutureFund: one month in," January 2026.

The reports that justify it — when the evidence base itself fails

The KPMG commitment above is one instance of a wider problem that goes to the heart of how the build-out is justified: the “independent” reports that governments and companies cite as proof are not always as solid as their headline numbers suggest. Two things about “The Value of Data Centres to Ireland” deserve to be on the record together.

First, how the headline number is built. The report’s 876,000 jobs are not jobs in data centres. They are spread across six entire sectors of the economy — ICT, finance, healthcare, transport, retail and professional services — and then “adjusted for their estimated dependency on digital infrastructure.” The Irish Times called this “an absurd argument,” and the reason is plain: by that logic, a large share of every services job in the country can be attributed to data centres. The verifiable reality is far smaller — around 3,300 people were employed in operational data centres in 2024, and the construction jobs that swell the figure are temporary, projected to fall toward zero by 2030. Even the Government’s own summary concedes the 2024 direct contribution was roughly 19,500 jobs (mostly temporary construction) and €2.2 billion in value. The load-bearing words are “enabled” and “underpinned” — consultant-speak meaning those sectors use digital infrastructure, not that the jobs exist because of data centres located here.

Second, the pattern behind it. This is not an isolated lapse by one firm. In the same period, three of the four largest advisory firms in the world were publicly caught moving unverified, AI-generated content into published reports. KPMG withdrew a report on “agentic AI” in 2026 after the Financial Times and the detection service GPTZero found it contained fabricated case studies about real organisations, including UBS, the UK’s NHS, Swiss Federal Railways and Transport for London. EY Canada withdrew a cybersecurity report after researchers found sixteen of its twenty-seven citations were fabricated, including a McKinsey study that did not exist. Deloitte refunded part of a A$440,000 Australian government contract over a report containing errors and invented references. And the Australian scandal runs deeper than a single withdrawn report: allegations concerning the misuse of confidential data cost KPMG senior executives their jobs and triggered a formal investigation by the corporate regulator, ASIC. By mid-2026 the firm's own partners were absorbing the cost directly — pay cuts of 13% flagged for the 2027 financial year (6% for more junior partners), with many inside the firm expecting the real figure to land closer to 30%. Senior partners have been leaving ahead of a "retirement scheme" payout — worth roughly a year's pay, calculated on the previous five years' earnings — before that sum is dragged down by the scandal's hit on earnings, a departure wave that leaves those who stay effectively subsidising the payouts of those who go. The Australian Labor government's own response has been publicly criticised in the financial press as "half-baked." In South Africa, KPMG was dismissed by the government after a second scandal in under a year.

The point is not that all expertise is worthless. It is that the authority arriving wrapped in a logo and a headline figure is exactly the authority that most needs checking. When you are told the data-centre economy is a proven net good because a report says so, the responsible questions are the ones this booklet applies to every claim: who paid for the report, how was the headline number constructed, and what did the commission ask for that the final document does not contain?

Using mission statements — practical routes

  • Greenwashing complaint to the CCPC. Where a company’s public sustainability claims are materially inconsistent with its actual practice in Ireland, a complaint can be filed with the Competition and Consumer Protection Commission. The EU Green Claims Directive will strengthen this route once enacted.

  • Planning-application cross-reference. File an AIE to An Coimisiún Pleanála and Cork County Council asking what environmental and sustainability commitments the applicant made in support of the EPark applications, including DC3 and the March 2025 extension. Commitments made to a public body are representations.

  • Investor pressure. Almost every Irish worker with a pension is an indirect investor in Microsoft, Google, Meta or Amazon. Pension trustees have fiduciary duties — asking your provider what engagement it undertakes on data-centre water disclosure at AGMs is a legitimate and powerful question.

  • The unclean-hands doctrine in planning. If a company cited sustainability commitments in a planning application and has demonstrably failed to honour them, that inconsistency can be raised in any judicial review of a later application. Past representations become part of the planning history.

FOI requests — KPMG, IBEC and pay transparency

File these. The answers, or the refusals, become the evidence.

To DETE (foi@enterprise.gov.ie): “The total cost to the public purse of commissioning the KPMG report ‘The Value of Data Centres to Ireland’, including all fees and associated costs — plus the full contract, tender documents, terms of reference, and all correspondence between DETE and KPMG relating to the commission.”

To DETE: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by KPMG in relation to this commission, given its existing commercial relationships with data-centre operators and the tech sector, and what due diligence was conducted on potential conflicts before the commission was awarded?”

To DETE: “The total cost of all external consultancy reports commissioned by DETE on data centres, AI policy and the Large Energy User Action Plan since 2020 — each report, its author, its cost and its terms of reference.”

To the Department of Children, Disability and Equality: “What representations did IBEC make regarding the timeline for transposing the EU Pay Transparency Directive? Please provide all correspondence, meeting minutes and submissions from IBEC on this matter since January 2025.”

To the Department of Enterprise: “What representations did IBEC make regarding data-centre water disclosure, the LEAP architecture and CRU/2025/236? Please provide all correspondence, meeting minutes and submissions.”

Cross-reference everything returned against lobbying.ie. Every meeting between IBEC and ministers or officials on these topics should be registered. If it is not — that is itself a story.

The same pattern, admitted openly, next door

Ireland is not unusual in this. The United Kingdom's own planning reform — curtailing objection rights to speed up energy infrastructure — has been traced to its actual origin by the people who made it, in their own words, on the record. Chancellor Rachel Reeves has said the approach was conceived at "a smoked salmon and scrambled egg breakfast with corporate lobbyists." Prime Minister Keir Starmer told the Times his own recognition that the planning system needed to change came from conversations with "leading CEOs." Journalist and campaigner George Monbiot, interviewing with Novara Media in July 2026, put the conclusion plainly: government is "prepared to be really vicious and authoritarian when the corporations demand it," while showing no comparable urgency on measures — a meat tax, dietary guidance — that have no corporate lobby pushing for them. The mechanism is the same one this booklet documents in Ireland; only the admission is more direct.

A working alternative exists next door too, and it is worth naming because it is the opposite of "get out of the way." Denmark's approach to community objections over new solar and wind schemes is to offer residents a direct financial share of the revenue the scheme generates — funding a new village hall, community centre, or youth club, for as long as the scheme operates. Monbiot's assessment of the two approaches, tested side by side: the Danish model gets infrastructure built while bringing people with it; the "shut up and put up" model, branding objectors as "blockers," "nimbies," and "zealots," gets things built faster in the short term and "antagonises people, turns them against you, turns them even towards reform" in the long term — undermining the same green transition it claims to be protecting.

The clearest single case study of the underlying pattern is carbon capture and storage — and it is a near-exact structural twin of this booklet's own KPMG and data-centre findings, just wearing different clothes. The UK government has committed £21.7 billion to CCS for "phase one" alone — a handful of projects in two clusters, with further clusters planned. Independent analysis by climate specialists Andrew Boswell and Simon Oldridge — work the government's own Climate Change Committee has not compiled or presented in one place — found the full projected cost through 2050 could reach £264 billion, of which roughly 75% would fall on ordinary bill-payers rather than the state, per the House of Commons Public Accounts Committee's own findings. That could mean up to £198 billion added to UK electricity bills for a program that, on the same analysis, only 5–6% of is actually directed at the genuinely hard-to-abate industries — cement and chemicals — it is publicly justified by. Nearly all the rest is attached to new fossil-fuel-burning infrastructure, much of it new gas plants built specifically to pair with the program, requiring increased imports of liquefied natural gas whose methane leakage across the extraction-to-power chain is not accounted for in the capture figures at all. The UK's own Climate Change Committee projects that by 2050, half of all fossil fuel burned in Britain will be tied to carbon capture programs. The honest description, on this evidence, is not a climate solution — it is continued fossil fuel infrastructure with a capture mechanism attached that has failed in the UK three times already (2005, 2007, 2012) and has never succeeded anywhere in the world at the scale now being funded.

And Monbiot's own prescription for handling exactly this kind of contested, technical-but-moral decision is the mechanism this booklet has built out in full elsewhere. Asked about geoengineering — a decision with the same shape as the ones covered throughout this booklet, technically complex, morally loaded, with entrenched interests on multiple sides — his answer was direct: "These can't be just technocratic decisions... citizens' assemblies would be a really good way of discussing these issues, backed by a proper parliamentary mandate." He named Ireland's own citizens' assemblies on abortion and marriage equality as the working precedent — a country that went, in his words, from a settled "impossible" to a settled "inevitable" within a decade, once the deliberative process was trusted with the question.

Sources: George Monbiot, interviewed by Michael Walker, Novara Media (July 2026). Rachel Reeves' "smoked salmon and scrambled egg breakfast" quote and Keir Starmer's Times interview, as cited in that conversation. UK carbon capture cost analysis — Andrew Boswell and Simon Oldridge, independent climate analysts, as cited by Monbiot; UK Climate Change Committee carbon budget projections; House of Commons Public Accounts Committee findings on the state/bill-payer cost split. Denmark's community revenue-sharing model for renewable energy schemes, as described by Monbiot from direct knowledge of the Danish approach.

The minister's own case, tested against the minister's own numbers

On 4 July 2026, Darragh O'Brien — Minister for Transport, Climate, Energy and the Environment, the department directly responsible for both the grid and the climate targets data centres bear on — published an op-ed making the government's case for continued data-centre expansion. It deserves to be engaged with directly, in his own words, rather than dismissed. He is not wrong that data centres now underpin ordinary digital life, or that energy-intensive industry is a normal feature of successful economies elsewhere in Europe. Those points stand. What follows is not a claim that the minister is acting in bad faith — it is a plain test of his own claims against his own numbers, and against the same week's reporting from his own colleagues.

He states data centres enabled "an estimated €104 billion in gross value added" in 2024 — roughly 20% of the economy. That figure is stated without a named source or methodology in the piece itself, and given this booklet's own extensive documentation of the "Value of Data Centres to Ireland" report's industry-aligned methodology elsewhere, any figure of this size and roundness deserves the same scrutiny before being repeated as fact. He also states plainly: "no household has not been connected to the network because of a data centre." That is a specific, falsifiable claim from a sitting minister, and belongs on the FOI list in this Part rather than taken at face value.

The more revealing gap sits between his piece and the one published the same week by Colin Hunt, chief executive of AIB — a bank whose business is directly financing this exact infrastructure. Hunt's piece uses the language of emergency: "the window is narrowing," "decide faster, build faster, connect faster," progress "impossible to postpone." Urgency is not evidence. And Hunt's own piece contains the evidence against his own urgency: he states that Ireland wastes 10% of its available green electricity because the grid cannot absorb it, and that 79.2% of the country's energy needs were still met by burning carbon in 2025. An argument for building faster, from a piece that admits a tenth of what already exists is going unused, is not an argument for speed. It is a documented case for fixing absorption first.

A live international comparison makes the "we are managing this responsibly" claim testable rather than rhetorical. A 2026 Allianz Trade study of data-centre water consumption by country put a hard number on the outcome, not the rhetoric: Switzerland's data centres consume roughly 0.7 billion litres of water annually — the second-lowest figure in Europe. Ireland's figure in the same study: 3.8 billion litres — more than five times higher, among the worst in Europe, behind only Germany and the UK. Switzerland's advantage is not a more persuasive minister. It is infrastructure Ireland does not yet have: the independent Swiss Datacenter Efficiency Association publishes a public tool scoring every data centre's actual energy and water performance from real operational data, not from company self-reporting, and a citizen survey by AlgorithmWatch CH found roughly 80% of Swiss respondents want mandatory disclosure of energy and water use written into law. The difference between the two countries is not ambition. It is who gets to check the number.

Sources: Darragh O'Brien, "Data centres will play a crucial role in shaping Ireland's economic future," Business Post (4 July 2026). Colin Hunt, "Ireland has momentum on energy infrastructure, now we need nerve," Business Post (2026), including the 10% curtailed renewable generation and 79.2% fossil-fuel-met energy figures, both stated in his own piece. Switzerland/Ireland water consumption comparison: Allianz Trade global data-centre climate study (2026), as reported by Organisator.ch. Swiss Datacenter Efficiency Association navigator tool and AlgorithmWatch CH citizen survey: SWI swissinfo.ch (2026).

Part 4E — The FDI Trap: Venture Capital, Dependency Scenarios, and the Diaspora Alternative

Ireland built the infrastructure for US AI dominance. It now cannot host the EU’s alternative. That is not a coincidence. It is the cost of thirty years of regulatory capture dressed up as Foreign Direct Investment.

What this chapter is about. This chapter examines the deeper financial machinery — venture capital, private equity, and the dependency that foreign direct investment can quietly build. It asks what happens to a host economy when the thing it depends on is structured to extract and move on.

“Whoever controls the volume of money in any country is absolute master of all industry and commerce.” — attributed to James A. Garfield

The concentration problem — documented

Ireland’s corporate tax receipts in 2025: €32.9 billion — 31% of total tax revenue. Three US companies account for 46% of it. Ten corporations account for more than half. The Irish Fiscal Advisory Council has called this concentration “alarmingly narrow,” built on revenue streams largely unrelated to real economic activity in Ireland — and warned that the Government is thinning its fiscal buffer just as the vulnerabilities multiply.

This is not a fringe concern. It is the official position of Ireland’s own fiscal watchdog. The campaign’s question is simple: why is the same Government that accepts this risk on taxation also handing over water, grid capacity and planning sovereignty with no reciprocal obligation at all?

The five FDI dependency scenarios — what history shows

Every country that has built its economic model on a heavy concentration of foreign direct investment has faced one or more of these. Ireland is not immune. The Fiscal Advisory Council, the OECD and independent economists have documented all of them.

Scenario 1 — The Tax Pivot. A change in US tax law, OECD minimum-tax rules or a bilateral treaty renegotiation causes one or more of the top three corporate tax contributors to restructure their Irish operations. Tax receipts fall by 20–40% in a single year. The NTMA itself describes Ireland’s receipts as sensitive to “shifts in global — and particularly US — tax, trade, and regulatory policy.” The OECD Pillar Two minimum tax of 15% is already changing the landscape.

Scenario 2 — The Grid Hostage. Data centres and hyperscalers have consumed so much grid capacity that Ireland cannot attract the next generation of manufacturing, green energy or indigenous tech investment. It cannot host the EU’s €20 billion AI gigafactory programme — five facilities, thousands of permanent high-skilled jobs, European IP — because the grid is already committed to serving the data centres that arrived under the current architecture. Ireland gave away the capacity. Now it cannot host the alternative.

Scenario 3 — The Water Shock. A severe drought — already experienced in Cork County in 2025, already producing Day Zero conditions in the Midleton catchment — forces emergency rationing. Data centres’ undisclosed water consumption becomes a public crisis. Operators cannot be required to reduce usage, because there are no disclosure obligations, no contractual limits and no enforcement mechanism. Communities face restrictions while corporations face none. The political consequences are significant; the legal tools are absent.

Scenario 4 — The Regulatory Shift. The EU introduces mandatory site-level water and energy disclosure — either through the Energy Efficiency Delegated Act, if the Microsoft confidentiality clause is overturned by the 35 MEPs including Lynn Boylan, or through new environmental legislation. Irish operators, having made no voluntary disclosures, face sudden mandatory reporting. The figures, when they emerge, are politically explosive. The grandfather clauses offer no protection from disclosure requirements.

Scenario 5 — The Proxy State Trap. Ireland, as the EU’s largest per-capita data-centre hub and the EU domicile of choice for US tech, finds itself caught between US corporate interests and EU digital-sovereignty objectives. Irish ministers chair EU Council configurations on digital regulation while hosting the infrastructure of the companies being regulated. The conflict of interest is structural, not personal — Ireland becomes unable to advocate for EU positions that conflict with its FDI model, not because its politicians are corrupt but because the architecture makes it impossible.

None of these require bad faith. All are the documented, predictable consequence of a model that prioritised short-term FDI receipts over long-term strategic sovereignty. The nitrogen fixer asks: what would Ireland look like if thirty years of corporate tax receipts had been invested in indigenous capacity instead of spent attracting the next wave of extraction?

Case Study — Goldman Sachs, Marcus, and €220 billion of Irish savings

In July 2026, the Business Post reported that Goldman Sachs had begun hiring staff in Ireland to assess launching Marcus — its digital consumer bank — in Dublin. It would be the Wall Street giant's first move into consumer banking anywhere in the European Union. A Goldman spokesperson confirmed it: "We are exploring bringing our award-winning Marcus deposits business to Ireland."

This case is worth sitting with precisely because it is not a simple story of extraction, and pretending it were would fail this booklet's own standard. There is a genuine, real benefit on offer to ordinary people here. Irish households hold around €220 billion in bank deposits and cash, according to the Central Bank of Ireland — and much of it sits in accounts at the two dominant domestic banks, AIB and Bank of Ireland, paying interest as low as 0.1%. Marcus offers promotional rates as high as 3.75% in the UK. On its face, a serious new competitor forcing better returns for Irish savers who have long been poorly served is exactly the reciprocal, mutually beneficial kind of foreign investment this booklet has been careful to distinguish from the parasitic kind. More competition for Irish deposits is, straightforwardly, good for Irish depositors. That should be said plainly.

But read why Goldman actually wants in, in its own reported terms, and the familiar shape reappears underneath the good news. The attraction is not primarily to enrich Irish savers. It is that Irish deposits give Goldman access to cheap retail funding it can use to finance its EU-wide activity — the deposits are gathered here; the lending, the investment, and the profit are deployed and captured across the EU and beyond. Ireland is attractive, the reporting notes, precisely because it is a "relatively uncompetitive" market after the crisis-era exits of NatWest and KBC left two domestic banks dominant and savers reluctant to switch. It is the same signature the rest of this booklet documents in water and grid and tax, now in household savings: the resource is harvested locally, the value is exported. A better rate for a while is the visible benefit; becoming a low-cost deposit tap for a global bank's continental operations is the structural reality underneath it.

And here is the part that matters most for a booklet about citizen agency: on this particular decision, the public gets essentially no say at all. Unlike a planning application — which, however narrowed, still has a public consultation window, a right to make a submission, an appeal — a banking authorisation is decided between the applicant, the Central Bank of Ireland, and the European Central Bank in Frankfurt, on technical prudential grounds: capital, governance, risk controls, the fitness of directors. There is no public objection step. No observation-on-appeal. No AIE-style right to the file. A decision that shapes where €220 billion of Irish household savings flows, and who profits from it, happens entirely inside a channel the public cannot formally enter. That absence of a say is not an oversight. For decisions of this kind, it is simply how the architecture is built — which is the whole reason a citizen's only remaining lever is the one this booklet is made of: to understand it, name it publicly, and refuse to let it pass unnoticed.

Sources: Laura Noonan, "Is Goldman Sachs about to shake up Irish banking?", Business Post, July 2026 (reporting Bloomberg); Central Bank of Ireland deposit figures; Central Bank of Ireland and Matheson/Practical Law on the credit-institution authorisation process (Central Bank Act 1971; CRD IV; ECB Single Supervisory Mechanism), which contains no public-consultation or third-party-objection stage.

Two Queues: Homes and Data Centres Wait for the Same Wire

There is a way to see this whole booklet's argument in a single, undeniable comparison — two queues, forming at the same window, for the same finite thing. The finite thing is connection capacity: the grid power and water-network capacity that any building, home or data hall, must be granted before it can open. Ireland does not have enough of it to give everyone who wants it. So there is a queue. And it is worth looking, plainly and with the official figures, at who is standing in it.

In the housing queue stand the people. In 2025, 36,284 homes were built — short of the Government's own target of 41,000, a target it quietly abandoned in November 2024 knowing it would miss. Social housing came in at 9,089 against a target of 10,000, while more than 113,000 people remained on social housing support lists. And here is the detail that matters most: roughly 22,500 units that already hold planning permission are set to expire uncommenced between 2025 and 2027 — around 21,000 of them apartments — many stalled precisely because they cannot secure utility connections. Uisce Éireann has stated it can connect a maximum of around 35,000 homes to the network under current capacity. The Tánaiste himself acknowledged that in some areas people cannot get planning permission to build a house at all, for want of a water connection.

In the data centre queue stand the companies. There are close to 100 data centres already operating in Ireland, with 14 more under construction and full planning permission granted for around 40 additional facilities. The industry's own lobby group, Digital Infrastructure Ireland, has publicly complained that €5.6 billion of data centres already have planning permission but no power connection — with a further €7.8 billion of investment in the pipeline behind them. These facilities already consume around 22% of the nation's entire metered electricity — up from 5% in 2015 — a figure EirGrid projects will climb toward 31–34% within a decade. For that fifth of the country's power, the sector directly employs roughly 21,000 people.

Now hold the two queues side by side. In one country, at one moment, roughly 21,000 already-permitted apartments are poised to expire unbuilt, partly for want of the utility connections the State cannot supply fast enough — while the data centre industry publicly and urgently demands that the Government find power for €5.6 billion of its own facilities, which are likewise waiting on the very same constrained connections. Two queues. One finite grid. One finite water network. One queue is homes for some fraction of the 113,000 people on the social housing list; the other is warehouses of servers. Asked about the data centres, the Minister with responsibility for energy, Darragh O'Brien, said: "FDI isn't like a tap, you can't turn it off for a few years and say we'll come back to it in five years' time." That urgency was spoken about the servers. No equivalent sentence was offered about the apartments, or the people.

The counter-argument, stated fairly — because it exists and must be answered. The industry's defenders would say this is a false choice. Data centres and homes do not, they argue, draw from an identical pool: much data-centre demand is on the high-voltage transmission network, while housing connects at distribution level, so a megawatt given to a server is not literally a megawatt taken from a house. They would add that data centres bring investment, tax and 21,000 jobs that help fund the very infrastructure housing needs; that the sector is increasingly required to bring its own generation or storage; and that the housing crisis has many causes — planning delays, construction costs, labour shortages — of which utility capacity is only one. Each of these points contains truth, and an honest booklet does not pretend otherwise.

But the counter-argument does not survive the thing it tries to explain away: the shared scarcity is real, and the State's own bodies say so. Grid and water capacity are not infinitely separable buckets; they draw on the same national investment budget, the same Uisce Éireann build programme, the same EirGrid reinforcement schedule, the same finite pool of engineers and capital. When Uisce Éireann itself states a ceiling of ~35,000 home connections, and the Tánaiste himself says people cannot get planning permission for want of water, the constraint is not hypothetical — it is the Government's own testimony. And the jobs-and-tax defence proves the point rather than rebutting it: if the data centres genuinely funded the infrastructure the country needs, the housing queue would be shrinking, not stalled with 21,000 permitted units about to expire. The choice is not false. It is simply being made quietly, in favour of the queue that lobbies hardest — and the Minister's own words reveal which queue that is.

Sources: CSO new dwelling completions 2025 (36,284); Department of Housing 41,000 target abandoned Nov 2024; social housing 9,089 of 10,000; 113,000 on social housing support (Labour, Conor Sheehan TD). 22,500 permissions expiring 2025–2027 and Uisce Éireann ~35,000-home ceiling — Banking & Payments Federation Ireland Housing Market Monitor, Q4 2024; Tánaiste re water connections (The Journal). Data centre counts — PublicPolicy.ie / ThinkBusiness 2024–2026. €5.6bn permitted-but-unpowered and €7.8bn pipeline — Digital Infrastructure Ireland, via Irish Times, 13 October 2025. Electricity share (22%/2024, 5%/2015, 31–34% projected), 21,000 jobs, and O'Brien "FDI isn't like a tap" quote — CSO/EirGrid, via The Journal, 13 January 2026. The counter-arguments are those advanced by industry bodies including Digital Infrastructure Ireland and IDA Ireland in public commentary.

Case Study — First in the World, Thirty-Second at Home

On 3 July 2026, the Business Post ran a headline that reads, at first glance, as unambiguous national triumph: "Ireland 'fastest growing international finance hub in world'." The London think tank New Financial had found that international financial activity in Ireland grew by 194% in real terms between 2015 and 2025 — against a global average of just 17%, and ahead of India (111%), China (67%), Switzerland (59%) and Singapore (53%). Ireland's finance sector had almost trebled in a decade. It is, on the surface, a success story, and it was reported as one.

Read it against the rest of this booklet, and it is something closer to a confession. The window is the tell: 2015 to 2025 is almost exactly the period over which data centres climbed from 5% to 22% of national electricity. These are not two unrelated booms. They are the same model — Ireland as a low-friction conduit for globally mobile capital — running in two different sectors at once, and being celebrated in both.

But the number that turns the headline inside out is buried lower in the same report, and it is devastating precisely because it is the report's own finding. While Ireland ranks among the top international finance centres in the world, it sits 32nd for domestic financial activity — and has slipped four places since 2015. Read that twice. Ireland is first in the world at hosting other people's finance, and 32nd, and falling, at financing its own economy. New Financial says it plainly: these are sectors that serve multinational clients "with relatively modest connections to financing the domestic economy." That single split — world-leading internationally, sliding domestically — is the entire thesis of this booklet rendered as one official statistic: the resource is gathered here, the value serves elsewhere, and the domestic base does not merely fail to benefit, it actively declines while the headline soars.

The counter-argument, stated fairly. Defenders of the model will say international financial services are a genuine national asset: they employ tens of thousands of well-paid people, generate substantial corporation and payroll tax, and cost Ireland little in natural resources compared to data centres or heavy industry — finance uses offices and talent, not aquifers. They will say the "32nd domestically" figure reflects the small size of Ireland's home market, not neglect of it, and that a small open economy specialising in globally traded services is doing exactly what economic logic recommends. These points are real, and the reinsurance and fintech activity underneath the ranking is legitimate, legal, and skilled work done by real people.

And yet the defence concedes the essential point while trying to soften it. Nobody in this booklet argues the activity is fake or the jobs unreal. The argument is about orientation — about who the machine is ultimately built to serve. A finance sector can be world-leading and still, by the report's own measure, be turning away from its own economy year on year. "The home market is small" explains why domestic activity is modest; it does not explain why it is falling during the exact decade international activity trebled. The two curves moving in opposite directions is not a size effect. It is a priority, revealed. Luxembourg — sixth in the world overall, 40th domestically — is the mirror that removes any doubt: the two states most celebrated as finance hubs are precisely the two whose finance sectors are most disconnected from their own people. Being world-class at this is defined by that disconnection. That is not a flaw in the model. It is the model, working exactly as designed — and it lands in the same season Irish citizens are told there is no water connection for their homes, and no tap that can be turned off for the servers.

Sources: Dominic McGrath, "Ireland 'fastest growing international finance hub in world'," Business Post, 3 July 2026; New Financial (London), International Financial Centres Index, 2026, as reported by the Irish Examiner and Business Post — 194% real-terms growth 2015–2025; 12th overall; 32nd domestically, down four places since 2015; Luxembourg 6th overall / 40th domestically. Counter-arguments are those advanced by IDA Ireland, the Irish Association of Investment Managers, and the Ireland for Finance strategy in public commentary.

The big funds — who they are and what they do

The data-centre boom is not financed primarily by the operating companies. It is financed by the largest asset-management and infrastructure funds in the world — funds managing money on behalf of pension holders, sovereign wealth funds and institutional investors globally. Understanding who owns the infrastructure is essential to understanding who benefits, and who to hold to account.

BlackRock — $12.5 trillion under management. The world’s largest asset manager, with a 15-year presence in Ireland. In 2024 it acquired Global Infrastructure Partners for $3 billion, building one of the largest infrastructure franchises in the world; its portfolio includes data centres across the US, UK and Europe, and its fifth flagship infrastructure fund raised $25.2 billion — the largest private infrastructure raise on record. Crucially, BlackRock’s strategy explicitly targets sectors where “supply has been constrained,” including data centres where power is scarce. Constrained supply means higher returns. The housing crisis, the grid crisis, the water stress — to an infrastructure fund these are not problems. They are the investment thesis.

Vanguard — $10 trillion under management. The world’s second-largest asset manager. As a passive index fund it holds significant stakes in every major operator and hyperscaler — Microsoft, Google, Meta, Amazon — as a matter of course. It does not direct strategy, but its scale means its AGM votes carry enormous weight, and it has consistently voted against shareholder resolutions demanding site-level water and energy disclosure.

The pattern — crisis as asset class. The largest infrastructure funds have made a structural decision: the digital transition, the housing shortage, the energy transition and the water stress are not problems to be solved but infrastructure gaps to be monetised. Every community that cannot build enough housing creates a rental-yield opportunity. Every strained grid creates a data-centre premium. Every water-stressed catchment creates a scarcity premium for whoever controls supply. This is not conspiracy — it is the disclosed investment thesis of the biggest funds on earth. The question for Ireland is whether public assets — the grid, the foreshore, the water — should be available to this logic at all, or governed under a different framework entirely.

A live example: the network you paid for, owned by a fund

The clearest current illustration of this pattern is not a data centre at all — it is the broadband under your own feet. The National Broadband Plan is one of the largest public infrastructure projects in the history of the State: roughly €2.7 billion of public money to bring fibre to the rural premises no commercial operator would serve — homes, farms, schools, the genuine closing of a real divide. In June 2026, Kilkenny became the first county fully built. The good is real, and worth saying plainly. But look at who owns the network the public is paying to build. National Broadband Ireland — the company that owns and operates it, and collects the wholesale charges for decades — has been 80% owned since 2022 by Asterion Industrial Partners, a Madrid-based infrastructure investment fund of exactly the kind described above. It was reported that the founding vehicle was awarded the contract in exchange for an equity investment of around €116,564. The public funds the build; a private fund owns the asset and the income stream that flows from it.

Think of it like this. It is worse than renting. When you rent, at least someone else built the house and carried the risk. This is as if a whole parish had pooled its wages, cleared the site and built the house with its own hands — and then, at the housewarming, been handed a lease instead of a deed. You live in the house you built. You pay rent on it, to a landlord in another country, who sets the rent and can raise it. And when the house is worth most — because everyone can see it is essential and always will be — he is free to sell it over your heads to the next fund, and keep every cent of the gain your work created. You pay, and pay, and never own the walls you raised.

Be fair about what this is and is not. Asterion is a legitimate, regulated infrastructure investor; the network is being delivered on time and on budget; rural fibre is a genuine public good and nobody should pretend otherwise. The point is not that the broadband is bad. The point is the structure — that the same financialisation logic mapped across this section, in which the public carries the cost and the risk while a private fund holds the asset and the upside, now runs through the very wires meant to connect rural Ireland. It is one more answer to the question this Part keeps asking: who ends up owning the things the public pays to build, and on what terms? And it is one more reason to watch, closely, where the next publicly-funded asset quietly changes hands.

Sources: Kilkenny as the first county with the National Broadband Plan main build complete (~19,000 premises; €72m State investment in the county) — RTÉ News and KCLR (18 June 2026). National Broadband Ireland (the trading name of NBI Infrastructure DAC and NBI Deployment DAC) 80% acquired by Asterion Industrial Partners in 2022, with Granahan McCourt (Granahan McCourt Capital and Tetrad Corporation) and Yakida Ltd holding the remainder; NBI as wholesale network operator — RTÉ, The Irish Times, Silicon Republic, Asterion and NBI governance pages (2022–2026). The reported ~€116,564 equity figure for the contract award — Business Post, as reported by Silicon Republic (2022); this is the reported equity investment, not the total of the shareholders’ contractual commitments and guarantees, and should be cited with that caveat. Asterion is a legitimate infrastructure investment manager; this example concerns the ownership structure of a publicly-funded asset, not any allegation of wrongdoing.

The Diaspora Sovereign Fund — a proposal

Around 70 million people worldwide claim Irish descent, including some of the wealthiest individuals and most influential communities in the US, the UK, Australia and Canada. This is not sentiment. It is capital.

A partial precedent already exists: the Irish Diaspora Loan Fund, a Central Bank-authorised investment fund for overseas investors seeking Irish residency. It works. It is also tiny and immigration-focused. The proposal here is different in kind — a citizen-owned, democratically governed Diaspora Sovereign Fund: not for residency, not for profit maximisation, but for strategic public ownership of the commons.

The structure: Irish citizens at home and abroad invest in a fund whose mandate is explicitly public-benefit-first. It acquires or holds strategic assets — port land, foreshore, grid infrastructure, water capacity — on behalf of the Irish public, with first-refusal rights for communities. Returns are reinvested in public goods. Governance runs through a permanent Citizens’ Assembly with diaspora representation.

The model is not without precedent. Norway’s Government Pension Fund Global ($1.5 trillion) was built from oil revenues with ethical guidelines, parliamentary oversight and a mandate to serve future generations rather than current shareholders; it holds nearly 1.5% of the entire global stock market for Norwegian citizens. Iceland, after 2008, took public ownership of its banks rather than bailing out private shareholders, imposed capital controls, prosecuted senior bankers and repaid its IMF loans ahead of schedule. Scotland’s National Investment Bank, established in 2020, runs a mission-led, public-benefit mandate rather than a profit-first one. And Ireland’s own ISIF ($28 billion) already carries a “double bottom line” mandate — commercial returns and economic activity in Ireland — and has invested in Cork Harbour port infrastructure for offshore wind. The architecture exists. The democratic mandate does not.

The contrast with the Future Ireland Fund (projected at €100 billion by 2035) is the point. That fund is built on volatile FDI corporate tax receipts, managed by civil servants under the NTMA, with no citizen governance, no democratic mandate and no community-first criteria. It will be drawn down from 2040 for pensions and health — legitimate purposes, but entirely passive and entirely inside the existing dependency model. The Diaspora Sovereign Fund inverts the logic: rather than waiting for FDI receipts to fund services, it mobilises Irish people worldwide to actively own the strategic assets currently being handed to foreign corporations.

Citizens’ Assembly — first dibs on Port of Cork and strategic land

The Port of Cork is a public asset. Cork Harbour foreshore is public. The former industrial land at Little Island, Ringaskiddy and Haulbowline is historically public or State-linked. These are the sites being converted to data-centre use, or proposed for it.

The demand: before any strategic State or harbour land is disposed of, converted or committed to private use, a Citizens’ Assembly with diaspora representation has first right of consideration. The first question is not “which corporation wants it?” but “what does this community, and this country, need it for?” Housing, public renewable energy, marine restoration, amenity, local enterprise — these have first call. Extraction comes last, if at all. This is the commons principle operationalised: it needs legislation, a statutory community-benefit-first test, but it also needs the political will that comes from organised public demand. That is what this campaign is building.

Stricter penalties — including prison — for white-collar and environmental crime

It is worth knowing what the law actually allows here. When a fish kill happens on an Irish river, the maximum fine per charge in the district court — where these cases are almost always heard — is €5,000. Not per fish. Not per year of damage to a fishery that can take a decade to recover. Per charge. The August 2025 Blackwater fish kill, which wiped out an estimated 42,000 fish and became the largest recorded fish kill in the state, is a case in point of how little consequence attaches even to catastrophic pollution — though it's worth being precise: the inter-agency investigation into that kill never established a cause, and explicitly found no causal link to any of the licensed operators it examined, including North Cork Co-op. What we can say is that the same co-op was separately fined just €4,000 and €8,000 on two later occasions for unrelated discharges into a Blackwater tributary — real convictions, at the statutory ceiling, for real pollution. That ceiling is the point. A person who steals €5,000 faces up to ten years in prison under the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. A company convicted of poisoning a river faces, at most, a fraction of that penalty, spread across as many charges as can be proven. That is not an anomaly. It is a policy choice.

And the Blackwater is not an outlier — it is the emblem of a national collapse now being spelled out, in the Oireachtas's own committee rooms, in language that leaves no room for reassurance. At a joint meeting of the Oireachtas committees on fisheries and on climate, environment and energy in late June 2026, Martin McEnroe, chairman of the Angling Council of Ireland, told TDs and senators that wild salmon could be gone from Irish rivers within a decade unless drastic action is taken "now, not tomorrow, yesterday." The numbers behind that warning are stark and official: annual salmon returns have fallen from an estimated 1.7–2 million fish in the 1970s to between 150,000 and 250,000 today — a decline of roughly 90% in a single human lifetime. Inland Fisheries Ireland's own landmark report documented 2,107 fish kills between 1969 and 2022, with agriculture the most common known cause, followed by eutrophication, industrial, municipal and other discharges; and a further ~19,000 fish were killed in just 30 recorded incidents between January 2023 and July 2024 alone.

But the most damning single line from that committee meeting is the one that ties directly back to this booklet's central argument about a system built not to add things up. McEnroe told the committee that a great many pollution incidents never make it into the official record at all — "there's not a day goes by that I don't hear of an incident of some kind of a fish kill or pollution that's never recorded." Sit with what that means: the 2,107 figure, alarming as it is, is an undercount by the admission of the people closest to the water. The official record is not the ceiling of the damage; it is only the portion that happened to be witnessed, reported, and logged. On the Blackwater specifically, TD Pádraig Mac Lochlainn said he found it "really disturbing" that such an ecological disaster saw nobody held responsible, and An Taisce's Dr Elaine McGoff called the failure to identify the source of the pollution quickly "a major failing" and the penalties for serious pollution offences "like a slap on the wrist, essentially." That is not a campaigner's phrasing imported from outside. It is sworn evidence before an Oireachtas committee — the State's own witnesses describing the State's own enforcement regime in exactly the terms this booklet has used throughout: penalties too small to deter, causes too rarely established, and a record that captures only the fraction of the harm that could not be missed.

Sources: Joint meeting of the Oireachtas Committees on Fisheries and Maritime Affairs and on Climate, Environment and Energy, late June 2026, as reported by the Irish Examiner (David Forsythe) and The Irish Times (30 June 2026); Angling Council of Ireland opening statement (Martin McEnroe); salmon return figures (1.7–2m in the 1970s vs 150,000–250,000 today, ~90% decline); Inland Fisheries Ireland, "Fish Kills in Ireland — History, Current Status and Recovery" (2,107 fish kills, 1969–2022; agriculture the leading known cause); IFI records of ~19,000 fish killed in 30 incidents, Jan 2023–Jul 2024; committee remarks of Pádraig Mac Lochlainn TD and Dr Elaine McGoff (An Taisce).

  • Scaled fines. Mandatory minimum fines for environmental breaches, scaled to the operator’s turnover — not a fixed €5,000 ceiling. The EU Environmental Crime Directive (2024) requires member states to criminalise serious environmental offences. Ireland must transpose it fully and promptly.

  • Personal liability. Personal criminal liability for directors and senior officers of companies that commit serious environmental or regulatory breaches — not just corporate fines absorbed as a cost of doing business.

  • The Icelandic precedent. The 2008 banking collapse cost Irish citizens roughly €64 billion. No senior banker was prosecuted. The Corporate Enforcement Authority must be resourced and mandated to prosecute, not merely investigate.

  • Criminalise hidden abstraction. Water abstraction without disclosure should be criminalised, not regulated. If you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it; if you will not disclose it, you should not be permitted to abstract it.

These are not radical proposals. They are standard practice in comparable jurisdictions. The radical thing is that Ireland does not have them.

On private gatherings of public influence — the transparency demand

The Bilderberg Group meets annually — around 130 senior politicians, business leaders, academics and media executives, under Chatham House rules, with no published agenda, no transcript and no accountability for what is discussed. Irish ministers and senior officials have attended.

The demand is not to ban private gatherings — people can meet whoever they wish. It is narrower and achievable: any Irish minister, senior civil servant, regulator or public official who attends a private gathering where public policy, regulation or procurement is discussed must disclose that they attended, who else was present, and the general topics. This is simply the lobbying-transparency requirement that already applies to formal meetings, extended to the informal gatherings where the same influence is exercised without the same scrutiny. The lobbying.ie register covers formal contacts; it does not cover private dinners, conference sidelines or invitation-only events — and that gap is where regulatory capture actually happens.

  • Mandatory disclosure by all public officials of attendance at any private gathering of more than five people where public policy is discussed, within 30 days.

  • Public and searchable — cross-referenceable with the lobbying.ie register.

  • SIPO powers to investigate undisclosed attendance and impose meaningful penalties.

This is not about stopping people meeting. It is about making visible what is already happening — so the public can see whose interests are being served when the doors are closed.

The ideology is not hidden — it was published

You do not need a conspiracy when the worldview is already in print. This booklet has insisted throughout that the system needs no secret, and here is the clearest proof of it. The most influential argument against the kind of democratic accountability this booklet demands was not whispered behind a closed door; it was published, under his own name, in 2009, by Peter Thiel — co-founder of PayPal and Palantir, the first outside investor in Facebook, and one of the most consequential financiers of the technology that now sits in Irish fields. In an essay for the Cato Institute titled The Education of a Libertarian, Thiel set out a thesis he has never retracted: that the task is not to win democratic politics but to escape it. In the line most often quoted from that essay, he wrote: “I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible.” Politics, in this view, is a trap to be engineered around; the goal is to build systems that place decisions beyond the reach of voters — to escape to some new and as-yet-undiscovered space, whether in cyberspace, on platforms floated on the open ocean, or eventually in outer space. He described an outright race between politics and technology, with human freedom as the prize, and urged his side to stop trying to persuade the public and start building its way out of needing to.

It is worth being precise about who he believed had broken democracy, because this is the part most people are shown out of context — and it is worse in context, not better. Thiel named two groups. The very idea of a capitalist democracy had become a contradiction, he argued, because of the vast growth in welfare recipients and the extension of the vote to women — two constituencies he frankly described as inconvenient for his politics. Read it plainly: the complaint is not that democracy is imperfect, but that democracy stopped delivering the right answers once the poor and women could vote in it. And the proposed remedy was not to win those voters over. It was to build a world that would not have to ask them. That is the intellectual root of every mechanism set out in Part 1 — the grandfather clause, the confidentiality clause, the fast-track that skips the county council, the strategic-infrastructure designation that lifts a decision up out of local reach. None of those Irish instruments were drafted by Thiel, and this booklet does not claim they were. But they rest on his premise: that the public is an obstacle to be routed around, and that the decisions that matter should be made where ordinary people cannot get at them.

Sixteen years later, in his own words again, he named the thing this booklet is asking for as the danger to be resisted. In a New York Times interview with Ross Douthat, published 26 June 2025, Thiel was asked directly what he meant by a public warning he had been giving about "the Antichrist." His answer is worth reading in full rather than summarised, because summary would soften it. He said the default political response to existential risk — nuclear weapons, pandemics, environmental collapse — is to reach for a "one-world government to control all the computers, log every single keystroke," and that this impulse toward global regulatory oversight is, in his own framing, "Antichrist or Armageddon": the choice between a coming totalitarian peacemaker and civilisational catastrophe. He named the FDA and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission as existing examples of exactly this kind of overreaching regulatory power, "regulating not just drugs in the U.S. but de facto in the whole world." And asked, directly, who he thought could plausibly wield that kind of power, he did not name a dictator or a tech tyrant. He said environmentalism is "the only thing people still believe in in Europe," stronger, in his account, than any political or religious alternative on offer — and named the specific person he considers its most likely vessel: "in our world, it's far more likely to be Greta Thunberg."

Set his own words next to this booklet's own argument and the alignment is exact, not metaphorical. The EU Environmental Crime Directive, the ecocide-comparable offences now entering force across the Union, the mandatory water and energy disclosure this booklet has spent hundreds of pages arguing for — all of it is precisely the "peace and safety" regulatory architecture Thiel names as his feared endpoint. He is not being read into this position. He stated it, plainly, in response to a direct question, in one of the most widely read newspapers in the world. Douthat himself pressed him on the obvious irony: given that Thiel is, in his own words, "deeply invested in Palantir, in military technology, in technologies of surveillance," might the tools he is building be exactly what a genuinely authoritarian figure would use, rather than the accountability structures he fears? Thiel's answer was not a denial. It was: "I obviously don't think that that's what I'm doing" — followed by a return to the FDA and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, and no further answer to the question actually asked.

The NYT interview was not a stray remark — it was the public edge of a sustained project. From 15 September to 6 October 2025, Thiel delivered a four-part lecture series titled "The Antichrist," hosted by ACTS 17 Collective at the Commonwealth Club in San Francisco. The event listing, publicly available, names the thinkers Thiel drew on across the series: René Girard, Francis Bacon, Jonathan Swift, John Henry Newman — and Carl Schmitt. That last name is worth knowing plainly rather than passing over: Schmitt was a German legal theorist and Nazi party member, best known for the doctrine that the true sovereign is "he who decides on the exception" — the figure with the power to suspend the ordinary legal order in a declared emergency. This booklet makes no claim about what was said inside those four lectures, which were explicitly billed as off-the-record. What is fact, and publicly listed under Thiel's own event: Schmitt's theory of emergency power was one of the frameworks he chose to have his audience think through alongside his warning about regulatory "peace and safety." A reader can draw their own conclusion about what it means that the same man warning against a coming one-world regulatory order also considered it worth an audience's time to study the theorist of legally suspending democracy in a crisis.

And the same man, asked a far simpler question, hesitated. In a 2025 New York Times interview, the columnist Ross Douthat asked Thiel whether he wanted the human race to endure. Thiel paused so long that Douthat remarked on it — twice — before he managed a qualified “yes.” It would be cheap to hang too much on one awkward moment, and this booklet will not pretend to read anyone’s mind. The hesitation is recorded here for one honest reason only: a worldview that treats ordinary, rooted, embodied human life — the household, the parish, the vote, the field — as a limitation to be transcended is the same worldview that can treat a county’s water and a nation’s grid as inputs to be drawn down without anyone local being asked. The line connecting the essay to the data centre is not a plot. It is a consistent preference — for exit over voice, for the engineered fact over the democratic argument, for the undiscovered country over the inconvenient neighbours.

Sources: Peter Thiel, “The Education of a Libertarian,” Cato Unbound, 13 April 2009 (his own published essay; the quoted line and the welfare/“franchise to women” passage are verbatim from it, here quoted minimally and otherwise paraphrased). The “escape from politics,” the cyberspace/seasteading/outer-space framing and the “race between politics and technology” are all from the same essay. The 2025 exchange on whether the human race should endure — the “Interesting Times with Ross Douthat” interview, The New York Times (summer 2025), as reported across multiple outlets. This section quotes Mr Thiel’s own published and recorded words and draws no inference beyond them; it makes no claim that he directs Irish policy, and it deliberately sets aside unrelated personal allegations as irrelevant to the argument and unsafe to assert.

First, ask what “politics” means

The whole argument hides inside one undefined word. When Thiel says the goal is to escape politics, he is relying on you not to ask what the word means — because the moment you define it, the move is exposed. Politics is not parties and elections; that is only its plumbing. At root — from the Greek polis, the community — politics is simply the way people who share a place, and who do not all agree, decide together how to live. The political theorist Bernard Crick called it a civilising activity, for a blunt reason: the alternative to politics is not harmony, it is force. You need politics precisely because people disagree and cannot avoid one another. Where there is no politics, there is only whoever is strongest, or richest, getting their way.

And here is the part that matters for everyone reading this. Politics is the one arena in which the person with no money still counts. The market hands out power in proportion to wealth — a billion euro, a billion votes. Politics, at its best, hands it out in proportion to numbers and voice — one person, one vote, and the right to stand up at the council meeting whether or not you own a thing. It is, structurally, the great leveller of the powerless. The East Cork household has no leverage in a boardroom or a bond market. It has leverage in politics — the vote, the objection, the submission, the freedom-of-information request.

So read his phrase again. “Technology as an alternative to politics” sounds like escaping gridlock and red tape — and who would defend gridlock? But define the word and the trick is plain: what is being escaped is accountability to people who can be neither bought nor outvoted. He even named who he believed had broken it — the rise in welfare recipients and the extension of the vote to women, the moment the franchise widened past property and past men. And the tell is in his own life: he did not escape politics at all. He funds candidates, mentored a vice-president, meets a head of state in Buenos Aires, sits in an invitation-only society. He is up to his neck in politics. What he stepped away from in California — just ahead of a wealth-tax vote — was the democratic part: the part where he could be outvoted. That is the sleight of hand the whole project runs on. Call democracy “politics.” Call the escape from democracy “technology.” And hope nobody asks for the definition.

The line worth keeping: when anyone tells you they want to escape politics, ask what they mean by the word. Politics is just the name for the room where the people who cannot buy their way out still have to be listened to. He does not want to escape politics. He wants to escape you.

The covert method: proxies, patience, and closed doors

A published case study, not a theory. Ryan Holiday’s 2018 book Conspiracy is the clearest account in print of how modern power gets its way without its name on the paperwork. It documents how the investor Peter Thiel, over several years and through intermediaries, secretly bankrolled Hulk Hogan’s privacy lawsuit against the website Gawker until the litigation bankrupted it. What makes the book useful here is exactly the discomfort it provokes. You can hold that Gawker’s own conduct was often cruel and indefensible — many did — and still be unsettled by the method: patient, indirect, conducted through proxies precisely so that the hand behind it stayed invisible until the work was done. Holiday presents it not as villainy but as instruction — a study in how a determined actor with capital achieves a strategic aim through other people’s names and a long clock. That is a documented strategy, on the public record, written up by an author with the principal’s cooperation. It is the opposite of a rumour.

The same logic, in the planning file. The reason this belongs in a booklet about Irish infrastructure is structural, and it must be put carefully. The method Conspiracy describes — keep the principal’s name off the public document, act through intermediaries, move patiently — is the same logic by which large infrastructure is routinely assembled: a generic special-purpose vehicle or holding company fronts the planning application, and the multinational it ultimately serves appears late, if at all (see the corporate-tracing steps in Parts 4B and 4F). To be explicit, because the clean-hands rule demands it: this is an observation about a recurring method, not a claim that Mr Thiel, or any named individual, is behind any particular Irish data centre. The point is the shape, not an accusation. The remedy is the same one this booklet keeps returning to — trace the Company Registration Office filing on any application, and judge each project on its own file.

The closed door, on Irish soil. In June 2026 a data leak gave that abstract concern a concrete, local form. The Swiss researcher maia arson crimew — who previously exposed the US No-Fly List — found internal records left open in the website code of Dialog, an invitation-only society co-founded by Thiel that has declined for roughly two decades to disclose its members; Wired obtained and verified the material. Dialog’s 2026 retreat is scheduled for 12–16 August at the Powerscourt Hotel in Co. Wicklow. The leaked agenda of off-the-record sessions, as reported, included titles such as “Bring Back Nuclear,” “Build-a-Cult” and “Navigating WWIII.” But the single detail that matters most for this booklet is duller and sharper than any lurid headline: Wired reported that none of the registrants used a government email address — every one signed up with a personal or corporate account, placing the entire gathering outside the reach of public-records law. A researcher at George Mason University told the Guardian that it is in precisely these rooms, where financial, technological and political power converge, that “agendas [are] being set and opinions [are] being shaped,” and called it “a problem for democracy.” The named invitees spanned the political spectrum, and several denied any involvement — so the point is not a list of the guilty. The point is the architecture: a standing, off-record forum of people who help set global technology and policy direction, meeting in Ireland, arranged so that attendance falls outside every transparency law a citizen could invoke.

Which is exactly the gap above. That is not a separate story from the transparency demand on the previous page; it is its proof. A lobbying register that captures the formal meeting and misses the invitation-only retreat is a register with a hole shaped precisely like this. The demand stands unchanged, and the Powerscourt booking is the argument for it: any Irish minister, official or regulator who attends a gathering where public policy is discussed should have to say that they were there, who else was, and what was on the table — whatever email address the invitation came to.

*Sources: Conspiracy: Peter Thiel, Hulk Hogan, Gawker, and the Anatomy of Intrigue by Ryan Holiday (2018) — the Thiel/Gawker/Hogan facts are long-settled public record. Dialog society leak — Wired (June 2026), which obtained and verified the records exposed by researcher maia arson crimew; corroborated by The Hollywood Reporter, The Washington Times, The Guardian and others. Powerscourt Hotel retreat dates (12–16 August 2026), reported session titles, and the finding that no registrant used a government email address — Wired, as reported. “A problem for democracy” — Prof. Janine Wedel (George Mason University), quoted in The Guardian. This section deliberately excludes unverified and off-topic material that circulates alongside this story: it makes no claim that any named individual directs Irish data-centre development; it does not assert what was or will be decided behind Dialog’s closed doors beyond the reported agenda titles; it notes that several named invitees have denied involvement; and it omits an unrelated and explicitly unconfirmed allegation reported elsewhere, on the principle that a transparency argument is strongest when it asserts only what the record will bear.

The escape, made literal

When the vote threatens to go the wrong way, those with enough wealth can simply leave. The escape Thiel sketched in theory in 2009 he had, by 2026, begun to put into practice. In May 2026 the New York Times reported that he had bought a roughly $12 million mansion in Buenos Aires, enrolled his children in school there, and met President Javier Milei several times — having left California in late 2025, just ahead of a residency deadline attached to a proposed one-time wealth tax on billionaires. The reporting describes the move as temporary, and he has not renounced his American base; his firms remain wound tightly into the US state, Palantir alone booking $687 million in US government revenue in a single quarter. So this is not the story of a man fleeing in the night. It is something quieter and more instructive: when a jurisdiction proposes to tax or constrain great wealth, great wealth can relocate to one that will not — to a head of state whose own cabinet welcomed billionaires to “the new land of freedom,” and which is reportedly designing a fast-track “golden passport” to match. Set that beside the rest of this booklet and the asymmetry is the entire argument in a single image. The East Cork household cannot move to escape the hosepipe ban or the substation on the hill; the dairy farmer cannot move the field out from under the nitrogen rules. Capital is mobile; people are rooted. The freedom to exit is not shared out equally — and a politics organised around exit is, by definition, a politics built for the people who can afford to take it.

Sources: Thiel’s Buenos Aires property, the meetings with President Milei, the timing of his departure from California relative to the proposed state wealth-tax residency deadline, and the “temporary” characterisation — The New York Times (28 May 2026), corroborated by Newsweek, The Washington Times, Newsgram and others (May–June 2026). Palantir’s ~$687m quarterly US government revenue — company reporting as cited in that coverage. The “new land of freedom” welcome — Argentine cabinet chief Manuel Adorni, as reported. The move is presented as documented fact plus the campaign’s own interpretation of its meaning (the mobility of capital versus the rootedness of communities); no claim is made that Mr Thiel has any role in Irish data-centre development. The "Antichrist" interview — Ross Douthat, "Peter Thiel and the Antichrist," "Interesting Times," The New York Times (26 June 2025), transcript published in full; all quotations taken directly from the published transcript. The "Antichrist" lecture series — event listing, ACTS 17 Collective, hosted at the Commonwealth Club, San Francisco, 15 September to 6 October 2025 (via Luma); named influences, dates, and venue are as publicly listed; the lecture content itself was off-the-record and is not claimed or described here.

Leaving is one option. Buying the outcome is the other.

The escape Thiel sketched has a twin, and it is louder, cheaper per vote, and already running. If one answer to a democratic decision you dislike is to leave for a friendlier jurisdiction, the other is to make sure the decision never binds you — by spending enough on the people who make it that they learn not to cross you. In 2025 that second answer acquired a name and a war chest. Leading the Future is a super-PAC network built to reward candidates friendly to the artificial-intelligence industry and to defeat those who would regulate it in ways its funders dislike. It launched with around $100 million and reported roughly $125 million raised by the end of the year. Its donors are not a mystery, because United States election law made them file: the venture firm Andreessen Horowitz, whose founders Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz each gave $12.5 million; the president and co-founder of OpenAI, Greg Brockman, who with his wife matched that; the Palantir co-founder Joe Lonsdale; the investor Ron Conway; and the AI company Perplexity. The model is borrowed openly from the crypto industry’s Fairshake, which spent something like $130 million in 2024 to unseat lawmakers it judged insufficiently friendly — a demonstration the AI money watched, and copied.

The stated goal is the Irish planning trick, translated into American electoral law. What the network says it wants is a single national AI framework — in its own words, “a responsible national framework” — that would override the patchwork of state laws. That sounds tidy until you notice what it means in practice: thirty-eight American states passed AI laws in 2025, and a federal framework that preempts them lifts the decision up, out of every state that might choose to protect its own residents, to a level where a handful of well-funded lobbies can contest it all at once. Readers of this booklet have met that move already. It is the strategic-infrastructure designation that lifts an Irish data-centre decision up out of the county council’s reach; it is the grandfather clause that settles the terms before the public arrives. The instinct is identical across the ocean and across the subject matter: find the level of government where ordinary people can no longer get at the decision, and make the decision there.

The tell is in how the campaign is fought, not what it claims to want. The network’s first target was Alex Bores, a New York state legislator who co-authored the RAISE Act — the Responsible AI Safety and Education Act, one of the first state AI-safety laws adopted anywhere — and who was running for a seat in Congress. A subsidiary, the Think Big PAC, went after him. But the attack ads, as reported, did not argue about artificial intelligence at all, for the plain reason that a message of fewer rules for AI companies is not one that wins votes. Instead they manufactured an unrelated line of attack, weaponising the candidate’s own past employment at Palantir on immigration work — which produces the small, clarifying absurdity of a Palantir co-founder’s money funding a campaign to destroy a candidate for once having worked at Palantir. The point was never the single seat. The point is the demonstration: spend enough, publicly enough, that the next legislator who considers an AI-safety bill remembers what happened to this one. Even OpenAI’s own chief executive, Sam Altman, has said publicly that the democratic process should remain more powerful than the companies — a sentiment his co-founder’s cheque to this PAC exists precisely to reverse.

And the same money reaches the layer above politics — the story you are told about it. Reporting in 2026, including an investigation by Model Republic and coverage in the technology press, linked the network to a purported news site that published AI-generated articles closely echoing the PAC’s deregulatory line and naming AI-safety researchers and advocates as targets — in one account, using automated agents posing as human journalists to solicit real quotes from real people. Presented as fact here is only what the reporting established: that a well-financed political project seeking fewer rules on AI was tied to manufactured “journalism” advancing that same aim. It is the media version of the electoral move — capture the channel, and you no longer have to win the argument on its merits.

Why this sits in an Irish booklet, and the line this booklet will not cross while making the point. The firms behind this PAC — the venture houses, OpenAI, Palantir — are the same names whose physical infrastructure lands in Irish fields and draws on Irish water and power, and the European Union, with its AI Act and Ireland’s own incoming AI Office, is the obvious next regulatory frontier they will contest. This is not America’s problem observed from a safe distance; it is the clearest live proof of the thesis this whole booklet turns on — that the governing instinct of this worldview is to place the decisions that matter beyond the reach of the people they affect, whether by leaving, by lifting the decision above local reach, or simply by spending enough that no one dares. And the caution, stated plainly because the standard demands it: this is not a verdict on artificial intelligence as a technology, nor on an entire industry, nor on the researchers and workers inside it. It is a specific, named, filed set of funders behind one specific political project.
