This booklet — openly assembled with the help of an AI, and honest about that on its own pages — holds no brief for a reflexive hostility to the tools. Its concern is the only one it has ever had: whether the public keeps the power to say no, and to whom that power is quietly being sold.

Sources: Leading the Future — launched 2025; ~$100m at launch and ~$125m raised by end-2025, with ~$70m cash on hand entering 2026: FEC filings as reported by NOTUS (30 January 2026), CNBC (30 January 2026), Axios (30 January 2026) and Wikipedia’s consolidated entry. Named donors and amounts (Andreessen Horowitz — Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz $12.5m each; OpenAI president Greg Brockman and spouse $12.5m each; Palantir co-founder Joe Lonsdale; Ron Conway/SV Angel; Perplexity): NOTUS and Axios, from the super PAC’s own FEC disclosure. The Fairshake model and ~$130m 2024 crypto spend: The Nation (21 May 2026) and Wall Street Journal as reported. The national-framework / federal-preemption aim and the 38 states with 2025 AI laws: The Nation and Axios. Alex Bores, the RAISE Act, and the Think Big PAC targeting him: Gizmodo (25 November 2025), CNBC and The Hill (2026); the attack-ad framing and its Palantir-employment angle as characterised by The Ezra Klein Show (NYT) and corroborated in reporting. The manufactured-journalism allegation (the Acutus/“Wire” site and AI agents posing as journalists): Model Republic investigation and Tom’s Hardware (April 2026), as summarised by Wikipedia — presented here strictly as reported. This section excludes a contested personal allegation concerning a named individual that appears in the source interview, as unverifiable and irrelevant to the argument; it names only funders whose contributions are matters of public FEC record, and it advances the democratic-accountability point without asserting any candidate’s guilt, innocence, or electoral outcome.

Two manifestos: Palantir’s, and the answer to it

Thiel’s essay was one man’s worldview; this is the company saying it out loud, under its own name. On the weekend of 19 April 2026, Palantir’s official account posted a twenty-two-point manifesto — a condensed version of The Technological Republic, the 2025 book by its chief executive Alexander Karp and its head of corporate affairs Nicholas Zamiska. Within two days it had been seen thirty-two million times. The argument is not hidden and needs no decoding: Silicon Valley owes the nation a debt and has an affirmative obligation to build for its defence; national service should be a universal duty; and, on the question that ought to give everyone pause, the point is not whether autonomous AI weapons will be built but who builds them first — so, it urges, dispense with the debates about the morality of it and proceed. The line that states the thesis most plainly is the one worth keeping: that the power which decides this century “will be built on software.” This is not a fringe document. The Times of London called the book the AI manifesto inspiring Keir Starmer’s government — which is to say the worldview is not only in print, it is in the room where policy is written, in the country next door.

It matters who Palantir is when it says this, and the record is not in dispute. The company was co-founded in 2003 by Peter Thiel and Karp, with early backing from the CIA’s venture arm. Its software is reported to sit inside Israeli military targeting during the Gaza conflict, the US Army’s Maven system, a reported $30 million contract to build the platform US immigration enforcement uses to identify and track people for deportation, and police surveillance in New York. When a company embedded that deeply in decisions about who is watched, detained and targeted announces that hard power now runs on software, it is not forecasting the weather. It is describing its own business.

The answer came four days later, from a man who helped build the thing they want to own. On 23 April 2026 Harry Halpin published an “Anti-Palantir Manifesto” on the blog of Nym, the privacy company he co-founded and runs. Halpin is no bystander to this technology. Before Nym he led the standardisation of the Web Cryptography API across every major browser at the World Wide Web Consortium, working under the web’s inventor, Tim Berners-Lee; he holds a doctorate in informatics; and he completed his postdoctoral work under the late French philosopher of technology Bernard Stiegler — the same Stiegler who spent his last years warning that the platforms were manufacturing a kind of engineered distraction and a loss of knowledge, which is precisely the concern this booklet raises in its chapter on curiosity and the schools. So the reply to Palantir’s chief executive did not come from a protest camp. It came from one of the architects of the web’s own cryptographic plumbing.

Strip away the heat, and the argument underneath is the serious one. Halpin’s case, stated as his, runs like this. Surveillance sold as protection against an external enemy rarely stays external — it turns inward, first onto immigrants and dissidents, then onto anyone who might organise, object, or simply want out. The capacity for mass surveillance that once belonged to states has been quietly privatised to corporations that answer to no vote and no election. And the next enclosure, in his account, arrives wearing the friendliest possible mask: biometric digital identity, mandated for ordinary access to the internet and justified in the name of protecting children. On the motive he ascribes to that a reader can keep their own counsel — but the trajectory he is pointing at is not hypothetical, because age-verification and digital-identity mandates are a live and advancing debate across Europe right now, and worth watching on exactly the terms he sets: who gets gatekept, and who decides.

His remedy is where this booklet has to be honest, because it disagrees with him — and the disagreement is the useful part. Halpin’s answer is compressed into a phrase that deliberately turns Karp’s own words back on him: rights, he argues, must be defended not by appeals to regulation but by “the hard power of code” — encryption and design that make surveillance difficult for whoever attempts it. That is not a slogan; it is the argument of his peer-reviewed work. In a 2025 paper in the journal Techné, Halpin traces how, after the Snowden revelations, the successive attempts to write a grand “Magna Carta for the Web” — a charter of digital rights — all failed, while the quiet, unglamorous maintenance of the internet’s encryption standards actually delivered the right to private communication, enforced in running code rather than in a document anyone could later repeal. Now set that against the whole method of this booklet, which spends hundreds of pages on freedom-of-information law, judicial review, EU directives and the enforcement of statutes. The two are not enemies, but they are a genuine argument, and it is worth having in the open: a right no one can enforce is only a wish, and Halpin is right that code sometimes enforces where law merely promises. This campaign’s wager is not code instead of law — it is law made real by being used, and code where the law cannot reach. His caution belongs on the record beside that wager, not airbrushed out of it.

And one more line has to be held here, precisely because Halpin is on the right side of the argument. His manifesto is a polemic, and in places it reaches for exactly the register this booklet has refused throughout — sweeping characterisation, and slurs aimed at unnamed office-holders that no evidence before us could stand over. This campaign takes his argument and leaves his invective, and it does so on principle rather than squeamishness: a case against surveillance is strongest when it indicts the architecture, and weakest the moment it name-calls, because the name-call is the one loose thread a hostile reader can pull to unravel everything true beside it. The standard is the standard whether the speaker is an adversary or an ally. What survives that filter, in Halpin’s case, is considerable — and it is the reason he earns a place in these pages at all. Palantir wants to be the operating system of the state. He is the standing proof that the people who build these systems are not one bloc marching in a single direction, and that a technologist’s public refusal is itself a form of stewardship — the counterpart, from inside the industry, to the government refusal documented next.

Sources: Palantir’s twenty-two-point manifesto, posted to X on 19 April 2026, condensing Alexander C. Karp and Nicholas W. Zamiska, The Technological Republic: Hard Power, Soft Belief, and the Future of the West (Crown Currency, 2025); its reach (~32 million views within two days) and full text as reported by Engadget (19 April 2026), TechPolicy.Press and others. “The AI manifesto inspiring Keir Starmer’s government” — The Times (London), as carried in the book’s own promotional matter. Palantir’s founding (2003, Thiel and Karp; In-Q-Tel/CIA early funding) and its reported deployments — Israeli military targeting during the Gaza conflict (contract renewed 2024), the US Army Maven Smart System, a reported ~$30 million US immigration-enforcement platform, and NYPD surveillance — as compiled from reporting by Wired, TechPolicy.Press and others (2025–2026); each is attributed as reported, and no claim is made here about any Irish deployment. Harry Halpin, “The Anti-Palantir Manifesto,” Nym blog (23 April 2026), published expressly as his stated personal beliefs; Halpin’s biography (co-founder and CEO of Nym Technologies; lead of the W3C Web Cryptography API under Tim Berners-Lee; PhD, University of Edinburgh; postdoctoral study under Bernard Stiegler; former research scientist at MIT and Inria de Paris) — Nym Technologies and Halpin’s own academic pages. The “code, not laws” thesis in scholarly form — Harry Halpin, “Immaterial Constitution: The Post-Snowden Maintenance of the Internet,” Techné: Research in Philosophy and Technology 29:1 (2025), doi:10.5840/techne20251113219. This section quotes each manifesto minimally, paraphrases their arguments, attributes contested characterisations to the person making them, and deliberately excludes the manifesto’s unverified and defamatory passages — including its slurs against unnamed office-holders — as unusable under this booklet’s clean-hands standard.

What stewardship actually looks like: the Swiss Army said no

Every other case study in this booklet documents a body that folded. This one is here because it didn't, and because the document proving it is public, dated, and survived a direct legal challenge from the company itself.

In December 2024, the planning staff of the Swiss Armed Forces (Armeestab) completed a 21-page internal evaluation of Palantir Technologies' "Asset Readiness" software, commissioned specifically to answer one question: should Switzerland's military use it. The report is genuinely a model of the kind of stewardship this booklet has spent hundreds of pages arguing for elsewhere — not reflexive hostility to a capable technology, but a clear-eyed weighing of a real capability against a real risk, with the risk allowed to win. The report itself calls Palantir's performance "beeindruckend" — impressive. It does not pretend otherwise. But its conclusion is unambiguous: "Die Schweizer Armee sollte Alternativen zu Palantir in Betracht ziehen" — the Swiss Army should consider alternatives to Palantir. The reasoning, in the report's own words, names exactly the dependency pattern this booklet keeps finding under different names in different sectors: increased reliance on a single US vendor, a threat to data sovereignty and national sovereignty, and — the sentence that matters most — uncertainty over who could ultimately access data shared with the company, given Palantir's base in the United States and its exposure to US intelligence and government access.

This was not a snap judgement, and it was not the first time Palantir was turned away. Journalists at Republik and the WAV research collective spent months compiling records showing Palantir had courted Swiss federal authorities persistently for seven years — at least nine separate approaches, from a 2018 product demonstration for then-finance minister Ueli Maurer, through a 2020 formal tender that Palantir failed on an unspecified "mandatory criterion," to a June 2024 pitch to the Swiss army chief himself at a security conference in Asia. Each time, the company was turned away. Switzerland became an important base for Palantir's operations anyway — a 60-person Zurich office the company itself describes as central to its European business — just never as a government customer.

Palantir disputed the reporting. A court examined the dispute and rejected it. The company sued Republik in the Zurich Commercial Court demanding a formal right of reply, objecting to being characterised as having run an "aggressive" campaign that repeatedly "blitzed" (failed). In June 2026 the court threw out all eleven of Palantir's specific complaints. Critically, the judgment records that Palantir did not dispute the underlying facts — the meetings happened, the army report exists, it reached the conclusion Republik said it reached. What Palantir objected to was the characterisation of those undisputed facts, and the court held that characterisation was a fair conclusion to draw, not a false statement of fact requiring correction. That is about as close to an independent judicial stress-test of a piece of investigative journalism as this booklet has been able to cite anywhere.

Switzerland was not alone in reaching this conclusion. Germany's own Bundeswehr cyber commander, Vice-Admiral Thomas Daum, separately and publicly ruled out Palantir for the German armed forces, telling Handelsblatt it was "unimaginable" to give the company's staff access to Germany's national data holdings — even while acknowledging NATO already uses a related Palantir product. Not every part of Germany reached the same view: Bavaria's state police adopted Palantir's "Vera" software regardless, and North Rhine-Westphalia's police force paid €22 million for a five-year licence plus €660,000 to €1.1 million annually in maintenance — the same sovereignty risk, accepted by one set of German public bodies and refused by another, inside the same country, in the same period.

The primary document — the Swiss Army's own 21-page evaluation, in the original German — is here: cdn.repub.ch/.../armeestaab-evaluation.pdf

Sources: Armeestab (Swiss Army planning staff), internal evaluation report, 4 December 2024 (primary document linked above). Republik, "Wie hartnäckig Palantir die Schweiz umwarb" (8 December 2025) and "Warum Palantir zum Risiko für die Schweiz wird" (9 December 2025), joint investigation with the WAV research collective. Zurich Commercial Court judgment on Palantir's Gegendarstellung claim, reported by Republik, "Palantir gegen die Republik: Das Urteil" (13 June 2026) — all eleven claims rejected. Vice-Admiral Thomas Daum's rejection of Palantir for the Bundeswehr — Handelsblatt, reported by augengeradeaus.net (April 2026). NRW licence costs and Bavaria's use of Palantir Vera — SRF, "Reporter: Palantir und die Schweiz" (2026).

Addendum: on stewardship, "love-bombing," and what a government is actually for

It is worth pausing on the Swiss Army document itself — not what it concluded, but that it exists at all. A government commissioned a careful, twenty-one-page, adversarial assessment of a powerful company that was actively courting it, allowed that assessment to reach an inconvenient conclusion, and let the conclusion stand even after the company sued. That is what stewardship looks like. It is not hostility to industry, and it is not reflexive caution. It is a government behaving as what it is supposed to be: a trustee, holding power on behalf of people who cannot each individually scrutinise a billion-euro procurement, and discharging that trust by actually doing the scrutiny — even, especially, when the company is charming, capable, and generous with its attention.

That last point matters, because the alternative to stewardship is rarely a bribe. It is a welcome. The pattern by which powerful companies secure favourable treatment is very often not corruption in the criminal sense but something softer and harder to prosecute: sustained attention, flattery, jobs announced at politically useful moments, a direct line to someone who "can solve any problem" — what campaigners have come to call "love-bombing." A minister who would refuse an envelope of cash will accept, without a second thought, the sense of importance that comes from being courted by a company the whole world is watching. The Swiss report is remarkable precisely because it is a government declining the wink and the nod — assessing the substance rather than basking in the courtship.

Ireland has a documented, if contested, example of the opposite posture, and honesty requires stating exactly what is and isn't established about it. Internal Facebook documents, surfaced in a California court case and reported by the Observer and Computer Weekly in 2019, claimed that then-Taoiseach Enda Kenny offered to use the "significant influence" of Ireland's 2013 EU Council presidency to lobby other member states on the EU data-protection regulation in Facebook's favour — the documents' own words — "even though technically Ireland is supposed to remain neutral in this role." Facebook's chief operating officer Sheryl Sandberg separately wrote to Kenny praising his "enormous progress" on data-protection regulation during the presidency and thanking him that his staff had "really internalised our concerns." A former Facebook executive's later memoir added that the company had been given, in effect, a direct government contact who "could solve any problem." What must be stated with equal clarity: this account is one-sided — it is Facebook's own internal record of events — and it was firmly disputed at the time. Both the sitting and former Data Protection Commissioners stated categorically that neither Kenny nor his officials had ever lobbied them on Facebook's behalf, and Kenny himself did not confirm the documents' characterisation. This booklet therefore does not assert that the lobbying occurred as described. It records that a powerful company believed, and documented internally, that it had a friend at the highest level of an Irish government during a period when that government held the EU presidency — and that the relationship was close enough for such a belief to be plausible. Whether or not the specific offer was made, the shape of the concern is the point: a government's presidency of the Council is meant to be held in trust for all member states, not deployed as leverage for a company headquartered on its own soil.

This is the heart of what stewardship means, and why its absence is so corrosive. A politician, a regulator, a planning official, a council chief executive — each holds power they did not generate and do not own. It belongs to the public, lent to them for a fixed term and a specific purpose. Water, grid capacity, planning permission, a citizen's personal data, a nation's negotiating position in Europe — these are not the office-holder's assets to trade for investment, jobs announcements, or the pleasant feeling of being courted by the powerful. The Environmental Crime Directive's "manifest breach" provision, the Protected Disclosures Act, the whole architecture of accountability set out earlier in this booklet, exists precisely because the temptation to treat borrowed power as owned power is constant, and because the softest forms of that temptation — flattery, access, the wink and the nod — leave no fingerprints.

And here is the conclusion the whole booklet has been building toward. Where stewardship holds — where a government commissions the honest report and lets it stand — citizens can, within reason, trust the system to check itself. But that trust is conditional, not automatic, and it is not owed. Where stewardship fails — where the courtship works, where the report is commissioned from the firm that profits, where the presidency is quietly lent to a donor's cause — the duty does not disappear. It transfers. It falls to the people themselves: to advocate, to demand transparency, to use every lawful instrument documented in this booklet, and to refuse the comfortable fiction that someone in authority is surely handling it. A trustee who cannot be trusted does not dissolve the trust. He obliges the beneficiaries to enforce it themselves. That is not cynicism. It is citizenship — and on the evidence gathered across these pages, it is no longer optional.

Sources: the Swiss Army evaluation and its litigation, as documented in the section above. The Enda Kenny/Facebook claims — internal Facebook documents reported by the Observer and Computer Weekly (2019), as covered by The Irish Times and Irish Examiner; Sheryl Sandberg's correspondence as reported; Sarah Wynn-Williams, Careless People (2025). The categorical denials — Data Protection Commissioners Billy Hawkes and Helen Dixon, as reported by The Irish Times (2019). The claims are presented strictly as Facebook's own contemporaneous internal account, disputed by the Irish regulators and never confirmed by Mr Kenny; no finding of wrongdoing is asserted or implied.

The contrast: what happens without that vigilance — and why Ireland should be watching the NHS closely

The Swiss Army evaluation is what stewardship looks like. The UK's National Health Service, over the same five years, is what its absence looks like — and it is close enough to home, and moving fast enough right now, that this booklet treats it as a live warning for Ireland's own health data, not a foreign case study.

The pattern is documented and has a name campaigners use for it: "land and expand." Palantir's relationship with the NHS began in March 2020 with a COVID data-store contract worth precisely £1 — extended repeatedly over three years while the real prize, a permanent national data platform, was being procured. In November 2023, a Palantir-led consortium won that prize: the Federated Data Platform, worth up to £330 million over seven years. From there the same company expanded into UK policing (2024), the Ministry of Defence — a £240 million contract awarded without competitive tender in December 2025, immediately following a Trump state visit — and, by March 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority's own internal intelligence data. An investigation by The Nerve, published January 2026, found at least 34 current and past UK state contracts across ten government departments, worth at least £670 million in total. The same shape, every time: a small, low-cost, seemingly reasonable entry point, expanding steadily into the most sensitive data the state holds.

The specific NHS concerns are exactly the ones this booklet has spent hundreds of pages naming in other contexts. Patient opt-outs — the standard mechanism letting people withhold their data from secondary use — do not apply to the Federated Data Platform, because of a legal direction issued under section 254 of the Health and Social Care Act 2012 that has never been published. NHS staff have reportedly been warned they could face disciplinary action for criticising the contract publicly. The British Medical Association, Amnesty International, the Good Law Project, Privacy International and Corporate Watch have all called for the contract to be terminated, backed by a formal Medact briefing sent to every NHS trust and Integrated Care Board in the country. Health workers physically blockaded NHS England's headquarters in protest in April 2024. A parliamentary committee has, within the past week of this booklet being written, formally called on the government to scrap the platform at its earliest legal opportunity — the contract's break clause falls in February 2027, and the decision is live now.

And the revolving door this booklet has documented in Ireland has an exact UK-NHS equivalent, with a name. Matthew Swindells left his post as NHS England's Deputy Chief Executive in 2019. Two months later he was working with Palantir, via the lobbying firm Global Counsel. He simultaneously held the position of joint chair across four NHS Trusts in North West London — the same trusts making decisions about Palantir's platform — until March 2026, and is reported to have privately urged colleagues to feed more patient data into the Palantir system while being paid by the company at the same time. The Trust board formally excluded him from Palantir-related decisions. That exclusion is itself the tell: an institution that has to build a formal wall around one person's conflict of interest has already told you the conflict was real.

The honest complication, stated fairly: NHS England disputes the bleaker framing, states no company can access patient data without explicit permission, and Palantir points to 110,078 additional patient procedures completed since the platform's rollout as evidence of real value delivered. Set against that: campaign group Foxglove's own data finds roughly 30% of English hospitals using Palantir's scheduling tools are completing fewer procedures than before adoption. Both figures are real. They do not describe the same hospitals, and neither cancels the other out — which is itself the most honest thing that can be said about a five-year-old contract this contested.

Why this belongs in an Irish booklet, plainly. Ireland has no equivalent Palantir contract with the HSE at the time of writing, and this booklet makes no claim that one exists. What it does claim is that the "land and expand" pattern is now documented, repeatedly, across multiple countries — a small pilot contract, a COVID-era foothold, a low headline price, followed by systematic expansion into the most sensitive data a state holds, defended each time as efficiency and modernisation. Switzerland's Army evaluated that exact pattern in advance and said no. The UK did not, and is now attempting an exit five years and hundreds of millions of pounds into a contract its own MPs, doctors and patients no longer trust. Ireland's health data — and its future AI Office, planning system, and public-sector procurement generally — should be evaluated against the Swiss model, in advance, not the NHS model, after the fact.

Sources: NHS Federated Data Platform origins, £1 initial contract, and November 2023 award — pharmaphorum (November 2023) and Medact, "Briefing: Concerns Regarding Palantir Technologies and NHS Data Systems" (2026). Section 254 legal direction and unpublished status; NHS staff warned over criticism — reports from Novara Media and the Financial Times, cited in UK Parliament Hansard debate, "NHS Federated Data Platform" (16 April 2026). MoD £240m no-tender contract and FCA contract — Medact briefing (2026), citing The Nerve investigation (January 2026). April 2024 NHS England headquarters blockade and campaign coalition — The Lowdown, "Palantir, the controversy, the contracts and the campaign against the FDP" (2026). Matthew Swindells' Global Counsel role and NW London Trust chairmanship — The Lowdown (2026). MPs' committee call to scrap the contract and Foxglove's 30%-fewer-procedures data — Computer Weekly, "MPs call on NHS to scrap Palantir and its Federated Data Platform" (2026). Break clause timing (February 2027) and government review — Stotles, "Palantir NHS contract: what the FDP break clause means for you" (June 2026).

The economics that don’t add up — and who underwrites the risk

There is a question worth asking plainly: if this industry is the future, why is so much of it losing so much money? The answer needs a distinction the headlines blur. The infrastructure giants — Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta and the chip-maker Nvidia — are enormously profitable, and fund their AI build-out from existing earnings; Nvidia in particular is minting money selling the shovels. It is the pure-play AI developers that burn cash, and even they differ. OpenAI is the deep loss-maker: on roughly $20 billion of annualised revenue in 2025 it reportedly lost in the order of $9 billion, with only about one in twenty of its users paying anything, and leaked internal projections — which keep shifting — putting losses near $14 billion in 2026 and profitability no earlier than the end of the decade. Anthropic, by contrast, projects break-even within a few years. “They are all losing money” is not true. “The labs burn while the giants sell the shovels” is.

Where does the money come from? Record private fundraising (a $40 billion round at a $300 billion valuation in 2025, a later mark of $500 billion, a rival’s $30 billion round at $380 billion in 2026), bank and private-equity debt, the giants’ balance sheets — and, increasingly, money moving in a circle. Nvidia committed up to $100 billion to OpenAI; OpenAI’s own chief financial officer acknowledged that money “will go back to Nvidia” in chip purchases. Nvidia also funds the cloud provider that buys its chips and rents the resulting compute back to OpenAI. Capital travelling in a loop, booked as growth at every turn. That circularity is the heart of the bubble question — and it matters here because Ireland’s corporate tax base, as this Part has shown, leans heavily on exactly these companies.

Then the part that should concern any country being made dependent. In November 2025, asked at a Wall Street Journal event how a trillion-dollar build-out would be financed, OpenAI’s chief financial officer suggested the US government might “backstop the guarantee that allows the financing to happen” — and, asked directly whether she meant a federal backstop, answered “Exactly.” The backlash was immediate. She walked it back within hours; the chief executive insisted the company neither has nor wants government guarantees; the White House’s own AI adviser said flatly there would be “no federal bailout for AI.” The denials are on the record. So is the original suggestion. And the underlying question — has this industry already become too big to fail? — is now openly debated, while governments edge in sideways: a state equity stake in a chip-maker, public land fast-tracked for data centres, tax credits for fabrication. The 2008 pattern set out elsewhere in this booklet is the warning: private profit in the good years, public risk when the bill arrives.

And it is no longer one bloc building. Alongside the US hyperscalers, the BRICS group is constructing a parallel, sovereign AI infrastructure — a $5 billion “Digital Sovereignty Fund” through its New Development Bank for data centres, research parks and chip-making; a shared “Digital Cloud Corridor” to run models independently of US and EU servers; and a BRICS+ AI alliance coordinated from Russia. This is not, as is sometimes claimed, those states adopting the viral consumer “agents” described later in this booklet — it is something with larger physical consequences: a second compute-and-data-centre build-out, racing the first, each bloc duplicating infrastructure rather than restraining it. Two arms races, both pouring concrete, both drawing power and water. The honest framing is this: the global footprint is not growing along one curve but several at once, and where they compound, the strain on energy and water rises faster than any single national plan accounts for. Whether that becomes, in the end, “exponential” is interpretation — but the direction is not in doubt. Even the 2026 AI Impact Summit in New Delhi, voicing the Global South’s concern, warned that a machine given a single objective without ethical guidance “may exhaust all global resources in pursuit of that objective.”

Sources: OpenAI revenue/loss projections — The Information and Wall Street Journal reporting, 2025–26 (figures are leaked internal projections and change between reports). Anthropic revenue and break-even projections — WSJ, 2025–26. Nvidia–OpenAI–CoreWeave financing circularity — OpenAI CFO remarks, 2025. “Backstop” episode — Wall Street Journal Tech Live, 5 Nov 2025; CNBC, CNN and OpenAI/White House statements, 6 Nov 2025. BRICS New Development Bank $5bn Digital Sovereignty Fund, Digital Cloud Corridor and BRICS+ AI alliance — BRICS NDB and summit communiqués, 2024–26. “Exhaust all global resources” — reporting on the 2026 AI Impact Summit, New Delhi. All financial figures presented as reported and as ranges, not as audited accounts.

Part 4F — The Camouflage: How a Data Centre Hides in the Planning System

As the words “data centre” became a flashpoint, the build-out did not stop. It changed costume. This is how to read the new disguise — and the law that made it easier.

What this chapter is about. This chapter is a practical field guide: how a data centre can enter the planning system under other names and categories, and how an ordinary person can learn to recognise one before the concrete is poured. Knowledge here is literally the power to object in time.

“The first step is to make the invisible visible.” — (this book)

The reclassification problem. A community that has learned to watch for the words “data centre” is watching for the wrong words. The applications that matter most now rarely lead with them. They lead with energy: a battery yard, a solar farm, a “decentralised energy resource,” a gas connection, an underground store. The server hall, where it appears at all, sits deep in the technical drawings as the thing the energy infrastructure exists to feed. Two real, converging pressures explain the shift, and neither is a rumour.

Pressure one: bring your own power. Since 2021 the Commission for Regulation of Utilities has required that a data centre seeking a new grid connection in the constrained regions provide its own on-site generation or storage to match its load — a policy hardened by the December 2025 connection decision (CRU/2025/236) covered in Part 1. The intended effect was grid protection. The actual effect was to move power and storage to the front of every application. If you must bring your own power, the power plant becomes the headline and the computing becomes the footnote. That is not a loophole anyone hid; it is the visible, logical consequence of a published rule. But it changes what an application looks like on a council’s website — and what an objector needs to recognise.

Pressure two: the Environment (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2025. This is the legislation referred to locally as “the miscellaneous provisions bill” (Bill No. 53 of 2025, sponsored by the Minister for Climate, Energy and the Environment). Its stated purpose is to streamline the EPA’s Industrial Emissions, Integrated Pollution Control and Waste licensing — and, in fairness to the text, it also states an aim of keeping “appropriate and proportionate environmental protections” in place. But three of its features matter for anyone tracking cumulative footprint, and they are worth stating precisely, because precision is what makes the point survive a lawyer:

  • It lets the EPA review only the part of a licence that is changing. Where criteria are met, the Agency can limit its assessment to the proposed change rather than reopening the whole installation. Read benignly, this spares operators from re-litigating an entire site over a minor amendment. Read warily, it is the mechanism by which a facility permitted as something modest can be expanded in stages — each stage assessed on its own narrow terms — without the cumulative environmental impact ever being weighed in one piece.

  • It creates EIA exemptions in “exceptional circumstances.” New sections (88C and 88D of the EPA Act 1992) allow an applicant, in civil-emergency, defence or “exceptional” cases, to seek exemption from the Environmental Impact Assessment requirement before applying for a licence, with the EPA obliged to secure the Directive’s objectives “by other means.” This transposes a real provision of the EU EIA Directive — but how widely “exceptional” is read is the whole question.

  • It puts decision timelines in the Minister’s hands. Fixed time limits for EPA decisions are to be set by ministerial regulation rather than on the face of the Act, “to make it easier to amend them.” Faster certainty for industry; less room, potentially, for scrutiny.

A note on honesty, in keeping with this booklet’s standard: the specific section numbers and mechanisms that circulate in informal and AI-generated summaries of this Bill are frequently wrong — invented “Section 16 partial reviews,” a misremembered “Section 146B,” claims that the Act instructs the State to refuse Freedom of Information requests. None of that is in the legislation, and repeating it is the fastest way to get a real concern dismissed. The verified concern is narrower and stronger: a licensing regime built for speed and partial review, sitting on top of a “bring your own power” rule, changes how a large project can be assembled — in pieces, led by energy infrastructure, on accelerated timelines. Check the Bill’s current status on the Oireachtas register before quoting it as enacted.

The model in the open: Red Admiral, Rochfortbridge. You do not need a hidden example to see the pattern, because the clearest one is entirely public. In June 2026 Westmeath County Council approved a €1 billion campus at Rochfortbridge from Red Admiral DC Ltd, a subsidiary of Lumcloon Energy: six data-centre buildings on a 600-acre site, co-located with its own “clean energy centre” — a 415-acre solar farm, fuel cells (supplied by SK ecoplant), and a 250MW two-hour battery energy storage system. The application went to the council directly only after An Coimisiún Pleanála ruled it was not Strategic Infrastructure Development. It drew more than sixty objections and was granted subject to 32 conditions. The point is not that anything was concealed — it was not — but that the shape is the new normal: the energy plant is the project, and the computing rides on it. And then the lesson every campaigner needs: the appeal against the approval was ruled invalid by An Coimisiún Pleanála, on a technicality — a missing acknowledgement of the local submission. Months of opposition can be undone by one missing receipt. Procedural precision is not pedantry; it is the whole game.

And they came back anyway. Forty residents of the surrounding townlands — Rochfortbridge, Collinstown, Farthingstown, Kiltotan, Oldtown — formally joined a fresh submission to An Coimisiún Pleanála rather than accept the technicality as the end of it. Willie Carey, a suckler and dry-stock farmer among them, named the cost the campaign keeps documenting in place after place: “It's been a divide-and-conquer approach — people who have grown up together are no longer speaking.” Their grounds were the ordinary, unglamorous ones that decide whether rural life keeps functioning: lack of consultation, the impact on the wells that supply their homes and farms, and flooding risk. The council itself will receive €8.24 million in planning contributions and a levy of almost €290,000 from the project — a reminder of exactly whose incentive lines up with the developer's, and whose does not. A spokesperson for Lumcloon Energy said the company had engaged in “an extensive consultation process” that led to design changes. Both things are true at once: a company can hold a consultation, and a community can still be left fighting to be heard. That is not a contradiction this booklet needs to resolve. It is the story.

The anchor-infrastructure model: Rathrush, Carlow. In June 2026 the Irish firm Net Zero Energy unveiled the €2 billion Rathrush Green Energy Park near Rathoe, Co. Carlow: surplus wind and solar power converted to green hydrogen, stored in lined rock caverns hewn into granite, and burned in turbines to return up to 600MW — about a tenth of Ireland’s peak demand — when the wind drops. As a long-duration storage project it is, on its own terms, a serious answer to renewable intermittency, and it should be described as one. But note what its own chief executive said when launching it: the scheme “provides reassurance and confidence for high-end electricity users seeking to locate here in Ireland.” That is the developer, on the record, naming large energy users as a reason the project matters. We do not assert that Rathrush is “secretly a data centre” — it is not, and the claim would be unverified. We observe the documented pattern it fits: build the dispatchable, behind-grid power first; the high-density consumers that power is built to attract follow afterwards, on separate applications. Anchor infrastructure clears the grid-capacity excuse. Watch what lands beside it next.

The vocabulary to watch. None of the terms below is proof of anything on its own — a battery yard is often just a battery yard, and the country genuinely needs storage and renewables. The watch-list is not “these words mean a hidden server farm.” It is: when these appear, ask who the power is ultimately for, and whether the cumulative footprint is being assessed as one thing or sliced into many. Set a keyword alert on your council’s planning portal for: Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) and “co-located” or “decentralised energy resource”; Above-Ground Installation (AGI) or gas “kiosks” (a high-pressure gas connection for on-site turbines); “Digital Hub,” “Enterprise Computing Centre,” “Advanced Technology Building (ATB)” or “Logistics and Data Management”; and “private wires” or “behind-the-meter” supply (power that bypasses the public grid, and so vanishes from EirGrid’s public connection queue). Learn one piece of arithmetic, too: capacity in planning files is often given in MVA (megavolt-amperes), not MW (megawatts). They are not the same — roughly, MW is MVA multiplied by the power factor, so a figure in MVA always describes a slightly smaller real-power draw, and the unfamiliar unit can make a very large load read as modest. If a “logistics” or “enterprise” application lists a Maximum Import Capacity in the tens of MVA, that is an industrial power profile, whatever the building is called.

Preparing the ground. The most under-watched moment of all comes before the planning decision is even made — the enabling and preparatory works. Two deserve a citizen’s eye, because both can put diggers on a green field long before anyone has agreed a data centre belongs there.

Archaeology as the first machinery on site. Irish law requires an archaeological assessment before large groundworks, which means the first heavy plant to arrive on a rural site often flies the flag of heritage protection rather than construction. Usually that is exactly what it is. But the line can be crossed, and the clearest documented instance is worth knowing. Neighbours of a €500 million EngineNode data centre at Bracetown and Gunnocks, north of Clonee in Co. Meath, took the developer to the High Court — and in 2022 Mr Justice Anthony Barr found that a second phase of excavation works, carried out between August and October 2020 under a second National Monuments Service licence while the planning decision was still under appeal, amounted to unauthorised development: in substance these were the archaeological-mitigation works that a condition of the permission required, begun before the permission was secure. The honest detail matters here, because it is what makes the point stick: the court held the boreholes on the site were exempt development, the developer denied any wrongdoing, and the neighbours’ wider challenge to the permission itself was later dismissed in 2023, so the project kept its consent. What endures is the principle — and one line. Of a defence that 8km of trenching amounted to merely “digging and refilling a number of holes,” opposing counsel told the court: “If 8km of trenches are just a number of holes, then the Atlantic Ocean is just water.” The lesson is not that every dig is a data centre. It is that the heritage phase is a genuine point of vigilance — that the conditions of a permission must be met before works begin, and that a watchful neighbour, using section 160 of the Planning and Development Act, can hold a developer to exactly that.

Water under the radar, and the substation that gives the game away. Two further enabling works repay attention. First, water: high-density cooling needs it, and the disclosure regime is weak — abstraction-licence applications have only had to be public since March 2025 (Part 3) — so a borehole or abstraction application on farmland near a suspected site is worth an AIE request before the concrete, not after. Second, the substation and grid connection: these are frequently consented as a separate application from the data centre itself — in the same Meath case, the grid-connection and substation works were the subject of their own challenge. A large new substation, or a “grid reinforcement” notice, appearing on or beside ordinary agricultural land ahead of any building, is one of the surest tells that something power-hungry is being readied.

The wider watch-list — question, don’t assume. Beyond the planning lexicon already in this Part, campaigners elsewhere report a fuller set of enabling-and-cover tactics worth putting to any suspect file: green-gas obligations satisfied on paper through purchased biomethane certificates while conventional gas is burned on the ground; “district heating,” “community greenhouse” or “vertical farming” elements that furnish the green justification for a server hall quietly designed as their heat source; and land-banking, where far more land and grid capacity is optioned than a stated project needs and then held for years. Treat these as questions to ask, not verdicts to deliver. Each is a legitimate activity in its own right; the test in every case is the same one this Part keeps returning to — who is the power, the water and the heat ultimately for, and is the whole footprint being assessed as a single thing? Verify locally before you assert.

The clean ask. This is the demand that follows, and it is modest enough to be unanswerable: that any application led by co-located generation or storage be assessed for its true intended end-use and its cumulative footprint, not just the component in front of the planner; that the new power to review only part of a licence, and to grant EIA exemptions in “exceptional” cases, never be used to let a facility reach its full scale in stages without a single, whole-site environmental assessment; and that Maximum Import Capacity and intended end-user be stated plainly, in MW as well as MVA, on the public file. Ask the question on the record. As ever, the refusal is as revealing as the answer.

*Sources: CRU on-site generation/storage requirement for data-centre grid connections (in place since 2021) and Direction CRU/2025/236 — Oireachtas Library & Research Service, “The future of data centres in Ireland” (20 March 2026); CRU. Environment (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2025 (No. 53 of 2025) — Oireachtas Bill page; Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment press release (2025); legal analyses by A&L Goodbody, Mason Hayes & Curran and Lexology (Oct–Dec 2025), confirming the EPA partial-licence-review power, the new sections 88C/88D EIA exemptions for civil-emergency/defence and exceptional circumstances, and ministerial power to set decision timelines by regulation; status should be re-checked on the Oireachtas register before being quoted as enacted. Red Admiral / Rochfortbridge — Westmeath County Council decision (2 June 2026); Datacenter Dynamics, Westmeath Independent, Offaly Independent and Midlands 103 (June 2026); appeal ruled invalid — Westmeath Examiner and Midlands 103 (19 June 2026); fresh forty-resident joint submission, Willie Carey, Frank Kelly and Willie Clancy quoted, €8.24m planning contributions and €290,000 levy — Irish Farmers Journal, Jacqueline Hogge (1 July 2026); developer: Red Admiral DC Ltd / Lumcloon Energy. Rathrush Green Energy Park — RTÉ News, Argus Media, Carlow Nationalist and Renewable Energy Magazine (8–17 June 2026); developer: Net Zero Energy (NZE); CEO Peter Harte quoted on “high-end electricity users.” MVA/MW distinction — standard electrical engineering (real power = apparent power × power factor). This section deliberately excludes claims that could not be verified to a primary source: invented Bill section numbers and mechanisms from AI-generated summaries; assertions that specifically named energy or storage projects are “secretly” data centres; specific figures for a “hidden” national pipeline; and the naming of individual holding companies, legal firms or directors as covert fronts. The rebranding lexicon is offered as a citizen monitoring tool — a list of classifications to question — not as a claim that any given application is a disguised data centre. The pattern is real and documented; each individual project must be judged on its own file. EngineNode / Bracetown & Gunnocks, Co. Meath — Coyne v EngineNode Ltd, High Court: Barr J (2022) on the section 160 application finding the second phase of excavation works unauthorised, with boreholes held exempt and the developer denying wrongdoing; Holland J (2023) dismissing the substantive challenge to the permission; reported in The Irish Times, Datacenter Dynamics and the Meath Chronicle (2021–2023). Water-abstraction publication requirement since March 2025 — as sourced in Part 3. The biomethane-certificate, district-heating/greenhouse/vertical-farming and land-banking items are flagged as patterns reported by campaigners, to be verified against each local file — not as established findings about any named Irish project.

Camouflage quick-check — what a disguised application can look like. None of these proves a hidden data centre; each is a legitimate activity in its own right. They are prompts to ask one question — who is the power, water and heat ultimately for, and is the whole footprint being assessed as one thing?

  • Leads with energy, not computing: a battery yard (BESS), solar farm, gas connection (AGI/“kiosks”), “decentralised energy resource,” or “clean energy centre” — with any server hall buried in the technical drawings.

  • Uses a softer label: “Digital Hub,” “Enterprise Computing Centre,” “Advanced Technology Building,” “Logistics and Data Management.”

  • States capacity in MVA rather than MW, making a large load read as modest (convert: real MW is the smaller number).

  • Mentions “private wires” or “behind-the-meter” supply — power that bypasses the public grid and vanishes from EirGrid’s connection queue.

  • A new substation or “grid reinforcement” appears on or beside agricultural land before any building.

  • Enabling works arrive first: archaeology, boreholes, or a water-abstraction application on nearby farmland — worth an AIE request before the concrete.

  • The project is split into separate applications (energy, substation, building, phases) so no single assessment weighs the cumulative whole.

  • Green justification via purchased biomethane certificates, “district heating,” “community greenhouse” or “vertical farming” elements — or far more land and grid capacity optioned than the stated project needs (land-banking).

Question, don’t assume. The pattern is real and documented; each application must be judged on its own file — verify locally before you assert.

Part 4G — The Global Playbook: The Same Architecture, Country by Country

The Irish pattern is not Irish. It is a template, and it travels. Swap in the local planning authority, the local water regulator, the local grid operator — the five-act sequence runs the same everywhere. Which means the counter-play travels too.

What this chapter is about. Where the map showed the pattern, this chapter shows the playbook being written into law abroad — and, crucially, where it is being stopped. It is a guide to reading legislation as it happens, in Midleton or Mumbai, using the same method this campaign uses county by county.

“Study the past if you would define the future.” — Confucius

In one breath. The Irish playbook is not Irish — it is a template that travels: the same five-act capture, the same camouflage, run hardest where people can least refuse. This Part shows how it is being written into law abroad (Italy, the UK, the EU), where it is already being stopped, and — step by step — how any citizen can read a suspect site before the concrete is poured. If you only want the actions, skip to the numbered toolkit; the print-and-keep Calls to Action pages at the back collect every one of them.

Part 4C mapped sixteen countries and one pattern. This Part does the next thing: it shows how that pattern is being built into law abroad, where it is already being stopped, and exactly how an ordinary person — in Midleton or Mumbai — can read a suspect site before the concrete is poured. The method this campaign uses county by county in Ireland works country by country everywhere else. Nothing below asks you to take anyone’s word for anything: each load-bearing claim is sourced, estimates are given as ranges, and where the evidence stops, the claim stops with it.

We have been here before — and we know exactly where it leads

This booklet has a section called “We Have Been Here Before,” about the bata scóir and the hedge schools. Here is the international version of the same warning, and it is not a metaphor.

Read the colonial-legacy column of the Part 4C map again and the targeting is hard to miss: the heaviest, least-regulated, least-consulted expansion lands again and again on the places that already carried the worst of an earlier extraction — India under the East India Company and the Crown; Indonesia under three and a half centuries of Dutch rule; Kenya and Nigeria under British administration; South Africa under colonialism and apartheid; Mexico, where local critics call the build-out a repeat of a “colonial legacy of resource extractivism,” and note that the undersea cables often follow the very routes once plied by slave ships. These are not the countries with the most spare water, power and democratic slack. They are frequently the ones with the least. That is not a coincidence to be explained away; it is the logic of the model, which goes where refusal is hardest.

There is a particular immorality in running this play through nations still healing from being plundered once already — and a sharper one still where the resource being taken is the one people cannot live without. To site water-hungry compute in regions already at or past Day Zero — Chennai in 2019, Cape Town in 2018, Querétaro in the worst drought in a century — is not an unfortunate side effect of progress. It is a choice to convert other people’s drinking water and grid stability into someone else’s quarterly earnings, in places chosen partly because the people there can be overridden. A country does not have to accept that the only way to be modern is to be mined.

The India double bind — and why it deserves its own paragraph. India is where the trap closes from two sides at once. On one side, the resource squeeze: installed data-centre capacity has gone from roughly 520 MW in 2020 to about 1.5 GW in 2025, and is projected to reach 4.5–6.5 GW by 2030, concentrated in Mumbai and Chennai — a city that has already hit complete municipal water failure once (CEEW, How Is Data Centre Infrastructure in India Shaping Power and Water Use?, 2026; figures elsewhere in Part 4C). India holds about 18% of the world’s people and roughly 4% of its freshwater. On the other side, the employment squeeze: India’s economy leans heavily on its IT and IT-enabled services sector — the export engine and the aspirational job ladder for millions of families — and that very sector is the one most exposed to the wave of automation these data centres exist to serve. The same build-out that drains a stressed aquifer is wiring the system that can hollow out the white-collar and call-centre work the region depends on. (The scale and direction of automation’s effect on Indian tech employment is contested and projected, not settled — it is named here as a serious, widely-flagged risk, not a foregone conclusion.) Set those two pressures side by side and the moral arithmetic is stark: a model that can take the water and the wages from the same communities, and call it development. Whole regional economies could be reshaped before the consent of the people living in them is ever sought. We should be able to say plainly that this is not acceptable — and that saying so is not anti-technology, it is pro-people.

That is the argument this Part rests on. Not “stop the machines,” but: the consent must come first, the costs must be counted, and the pattern of choosing the powerless must end.

How governments are clearing the path — the architecture, written into law

The clearest proof that this is a template, not a coincidence, is that several governments are now writing the same accelerant into statute — usually under the banners of “national interest,” “security,” or “energy.” The point of naming these is not despair; it is that each one is a documented, citable instrument an opponent can point to, and several create new openings as well as new walls.

Italy — the single door. In February 2026 Italy enacted Law Decree No. 21/2026 (the “Decreto Bollette,” or Energy Bills Decree), which created a single national authorisation procedure (procedimento unico) for data-centre construction, expansion and grid connection. It collapses environmental, landscape, cultural-heritage, water-use and emissions approvals into one decision, caps the whole process at ten months (extendable to thirteen), and halves the timeline for environmental impact assessment. Where a project is declared of “preeminent national strategic interest” — broadly, foreign investment above €1 billion under Article 13 of Decree-Law 104/2023 — authorisation passes to a Special Commissioner appointed by the Prime Minister, in a single decision that can amend local planning instruments and replace regional and municipal clearances. In plain terms: the bigger the money, the further the decision moves from the people who live there. (Sources: A&O Shearman, Greenberg Traurig, Latham & Watkins and Bird & Bird client alerts, Feb–Mar 2026; the decree was to be converted into law by the Italian Parliament, with possible amendment, by 21 April 2026 — re-check status before quoting as settled.)

United Kingdom — the security label. On 12 September 2024 the UK designated data centres as Critical National Infrastructure, putting them on the same legal tier as water, energy and the emergency services — the first such designation since 2015 (DSIT statement; Computer Weekly; DCD, Sept 2024). On its own, CNI status is mostly about cyber-resilience and investor confidence. But it arrived alongside a parallel push to fast-track data-centre planning by treating projects as nationally significant, and the combination signals the same direction of travel: lift the decision above the local council, and frame any local “no” as a risk to the national interest.

The European Union — the lock and, this time, the key. Two EU instruments matter here and pull in opposite directions. The lock is the confidentiality carve-out inside the Energy Efficiency Directive’s data-centre reporting scheme — the same secrecy mechanism this booklet flags throughout, which has left a large share of eligible European facilities effectively unreported. The key is newer and sharper: Directive (EU) 2024/1203, the recast Environmental Crime Directive, in force since May 2024, which for the first time creates offences “comparable to ecocide.” It explicitly lists the unlawful depletion of water resources among its offences; for the gravest “qualified” offences — conduct causing widespread, substantial and either irreversible or long-lasting damage to an ecosystem — member states must provide custodial sentences of at least eight years for individuals (rising to at least ten where an offence causes a person’s death), and corporate fines of up to 5% of worldwide turnover or €40 million. The transposition deadline for all member states, Ireland included, was 21 May 2026. (Sources: Directive (EU) 2024/1203, Articles 3, 5, 7; European Parliament press release, Feb 2024; eucrim; UCLA Promise Institute Europe, 2026; Stop Ecocide International. Note the correction to widely-circulated summaries: the ecocide-comparable threshold carries a minimum-maximum of eight years, not ten — ten attaches to offences causing death.) The significance is plain: a citizen no longer has to wait for an international tribunal to invent a crime. The instrument that criminalises drying out an aquifer or destroying a protected habitat already exists in European law — and it points at decision-makers personally.

Ireland — for completeness. Ireland’s own version sits in Parts 1 and 4C: the CRU’s December 2025 decision (CRU/2025/236) ending the de-facto 2021 connection moratorium, with the “bring your own power,” 80%-Irish-renewables and locational conditions, and the grandfather clause that exempts the existing pipeline. Comparatively, Ireland’s regime is more demanding than Italy’s open door and less restrictive than the Netherlands’ designated-sites cap or Singapore’s efficiency-only “call for applications.” The Irish move is not the worst on this list. It is on the list.

The new frontiers — where it goes when local oversight finally bites

As traditional hubs tighten, the industry migrates — and some of the migration is literally off the map. These are flagged here as reported and emerging, not as settled fact, because they are new and fast-moving:

  • Underwater. Sealed, seabed and floating data centres — including a commercial subsea facility reported off Shanghai — use raw seawater for cooling and sit in the grey zone between domestic environmental law and maritime registries, beyond the reach of any planning board.

  • Orbital. Both a SpaceX/xAI plan and Google’s “Project Suncatcher” propose putting compute into orbit. Under the 1967 Outer Space Treaty an object in space is governed only by the nation that launched it — a governance gap that, if realised, would place computation physically beyond any local environmental agency, tax authority or community.

  • In plain sight. Closer to home, “adaptive reuse” turns disused retail sheds, factories and warehouses into modular, pre-assembled server halls within weeks — a high-density compute load behind the face of an ordinary, abandoned-looking industrial unit.

The lesson is not to chase every frontier; it is that secrecy is the constant. Wherever the building is hardest to see, the same investigative instincts apply: follow the power, the water, the cables and the paperwork.

The escalation ladder: from a county objection to an international complaint

When local channels are captured or overridden, the law connects upward. These rungs already exist; the campaign’s job is to make ordinary people aware they can climb them. (Detailed Irish routes are in Parts 6 and 6B; this is the international frame.)

  • Rung 1 — Environmental information (Aarhus / AIE). Under the Aarhus Convention, public bodies cannot hide emissions, water or environmental-impact data behind “commercial confidentiality” or an NDA. In Europe, use AIE rather than ordinary FOI. Action: file an AIE for any registered or licensed abstraction at a named site, and treat a missing entry for a major water user as itself a finding worth a follow-up request.

  • Rung 2 — Environmental assessment and access to justice (EU EIA law). Challenge failures of environmental impact assessment, Appropriate Assessment, or public-participation rights. In Ireland this is the Habitats Directive route that sits above the planning system (Part 6B).

  • Rung 3 — The human right to a healthy environment (UN HRC Resolution 48/13, 2021). When a facility depletes a regional aquifer or runs heavy diesel through a residential air corridor, it stops being a zoning matter and becomes a rights matter. Action: a formal submission to the UN Special Rapporteur on human rights and the environment puts the camouflage on the world stage even where it cannot, by itself, jail anyone.

  • Rung 4 — Transboundary water. Where extraction affects a shared basin or river crossing a border, transboundary-water principles can elevate a local dispute into an international one.

  • Rung 5 — The money (Equator Principles / UN PRI). Large projects rely on financiers bound by responsible-investment and due-diligence commitments. Action: a compliance complaint to a lender’s auditing board, evidencing that consent was bypassed via NDA or shell, can put financing at risk.

  • Rung 6 — Criminal liability (EU Environmental Crime Directive 2024/1203). As above — national prosecutors across the EU can now, in principle, be asked to consider ecocide-comparable conduct, bypassing a captured local board entirely.

  • Rung 7 — The international campaign (Stop Ecocide International). The live effort to make ecocide the fifth crime at the International Criminal Court. Not yet law; a place to add weight.

No single rung is a magic key, and the international ones are slow. The value is cumulative: a dossier built once, locally, can be aimed at several of these at once.

The citizen investigator’s toolkit — what to look for, in order

This is the practical heart of the Part, and it is deliberately dense — but every item is a thing one person can do. Offered, as in Part 4F, as a monitoring tool: a list of signals to check, not a claim that any given application is a disguised data centre. Each project must be judged on its own file. Work down the list:

  • Read the land and registry trail. Search the corporate registry (CRO in Ireland, Tailte Éireann for land) for newly-formed companies with generic names — Infrastructure, Systems, Hosting, Data, Logistics, Digital, Ventures. Cross-check the directors: corporate lawyers, real-estate managers and executive assistants whose professional histories trace back to a hyperscaler are a recognised signal. Watch for the same anonymous entity quietly buying large, contiguous parcels of rural or historic land.

  • Take the utility and grid fingerprints. Compute cannot hide its appetite. Check the grid operator’s public portals (EirGrid here) for sudden large megawatt/MVA requests or new high-voltage substation applications in rural areas. Scan water-authority logs for industrial-extraction or large-connection requests disproportionate to a “warehouse.” Grid-interconnection filings often surface 12–18 months before any planning vote — the earliest public warning there is.

  • Translate the planning euphemism. Don’t search “data centre.” Search the camouflage vocabulary: data storage facility; information/communications hub; high-density logistics centre; light-industrial technology park; enterprise and employment zone; battery/BESS; private wire. Set keyword alerts on the council portal.

  • Read the blueprint for the giveaways. Download the application PDFs and look for the tells of a server hall wearing a warehouse’s clothes: rows of outdoor backup-generator slots; large roof or perimeter cooling plant; a vast, near-windowless monolith; and the single clearest signal — a building of tens of thousands of square metres with parking for only 20–30 cars, because a running data centre needs almost no people.

  • Watch the ground. Before permission is even decided, note heavy earth-moving framed as “pre-development archaeological assessment,” and small drilling rigs taking soil cores on farmland (checking whether bedrock will carry server blocks and transformers). Photograph and date it. Caveat, and it matters: in Ireland the one litigated example — the EngineNode works at Bracetown/Gunnocks, Co. Meath — produced a 2022 High Court finding that a second phase of excavation was unauthorised, with the boreholes held exempt and the developer denying wrongdoing, and a 2023 dismissal of the substantive challenge (Part 4F). Treat early groundwork as a question to ask, on the record, not as proof of a hidden build.

  • Use the eye in the sky. Free Sentinel-2 (ESA) or Landsat (NASA) infrared imagery will show a persistent, concentrated thermal plume over a heavy compute load even when the building is insulated or partly underground. A bright, steady heat signature over an “empty” shed is worth a closer look.

  • Track the hardware at the port. Shipping manifests are public records. Aggregators (ImportYeti, Panjiva, Volza, Zauba) and national customs systems let you watch for the specific Harmonized System codes for servers and cooling plant — HS 8471 (data-processing machines), 8473.30 (parts), 8419.50 (heat exchangers), 8504.40 (UPS/power) — consigned to contract manufacturers (Foxconn, Quanta/QCT, Wiwynn, Inspur) rather than to the famous brand. A sudden cluster of very heavy 8471 shipments into your nearest port inside a 30–60 day window is hardware arriving to fill a hall. (Note: not every country exposes consignee names — Ireland routes through Revenue’s import systems and Dublin Port/Port of Cork; some jurisdictions only allow aggregate trade data.)

  • Pool it. None of this works alone. Form or join a watch group; crowdfund the registry searches; share findings into the open-source mapping networks (and into our own Ripple Register). A coalition turns scattered local worry into a pattern a journalist, a regulator or a court can use.

Call to action — read one suspect site. Take a single development you have heard about and run the eight moves above: the registry, the grid and water filings, the planning euphemisms, the blueprint tells, the satellite heat signature, the port manifests — then log it in the Ripple Register and tell one other person. One site, by one person, is how the national map gets built. Detail: the eight-step toolkit above; templates at the back and on the companion site.

The wins are real — this is not a counsel of despair

The most important correction to make to any “Big Tech is unstoppable” framing is that it is false, and the receipts are public.

In the United States, Data Center Watch — a tracker run by the intelligence firm 10a Labs, which is careful to say it does not attribute every delay solely to opposition — counts roughly $64 billion in data-centre projects blocked or delayed over two years to March 2025 ($18bn blocked, $46bn delayed), driven by at least 142 local groups across 24 states, with later updates putting the number of groups higher still. The backlash is bipartisan. Polling in 2026 found a clear majority of Americans oppose data centres in their own communities, with electricity costs the leading reason.

A few concrete results worth holding onto: in March 2026 Microsoft pledged to stop using non-disclosure agreements with local governments as a default and to terminate active ones worldwide — a direct retreat on the secrecy tactic, and, as open-government advocates noted, one that came only after sustained public pressure (and one that still exempts land deals). Towns from Wiscasset to Lewiston have paused or voted down multi-billion-dollar projects. And in a useful, honest example of how hard the last yard is: Maine’s legislature passed the first statewide moratorium in the country on data centres over 20 MW — only for the Governor to veto it in April 2026 over a single carve-out, before moving to convene a study council instead. The lesson is not that the fight is lost; at least a dozen states are weighing similar measures, and the movement is plainly growing. The lesson is that a near-miss is still a marker, and that the work is won community by community.

Erin Brockovich’s citizen-reported US data-centre map (Part 4C) is the model, and the principle is the same one this whole booklet turns on: attack the secrecy, not just the building. Every country in the Part 4C table needs its own version. Ireland’s — county by county, fed by the toolkit above and logged in the Ripple Register — is well within reach.

Worth a Look: Active Digs and the Question to Ask

Archaeology and infrastructure are quietly bound together. Under the “polluter pays” principle, the law in most countries makes a developer fund the archaeological assessment before they are allowed to build — so a dig is often the first visible sign that something large is coming, and the public record of who commissioned it names who is about to break ground. That is not a theory; it is how planning works. It is also why a dig is worth a look — not a reason for suspicion.

A dig is a question, not a smoking gun. Almost all of the excavations below are exactly what they say they are — research seasons, field schools, heritage projects, salvage work ahead of ordinary housing or roads. The point is not that any one of them hides anything. The point is that, because developers pay for archaeology before they build, a dig can be an early marker of land being assembled — so the honest, clean-hands move is simply to ask, on the record: who commissioned this one, and what is being put together around it? Never assert. Just ask, and let the paperwork answer.

Only a select few — and the list is the start, not the end. These are a handful of examples, current at the time of writing; there are many more, and digs open and close every season. The point is not to take this list as complete — it is to investigate: to pull the record of these, and of any dig near a grid, water or land corridor you are watching, and see who paid for it. Every register named below is public and searchable. And the four countries here are only where this campaign happened to look — the same check applies everywhere the build is going: every country has its own excavation or heritage register, and the same question travels with it. Wherever you are, find your local register and ask it. Start here; do not stop here.

Ireland. Prospecthill / Maree Neolithic court tomb, Co. Galway; the Blackfriary, Trim, Co. Meath; the Green Fort, Sligo; St Aidan’s Monastery and the Lost Town of Carrig (Ferrycarrig), Co. Wexford; and the Boyne Valley (the Knowth–Dowth–Newgrange landscape). Where to check everything: excavations.ie holds a summary of every licensed excavation in Ireland, North and South, from 1970 to today, and the report often names who funded the work; cross-refer the National Monuments Service and the Royal Irish Academy grant lists.

Italy. The Roman baths at Cosa, Tuscany; the Etruscan necropolis at Tuscania (Sasso Pinzuto); Poggio Civitate at Murlo; Gabii near Rome (where a large Roman water basin was recently uncovered); Populonia / Poggio del Molino; and the continuing work at Pompeii. Where to check: Italy’s Ministero della Cultura and the regional soprintendenze record authorised excavations.

Mexico. Coatepec / San Lucas, Veracruz — a salvage dig that began precisely because the land was rezoned for residential development; the Tula altar, Hidalgo, found during salvage work along a rail line; the cenote beneath the main plaza at Chichén Itzá; and Aguada Fénix, Tabasco. Where to check: INAH, the National Institute of Anthropology and History, publishes salvage and research projects — and note how often the trigger is a change of land use.

Australia. Murujuga, Western Australia (rock art, and the first underwater shelf sites); the Big Dig at The Rocks, Sydney; and the many salvage excavations across New South Wales and Victoria run ahead of roads, rail and development under the heritage Acts. Where to check: state heritage registers and the AHIMS database — with one respectful caveat, that Aboriginal site locations are often deliberately withheld for cultural-sensitivity reasons, and that confidentiality must be honoured.

Call to action — check who is paying for the dig. Pick one excavation near a grid, water or land corridor you are watching and pull its record: in Ireland, search excavations.ie for the licence and the funder; elsewhere, the national heritage body. If a research institution commissioned it, that is the answer. If a development company did, that is a documented fact worth a question — asked on the record, never as an accusation. Detail: this section; cross-refer Part 4F (the EngineNode judgment) and the eight-step toolkit above.

A clean-hands note on going global

Two cautions, so the campaign’s international claims stay as defensible as its Irish ones. First, planning, water and corporate law differ by country; a tactic that works under the Aarhus Convention in Europe may need a different instrument elsewhere — name the local body, don’t assume the Irish one. Second, the global examples here are kept to the documented ones, and contested specifics — which named project is “really” which company, who sits behind which shell — are deliberately left as questions to be answered from each local file, not asserted. The pattern is real and sourced. Each individual site must still be judged on its own paperwork. That discipline is not a weakness of the argument. It is what makes it impossible to wave away.

Part 4H — The Good Actors: Proof That This Is a Choice

The whole argument of this booklet rests on one claim — that none of this is inevitable. It is chosen, and it can be chosen differently. The clearest proof is the countries and companies already choosing differently. Name them, because praise is leverage too: it shows everyone else that another way exists, and that it works.

What this chapter is about. This chapter is the antidote to despair. It gathers the places, policies and people who did it differently — proof that the extractive outcome is a choice, not a law of nature, and that refusal works.

“Never doubt that a small group of thoughtful, committed citizens can change the world; indeed, it’s the only thing that ever has.” — Margaret Mead

The four marks of a good actor. Reciprocity — the heat and the value come back to the community, not just the bill. Disclosure — no NDAs, no hidden water and power. Restraint — the willingness to say “no,” or “only if.” Accountability — real consequences for real harm. Denmark, Finland, Belgium, the Netherlands and Singapore each pass at least one of these tests. The point of this Part is simple: Ireland could pass all four.

Denmark — the model, done properly. Denmark is the example this booklet keeps returning to because it treats the server hall as a neighbour rather than a tenant. Its data centres run on a grid that is overwhelmingly renewable; the Meta facility at Odense pipes its waste heat into the district-heating network, warming on the order of eleven thousand homes; and an all-party waste-heat agreement made that reciprocity the norm rather than the exception. When the grid came under strain in March 2026, Denmark did the thing Ireland would not do for years — it imposed a moratorium to protect capacity, rather than waving the next project through. And Denmark has gone further than infrastructure: in a move begun in June 2025 and moving into force in 2026, it is granting every citizen a copyright-style right over their own face, voice and likeness — the first country in Europe to treat your identity as something you own, with the power to demand takedown and compensation when an AI system clones it without consent. (Status: a draft amendment to the Danish Copyright Act, expected to take effect in 2026 — check current status before quoting as fully enacted; sources: Danish Ministry of Culture; European Parliament research service; Schjødt and Plesner legal analyses, 2025–2026.)
