That is what it looks like when a government decides the citizen, not the platform, is the rights-holder.

Finland — heat as a public good. Finland runs one of the cleanest grids in Europe and has turned data-centre waste heat into civic infrastructure: a Microsoft–Fortum scheme is designed to channel recovered server heat into district heating serving on the order of a quarter of a million people. The lesson is the one Ireland keeps missing — the heat a data centre throws away is not a nuisance to be vented into a river; it is a resource to be given back.

Belgium — first to name the crime. In February 2024, ahead of the EU’s own directive, Belgium became the first European country to write ecocide into its penal code. It matters because it proves the legal instrument this booklet points to is not theoretical: a national parliament has already decided that the gravest environmental harm should carry criminal weight, and that decision-makers can be held to it.

The Netherlands and Singapore — the discipline of “only if.” Two states show that restraint is a governing option, not a fantasy. The Netherlands holds enduring restrictions on new hyperscale data centres — broadly, those with IT loads of 70 MW or more — confining them to a small number of designated sites. Singapore imposed an outright moratorium on new data centres in 2019 and now runs a competitive “call for applications” under which only the most efficient, low-emission facilities are permitted at all. Neither country said “never.” Both said “only if” — and made the industry meet the condition rather than the other way round.

The European Union — a mixed report, told honestly. Clean hands means crediting the good even where the same body has done harm. The EU earns a black mark for the Energy Efficiency Directive’s confidentiality carve-out — the secrecy mechanism this booklet criticises throughout. But it also produced the sharpest tool on the table: the Environmental Crime Directive (2024/1203), which created offences “comparable to ecocide” and put decision-makers personally within reach of the criminal law. And Denmark, holding the Council presidency, has used the platform to push likeness rights across the bloc. Institutions are not all-good or all-bad; the job is to back the good instruments and name the bad ones.

What good looks like, distilled. Read those examples against the Part 4C map and the contrast is the whole argument: the difference between a Denmark and an extraction site is not geography or luck — it is whether the community was consulted, whether the costs were counted, and whether the answer was allowed to be “no.” These are the countries standing on the Door Three side of Part 15B’s Sliding Doors. Ireland — with its renewable potential, its district-heating opportunities, its EU legal cover and its tradition of the commons — has every ingredient to join them. It is a choice. This booklet is an argument for making it.

Call to action — reward the good actors. When you choose a provider, cite an example, or write to a representative, name and back the ones doing it right: the disclosing, the reciprocal, the restrained. Ask your council and your TD why Ireland cannot do what Denmark, Finland and Singapore already do. Praise is leverage — use it. Detail: this Part; cross-refer Part 4C (Denmark and Finland as models) and Part 15B (Door Three).

Part 5 — Surveillance & Centralisation: One Pattern, Not Three Issues

Surveillance powers expanding while independent oversight is disbanded. One pattern, not three issues.

This chapter's argument has a name in the academic literature, and it is worth knowing. Shoshana Zuboff's The Age of Surveillance Capitalism (2019) is the foundational scholarly account of the pattern this chapter documents case by case: that personal data extraction is not a side effect of convenience but a new logic of capital accumulation in its own right, one that treats human experience itself as free raw material to be captured, predicted, and sold. Zuboff's own argument for what to do about it lands close to this booklet's own instinct — that the harm is structural, so the response has to be collective and political, not a matter of individual users managing their own settings more carefully.

What this chapter is about. This chapter argues that surveillance powers, health-data centralisation, media concentration and the new AI law are not separate stories but one — the steady expansion of how clearly the powerful can see us, as our ability to see them narrows. It runs from Garda recording powers to the doorbell on the porch to the Bill being rushed through under an EU deadline.

“He who has the information has the power.” — (common maxim)

The infrastructure of perception. The same forces that build the physical server halls also shape what can be seen of them. The State’s sight of us expands — facial recognition, ANPR, centralised health data — while our sight of them narrows: a media landscape whose ownership keeps concentrating, and a university sector whose funding quietly leans on the firms it should be scrutinising. Health data behind a one-way mirror, the press behind a captured one, the campus behind a third. That is why these sit in one Part and not three.

The Garda Recording Devices (Amendment) Bill 2025

Introduced 27 May 2026, currently at Second Stage. What it does that most people do not realise:

  • Facial-recognition technology — without judicial oversight.

  • Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) — sustained monitoring of a vehicle for up to three months, signed off internally by a Superintendent, with no judge required.

  • Drones, body-worn and animal-worn cameras in public spaces.

  • Live access to third-party CCTV.

  • A redefinition of “surveillance device” that excludes video cameras — so the most common form of surveillance no longer legally counts as surveillance, removing the judicial check that previously applied.

The disbanded AI Advisory Council’s own February 2025 advice paper classified facial recognition as “high-risk” and said it required independent evaluation before procurement. The Bill progressed anyway. The ICCL and Digital Rights Ireland are fighting it.

The doorbell that watches the street

Surveillance does not only descend from the State. It also rises from the porch — sold as convenience, normalised one household at a time. It is worth looking at, because it shows the same pattern this Part describes at a scale you can see from the footpath.

In December 2025, Amazon’s Ring — the smart-doorbell company it bought for a billion dollars — launched a facial-recognition feature called “Familiar Faces.” Built into a consumer doorbell, it identifies and names regular visitors, so the owner gets an alert like “Dad is at the door.” The owner opts in. The whole problem is that the people whose faces are scanned cannot opt out: the delivery worker, the postman, the canvasser, the child at the gate, the neighbour on the public path — all enrolled in biometric scanning without their knowledge or consent. As the Electronic Frontier Foundation put it, a feature to recognise your friend at the door today can be repurposed for mass surveillance tomorrow.

This is now in court. In June 2026 a Virginia resident filed a class action in Seattle alleging Ring collected and stored the facial data of millions of passers-by without consent. Tellingly, the feature is unavailable in Illinois, Texas and Portland, Oregon — the jurisdictions with strong biometric-privacy laws. Amazon already knows where it is not allowed to do this; it simply does it where the law has not yet caught up. A US senator has urged Amazon to abandon the rollout, warning it could become “a new pipeline” for government data — and the warning is not idle: Ring built a service through which police could request footage from users, and it paid a US$5.8 million settlement to the Federal Trade Commission over employees improperly accessing customers’ private video.

Why does a doorbell belong beside Garda recording powers and health-data centralisation? Because it is the same instrument, only smaller, and therefore easier to see whole. Recall the engine this booklet keeps returning to: whoever holds the knowledge sees first. Here it is on a domestic scale. A convenience is offered as a gift — safety, a friendly alert. To provide it, a resource is taken: the biometric data of everyone who passes, including those who never agreed. The taking is normalised as protection. And the data, once gathered, flows toward the platform and, potentially, the State. The owner of the doorbell is not the villain; they wanted to feel safe. They are the node — as we are all, increasingly, the node — through which the extraction runs. The doorbell simply makes visible, at the scale of a single house, what the data centre does at the scale of a nation.

Money that can be switched off — programmable currency and the third lock

There is one more piece of the infrastructure of perception, and it is the one closest to the bone, because it touches not what you say or where you go but what you are allowed to buy. It belongs in this Part for the same reason the doorbell does: it is the same logic — convenience offered, control retained — at the scale of the household budget.

Start with a plain description of how money is changing, and then with the words of the people building it, because they are franker than any critic. A cash transaction has two parties: you and the person you pay. A modern electronic payment adds a third party — the bank or processor — which can, in defined circumstances (sanctions, fraud, a court order), block it. That third check has always existed, but it has been slow, manual, and human: a banker who knows you, a process, a record. What is now technically possible is to make that third check automatic — software that screens every transaction in real time against a set of rules, and permits or refuses it instantly, at scale, with no human in the loop. Money, in other words, that can be programmed: allowed for some purposes and not others, for some people and not others, switched on or off by whoever writes the rules.

This is not a fringe fear; it is the stated design goal, described approvingly by the man at the top of the system. In October 2020, at an International Monetary Fund seminar, the General Manager of the Bank for International Settlements — the central bank of central banks — Agustín Carstens, explained the appeal of a central bank digital currency in terms that need no gloss: a CBDC gives the central bank “absolute control” over the rules that determine how the money can be used, along with the technology to enforce that control. He contrasted it directly with cash, the point being that cash is anonymous and uncontrollable, and that this is precisely the property the new form of money removes. That is not a leak or an allegation. It is the case for the technology, made by its most senior global advocate, on the record.

In fairness — and the fairness matters, because this is a genuine debate and not a settled plot — not everyone inside the system agrees, and the dissent is on the record too. Neel Kashkari, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, has repeatedly asked what problem a CBDC actually solves that existing instant-payment apps do not, and warned that the real difference such a system introduces is the power to surveil and to impose restrictions. A Federal Reserve governor, Michelle Bowman, has warned of “the politicization of the payments system” — the risk that the rails money runs on become a tool for ends that have nothing to do with payments. The CFA Institute, hardly a radical body, notes plainly that the privacy impact depends entirely on how these systems are designed. So the technology is not a destiny, and this booklet does not claim it is. The point is narrower and firmer: the capability Carstens described is real, it is being built, and whether it ends in convenience or control depends on rules that are being written now — and on whether the public is watching while they are.

Two developments make this an Irish and European question, not an American curiosity. The European Central Bank is well advanced on a digital euro, with preparatory work running toward a possible launch around the end of the decade; whatever its final design, it will be the form this question takes here, and the design choices — how much privacy, how much programmability, how much human recourse — are being settled in advance of any public vote. And in the United States, legislation such as the GENIUS Act has moved the action from government-issued CBDCs to privately issued, dollar-pegged stablecoins running on programmable ledgers — which raises the same control question with less public accountability, not more, because a private issuer answers to its shareholders and its regulators’ rulebook, not to an electorate.

Set this beside the rest of this booklet and the shape is familiar to the point of monotony, which is the whole point — it is always the same shape. A capability is introduced as a convenience and a safeguard (faster payments, fraud protection, sanctions enforcement). To deliver it, a decision is moved to where the public cannot easily reach it — here, into the rules governing money itself, written by central banks and private issuers and enforced automatically. And the people building it describe the control openly, because in the language of efficiency and safety it does not sound like control at all. This is the monetary expression of the preference traced elsewhere in this book — through the planning fast-track that lifts a decision above the county council, through the confidentiality clause that puts consumption beyond view, and through the published worldview, set out in Part 5’s account of one technologist’s own essays, that treats democratic accountability as an obstacle to be engineered around. Exit over voice; the automated rule over the human judgement; the decision placed where the vote cannot reach it. The question to keep asking, of the digital euro and of every programmable-money proposal that follows, is the only one that has ever mattered here: who writes the rules, who can switch the money off, and what recourse does an ordinary person have when they do?

Sources: Agustín Carstens (General Manager, Bank for International Settlements), IMF seminar “Cross-Border Payments — A Vision for the Future,” 19 October 2020 — the “absolute control … and the technology to enforce that” remark is verbatim and widely reported, including by the CFA Institute. Neel Kashkari (President, Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis) — public remarks questioning the case for a CBDC and noting its surveillance/control implications. Federal Reserve Governor Michelle Bowman — warning on “the politicization of the payments system” (CFA Institute). European Central Bank digital-euro programme — ECB preparatory-phase materials. GENIUS Act and US stablecoin rulemaking — US legislation and Treasury rulemaking, 2025–2026. This section relies only on the public, on-the-record statements of named officials and on the design of named legislation; it makes no claim of a coordinated plan, and it presents the internal disagreement (Kashkari, Bowman) as faithfully as the advocacy (Carstens).

Health-data centralisation — whose data, whose innovation?

The Health Information Act 2026 was signed by the President on 30 April 2026, providing the legal basis for a Digital Health Record for every patient in Ireland. The HSE has begun procurement, with a regional rollout planned over seven to eight years.

The part that matters most is the part least discussed: secondary use. The Act establishes a framework for the secondary use of anonymised and pseudonymised health data — for research, innovation, teaching and training — sitting under the EU European Health Data Space, with implementation deadlines falling between 2026 and 2030.

The one-way mirror. The same State that will not compel a data centre to disclose how much water it takes from Cork Harbour is building a permit system to share citizens’ health data for “innovation.” Transparency is being made to flow in one direction only — out of people, never out of corporations. That asymmetry is the whole story.

The legitimate, answerable questions:

  • Who sits on the national health-data access body — and who appoints them?

  • Can commercial AI firms access pseudonymised records for model training under the banner of “research and innovation”?

  • Where will the data physically live — Irish servers, EU servers, or US commercial cloud?

  • What is the re-identification risk of “pseudonymised” data held at national scale?

  • Is there a public-ownership guarantee and an exit strategy if a supplier is acquired or a contract changes?

The public-good test — start with the null results

If the State is centralising health data for “research and innovation,” there is a clear, answerable public-benefit test it should pass first. Use the data to fix one of the most wasteful and least-discussed problems in medicine: the null result. Only about half of all clinical research is ever published, and the half that disappears is overwhelmingly the trials that found no effect. The consequence is not just distorted science — it is that other researchers, not knowing a trial already failed, run it again, wasting public money and exposing a fresh group of patients to the risks of a study whose answer is already known.

A framework that genuinely served the public would map and publish the null set first, so the wasteful, risky duplication ends. The demand is simple: before a single pseudonymised record is opened to commercial AI training under the banner of “innovation,” the same system must first be used to end the duplication of failed trials. Public benefit before private extraction. Prove the one-way mirror can flow the other way. The State can compel the publication of null clinical results in the public interest — but it will not compel a data centre to publish how much water it draws from Cork Harbour. The tools exist. They are pointed in only one direction.

Clean-hands note: the precise term is the null or negative result — a trial that found no statistically significant effect. The public-health case for publishing them, and for preventing duplicate trials, is well established and uncontested. Verify the specific percentages against the cited sources before quoting publicly.

The captured mirror — media plurality in Ireland

A democracy that cannot see itself clearly cannot defend itself. The same concentration-and-capture pattern that runs through energy, water and planning runs through the one institution meant to hold all of them to account: the press. If the mirror is owned by few hands, and trusted by fewer people each year, the watchdog goes quiet exactly when it is needed most.

The figures are documented. The Reuters Institute / Coimisiún na Meán Digital News Report for Ireland 2026, published in Cork in June 2026, found trust in news down nine percentage points in a single year to 42% — among the steepest drops recorded anywhere — while active news avoidance reached an all-time high of 47%, almost half the adult population turning away. Trust in established brands held up (RTÉ News and local radio at 71%), which tells its own story: people still trust the familiar names but are losing faith in the information system as a whole. Into that vacuum step the AI chatbots, already cited as a news source by a rising share of Irish adults — the machines becoming the mirror.

The ownership is concentrated to a degree the EU itself flags as a risk. Ireland’s Media Pluralism Monitor assessment places the country in the high-risk category for plurality, and a report commissioned by MEP Lynn Boylan concluded that Ireland has “one of the most concentrated media markets of any democracy.” Four of the five largest print groups are now owned outside the State; the main commercial television station is US-owned; and a single supplier feeds the news bulletins to the entire 31-station “independent” national radio network. The word independent, again, made to mean its opposite.

This is not a side issue to the data-centre fight. It is part of why the fight has been so quiet. The five-Act sequence passed without sustained scrutiny in part because the institutions meant to scrutinise it are concentrated, foreign-directed, stretched thin and increasingly distrusted. A hollowed-out press is not a separate failure from regulatory capture. It is the same failure, in the organ whose entire job was to catch the others.

On the record: ask the Department of Communications what action has been taken on the Media Pluralism Monitor’s high-risk finding for Ireland, and on the recommendations of the report on concentration of media ownership. Support the public-service and independent local outlets that still command trust — local and regional news is, per the 2026 report, where trust remains highest and capture is hardest.

The captured campus — who funds the people who should be warning us

There is a question underneath this whole campaign: why are more academics — the economists, engineers, planners, climate and water scientists who can see exactly what is being built — not saying so, loudly and often? The honest answer is uncomfortable, and it is documented. The people best placed to critique the industry are increasingly dependent on it for funding, research access and their students’ careers.

A February 2025 policy paper from UCD’s Geary Institute laid out the mechanism. Irish universities are funded through a model that deliberately blends State and industry money — the State research-centre programme has run on a cost-share basis where a substantial minimum share of each centre’s budget must come from industry. The same paper documented corporate-sponsored professorships carrying a company’s name, and corporate-funded scholarships and buildings, while noting there is no comprehensive public register of how much industry money flows in or on what terms. A 2025 Irish Times investigation put the question plainly in its headline: “Irish universities take millions from big business — are they too close for comfort?”

This is the same capture pattern, one layer deeper. When a researcher’s centre, salary, PhD students and next grant depend on industry partners — and when those same companies sit on the conference committees and control the datasets academic careers are built on — the effect is rarely a crude order to stay silent. It is subtler: self-censorship. The Academic Freedom Index records that most of the world’s population now lives under conditions that restrict academic freedom. The chilling effect does not need to be ordered. It only needs to be understood.

The same logic reaches into schools, where corporate-sponsored coding and digital-skills programmes — branded as opportunity — quietly shape which futures are presented to children as the only sensible ones. A generation is being trained for the very industry whose architecture threatens their water, their grid and, as the mounting evidence on youth wellbeing shows, their mental health. The people paid to prepare them are paid, increasingly, by the same hands.

None of this means industry funding is inherently corrupt, or that the many honest academics inside these systems are compromised. It means the structure produces a predictable silence — and that silence is itself evidence. The demand is clean: a full public register of industry funding to every higher-education institution, every named professorship and every corporate schools programme, with the terms disclosed. You cannot assess whether a critique has been chilled until you can see who is paying for the room.

On the record: ask each university and the Higher Education Authority for a full register of industry funding, named professorships, corporate scholarships and the contractual terms attached — and what conflict-of-interest and academic-freedom protections apply. Ask the Department of Education for a list of corporate-sponsored digital-skills and coding programmes in schools, the companies behind them, and what independent oversight governs their content.

One thing must be said clearly, because it shapes how students should act. The target is not the universities, and not the lecturers and researchers inside them. They are not the enemy. They are very often caught in the same trap — dependent on the same funding, constrained by the same structures, unable to say freely what they can plainly see. Students who mobilise on this should mobilise not against their own institutions but alongside the people in them: demanding the funding register, the conflict-of-interest disclosures and the academic-freedom protections that would let their lecturers speak without fear. A student body and a teaching staff that both want the truth told are natural allies against the structure that silences them both.

The Recording Devices Bill centralises surveillance. The HSE health record centralises health data. The data centres centralise energy and water extraction. The media concentrates into ever fewer hands. These are not separate issues. They are one pattern.

Governing what it does, not what it consumes — the AI Bill and the Presidency

Two things happened within days of each other in June 2026, and read side by side they tell you most of what you need to know about how Ireland approaches the industry at the centre of this booklet.

First, the Government published the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill 2026, describing it — in its own words — as proof of Ireland’s commitment to “human-centric AI” that “defends the rights of its citizens.” The Bill is, in plain terms, the domestic plumbing for the EU AI Act: it does not write Ireland’s own AI rules, it builds the machinery to enforce the EU’s. It establishes the AI Office of Ireland (which must be operational by 1 August 2026, an EU deadline) and spreads enforcement across a “distributed model” of around fifteen existing sectoral regulators — the Central Bank for financial AI, ComReg for digital infrastructure, the Commission for Regulation of Utilities for AI in water and electricity supply, the Data Protection Commission for biometrics, and so on. Penalties can reach 7% of worldwide turnover.

It is necessary legislation. The question is what it leaves out — and the answer is the thing this whole booklet is about. The Bill governs AI systems — their bias, safety, transparency and effect on rights — but it says nothing about the water, energy and grid the AI physically runs on. There is no requirement to assess or report the resource footprint of the compute behind a model. In the most data-centre-intensive economy in Europe, the State is legislating to govern what AI is allowed to do while saying nothing about what it is allowed to consume. And the distributed model means the same structural gap appears yet again: the AI Office coordinates but is not itself a market-surveillance authority and holds no sector of its own, so no single body is charged with seeing the whole picture. Who adds up the total? On the current text, no one. (On the related demand that the Office be made genuinely independent of the department that promotes the industry, see Part 7; on the frontier the Bill cannot even see — living-tissue computing — see the Brain in the Dish.)

Second, and at the same moment, Ireland took the Presidency of the Council of the EU (1 July to 31 December 2026), whose digital centrepiece is a “simplification” agenda — four “Omnibus” packages that, critics say, amount to deregulation across the environmental, digital, AI and energy spheres. On AI specifically, the Presidency is prioritising the AI Omnibus, which would push the AI Act’s high-risk compliance deadline back from August 2026 to as late as December 2027 and loosen protections around personal-data use in AI development.

In fairness, “simplification” is genuinely contested: supporters, pointing to the Draghi report on Europe’s competitiveness gap, argue it cuts duplicative red tape, and that case deserves to be stated. But the structural fact deserves to be stated just as plainly, and it needs no allegation of motive to be troubling: the country that hosts the European headquarters of the world’s largest technology firms, and whose corporate-tax base leans substantially on them, is the country now holding the pen on Europe’s AI and sustainability rules — and using it to simplify, delay and loosen them. A review of the tech-industry submissions to Ireland’s own Presidency consultation found firms openly equating “competitiveness” with deregulation, one of the largest offering, unprompted, a call for “a complete overhaul of Europe’s digital rulebook.” Lay the two facts side by side — the dependence, and the deregulatory direction of the Presidency it chairs — and the reader can draw the conclusion. No secret meeting need be alleged. The structure is the story.

The same pattern — strong protective language, thinner substance — runs through the Presidency’s flagship social theme: protecting children online. The Taoiseach has made it a headline goal, pushing for an EU-wide “digital age of majority,” an under-16 social-media restriction and age verification, promising “far greater protection for children online.” Yet the day after the AI Bill’s publication, the Children’s Rights Alliance — a respected advocacy body, not a partisan voice — pointed to a real gap: the Bill, it said, fails to recognise children as a vulnerable category, and “while lip service is paid to children’s safety online, the reality is that children and young people are engaging with unregulated systems and products.” A member of the Government’s own benches, Fianna Fáil TD Malcolm Byrne, sharpened the point: the State “is not placing enough pressure on the social media companies, instead they are placing the pressures on children and young people,” and called for a “digital duty of care” on the platforms themselves. That is precisely the argument this booklet makes about the outrage economy, voiced from inside the governing party: age verification asks the child to prove their age while leaving untouched the platform whose business model profits from keeping that child enraged and scrolling. It treats the symptom and spares the cause. (Other countries have at least moved on the symptom: Australia legislated an under-16 social-media restriction in December 2025 and moved to toughen it and double platform penalties in 2026 — so the debate over whether age-gating works is live and real, not abstract; Deputy Byrne also warned, fairly, that a blunt ban could push children toward more dangerous corners of the internet and must come with education, not just gates.)

Read together, these are not separate stories. They are one shape: a State that presents itself, sincerely in its language, as the careful steward of AI and protector of its citizens — while the substance points the other way. The law governs what AI does but not what it consumes. The enforcement is split so no one sees the whole. The Presidency protects children with one hand and deregulates with the other. None of this requires a hidden plot; it requires only a set of incentives pointing steadily in one direction while the rhetoric points in another. Naming that gap precisely — not “they are hypocrites,” which invites a denial, but “here is what was said, here is what was done, here is who benefits” — is the work, because it is an account anyone can check.

*Sources: Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill 2026 and General Scheme — DETE / gov.ie (Feb–June 2026); the distributed model of c.15 competent authorities and the 1 August 2026 AI Office deadline — DETE briefing note to the Joint Committee on Enterprise, Tourism and Employment (4 March 2026). EU Presidency priorities and the “simplification”/Omnibus agenda — Irish Government Presidency programme (June 2026), RTÉ and TechPolicy.Press; the AI Omnibus deadline shift to December 2027 — TechPolicy.Press (June 2026). Children’s Rights Alliance critique and Deputy Malcolm Byrne’s comments — RTÉ (June 2026). Australia under-16 restriction — RTÉ / The Journal (Dec 2025; toughened 2026).

Mask of Virtue — A Snapshot of Recent News (June 2026)

This booklet has named a single signature for the extractive mechanism: it takes, and it hides the taking behind a mask of virtue, care or progress. What follows is one ordinary week of national news, read through one question — what does the framing flatter, and what does it bury? The stories are real and described as published; the point is the pattern, not any single report.

What this chapter is about. A short interlude: a snapshot of how the story was being told in the news of a single month, and how the language of virtue and progress is used to dress the same old mechanism. It is a lesson in reading the press as carefully as the planning file.

“All that is necessary for the triumph of evil is that good men do nothing.” — commonly attributed to Edmund Burke

The external shock.

On 18 June the Central Bank revised its inflation forecasts upward — a baseline near 3.5% this year, and a severe scenario approaching 5% in 2027 — and the coverage led with the cause: a foreign war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The mask is the external shock: inflation as weather, blown in from elsewhere, nobody’s decision. Lower in the same bulletin sits the part that did not lead. The Bank expects the economy to contract this year, yet raised its domestic-demand forecast — crediting investment in artificial intelligence and data centres with offsetting weaker household spending. The very build-out that deepens Ireland’s energy exposure is what holds the demand figure up. And stripped of windfall corporation tax, the underlying deficit is set to double, leaving the State far less room to respond. The shock is real. So is the dependency it exposes — and only one of the two made the headline.

The protector.

The same day, the Government launched a thirty-point plan against financial crime and money laundering, the Tánaiste observing that financial crime is “not a victimless crime.” True — and the measures, including verifying company filings and forcing disclosure of ultimate ownership, are sound. The mask is the protector. What goes unsaid is that the State announcing a crackdown on corporate opacity is the same jurisdiction that built itself into a favoured domicile for opaque corporate structures and the funds described in Part 4. It will pursue the criminal’s hidden money while leaving the legal, incentivised extraction it hosts entirely intact. Following the money — but only the money without a lobbyist.

The concern.

The day before, the EPA reported no overall improvement in water quality for 2025: more than two-fifths of river sites carrying excess nitrogen, the main pressures named as agriculture and wastewater, and the loss of the highest-status waters called “a serious warning sign.” The mask is concern. But this is a ritual repeated almost word-for-word every year — the regulator measures, names the pressure, urges urgency, and the pressure rolls on, because measuring and warning is very nearly the whole of its power. And note what cannot appear in those figures: the per-site water use and thermal discharge of data centres, which is precisely the information no operator will disclose. The result is a regulator reduced to writing the annual obituary of waters it has no authority to save — with the newest extractor’s draw not even on the page.

Green progress.

Also this week: solar is soaring — rooftop capacity past a gigawatt, tens of thousands of homes now generating their own power — and electricity bills are still rising, among the highest in the EU. The mask is green progress, and the householders earned the credit. But the structural drivers of the price sit in the same coverage: grid costs, import dependence, and data centres now consuming more than a fifth of the State’s electricity — the wider large-energy-user category nearer a third. Households decarbonise their own roofs while the grid they feed is drained by the server halls. The virtue is the homeowner’s; the gain leaks to the structure; the bill climbs anyway.

Strategy and confidence.

In the global frame: Goldman Sachs announced more than a trillion dollars of merger-and-acquisition deals in a single half-year — a record — with worldwide volumes past $2.6 trillion as, in the bank’s own telling, artificial intelligence and consolidation reshape entire industries. The mask is strategy and confidence: boards taking a long-term strategic view to capture scale. The plain meaning of record consolidation is harder: fewer hands holding more, the extraction machine posting its best half ever — in the same week a citizen on legal aid in Ireland could not get a solicitor.

Efficiency and fairness.

Which was the week’s quietest scandal. Solicitors withdrew criminal legal-aid services across the country and thousands of cases were adjourned, in protest at a flat-fee model imposed from 1 July by emergency legislation, with no negotiation. The mask is efficiency and fairness — the Department presents it as ending unnecessary adjournments and paying a generous set fee. The solicitors say it makes defending the poorest unviable, and that the adjournments are driven by the State’s own shortfalls in court, probation and mental-health resources. Austerity dressed as reform, with the blame pre-assigned to the people refusing it.

The soft sell.

Around the hard news runs the softer framing that prepares the ground. An explainer wonders whether AI will make a business degree worthless — inevitability posed as curiosity, never quite asking who profits from making human learning look obsolete. Workers are urged to “pause” before opting out of the new auto-enrolment pension — the gentle, paternal nudge toward a financial product, in which care and salesmanship have become hard to tell apart.

Kinship.

And presiding over the month, the warmest frame of all: the Canadian Prime Minister’s visit, reported above all as a homecoming — Mayo grandparents, kinship, a cultural celebration. Carried inside the warmth: a new AI-cooperation framework, a Canadian firm engaged to build facilities for the public health service, a $160-million “AI hub” for Cork, and the final ratification of CETA — whose investor-court provisions the Irish Supreme Court had previously found repugnant to the Constitution, now legislated around on the eve of Ireland’s EU Presidency. The homecoming is genuine. It is also the wrapping.

The anaesthetic.

Set them side by side and it is not seven stories but one sentence — the parasitic signature named earlier: it extracts, and it hides the extraction behind a mask of virtue, care or progress. The protector, the green homeowner, the concerned regulator, the strategic board, the returning son — each frame flatters someone, so that the structure underneath is never the subject of the sentence. Virtue is the anaesthetic; the taking happens while you are moved. None of it requires a conspiracy, and naming it is not cynicism. It is simply declining the anaesthetic — and asking, of every story that flatters: who is the actor here, and what is being taken while I admire the costume?

A note on method, in keeping with this booklet’s standard: every item above is described as it was published in a single week of June 2026; the framing habits identified are common to mainstream news generally and are offered as a structural pattern, not a coordinated plot; and where causes are contested — the water pressures, the inflation drivers — the documented attribution has been kept distinct from interpretation. Sources: RTÉ News, The Irish Times, The Journal, Irish Examiner and Reuters reporting, 13–18 June 2026; Central Bank of Ireland Quarterly Bulletin; EPA “Water Quality in 2025: An Indicators Report” (17 June 2026); Solar Ireland “Scale of Solar” reporting; Government of Ireland and Government of Canada statements on the Canada–Ireland partnership (13 June 2026); Dealogic data as cited by Goldman Sachs.

Part 6 — What You Can Do: FOI, AIE, Legal Routes & Templates

The architecture is hidden, not sealed. FOI, AIE and the European floor are the levers that prise it open — and not one of them needs anyone’s permission to pull.

What this chapter is about. This is the toolkit. It turns everything before it into action — the Freedom of Information and Access to Information on the Environment requests, the legal routes, the ready-to-send templates that let any citizen force the questions into the open. The boxes in green throughout are yours to copy and use.

“Sunlight is said to be the best of disinfectants.” — Louis Brandeis

Urgent — open consultation deadlines

  • National Cyber Security Strategy — closes 21 August 2026 at 12pm (Department of Justice, Home Affairs and Migration; online SmartSurvey form). The full ready-made submission toolkit for this one is immediately below.

  • National Nature Restoration Plan — closes 1 July 2026.

  • National Bioresources Strategy — closes 22 July 2026.

  • Galway Wastewater Strategy — closes 24 July 2026 (extends the Cork Harbour argument nationally).

The National Cyber Security Strategy submission — a ready-made toolkit (closes 21 August 2026)

A live Government consultation with a hard deadline. A submission from you — in your own words, before 21 August 2026 — is one of the most useful actions in this whole toolkit. What follows is ready-made: the argument, the sources, the exact asks, and the steps. Adapt it and submit it.

The clock. The Department of Justice, Home Affairs and Migration opened a public consultation on the draft National Cyber Security Strategy on 9 July 2026. It closes Friday 21 August 2026 at 12:00pm — six weeks, open to anyone living in Ireland, through an online SmartSurvey form linked from the gov.ie consultation page. The draft is built on three pillars: Detect and Defend, Enhance National Resilience, and Strengthen our Cyber Eco-System. Everything below belongs under the second pillar. You do not need to be an expert. You need six weeks and your own name.

Why I am writing about cyber security at all

I run a campaign about water and data centres. So why a submission on cyber security? Because the two turned out to be the same question wearing different coats. A strategy meant to protect Ireland's critical national infrastructure cannot be complete if it stays silent on the concentration of that infrastructure, on the resource dependencies nobody measures, and on who actually owns and controls it. I am not against digital infrastructure. I am against building it in a way we cannot see, cannot account for, and no longer control. That is a resilience problem before it is anything else.

I have tried to write this the way I write everything: every load-bearing claim traced to a named source, nothing imputed to any individual, the system indicted rather than any person. Where something is a concern rather than a proven fact, I have said so plainly. That discipline is not timidity. It is what makes this hard to dismiss.

Cyber resilience means facing concentration

Ireland hosts, on the State's own long-standing estimate, roughly 30% of all EU data, and the European operations of many of the world's largest technology firms. The Oireachtas Library & Research Service counts around 121 data centres operated by 24 providers, about 97% of them clustered in the wider Dublin area. Data centres consumed 22% of Ireland's metered electricity in 2024 — up from 5% in 2015 — and EirGrid projects that to reach 31% by 2034, with data centres accounting for 89% of the projected growth in electricity demand over the coming decade (CSO; EirGrid; CRU decision CRU/2025236, December 2025).

A concentration of that scale is an economic asset and a strategic exposure at the same time. It raises the consequence of any single failure and makes Ireland a disproportionately valuable target. The State's own 2025 National Cyber Risk Assessment — which the Department names as the evidence base for this very Strategy — is reported to have raised the likelihood of attacks on digital infrastructure to "Likely or Probable," the impact rating for energy to "Significant" because of cascading effects, and the likelihood for water to a "Real Possibility." If the State's own assessors have already found that digital infrastructure, energy and water do not fail in isolation, the Strategy must carry that finding through into concrete cross-sector planning — not leave it sitting in the assessment.

The rebuttal I already know is coming: "but it's 80% renewable"

I want to meet this head-on, because it is the first thing that will be said back to me, and because being fair to it is the whole method here. In December 2025 the CRU decided that new data centres above 1MVA must meet at least 80% of their annual electricity demand with additional renewable generation built in Ireland (CRU/2025236). That is a real rule and a genuine improvement, and anyone who pretends otherwise is not arguing in good faith.

But hold it up to the light honestly:

  • It is an annual paper-match, not real-time power. Data centres run around the clock; wind and solar do not. Matching 80% of a year's total demand through contracts is not the same as being powered by clean electricity at 3am on a still night.
  • There is a six-year glide path. The requirement does not bite at switch-on. It is a target for later.
  • The regulator concedes it lacks enforcement powers. In its own draft, the CRU acknowledged it would not receive powers to mandate renewable procurement or cap emissions; compliance runs through contractual obligations (TechPolicy.Press, December 2025). A rule the regulator says it cannot fully enforce is a promise, not a guarantee.
  • The renewables claims are not independently tracked. Corporate power purchase agreements are not recorded at national or EU level (Oireachtas Library & Research Service), so the 80% cannot be verified from the outside.
  • The fallback is fossil. Faced with grid constraints, some operators have turned to on-site gas and diesel generation, making each site an industrial emitter; the State itself commissioned roughly €1bn of emergency gas generation near Dublin. And Ireland draws about 80% of its total energy from fossil fuels to begin with (Paul Deane, University College Cork).

So the honest position is not that the 80% rule is a lie. It is that it is an annual, deferred, largely unverifiable, under-enforced target sitting on top of a fossil-heavy grid. That is worth saying calmly and exactly, because the calm exact version is the one that holds.

The blind spot: what we refuse to measure, we cannot protect

Here is where my own work bears directly on cyber resilience. The critical infrastructure this Strategy exists to protect currently runs with holes in the very resource data a resilience plan depends on:

  • Uisce Éireann has said it holds no information on data centres' private borehole water use, because it lies outside its remit.
  • The EPA's water-abstraction register lists no data centres, and only 16 of the ~121 sites hold an EPA industrial-emissions licence (as at November 2024).
  • Operators face no legal duty to disclose their water-consumption volumes, and the chemistry of any thermal discharge is unmonitored.
  • There is no single authoritative public register of data centres — their number varies by source, and the CRU has itself called for a "State-led approach encompassing spatial planning," which is a polite way of saying one does not yet exist.

Whatever you think of these facts environmentally, they are a resilience problem. You cannot risk-assess, secure, or ration an infrastructure whose basic resource draw is invisible to the regulators nominally charged with it. What we refuse to measure, we cannot protect.

How the ground shifted: agency removed one lawful move at a time

None of this was a conspiracy. It is worse than that, and more fightable: it was assembled in daylight, one lawful move at a time, until a citizen's ability to see and to object had quietly thinned. Consider the moves, each defensible on its own, damning in sum.

The courthouse door narrowed. The Planning and Development Act 2024 overhauled planning judicial review. It introduced more restrictive standing rules, removed the right of appeal to the Court of Appeal (leaving appeal only where a point of "exceptional public importance" is certified), set an eight-week window to bring proceedings, restricted applicants to the grounds in their original filing, and replaced the settled costs rules with a new regime. The Law Society, the Council of the Bar of Ireland, the Oireachtas Library & Research Service, legal academics, and — as reported — the President himself have all raised concerns that these changes may narrow access to justice and sit uneasily with the Constitution, EU law and the Aarhus Convention (Irish Legal News; Law Society Gazette; Oireachtas L&RS briefing, January 2026).

And the case that JR was ever the bottleneck does not hold. Only about 3–5% of planning decisions are challenged at all (Alison Hough BL, Law Society conference, November 2025). The judge in charge of the Planning and Environment Court noted roughly 247 live cases covering under 7,000 homes — against some 100,000 homes already permitted and unbuilt. Judicial review is not what is clogging the pipe. But it is the citizen's main check on power, and it is the thing that was narrowed.

Standing now depends on having objected early. Under the new Act, an individual who was not "directly or indirectly materially affected" generally keeps standing to seek review only if they made a valid submission during the planning process itself. In plain terms: if you did not object at the right early moment, you can lose the right to challenge later. Participation is no longer a door that stays open — it is a window that closes quietly.

The map itself is hard to read. With no single register, only partial EPA licensing, and counts that differ from source to source, an ordinary citizen cannot easily find out how many data centres there are, where they are, or what they draw. This is not necessarily anyone's deliberate concealment — but the effect is the same: a sector this large should not be this hard to see.

And control was deferred to regulators without the powers to control. Rather than resolve the political trade-off between digital growth and sustainability, the State handed it to regulators tasked with managing that growth smoothly — while, on the CRU's own admission, withholding the enforcement powers that would let them actually direct it (TechPolicy.Press, December 2025).

That is the pattern. No single villain. A sequence of lawful, reasonable-sounding moves that, assembled, leaves the citizen with less to see and less to say. Naming the pattern is the point.

The questions cyber security must answer first

A cyber security strategy that hardens the walls of a house we no longer own has protected the wrong thing. Before we deepen our dependence, four questions need answering — and I raise them as concerns to be addressed, not as accusations proven.

1. Ownership: whose infrastructure is this, and where do the profits and the control go? Ireland's digital economy is extraordinarily concentrated in foreign hands. In 2023, 88% of all corporation tax was paid by foreign multinationals, and 57% of it by just ten companies (CSO/Revenue, via TechPolicy.Press). The infrastructure these firms operate here is controlled elsewhere, and its profits flow elsewhere. Tying the nation's public and economic life ever more tightly into infrastructure whose control sits in another jurisdiction is a sovereignty decision, and it deserves to be made deliberately rather than by drift. The contents of that infrastructure — the data — may also, in the ordinary course of these firms' data-driven business models, become raw material for further data harvesting. I do not assert that any specific misuse has occurred. I say that these questions must be answered before we deepen the dependency, not after.

2. Anonymity: is the promise as solid as it sounds? Citizens are routinely assured their data is "anonymised." But under the GDPR there is a crucial and shifting line between pseudonymised data (still personal, re-identifiable with a key, still protected) and anonymised data (deemed permanently unidentifiable, and therefore outside the GDPR entirely). Recent EU court rulings have made that line recipient-relative — the same dataset can be treated as "anonymous" in the hands of one party and personal in another — which "could open new pathways for data reuse" (Freshfields analysis of the April 2026 General Court ruling). At the same time, the EU is building large data-pooling frameworks such as the European Health Data Space for the secondary reuse of data. And researchers have long warned that truly irreversible anonymisation — eliminating all means of re-identification — is close to impossible to guarantee at big-data scale (Article 29 Working Party; academic literature). My concern is not that a promise was knowingly broken. It is that the promise of anonymity rests on legal and technical classifications the law is currently loosening, precisely as the machinery for pooling and reusing data is being built.

3. Lock-in: does hardening our cyber security quietly hand away control of our public services? This is the one I most want the Strategy to sit with. If national cyber resilience is built on top of concentrated, privately owned, foreign-controlled hyperscale infrastructure, then the very act of securing our public services could bind them to that infrastructure — a surveillance-and-dependency economy in which the State no longer holds the keys to its own systems. Resilience that deepens lock-in is not resilience; it is a more comfortable cage. I ask the Strategy to treat vendor lock-in and loss of public-service autonomy as first-order cyber risks, not afterthoughts.

4. The revolving door. For a sector that must be held at arm's length, it matters who is doing the holding. Ireland has recognised this risk in law: the Regulation of Lobbying Act 2015 created a public lobbying register (lobbying.ie) and a one-year "cooling-off" period restricting senior public officials — Ministers, TDs, Senators, MEPs, special advisers, secretaries general — from lobbying after they leave office. Tellingly, the original Act had no way to enforce that cooling-off period, and the State had to return in 2023 with the Regulation of Lobbying (Amendment) Act to add sanctions of up to €25,000 and lobbying bans, commencing June 2024 (SIPO; lobbying.ie). That the safeguard had to be strengthened tells you the risk is real and was under-guarded. I name no individual. I ask only that, for infrastructure this strategically important, lobbying transparency and cooling-off rules be applied with the strictest rigour, and that officials with prior industry ties be kept at a measured, discerning distance from the decisions that affect that industry. Any citizen can check who has been lobbying whom, on the public register, at lobbying.ie.

The worked example: Meta, in three moves

Everything above stops being abstract when you watch one company do all of it at once — lawfully, on the record, which is exactly the point. Meta's European operations are headquartered in Dublin, and its lead data-protection regulator for the entire EU is therefore Irish.

  • It reaches into private photos. Through 2025–2026 Meta rolled out a "camera roll cloud processing" feature (tested in the US and Canada, then offered opt-in in the EU and UK) that asks Facebook users to let it upload and analyse photos from their phone — including ones never posted — down to the facial features. Meta already trains its AI on public posts since 2007 (EU users can opt out; US users cannot). On the private camera-roll photos it said they are "not used to improve AI models in this test" — but declined to rule out training on them in future (The Verge; TechCrunch). You can turn this off: Facebook → Settings → Camera Roll Sharing Suggestions / cloud processing → off.
  • It moved to silence the whistleblower. Sarah Wynn-Williams, Facebook's former global public policy director, published Careless People (2025), filed SEC and DOJ complaints, and testified to the Senate. Meta did not sue for libel; it obtained a private-arbitration gag order barring her from promoting the book or "disparaging" the company, and pursued penalties of $50,000 per alleged breach — even seeking sanctions when she sat silent on a festival stage. She sued in June 2026 to strike the gag as an unconstitutional restraint on speech. Meta says she signed a non-disparagement clause for a large severance, that an arbitrator found she breached it, and that the book is false. The mechanism is the point: private contract muscle used to silence someone who went to Congress — the opposite of what Ireland's Protected Disclosures Act is for.
  • It outspends everyone on the rules. Meta is the EU's single largest tech lobbyist (~€10m/year on the EU Transparency Register), part of a digital-sector effort of ~€151m/year that fields more lobbyists than there are MEPs (Corporate Europe Observatory / LobbyControl, 2025). The lobby group DigitalEurope, of which Meta is a member, led the push to classify data centres' energy and water figures as confidential — the secrecy clause at the heart of this whole problem (Investigate Europe / The Journal, 2026).

And in Ireland, the circle closes. In September 2025 a former Meta lobbyist (who led Facebook's European-HQ lobbying 2015–2019, and had worked until shortly before at a tech-lobbying firm) was appointed Ireland's Data Protection Commissioner — the very office that regulates Meta for the whole EU — with no cooling-off period. The Irish Council for Civil Liberties has filed a formal complaint to the European Commission that Ireland failed to protect the regulator's independence, which EU law says must be "above any suspicion of partiality." No individual is accused of anything here; the concern is process and the appearance of independence. You can read Meta's Irish lobbying yourself at lobbying.ie.

It can be done right: Estonia kept the infrastructure

None of this is an argument against digital government. It is an argument for doing it sovereignly — and there is a working proof that you can. After suffering one of the first nation-scale cyber attacks in 2007, Estonia built a digital state around sovereignty and citizen control rather than dependency:

  • X-Road, its data-exchange backbone, is decentralised and open-source (published on GitHub under the permissive MIT licence). Data stays with its original custodian rather than being pooled, is shared only with consent, and every access is logged so citizens can see who looked at their data and when.
  • The "once-only" principle — that the State may ask for a piece of your data only once — is written into law, not just policy.
  • Core services run on a state-owned government cloud (G-Cloud), keeping critical infrastructure in public hands.
  • A "data embassy" in Luxembourg holds a backup of the nation's critical data with the same diplomatic inviolability as an embassy building — sovereign resilience against even catastrophic failure.
  • Estonia now exports the model through its e-Governance Academy, which has supported over 60 countries.

Estonia is not utopia, and I will not pretend it is: its digital-ID system is operated by private companies under state oversight, it has weathered its own security scares, and scholars debate how far the model travels. But it demonstrates the thing that matters here — that a country can have world-class digital infrastructure that is transparent by design, resilient, and owned by its own people. That is the standard to measure ourselves against: not whether we host the most data, but whether we control the infrastructure that holds it.

What I am asking the Strategy to do

  1. Name data-centre concentration (power, water, cooling) as a critical-infrastructure interdependency and resilience risk, and carry the 2025 National Cyber Risk Assessment's own findings through into cross-sector planning under the logic of NIS2.
  2. Require resource-draw disclosure — water, energy, cooling chemistry — to the relevant regulators (EPA, Uisce Éireann, EirGrid/CRU) as a precondition of resilience planning for large facilities. Transparency is a security measure, not a burden.
  3. Establish a single public register of data centres, their locations, and their resource draw, so citizens and regulators can see the sector whole.
  4. Treat vendor lock-in and loss of public-service autonomy as first-order cyber risks, and commit to sovereign, auditable, ideally open-source infrastructure for critical public services — the Estonian standard.
  5. Deal with the ownership and data-reuse questions before deepening dependency, not after — including honest treatment of where control and profit sit, and of how "anonymised" data may be pooled and reused.
  6. Apply lobbying-transparency and cooling-off rules with maximum rigour to this sector, and keep officials with industry ties at arm's length from decisions affecting it.
  7. Commission an independent stress-test of combined grid-and-water resilience under projected data-centre load growth, and publish the risk picture accessibly for citizens, not only for industry stakeholders.

Toolkit: what you can actually do

1. Make your own submission before 21 August 2026, 12pm. Go to the gov.ie page for the "Public consultation on the draft National Cyber Security Strategy" and follow the SmartSurvey link. You can adapt any part of this document into your answers, in your own words. Submissions may be published and are subject to Freedom of Information — so do not include personal information you would not want on the record.

2. The laws worth citing — in plain English. - Planning and Development Act 2024 — overhauled planning judicial review (standing, costs, removal of the Court of Appeal route, eight-week window). The reason your right to object and to challenge has narrowed. - Aarhus Convention — Ireland's binding international commitment that environmental justice must not be "prohibitively expensive" and that the public has rights of access to information, participation and justice. The yardstick the 2024 Act is measured against. - Regulation of Lobbying Act 2015, as amended in 2023 — the public lobbying register (lobbying.ie) and the one-year cooling-off period, now with sanctions. How to check who is lobbying whom. - Protected Disclosures Act 2014, as amended 2022 — whistleblower protection (see below). - GDPR, Articles 4(5) and the pseudonymised/anonymised distinction — the basis of the anonymity question in Part 3.

3. Check the lobbying register yourself. At lobbying.ie you can search, free, for who has been lobbying which public officials about what — including Meta's own Irish lobbying returns (search "Meta Platforms Ireland"). It is one of the few genuinely open windows onto the process, and the Meta example in this document is drawn from exactly this kind of public record: the Irish register, plus the EU Transparency Register (transparency-register.europa.eu) and the independent tracker LobbyFacts.eu, where you can see Meta's ~€10m-a-year EU lobbying for yourself. If you want to understand who is shaping a decision, start here. Use it.

4. If you work inside this and you see wrongdoing: you have protection. Under the Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (as amended 2022), a worker — broadly defined, including contractors, agency staff, board members and more — who discloses information they reasonably believe shows wrongdoing is legally protected from dismissal and penalisation. Crucially, "relevant wrongdoing" explicitly includes damage to the environment and the concealment or destruction of information about wrongdoing. You can disclose internally, to a prescribed regulator, or externally to the Office of the Protected Disclosures Commissioner (within the Ombudsman's office). Your identity is protected in law. Free, confidential advice is available from Transparency International Ireland's "Speak Up" helpline: 1800 844 866, and free legal advice from the Transparency Legal Advice Centre. You do not have to be certain wrongdoing occurred — only reasonably believe it, on reasonable grounds.

Sources for this submission toolkit

  • Consultation details, three pillars, deadline, FOI: gov.ie, "Public consultation on the draft National Cyber Security Strategy" (Dept of Justice, Home Affairs and Migration, published 9 July 2026; closes 21 August 2026, 12pm).
  • Data-centre numbers, EPA licensing, Dublin clustering, electricity share and projections: Oireachtas Library & Research Service, "The future of data centres in Ireland" (2025); CRU decision CRU/2025236 (12 December 2025); CSO; EirGrid ten-year forecasts.
  • 2025 National Cyber Risk Assessment risk ratings: reported via IISF summary; primary document linked on the gov.ie consultation page (host blocks automated access — confirm exact wording before quoting).
  • CRU 80% renewable rule, six-year glide path, additionality: CRU/2025236 (December 2025); Arthur Cox, Pinsent Masons, re24.energy analyses (December 2025). Lack of CRU enforcement powers; deferral to regulators; €1bn emergency gas; ~80% fossil energy (Paul Deane, UCC): TechPolicy.Press, "What Ireland's Data Center Crisis Means for the EU's AI Sovereignty Plans" (December 2025). PPAs untracked nationally: Oireachtas L&RS (2025).
  • Water/abstraction/thermal-discharge non-disclosure: EPA and Uisce Éireann positions as reported by the Dublin Inquirer (2026), as sourced in the main booklet.
  • Planning and Development Act 2024 judicial-review reforms and criticism: gov.ie "Key Policy Reforms"; Mason Hayes Curran; Philip Lee; Irish Legal News, "Beware of the judicial review red herring" (November 2025); Law Society Gazette (November 2025); Oireachtas L&RS briefing (January 2026). 3–5% challenge rate; live-case figures: Alison Hough BL and Mr Justice Humphreys, Law Society conference (November 2025).
  • Corporation-tax concentration (88% foreign MNCs; 57% from 10 firms): CSO/Revenue via TechPolicy.Press (December 2025); Central Bank of Ireland.
  • Pseudonymisation/anonymisation, recipient-relative anonymity, data reuse: Freshfields and Pinsent Masons analyses of the EU General Court ruling (26 April 2026, SRB case); DPO Centre; Article 29 Working Party; European Health Data Space materials.
  • Estonia — X-Road, once-only principle, G-Cloud, data embassy, e-Governance Academy, 2007 attacks: e-Estonia; Nortal; Future Shift Labs; LOTI; Privacy International; Google Cloud (data embassies). Caveats (private e-ID operation; replicability critiques): Privacy International; policyreview.info.
  • Regulation of Lobbying Act 2015 and 2023 Amendment (cooling-off, sanctions, DPOs, register): lobbying.ie; SIPO; Arthur Cox; McCann FitzGerald; Open Government Partnership.
  • Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (as amended 2022): Citizens Information; Workplace Relations Commission; gov.ie; Transparency International Ireland.
  • Meta worked example — camera-roll cloud processing: The Verge and TechCrunch reporting (2025–2026). Sarah Wynn-Williams / Careless People and the arbitration gag: AWO Agency, Associated Press, The Guardian, KQED, Wall Street Journal (2025–2026), with Meta's response stated as reported and her allegations treated as contested. Meta EU lobbying (~€10m/year; ~€151m sector; lobbyists outnumbering MEPs): Corporate Europe Observatory and LobbyControl analysis of the EU Transparency Register (October 2025); LobbyFacts. DigitalEurope data-centre confidentiality lobbying: Investigate Europe / The Journal Investigates (April 2026). Irish DPC appointment and cooling-off concern: Irish Times (17 September 2025), Irish Examiner (24 October 2025), and the Irish Council for Civil Liberties complaint to the European Commission (October 2025); lobbying.ie. No individual is named; the concern is process and the appearance of independence.

This document names no living individual. Every factual claim is traced to a named public source; every concern is flagged as a concern rather than a proven fact. Corrections welcome — accuracy is the point.

FOI requests — templates

Each of these is a specific, answerable question put to the body that holds the record. Filing them is itself the campaign — the answers, or the refusals, become the evidence.

FOI to DETE (foi@enterprise.gov.ie): three questions worth putting on the record.

  • “On what date, by whom, and on what stated grounds did the AI Advisory Council cease to operate? Request all correspondence, minutes and submissions relating to the decision, including any role of the relevant Minister of State.”

  • “What action has been taken on each recommendation of the Oireachtas Joint Committee on AI’s interim reports — specifically (a) independence of the National AI Office from DETE, (b) a Citizens’ Assembly on AI, and (c) placing the AI Advisory Council on a permanent statutory footing?”

  • “What post-employment notifications have been made by former DETE officials involved in data-centre policy to SIPO in the last five years?”

FOI to the Department of Justice: “What consideration was given to the AI Advisory Council’s February 2025 advice paper classifying facial-recognition technology as high-risk before the Garda Recording Devices Bill was advanced? Request the analysis relied upon.”

FOI to the CRU: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by CRU board members and senior officials in relation to CRU/2025/236? What post-employment notifications have been made to SIPO by former CRU officials in the last five years?”

AIE requests (Access to Information on the Environment)

  • To EirGrid: the DC1 energisation date (filed — chase if no reply within four weeks).

  • To Cork County Council: the environmental assessment for the March 2025 EPark extension.

  • To the EPA: what environmental assessment was conducted for EPark’s water allocation?

  • To your local authority: what environmental assessments were done? Was an Appropriate Assessment carried out? What water and thermal conditions were attached?

Legal routes — in order of readiness

  • Judicial review of the CRU grandfather clause (CRU/2025/236) — contact Friends of the Earth Ireland on viability and funding.

  • Aarhus Compliance Committee complaint (Geneva) — AIE blockages and the secrecy clause.

  • European Commission complaint under the Habitats Directive — Cork Harbour Natura 2000, absence of Appropriate Assessment.

  • European Parliament petition — Ireland’s missed transposition deadlines for Directive 2023/1791.

A grid connection can be revoked or suspended if it was granted under a flawed regulatory process. The fight is not to demolish a building — it is to challenge the legal basis of the connection itself.

Stopping the Next One: An Escalation Toolkit

Most objections die at the local level because they stay local. The tools below sit above that level — slower, but harder for a developer to out-lawyer with a single planning appeal.

The OECD National Contact Point (Ireland). Any person or group with a legitimate interest can file a "specific instance" complaint against a multinational enterprise operating in Ireland for failing to meet the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises — covering environmental disclosure, human rights due diligence, and meaningful consultation. Non-judicial, non-binding, but every case is logged publicly and the NCP issues a final statement that carries real reputational weight. Send to: oecdncp@enterprise.gov.ie, National Contact Point for OECD MNE Guidelines, Trade Division, DETE, 23 Kildare Street, Dublin 2.

The Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee. Any member of the public can submit a "communication" arguing Ireland has failed to guarantee access to environmental information, meaningful public participation, or access to justice — exactly the ground the fast-track planning and short consultation windows sit on. Ireland has been subject to the ACCC since 2013. Strict format: max 6,000 words, numbered paragraphs. Send to: aarhus.compliance@un.org.

Breach of EU Law complaint to the European Commission. Any person can lodge a complaint that a Member State's law or practice breaches EU law — including the Habitats Directive and Water Framework Directive grounds already documented in this booklet. No lawyer required. Can trigger a formal infringement procedure. Submitted via the Commission's online complaint form, europa.eu.

The domestic Aarhus route. Separately from the UN complaint above, Ireland's own transposing legislation means a judge must take Aarhus compliance into account in relevant judicial review cases — an Aarhus argument can be raised inside a domestic case, not only sent to Geneva.

OECD Watch (oecdwatch.org) — an NGO network that helps communities prepare NCP complaints, including cases spanning more than one country's contact point at once.

Judicial review of An Coimisiún Pleanála decisions. Any decision — data centre, apartment block, any large-scale development — can be challenged in the High Court, but the window is short and strictly enforced: leave to apply must be sought within eight weeks of the decision under Section 50 of the Planning and Development Act 2000. Where the development required an Environmental Impact Assessment, a judicial review that includes even one ground relating to the EIA or Habitats Directives qualifies for Aarhus costs protection under Section 50B — an unsuccessful applicant is shielded from paying the other side's costs, a protection the Supreme Court confirmed applies broadly to all grounds raised, not just the environmental one, in Heather Hill v An Bord Pleanála. This route requires a solicitor and is not a do-it-yourself template; a "letter before action" to the planning authority is the recommended first step, sent as early as possible within the eight-week window.

A caveat worth knowing before relying on this route: judicial review is being deliberately weakened, in stages, right now. In November 2025, government agreed to extend certain Planning and Development Act 2024 reforms backward, so they apply even to decisions made under the older 2000 Act framework above. The most significant change is remittal: where a court previously would quash a flawed permission outright, it can now send the decision back to fix only the specific procedural defect found, while the permission itself survives. Winning a judicial review, under this regime, more often delays and patches a decision than kills it. A further, separate Civil Reform Bill — its general scheme published January 2026, and criticised by both the Law Society and the Council of the Bar of Ireland — proposes making the remedy harder to obtain at all, by building "public interest" balancing directly into whether a court grants relief. Given the government's own stated priority of accelerating infrastructure delivery, that balancing test could weigh "this development is needed" against a genuine legal flaw in how it was approved. None of this closes the judicial review route. It does mean a solicitor should be asked, specifically, what remittal would mean for the case at hand — a "win" may no longer mean what it used to.

The mechanism, laid out in the order it actually happened. No single one of these changes reads as dramatic in isolation, which is exactly how this kind of erosion works. Laid end to end, the direction is unmistakable:

  • 2021 — the CRU blocks new data-centre grid connections in greater Dublin, citing security of supply. This is the one real brake in the whole sequence.
  • December 2025 — the CRU's new Large Energy User connection policy reopens a formal pathway back onto the grid, reframed as an environmental condition (80% self-sourced renewables) rather than what it also is: the mechanism that undoes the 2021 block.
  • January 2026 — LEAP, the government's 17-point plan, ends the Dublin moratorium outright and introduces "plan-led" regional siting; industry calls it, in its own words, the thing that "ends the moratorium."
  • 2026 — private wires legislation lets data centres build direct connections to power sources, moving them out of the same visible public queue as housing.
  • November 2025 — judicial review reforms are extended backward to cover decisions made under the old planning law, introducing remittal.
  • January 2026 — the Civil Reform Bill's general scheme proposes narrowing judicial review's availability further still.

Set against that sequence: the EU Environmental Crime Directive, reaching full force in May 2026, is the one clearly protective mechanism moving in the opposite direction — reaching officials and board members directly for unlawful permits, regardless of whether a civil judicial review would succeed under the weakened regime above. Both sequences are real, moving at the same time, in the same country. Which one gets resourced and used is not yet decided — and that is exactly where organised public pressure still has somewhere to land. A structured, sourced version of this exact sequence — built for mapping the connections between each mechanism — is maintained as a companion resource to this booklet, importable into Kumu.io, so the chain can be explored visually rather than only read in prose.

The Compulsory Purchase Order route. Where a site's public importance outweighs a private developer's claim on it — cultural, historical, or in cases of unresolved human remains — a local authority or central government can compulsorily acquire land, including land already granted planning permission. A council motion is the starting mechanism: councillors can vote to instruct officials to pursue a CPO with central government, as happened at Bessborough. This is a political pressure point, not a legal filing — it moves when TDs and ministers are named directly and asked publicly where they stand.

A note on precedent: environmental harm has been recognised as an Article 8 (right to private and family life) violation by the European Court of Human Rights in genuine cases — but claims citing a case called "Smith v UK (2024)" in circulation online are false; no such case exists. Use the principle, never that citation.

The legal grounds — case law worth knowing

None of what follows is legal advice, and every route here needs a solicitor before it becomes a filing. But knowing the actual shape of the law — not an inflated version of it — is what lets a campaign ask the right question of the right lawyer, rather than walking in with a claim that collapses on first contact. Held to that standard, here is the real legal ground this campaign can stand on.

The Habitats Directive "no reasonable scientific doubt" test. Under Article 6(3) of the EU Habitats Directive, a plan or project likely to affect a protected site can only be approved if the competent authority is certain, with no reasonable scientific doubt remaining, that it won't adversely affect the site's integrity. The foundational EU case is Waddenzee (C-127/02).
