In Ireland, the cases actually being applied are People Over Wind (C-323/17) and Sweetman (C-258/11) — both narrow the room for approving a project on the basis that mitigation measures will probably work; People Over Wind in particular held that you cannot take proposed mitigation into account at the screening stage at all, only at the full assessment stage. This is a real, live ground for challenging a data-centre permission near a Natura 2000 site with no Appropriate Assessment — not a guarantee of the outcome, but a genuine legal question with genuine teeth.

Kelly v An Bord Pleanála [2014] IEHC 400. A real, citable Irish High Court case (Finlay Geoghegan J) on the appropriate-assessment screening test — useful as a precedent for how that screening should actually be conducted. It is not a case that guarantees an injunction or a "total stay" on the strength of a sliver of doubt; Irish courts generally apply the deferential O'Keeffe standard to planning decisions, meaning a decision will usually stand unless it's genuinely irrational. Use Kelly as a live ground to argue from, never as a promised result.

Schrems II (C-311/18). The real, landmark EU case on transatlantic data transfers, driven by concerns about US surveillance law (the CLOUD Act, FISA 702) reaching data physically stored in the EU. It is directly relevant to any Irish-hosted data centre handling EU citizens' data under US corporate control — but "this freezes all metro or health data transfers" is an overstatement of what the case actually does. It's a basis for a genuine challenge to inadequate data-transfer safeguards, not an automatic freeze.

The EU AI Act, Article 27 — the Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment. Real, current law: deployers of high-risk AI systems (which now includes some Garda and public-sector uses under discussion elsewhere in this booklet) must complete a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment before deployment. This is a concrete, citable requirement to ask about directly in an FOI — "was the Article 27 assessment completed, and can I see it?" is a clean, answerable question.

The AI Act's biometric provisions. Real, but narrower than sometimes claimed: the Act does not blanket-ban real-time remote biometric identification in public places — it restricts it, with law-enforcement exceptions. Useful ground, but overstating it as an outright ban is the kind of claim that gets a whole argument dismissed once someone checks.

A note on how to use all of this: every case above is real and citable. The discipline is in the gap between what a case actually held and what it would be satisfying to claim it held — and that gap is exactly where an opponent's lawyer will attack first. Bring the real version to your own solicitor; it's stronger than the inflated one, because it survives contact.

Check your local area — step by step

  • Search the planning portal for “data centre”, “data storage”, “digital infrastructure”, “technology campus” — last five to ten years.

  • Read the conditions — renewable-energy requirements, water-usage limits, thermal-outfall conditions, reporting obligations. If they are absent, that is the story.

  • Check EirGrid’s grid-connection information and cross-reference dates against December 2025 — anything before then may be grandfathered.

  • Use npws.ie to check for Natura 2000 proximity. Absence of an Appropriate Assessment is a legal vulnerability.

  • File an AIE request.

  • Search lobbying.ie and SIPO.ie for influence and revolving-door movements.

  • Check your regional wastewater strategy on consult.water.ie.

  • Become a citizen scientist — test the water. Record a baseline now. Log temperature upstream and downstream.

The noise nobody is measuring — run your own survey

The KPMG report the State paid €104,938 for does not mention noise once, anywhere in its 181 pages. That is not a small omission. Ireland's own EPA has called environmental noise the second-biggest environmental cause of health problems in the EU, with over a million people in Ireland exposed above WHO thresholds — and yet only 6 of 31 local authorities enforced noise well in 2022. The World Health Organization's Environmental Noise Guidelines for the European Region link environmental noise to annoyance, cardiovascular and metabolic effects, cognitive impairment, sleep disturbance and adverse birth outcomes; for children specifically, a WHO systematic review found moderate-quality evidence that noise exposure impairs reading comprehension and long-term memory. The European Environment Agency attributes roughly 12,000 premature deaths a year and 12,500 learning-impaired schoolchildren across Europe to environmental noise.

Why the official number misses it. Regulators and operators report noise as a single A-weighted, hour-averaged figure — dB(A) — a metric built around how we hear speech. It systematically under-weights the low-frequency hum that data-centre cooling fans, chillers and backup generators actually produce. A facility can read "compliant" on paper while nobody nearby can sleep, because low-frequency and tonal noise penetrate walls and make rooms resonate — the problem is often worse indoors and at night, which is exactly when and where a daytime, outdoor, A-weighted spot-check isn't looking.

What's already being reported elsewhere. In Northern Virginia, the world's densest data-centre corridor, measured levels of 40–59 dB on residential property — technically within local ordinances — still left residents unable to sleep or concentrate. In Granbury, Texas, dozens of residents near a data centre reported vertigo, nausea, high blood pressure, migraines and insomnia; a class action followed. Acoustics researchers note there isn't much dedicated research on data-centre noise specifically yet, and infrasound claims in particular remain the most speculative end of this — flagged here as needing further study, not asserted as settled fact. There is no Irish-specific evidence yet. That absence is not proof of safety. It's proof nobody has measured it here.

How to measure it yourself. A full field protocol is available at theripplerebellion.github.io — the short version:

  • Capture dB(A), dB(C) and unweighted (dB(Z)) at the same time and position; a large gap between dB(C) and dB(A) is the tell for low-frequency content a standard reading misses.
  • Always take a night-time reading (23:00–07:00, when complaints cluster) alongside a daytime comparison, and measure indoors as well as outdoors — bedrooms matter most.
  • A phone app can tell you where to point a real meter; it cannot substitute for one, because phone microphones roll off at exactly the low frequencies that matter here. A calibrated Class 1 or 2 meter, borrowed from a university acoustics department or hired through an Association of Acoustic Consultants of Ireland member, is what a planning objection or a Section 108 EPA Act application actually needs.
  • Measure only from public ground or your own property. Log everything — date, time, weather, distance, calibration — because incomplete metadata makes a reading unusable later.
  • Log every reading to the Ripple Register, and pair anything intended for formal use with an acoustician.

This is not about proving a specific illness from a sound-level reading — that overclaims what the data can say. It is about putting an actual, defensible number next to a report that spent 181 pages not mentioning the word at all.

Who to contact — Ireland’s environmental & fisheries bodies

  • Inland Fisheries Ireland (IFI) — 24/7 confidential hotline: 0818 34 74 24. Call FIRST to report any fish kill, pollution or habitat damage.

  • Local Authority (County Council) — first responder for pollution; holds planning files; handles discharge consents.

  • Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) — complaint form at epa.ie.

  • Uisce Éireann — subject of submissions and AIE requests.

  • National Parks & Wildlife Service (NPWS) — npws.ie for Natura 2000 maps; report wildlife or habitat harm.

  • An Coimisiún Pleanála — where major data-centre permissions are decided. Submissions possible.

  • OCEI (Office of the Commissioner for Environmental Information) — appeal here if an AIE is refused. Do not give up at the first “no”.

Who you can escalate to — external and international routes

When the domestic channels stall — and this booklet documents how often they do — the case can be taken outside them. What can be evidenced here is capture, opacity and possible breaches of environmental and information law — not criminal bribery — so the strongest routes are the environmental-rights and transparency bodies, brought a documented dossier rather than an accusation.

  • Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee (UNECE). For breaches of the public’s right to environmental information, participation and access to justice — exactly the rights eroded by confidentiality clauses and consultations timed to fail. Ireland is a party, and any member of the public can make a “communication.” Send: the documented instances of withheld information and curtailed participation.

  • European Commission (DG Environment). A citizen complaint that Ireland is breaching EU environmental law — the Water Framework Directive, the Habitats Directive, the EIA Directive, the Energy Efficiency Directive. Free and online. Send: the specific provision and the facts that breach it.

  • European Parliament — Committee on Petitions (PETI). Any EU resident can petition about how EU law is applied in their country; a petition can trigger hearings and questions to the Commission. Send: a short petition setting out the pattern.

  • UN Special Rapporteurs — on human rights and the environment, on toxics, and on the human right to water and sanitation. They accept submissions and can issue formal communications to the State. Send: the human-rights-and-environment framing of the harm.

  • OECD National Contact Point (in the Department of Enterprise). You can file a “specific instance” against a named multinational for breaching the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, which cover environment, disclosure and human rights. Send: the company, the Guideline, the conduct.

  • Transparency International Ireland. For the lobbying, revolving-door and regulatory-capture dimension — and, for anyone inside the system who needs to speak up safely, their confidential Speak Up helpline.

  • Investigative and legal allies. ClientEarth and An Taisce (environmental law and planning standing); Friends of the Earth (live judicial-review experience); Global Witness (Big Tech and resource extraction); and cross-border investigative networks such as OCCRP — to whom you bring the dossier, and who have the resources to dig further.

The rule for all of them is the rule of this whole booklet: bring documented breaches to the body equipped to act on them. Evidence, to the right venue — never an accusation in search of one.

A Word to Everyone on the Inside: The Case for Speaking Now

This section is for the person reading this who already knows something. A committee member who watched a decision get waved through without the checks it needed. A council officer who has seen a file altered. A junior engineer who signed off on a figure they weren't sure was true. A bank employee who has seen exactly which projects get financed without the questions being asked. This is written for you, at whatever scale you sit at — because the argument is the same whether the sum involved is a few thousand euro or several billion.

The information defence is closing, and it is closing on the record. For decades, the honest excuse for institutional silence was some version of "we didn't have the tools, the law wasn't clear, nobody could have known how bad this would get." That excuse gets weaker with every page of this booklet. The EU Environmental Crime Directive, criminalising environmental harm "comparable to ecocide" and the granting of any permit in manifest breach of the law behind it, is in force now and fully binding on every member state by May 2026. The European Court of Human Rights has already ruled, in Verein KlimaSeniorinnen Schweiz v. Switzerland, that states carry a positive legal duty under the Convention to act on environmental harm — not a moral suggestion, a decided legal obligation. Ireland's own Protected Disclosures Act, strengthened in 2022, now protects volunteers, board members, and unpaid committee members, not only employees. None of this is obscure or hidden in a footnote. It is published, dated, and binding. Anyone who chooses silence from this point forward is choosing it with full knowledge that the legal and moral architecture for speaking was there, and freely available, the whole time. That is not a small distinction. It is the difference between an excusable failure to see and a documented decision to look away.

Human rights and environmental obligations are not optional extras that a local decision can quietly override. EU law carries a real, settled doctrine — supremacy — meaning national and regional rules cannot lawfully be used to undercut obligations the state has already signed up to at EU or Convention level. A confidentiality clause in a local contract, a "commercially sensitive" stamp on a file, a regional planning authority's own internal culture — none of these are, in law, capable of overriding a citizen's right to a clean water supply, a habitable environment, or the access-to-information and access-to-justice guarantees of the Aarhus Convention. Where a regional or local rule conflicts with those higher obligations, the higher obligation is what actually governs — the local rule was never entitled to the final word it's often treated as having.

The legacy point is not rhetorical flourish. It is literally true, and worth sitting with rather than brushing past. Every one of the people named across this booklet's darker chapters — the KPMG partners, the regulators who marked their own homework, the officials who let a file move at exactly the speed required to look like process — made an individual, personal choice at some specific moment, in some specific room, to say nothing. History does not usually remember the meeting. It remembers the decision. The Scrap Saturday cancellation is remembered by name, decades later, precisely because someone made a choice about what RTÉ was and wasn't willing to broadcast, and that choice outlived the news cycle it happened in. Every reader of this book, at whatever scale their own knowledge sits, is one report away from being either the person history asks "how did nobody say anything" about, or the person who did.

Why this matters at scale, not just individually. One disclosure closes one file. A culture where speaking up is the normal, expected, protected response — from a school fundraiser committee to a regulator's boardroom — is the only mechanism that scales fast enough to matter against a genuine crisis moving at the speed this booklet has documented: water tables, grid capacity, farmland, childhoods, all being spent faster than any single investigation can keep pace with. Prevention, not correction after the fact, is the only version of this that actually protects what's left. That requires many small, individually unremarkable acts of honesty, at every level simultaneously, not one dramatic exposure years after the damage is done.

How to do it with clean hands, at any scale:

  1. Get free, confidential advice before you do anything formal. Transparency International Ireland's Speak Up helpline exists specifically for this — to help you think through whether what you've seen clears the bar, and how to proceed, before you've committed to any specific step.
  2. Write it down, contemporaneously, before memory softens it. Dates, who said what, what documents exist. The strongest disclosure is the one where the record does the talking, not recollection alone.
  3. Report internally first, in writing, to the appropriate body — not necessarily the person whose decision you're questioning. A board, not just an individual within it.
  4. If internal channels don't act, escalate to the correct prescribed person — the Office of the Protected Disclosures Commissioner exists specifically to route you to the right one if you're unsure.
  5. You can start anonymous. If your identity later becomes known and you're penalised for it, the law still treats you as a protected discloser.
  6. Public disclosure — a book, the press, a public meeting — is the last resort, not the first move, and it carries full legal protection only once the channels above have been tried, or where you can reasonably show evidence would otherwise be concealed. This applies whether the wrongdoing is a fraudulent contractor at a school fundraiser or a boardroom decision worth hundreds of millions. The scale changes. The clean-hands sequence does not.

This isn't a call to recklessness. It's the opposite — a case for doing the single most protected, most legally armoured version of the thing you already know needs doing.

Part 6B — Constitutional Leverage: What the President Can Do, and What Citizens Can Ask

The Constitution does not dissolve at the request of a crowd. But it does contain tools that a crowd can make impossible to ignore.

What this chapter is about. This chapter looks at the formal levers that already exist within the State’s own architecture — what the President can do, and what citizens are entitled to ask — and how to use them. It is about finding the power that is already lawfully there.

“Let the people know the facts, and the country will be safe.” — Abraham Lincoln

What the President cannot do

Under Bunreacht na hÉireann, the President is not an executive president. The role is largely ceremonial. The President cannot dissolve the Dáil on their own initiative, cannot refuse a dissolution requested by a Taoiseach who still commands a majority, cannot veto legislation, and cannot act on public petition directly. Flooding TDs does not trigger any automatic constitutional mechanism. That is the honest answer.

What the President can do — Article 26

The President can refer a bill to the Supreme Court before signing it, to test its constitutionality. This is Article 26. If someone made a credible legal argument that the Planning and Development (Amendment) Act 2025, the Garda Recording Devices Bill, or the Environment (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2026 violated constitutional rights — and that argument reached the President publicly and formally — a referral is not impossible.

The catch: if the Supreme Court upholds the bill under Article 26, it can never be challenged again on those grounds. So Article 26 is a high-stakes move. It needs a strong legal case, not just political pressure.

The relevant constitutional rights to argue: the unenumerated right to a healthy environment (established in case law); the right to bodily integrity; the right to earn a livelihood (relevant where data-centre water extraction affects fishing communities); and Article 40.3 on the personal rights of citizens. None of these are certain arguments. All of them are legitimate ones to put before a court.

The Council of State — the mechanism to know

Before exercising any discretionary function, the President may convene the Council of State — an advisory body that includes the Taoiseach, Tánaiste, Chief Justice, and former presidents and taoisigh. Making the case publicly and loudly that legislation violates constitutional rights creates the conditions in which a President might convene the Council and then use Article 26.

This is not a guarantee. No President has ever refused to sign a bill after the Council of State met. But the Council meeting is itself a public signal — that constitutional concerns are being taken seriously at the highest level.

What actually works — the honest political answer

No dissolution clause is triggered by public pressure. But what flooding TDs does — what has always worked in Irish political history — is change the political calculation for individual TDs facing an election. A TD receiving hundreds of constituent letters on a specific issue must make a public choice: defend the policy or distance from it. That choice, made publicly and on the record, is the accountability mechanism.

The goal is not dissolution. The goal is a public record: which TDs supported mandatory water disclosure, which opposed a Citizens’ Assembly, which voted for the Garda Recording Devices Bill without judicial oversight. That record goes into the next election. The nitrogen fixers remember.

The European routes — often faster than domestic law

  • Aarhus Compliance Committee (Geneva) — where AIE blockages and the EU secrecy clause can be challenged. Ireland has been found non-compliant with the Aarhus Convention before.

  • European Commission complaint under the Habitats Directive — Cork Harbour Natura 2000, absence of Appropriate Assessment. The Commission can take infringement proceedings against Ireland.

The outside referees have already looked — and they agree with you

You do not have to imagine an independent authority examining Ireland; one already has. In February 2026 the UN Special Rapporteur on the human right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment, Astrid Puentes Riaño, conducted a ten-day official country visit to Ireland — meeting government, civil society and academics, and travelling to Galway and Cork, including a roundtable at UCC. Her end-of-visit findings read, in places, like this booklet rendered in the language of an international human-rights mandate. She said plainly that Ireland should not permit the building of more data centres without assessing their environmental effects — naming data centres specifically, alongside the Dublin Airport cap, LNG imports and a prolonged nitrates derogation, as decisions being taken without properly weighing the climate cost. She expressed concern at moves to restrict access to judicial review in environmental cases, and warned of a developing narrative in government and business circles in which environmental defenders are “stigmatised.” She underscored that access to justice is a human right under the Aarhus Convention. And she put the choice in a single sentence: rather than adopting “regressive amendments,” Ireland should “strengthen enforcement, monitoring capacity and meaningful access to information, public participation and access to justice.”

This matters for strategy, not just morale. It means the campaign’s central claims are not a local grievance to be waved away; they are the documented findings of an independent UN expert, headed for a full report to the UN Human Rights Council in March 2027. That is a fixed external reference point — one campaigners can cite, quote and build submissions around, and a yardstick against which every future Irish decision can be measured in public. Note, too, what she said about the law. She observed that while Ireland’s Supreme Court, in Friends of the Irish Environment v. Government, found no standalone constitutional right to a healthy environment, such a right is implicit in the rights to life and bodily integrity, and can be given force through statute, EU law and policy. And she flagged the hard number the State has itself now conceded — that the legally binding target of a 50% emissions cut by 2030 will not be met, with the responsible Minister putting the likely figure at around 22%.

So the “outside help” already has names and addresses. The instinct that Ireland’s regulatory gap is too important to be left to Ireland alone is a sound one — and the channels are real, not rhetorical. They are listed above, and worth setting out as a single escalation ladder: the European Commission (infringement proceedings under the Habitats Directive over Cork Harbour); the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee in Geneva (where Ireland has been found non-compliant before, on access to information and justice); and the UN Special Rapporteur herself, whose mandate explicitly invites civil-society input ahead of that 2027 report. The point is not to wait to be rescued. It is to feed the referees who are already on the pitch.

Sources: UN Special Rapporteur Astrid Puentes Riaño, official country visit to Ireland 9–20 February 2026 (Galway and Cork, including a UCC roundtable) — OHCHR press releases (4 and 20 February 2026); UCC Centre for Law & the Environment. End-of-visit findings (more data centres should not be permitted without environmental assessment; concern over restricting judicial review; environmental defenders "stigmatised"; access to justice as an Aarhus right; "strengthen enforcement… rather than regressive amendments") — The Irish Times (21 February 2026), OHCHR, JURIST and others, reported consistently. Friends of the Irish Environment v. Government of Ireland (no standalone constitutional right to a healthy environment; implicit in the rights to life and bodily integrity) — Supreme Court, as summarised by JURIST. 50% by 2030 emissions target to be missed, ~22% projected — Minister for Climate, reported February 2026. Full report due to the UN Human Rights Council in March 2027. The EU and Aarhus mechanisms are as set out earlier in this Part.

The Double Bind: the same years the toolkit was built, the toolkit was narrowed

This booklet gives you tools — FOI templates, AIE requests, judicial review pathways, the levers this campaign has used throughout. It would be dishonest not to say plainly, in the same breath, that the years in which data centre pressure has intensified are the same years in which the legal room to object to it has been quietly, deliberately narrowed. Not as a conspiracy — as a documented legislative choice, made in public, defensible line by line, that adds up to something citizens experience as a closing door.

Standing narrows. Under the Planning and Development Act 2024, an ordinary objector must now show "sufficient interest" — broadly, that they are personally and materially affected — to bring a judicial review at all. Long-established environmental NGOs retain a wider right to challenge on the environment's behalf, provided they can show a year's track record and a qualifying constitution. A newly formed local group, or a citizen affected by something not yet legally recognised as "material" — a lost view, a threatened water table, a compromised heritage site — may not clear that bar.

The clock no longer runs out in your favour. Previously, tying a project up in court for years could see its planning permission expire naturally. The 2024 Act now pauses the permission's lifespan for the duration of any judicial review, so a developer who loses time in court simply gets that time back afterwards. The old "delay it to death" strategy — imperfect, but real — no longer works.

The cost of being right no longer covers the cost of proving it. In May 2026, the Government signed the Planning and Development (Costs of Part 9 Judicial Review Proceedings) Regulations 2026 (S.I. No. 200 of 2026), introducing a fixed scale of legal fees for environmental judicial reviews. The Bar of Ireland and the Law Society of Ireland both formally objected, warning in their submissions that the scale caps recoverable costs "well below the real cost" of bringing these cases — meaning a citizen group can win a case, prove a decision unlawful, and still be left financially worse off than before they started. The Bar of Ireland went further: it warned the reform would "effectively destroy" the "no foal, no fee" model that has made public-interest environmental litigation possible in Ireland at all, and noted that the UK's near-identical costs-cap regime — the explicit template for Ireland's — is currently the subject of a live non-compliance investigation by the Aarhus Convention's own Compliance Committee.

None of this had to be justified as anti-citizen, because none of it was framed that way. Each change was presented as efficiency, cost predictability, or fairness to the taxpayer. Each, examined individually, has a defensible rationale. Added together, in the years data centre expansion accelerated fastest, they form a single direction of travel: harder to qualify to object, harder to outlast a developer's timeline, and financially riskier to be right. The UN Special Rapporteur's own words, quoted above, are the plainest official statement of the same concern: "regressive amendments" narrowing "meaningful access to information, public participation and access to justice," at the exact moment those tools are needed most.

This is not a reason to put the toolkit down. It is a reason to use it with open eyes — to know, before you begin, that the path has been made narrower on purpose, that winning may cost you regardless, and that the same discipline this booklet has argued for throughout — good faith, precision, documentation, patience — is what still gets through a door that has been made deliberately harder to open.

Sources: Planning and Development Act 2024, Part 9 (gov.ie, Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage, "Key Policy Reforms"); Planning and Development Act 2024 (Commencement) (No. 3) Order 2025 (S.I. No. 379 of 2025); Planning and Development (Costs of Part 9 Judicial Review Proceedings) Regulations 2026 (S.I. No. 200 of 2026), signed by Ministers Darragh O'Brien and Jack Chambers, May 2026; The Bar of Ireland, submission to the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment on the proposed Scale of Fees, January 2026; Law Society of Ireland Gazette, "Judicial review at centre of infrastructure plan," December 2025; Oireachtas Library & Research Service briefing, "Proposed legislation affecting judicial review of planning decisions," January 2026.

The law already has a word for the worst of it: ecocide

On the language of “warfare.” It is tempting, when a country’s water, grid and land are taken without its consent, to reach for the language of war — invasion, occupation, a crime against the nation. The instinct is worth examining rather than simply indulging. Call an unassessed data centre a “war crime” and you hand every critic the easiest possible dismissal; the real argument then dies with the exaggeration. So treat “warfare” as a question, not a verdict: is the patient, lawful capture of a nation’s strategic resources a genuinely new kind of contest over sovereignty? Perhaps — but the honest place to put that question is on the page as a question, while the actionable version of the same instinct already exists in hard law, and it has a precise name: ecocide.

What actually exists. In April 2024 the European Union adopted a revised Environmental Crime Directive — Directive (EU) 2024/1203 — the first time an international body has criminalised the gravest environmental destruction. It does not use the word “ecocide” in its operative text, but its own recitals describe the new “qualified offences” as conduct “comparable to ecocide”: causing the destruction of, or widespread and substantial damage that is irreversible or long-lasting to, an ecosystem of considerable size or value, or a habitat within a protected site. The penalties are not symbolic. Member states must provide custodial sentences of up to at least ten years for individuals for these qualified offences, and fines of at least 3% of worldwide annual turnover for companies — with liability reaching, where the conditions are met, to company directors. Belgium and France have already legislated their own national ecocide offences.

Why this is Ireland’s business, now. Two things make this directly relevant rather than a distant abstraction. First, every member state — Ireland included — was required to transpose the directive into national law by 21 May 2026, which places it on the very same list of EU deadlines that, as Part 1 documents, Ireland has a habit of missing. Whether, when and how robustly Ireland transposes it is a fair and pressing subject for an AIE request and a parliamentary question alike. Second, the directive’s own threshold language — “a habitat within a protected site” — describes, almost word for word, what Cork Harbour is: a Natura 2000 Special Protection Area and Special Area of Conservation. To be exact, because the standard of this booklet demands it: this is not a claim that any existing Irish facility constitutes ecocide. That is a high bar, set high on purpose, and it would have to be proved on evidence. It is the narrower, sturdier point — the legal tool for treating catastrophic, irreversible harm to a protected ecosystem as a serious crime now exists in European law; Ireland is obliged to bring it into Irish law; and a citizen is entitled to ask exactly how the State intends to do so, before the harm and not after.

*Sources: Directive (EU) 2024/1203 (revised Environmental Crime Directive), adopted 11 April 2024, in force 20 May 2024, transposition deadline 21 May 2026 — Official Journal of the EU; legal analyses by Linklaters, Peters & Peters and eucrim (2024). The “comparable to ecocide” language appears in the Directive’s recitals (e.g. Recital 21). Penalty levels (up to at least ten years for individuals for qualified offences; corporate fines of at least 3% of worldwide turnover; liability extending to directors) — as above. European Parliament vote 499–100–23 (February 2024). National ecocide provisions in Belgium and France — as reported. Stop Ecocide International’s 2021 definition. Cork Harbour Natura 2000 (SPA/SAC) status — as cited elsewhere in this booklet. This section asserts the existence of the legal instrument and Ireland’s transposition obligation; it explicitly does not assert that any named facility meets the ecocide threshold.

  • European Parliament petition — Ireland missed multiple transposition deadlines for EU Directive 2023/1791 on energy-efficiency reporting. Petitions are public, searchable and embarrassing.

  • Court of Justice of the EU — the ultimate route if Commission proceedings are opened. Ireland has been found in breach of EU environmental law before and paid the consequences.

The European routes are not faster in absolute terms — but they operate outside the Irish political system, which means they cannot be quietly shelved by a minister. Once a Commission complaint is filed, it has its own momentum.

Part 7 — Schools, Youth & All Citizens: Mobilise

History shows we can mobilise entire nations. The capacity for collective action hasn’t gone anywhere.

What this chapter is about. This chapter is the call to organise — in schools, among young people, and across the wider public. It is about turning understanding into a movement, and includes the demand that the new AI Office be made genuinely independent of the department that promotes the industry.

“Education is the most powerful weapon which you can use to change the world.” — Nelson Mandela

The central demand: a permanent Citizens’ Assembly

The Oireachtas Joint Committee on Artificial Intelligence has now produced more than one report — its First Interim Report (16 December 2025, 85 recommendations) and its Second Interim Report (9 June 2026, 39 recommendations). Across them, two recommendations recur: that Ireland’s AI oversight be genuinely independent of DETE — the June report goes so far as to recommend the National AI Office be pulled out of the Department of Enterprise entirely and made an independent State agency with its own budget — and that the Government establish a Citizens’ Assembly on Artificial Intelligence, Digitalisation and Technology. Both reports. Both went unimplemented.

“That men do not learn very much from the lessons of history is the most important of all the lessons that history has to teach.” — Aldous Huxley

There is a working model to point to. Ostbelgien — the German-speaking Community of Belgium — became the first region in the world with a permanent, law-mandated Citizens’ Council that sets the agenda and convenes Citizens’ Assemblies, with systematic follow-up so recommendations cannot simply be shelved. Belgium made deliberation permanent; Ireland — which pioneered so much of the method — still convenes its assemblies ad hoc, one issue at a time, at the government’s discretion.

The ask: take what already works — ours and Ostbelgien’s — and make it permanent. A standing Citizens’ Assembly with real teeth: agenda-setting power, mandatory follow-up, independence from DETE, and youth representation built in.

The bigger demand: a permanent National Assembly on Strategic Resources

What we are calling for is a permanent National Assembly on Strategic Resources — a standing, independent citizens’ body with a remit over the commons: land and foreshore, water, grid and energy capacity, and health and personal data. Not a one-off that reports and dissolves, but an ongoing democratic check with real powers of scrutiny, disclosure and consent over how Ireland’s strategic resources are allocated, and to whom.

Its core powers should include: mandatory disclosure from any operator seeking a strategic resource; a community-and-public-benefit-first test on strategic land and major grid or water connections; binding consultation before the disposal of State and harbour assets; and oversight of how citizens’ health and personal data may be used, and by whom.

Demand independence for the new AI Office

The Government’s planned National AI Office is set to be housed inside DETE — the same department whose job is to promote enterprise and attract the very industry the Office is meant to oversee. The Oireachtas Committee flagged the conflict and recommended structural independence. The demand is simple: an overseer housed inside the cheerleader’s office is not an overseer.

The €2 billion question — a warning from across the water

Ireland’s National Training Fund is built entirely from a 1% levy on employers — the small businesses, the trades, the farmers, the people who can least spare it. By the end of 2024 it had built up a surplus of roughly €1.8 billion, sitting unspent. Then it was unlocked: the National Training Fund (Amendment) Act 2025 was approved by Cabinet, passed through the Oireachtas, and signed into law on 23 December 2025. There was no meaningful consultation with the levy-payers whose money it is. The SME body ISME said exactly that — that small firms were not consulted, and that a fund built from their levy was being redirected over their heads, in its words because “SMEs are not paying a levy to provide funding support for universities.”

And follow where it went. The bulk of the €1.485 billion package was steered into the higher-education and university sector. Sitting alongside it is a €4.55 billion Tertiary Capital Plan for 2026–2030, of which more than €2.45 billion goes to research and innovation — and the named flagship priorities are artificial intelligence, semiconductors, high-performance computing and deep-tech: a new national high-performance computing system, a major expansion of the Tyndall National Institute for semiconductor research, and a €460 million network of research centres with dedicated AI and semiconductor centres, built in partnership with more than a hundred multinational corporations. None of this is hidden; it is announced in press releases, in the language of talent and opportunity. But say plainly what the branding obscures, because it is the whole pattern in one place: the levy taken from the squeezed — the SME, the tradesperson, the farmer — is being spent, without their consent, to build the upstream research layer of the very AI, chip and compute economy that is drawing down their water and their grid. The money paid by those being extracted from is feeding the system doing the extracting.

That is the opposite of diversification, and diversification is exactly what an economy this exposed needs. A fund this size, paid by the indigenous base, should be widening that base — into the trades and apprenticeships the country is genuinely short of, into water and retrofit and care work, into the green economy, and above all into restoration. Instead it deepens the single dependency the rest of this booklet warns about. The levy-payers were not asked. They should have been. And it is not too late to ask, because the plan runs to 2030 and every future tranche is decided, in the open, in the annual Budget — which is precisely where this can still be turned.

In June 2025, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer unveiled a flagship education package — “TechFirst” — £187 million to bring AI learning to a million secondary-school students, delivered in partnership with Google, Microsoft and Amazon. The branding was education and opportunity. Measured against Britain’s £116 billion annual education budget, £187 million is roughly 0.16% of education spending — nearly all of it routed through AI companies. Critics described AI “squatting over” the government, with ministers repeating that it will deliver growth like a mantra.

This is exactly what Irish people do not want for the €2 billion National Training Fund. A fund branded as opportunity for young people should not become a subsidy pipeline for AI infrastructure and US tech partnerships. Instead, redirect it toward:

  • Water-infrastructure engineers and hydrologists — fix the pipes before the harbours are overwhelmed.

  • Environmental scientists and marine biologists — monitor and protect Cork Harbour, the Shannon, every Natura 2000 site.

  • Health-informatics specialists — under public ownership, with data sovereignty protected.

  • Offshore renewable supply chains — Irish graduates, Irish IP, Irish conditionality on every procurement contract (the Norway and Denmark model).

  • Climate-resilience planning — flood engineers, planners and ecologists building for the Ireland of 2040.

  • River restocking and habitat restoration — fund the restocking of salmon and trout on the Blackwater, the Glyde, the Boyne and every river hit by fish kills, with the habitat repair that makes restocking last. The Boyne salmon run has fallen to roughly 10% of its conservation threshold. Restocking without habitat repair is pouring fish into a broken system.

  • A national citizen-science water-monitoring programme — training ordinary people to take credible, dated, methodologically sound readings, and specifically to log water temperature upstream and downstream of every outfall. Thermal discharge is the unmonitored data-centre impact and the single most useful, least-collected piece of evidence. Fund the equipment and the training, and build the independent baseline operators currently hide behind commercial confidentiality.

  • A standing independent regulatory-systems review — a permanent, structurally independent unit to audit the whole regulatory architecture as one connected system. Detailed in the next section.

And use it, above all, to rewild. The Fund’s own remit already names the green economy as a priority skills area — which makes the most obvious use of it the one nobody is funding at scale: training and paying people to restore the country itself. Peatland and cutaway-bog rehabilitation on the old Bord na Móna lands, native woodland and hedgerow planting, river and salmon-habitat repair, the National Nature Restoration Plan turned from a consultation document into actual jobs — this is skilled work, much of it work for the trades, and it roots people in the places they live instead of training them, once again, to feed an industry that banks its profits elsewhere. A training fund paid for by Irish employers should put Irish hands to the repair of Irish ground. That is the nitrogen fixer’s economy: not extraction retrained, but restoration made into a livelihood.

Sources: National Training Fund surplus (~€1.8bn at end-2024) and the €1.485bn unlocking — National Training Fund (Amendment) Act 2025 (signed 23 December 2025; commenced 19 January 2026); Department of Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science (DFHERIS); ESRI roadmap; Parliamentary Budget Office. ISME objection that SMEs were not consulted — ISME statements (2025). €4.55bn Tertiary Sector Capital Investment Plan 2026–2030 and the €2.45bn research allocation; new high-performance computing system (University of Galway / ICHEC); Tyndall National Institute semiconductor expansion; the €460m Rinn research network with dedicated AI and semiconductor centres and 100+ multinational partners — DFHERIS and Research Ireland (Taighde Éireann) press releases (Dec 2025–June 2026). The characterisation of this as “feeding the system that extracts” is offered as interpretation of the documented allocation, not as a claim of unlawful or secret conduct; the allocations are public.

Retrain the system, and the people who run it

Redirecting the National Training Fund is not only about training new people. It is about retraining the system itself — and the people inside it who have come to treat industry capture as normal.

Two things are needed. First, a standing independent regulatory-systems review: a permanent unit of systems analysts and pattern-spotters, recruited for independence and structurally insulated from the industries they examine, tasked with auditing the whole regulatory architecture as one connected system. The reason no single official stopped the five-Act sequence, the grandfather clause, the disbandment of the Advisory Council and the missed EU deadlines is that no one was tasked with seeing the whole board. Departments saw their own files. Nobody owned the pattern. This unit would.

Its independence cannot be a matter of good intentions — it must be structural: fixed, non-renewable terms; a legal bar on any post-employment route into the regulated industries; deliberately diverse professional and lived-experience backgrounds so that no single worldview dominates; and a statutory duty to publish its findings. The precedent already exists in Irish public life — the Comptroller and Auditor General audits public spending independently; the Irish Fiscal Advisory Council scrutinises budgetary policy independently. What is missing is an equivalent body for regulatory coherence and capture.

Second, and harder: government itself needs to relearn the value of genuinely independent advice. The AI Advisory Council included representatives of the very companies it was meant to scrutinise — and was then disbanded. An advisory body stacked with the regulated, or dissolved when inconvenient, is not advice. It is theatre. Real independence means advisors with security of tenure, diversity of background, no commercial stake in the outcome, and a remit that cannot be quietly withdrawn. The demand is that “independent” be made to mean what it says.

The leadership problem: how we choose, and how we could choose better

Behind every captured system is a selection problem. We promote confidence over competence, certainty over honesty, and people who perform authority over people who actually hold knowledge — and then act surprised when the systems they run fail in exactly the way they were built to.

Adam Grant’s research on organisational psychology shows that we systematically mistake the loudest, most self-assured voice for the wisest, and that the capacity to rethink — to change one’s mind in the face of evidence — is a leadership strength we treat as a weakness. Brené Brown’s work on daring leadership makes the companion point: real leadership requires the vulnerability to say “I was wrong,” “I don’t know,” “I need to look again” — the exact quality a captured system cannot tolerate, because admitting error means admitting the pattern. The armoured, never-wrong posture that regulatory capture runs on is not strength. It is the tell.

Systems do not fail because of monsters. They fail because most people do their narrow job well and never ask what it adds up to — what Hannah Arendt called the banality of evil: harm done not by the cruel but by the unthinking, the functionary who defers to protocol and never looks up from their own file. The planner who processed the extension as “a technical extension.” The official who saw only their own department. The advisor who gave industry-friendly advice because that was the brief. The regulator who let the transparency deadline pass because enforcing it was not specifically their job. None of them villains. Each did their narrow task correctly. The harm lived in the aggregate that no one was responsible for seeing.

Arendt’s antidote to the banality of evil was thinking — the refusal to stop at “that is not my department,” the insistence on looking up and seeing the whole. That is precisely the quality this campaign is built on, and precisely the quality our institutions select against. We promote the compliant over the questioning. We call the person who will not let a pattern go “difficult,” “obsessive,” “too much.” We reward those who perform the smooth administration of the system and quietly remove those who ask what the system is for.

Recognising the strengths we currently discard

There is a particular injustice buried in this — and a particular opportunity. The minds most likely to see the pattern, to refuse the comfortable narrative, to be unable to look away, are very often the minds that could never follow the conventional protocol in the first place. The neurodivergent. The ones who were “difficult” in school because they asked the question everyone else had been trained not to ask. The ones whose wiring makes them unable to un-see the connection between the cobalt and the child, the data centre and the fish kill, the missed deadline and the lobby group.

The same wiring that makes someone unable to comply with a protocol that makes no sense is the wiring that makes them able to spot the flaw in a system everyone else has normalised. We have been treating that as a defect to be managed. It is a civic strength we have been throwing away. The nitrogen fixers — Catherine Corless paying for the death certificates herself, Maurice McCabe refusing to let the pattern go, the pattern-spotters called obsessive right up until they were proven right — are disproportionately the people who did not, and could not, simply go along.

So the demand widens. A National Training Fund that genuinely served the future would not only train the engineers, scientists and health-informatics specialists the country needs. It would explicitly train people in pattern recognition and systems thinking — the literacy of seeing the whole board — and build assessment and recruitment that recognises the strengths of minds that do not follow conventional protocols, rather than filtering them out at the first standardised hurdle.

  • Fund pattern-recognition and systems-thinking as a taught literacy — in schools and in retraining, not as a niche skill but as core civic education for a world of connected, capture-prone systems.

  • Build recruitment that values neurodivergent strengths — pattern-spotting, refusal to normalise, the inability to look away — for the regulatory-review unit, the Citizens’ Assembly and the public service, instead of screening them out.

  • Recognise lived experience and a track record of being proven right while dismissed as a genuine qualification, alongside conventional credentials. The school of life teaches things the protocol cannot.

This is the inversion the whole campaign points toward. The system selects for the functionary and discards the pattern-spotter. Reverse it. Put the people who cannot stop thinking where the seeing is done — and fund the country to recognise, rather than scrub out, exactly the minds that break the chain.

The student letter

“I am a student at [school], [town]. I am writing to ask you to support a permanent Citizens’ Assembly on AI policy in Ireland — as recommended by the Oireachtas AI Committee in December 2025 and again in June 2026. Decisions about AI, data centres, water, energy and surveillance are being made now that will shape the country I inherit. I deserve a say. So does every young person in Ireland. Will you support this — yes or no?”

For teachers & school groups

  • Run a class exercise: find your nearest data centre on the planning portal and read its conditions.

  • Write a class letter to your local TD demanding the Citizens’ Assembly.

  • Map local large-energy infrastructure: who approved it, and when?

  • Invite a local environmental group: Futureproof Clare, CHASE, Ripple Rebellion.

The all-citizens letter

“I am writing as a citizen regarding three connected concerns: the expansion of unregulated data-centre infrastructure; the centralisation of health data without adequate sovereignty protections; and the Garda Recording Devices Bill removing judicial oversight from surveillance. I call on you to support: a permanent Citizens’ Assembly on AI and digital infrastructure; mandatory water and energy disclosure for all data centres; independent judicial oversight for all surveillance technologies; and a guarantee that Irish health data remains under Irish public control. I expect a written response. I will be sharing it publicly. [Name, Address, Constituency]”

Part 8 — Every County: Find Out What’s Planned Near You

How to read a planning file — a five-minute method. When you open an application on your council’s planning portal (or on An Coimisiún Pleanála’s site for an appeal), you do not need to read all of it. Go straight to these, in order:

  • The application form and description. Note the exact wording of the development described, and the named applicant. Write the applicant’s name down — you will check it later.

  • Who the applicant actually is. Search that name on the Companies Registration Office (core.cro.ie) and the beneficial-ownership register (rbo.gov.ie). If it is a company set up only recently, or one whose directors and owners trace elsewhere, that is not wrongdoing — but it tells you the real decision-maker may sit a layer back. Note it.

  • The site notice and newspaper notice dates. These set the clock. Submissions usually must be made within five weeks of the application being lodged, on payment of the fee. Miss the window and your objection cannot be considered — so check this first.

  • The engineering and environmental drawings, not the brochure. The glossy planning statement is the costume. The truth is in the technical documents: the Maximum Import Capacity (in MW or MVA — convert MVA to the smaller real MW figure), any substation or grid-connection detail, any reference to on-site generation, batteries, gas connection, water abstraction, or cooling. A large power or water figure is the project, whatever the building is called.

  • The Appropriate Assessment / EIA screening. If the site is near a Natura 2000 area (such as Cork Harbour), check whether a proper Appropriate Assessment was done or screened out — and on what basis. A screened-out assessment near a protected site is one of the most challengeable points there is.

  • The conditions, if granted. If permission is granted, the conditions are where the real limits (or their absence) live — and where you check whether enabling works have lawfully begun. Works started before a condition is met can be unauthorised development.

And the one that undoes campaigns: keep your own dated acknowledgement of every submission you make. An appeal can be ruled invalid over a single missing receipt. Procedural precision is not pedantry — it is the whole game.

This is not only a Cork story. Every county should know what is being built, connected and brought online on its own ground.

What this chapter is about. A practical, county-by-county orientation: how to find out what is planned near you, and how to act on it locally. The national pattern is fought one local map at a time.

“All politics is local.” — Tip O’Neill

Ireland hosts one of Europe’s largest data-centre clusters relative to its size. Data centres rose from about 5% of national electricity demand in 2015 to roughly 22% by 2024. Dublin data centres account for around half of regional electricity demand — and because the Dublin grid is constrained, the pressure is now pushing outward, toward any county with spare grid or water capacity.

If your county is not named in this booklet, that is not reassurance — it is the reason to check. The “case-by-case” rule means any area with capacity is a candidate.

Learn from Erin Brockovich — the citizen-map model

Erin Brockovich’s brockovichdatacenter.com — citizen-reported locations, photos and firsthand accounts — logged thousands of submissions from dozens of US states within weeks of launching. Her legal team is preparing civil actions built on the right to environmental information, aimed squarely at nullifying the confidentiality clauses that hide water and electricity use.

A citizen map of Irish data centres, county by county, is well within reach. The step-by-step in Part 6 is how to feed it. Thirty-two counties. One pattern, made visible.

What communities are already reporting

  • Water: millions of litres drawn for cooling, straining local supply.

  • Electricity bills: residential rates rising to fund the grid upgrades the centres require.

  • Noise: constant low hum from cooling systems and backup generators.

  • Grid and housing: capacity taken before homes can connect.

  • Air and health: diesel backup generators running during grid stress.

  • Consultation: communities told after the fact, not before — the recurring grievance everywhere this has happened.

If you are hearing any of these near you — write it down, date it, photograph what you can from public ground, and report it.

Part 9 — A Call to Those Inside the Industry

If you work in this industry and you know what is not being disclosed — the law protects you when you speak up.

What this chapter is about. This chapter speaks directly and without hostility to the people who work inside the industry — engineers, planners, professionals — who may see what is wrong and feel unable to say so. It is an invitation, not an accusation.

“Never be afraid to raise your voice for honesty and truth and compassion.” — William Faulkner

What the law protects

Under the Protected Disclosures Act 2014, as amended by the Protected Disclosures (Amendment) Act 2022, workers across the public, private and not-for-profit sectors are protected from dismissal or penalisation when they report wrongdoing that came to their attention in a work-related context and that they reasonably believe to be true. The 2022 Act expressly extended protection to breaches of EU law on the protection of the environment and on public procurement — the very areas this investigation concerns.

  • Broad coverage — protection extends across public, private and not-for-profit sectors.

  • Anonymous disclosures — if your identity later becomes known, you remain protected if you are penalised.

  • The burden flips — in any penalisation case, the law presumes the penalisation resulted from your disclosure. The employer must prove otherwise.

How to speak up safely

Transparency International Ireland — Speak Up Helpline: 1800 844 866. Free and confidential, with a secure report form, email, post and Signal contact. Get advice first. Understand your protections. Then decide. The law is on the side of the person who tells the truth.

A boycott from within

In 1880 the people of Mayo simply withdrew their labour and their cooperation from Captain Boycott. They did not riot. They did not break the law. They declined to participate in something they believed was wrong. The word “boycott” entered every language on earth because of it.

That same power belongs to the people inside this industry today — the engineers, electricians, planners, surveyors, hauliers, fit-out crews, consultants, civil servants and contractors whose hands actually build and run these data centres. Nothing rises from the ground without you.

You can boycott too — for the sake of the future. You can refuse a contract that has no water disclosure. You can decline to sign off on a project that skipped Appropriate Assessment. You can ask your union, your professional body, your firm to adopt a standard: we will not build extraction without reciprocity. The people who pour the concrete and pull the cable hold more power than they are ever told they have. That is the Boycott tradition, alive again, in the hands of the people building the thing itself.

The hardest option — and the strongest: collective refusal

Be honest about the scale of this one. Refusing a single contract, declining to sign off on a single project — that is something one person can do tomorrow, alone, and lawfully. What follows is bigger, and it would take years, not weeks: enough of the people whose hands actually raise these buildings choosing, together, not to.

It is the oldest lever there is, because it withholds the one thing the machine cannot manufacture for itself — willing hands. Nothing here pours its own concrete, pulls its own cable, signs its own compliance forms or stamps its own permission. Every stage runs on people who could, in principle, decline. “I was only following orders” stopped being a defence at Nuremberg, and the research that came after — Milgram’s obedience experiments, Christopher Browning’s Ordinary Men — showed how ordinary the machinery usually is, and how much of it survives only because each person assumes the responsibility sits one rung up. It rarely does. It sits with the hands.

So picture it: not a riot, not a stunt, but the slow, patient building of a position across the trades, the professional bodies and the unions, until “no extraction without reciprocity” is simply the standard of the sector. That is a mountain of organising — numbers, solidarity, and above all a way to make refusal survivable for the worker who cannot afford to lose the job. That last part is exactly what a People’s Sovereign Fund (Part 18) could underwrite: a hardship-and-solidarity fund for the people who say no.

Keep it lawful and keep it clean, because that is where its power lives. This is individual conscience and professional judgment — you may decline work, and you may refuse to certify what should not be certified — together with collective standard-setting through your union and your professional body, acting within the law. Not contracts broken; not a soul coerced. The Land League boycott terrified an empire precisely because it broke no law. It simply withdrew.

Is it hard? Enormously. Has it been done before, by people with far less than we have? Yes. And if it is possible, then we are obliged at least to try.

Part 10 — Allies, Media & Coalition

No one wins this alone. The allies, the journalists and the coalition already exist — this is where to find them.

What this chapter is about. This chapter maps the allies — the TDs, MEPs, unions, journalists and organisations already doing the work — and how to build the coalition that turns isolated objections into a force.

“If you want to go fast, go alone. If you want to go far, go together.” — African proverb

Political allies

  • Malcolm Byrne TD — chairs the Oireachtas AI Committee (the Citizens’ Assembly recommendation).

  • Lynn Boylan MEP — fighting the EU secrecy clause; send her the CRU documentation.

  • Jennifer Whitmore TD.

  • Professor Hannah Daly (UCC) — appeared before the AI Committee on 8 June 2026.

  • Professor Barry O’Sullivan (UCC) — was on the AI Advisory Council.

  • Nathan Quinlan (University of Galway).

  • Professor David Farrell (UCD) — research leader of Ireland’s Citizens’ Assembly and co-author of the standard design methodology for deliberative assemblies.

Coalition to build

  • CHASE (Cork Harbour Alliance for a Safe Environment) — existing community standing.

  • Local fishing communities along Cork Harbour — natural allies, not yet fully asked.

  • Futureproof Clare — six years of hard-won experience.

  • Friends of the Earth Ireland, An Taisce, ICCL, ICTU.

Media to target

  • RTÉ Investigates — the regulatory architecture and the missed EirGrid deadline is their format.

  • The Guardian environment desk — the Trevelyan’s Corn framing is internationally legible.

  • Karen Hao (The Atlantic) — already covering AI’s environmental impact.

  • Bloomberg Green — the financing angle.

Part 11 — Alternatives: Diversifying Our Internet, Measuring What Matters

Every monopoly depends on us all using the same few doors. Use different doors. And measure what comes through them.

The idea that a shared resource can be governed well without either privatising it or leaving it to a distant state has a Nobel Prize behind it. Elinor Ostrom's Governing the Commons (1990) overturned the assumption, dominant in economics for decades, that shared resources are inevitably degraded unless privately owned or centrally controlled — the so-called "tragedy of the commons." Ostrom's fieldwork, across real fisheries, forests and irrigation systems worldwide, found the opposite was often true: communities that governed a shared resource themselves, with clear boundaries, real accountability, and rules they had a hand in making, sustained it better than either a private owner or a distant regulator managed to. She won the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences for the work in 2009 — the first woman to do so. It is the clearest academic case that water, a grid, and an internet do not have to be owned by a handful of distant funds to be managed well, and it underwrites every alternative this chapter proposes.

What this chapter is about. This chapter is constructive: it sets out the alternatives — to a single extractive model of the internet, and to measuring a country’s worth by GDP alone. It asks what a healthier digital economy and a truer measure of national wellbeing would look like.

“Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted.” — attributed to William Bruce Cameron

The economics of measurement — why this is not a tech tip

Peter Drucker is widely credited with the management principle: “what gets measured gets managed.” The idea is older and deeper than the slogan. Lord Kelvin put it in 1883: “When you can measure what you are speaking about, and express it in numbers, you know something about it; but when you cannot measure it … your knowledge is of a meagre and unsatisfactory kind.”

Economics was built on this insight. National accounts — GDP, GNP, trade balances — were invented in the 1930s and 1940s precisely because governments realised they could not manage an economy they could not measure. Simon Kuznets, who designed the US national accounts in 1934, warned at the time that “the welfare of a nation can scarcely be inferred from a measurement of national income.” He was right. GDP measures output. It does not measure water quality, community cohesion, ecological health, or whether a harbour is being quietly depleted by undisclosed industrial abstraction.

The data centres are not in the national accounts in any meaningful way. Their water consumption is not measured. Their thermal discharge is not measured. Their grid draw is measured in aggregate but not attributed to individual sites. Their waste heat — the heat that could warm tens of thousands of Irish homes — is not measured because there is no obligation to capture it. You cannot manage what you do not measure. Ireland is not measuring the cost. It is only counting the receipts.

This is why the diversification argument is not really about switching apps. It is about who controls the measurement — and therefore who controls the management. When five US corporations control the dominant search engine, the dominant social graph, the dominant cloud infrastructure and the dominant AI training pipeline at once, they also control what gets counted, what gets optimised, and what gets left out of the ledger entirely. Diversification breaks that concentration. It is an economic act, not a lifestyle choice.

The origins of the internet — what was lost

The internet was not built by corporations. It was built by publicly funded researchers, universities, and the US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) in the 1960s and 1970s. The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989 and given to the world without patent or licence fee. He has said explicitly that he regrets what it became — a commercial surveillance infrastructure rather than the open, decentralised knowledge commons he designed.

The open-source movement — Linux, Apache, Mozilla, Python, Wikipedia — is the internet Berners-Lee imagined: built by shared human effort, owned by no one, improved by everyone. GitHub, now owned by Microsoft, hosts the majority of the world’s open-source code. That concentration is itself a risk — one company owning the infrastructure of the commons. Which is why the alternatives matter.

“The web is for everyone and collectively we hold the power to change it. It will not be easy. But for the sake of the web and humanity, we have no choice.” — Tim Berners-Lee, 2018

Diversifying your internet — the practical guide

Search. DuckDuckGo (no tracking, no personalisation bubble). Ecosia (Bing results, plants trees, renewable-powered servers, B Corp). Startpage (Google results without Google tracking, Dutch, EU data law). Kagi (paid, no ads, no tracking — the business model is your subscription, not your attention).

Social media. Bluesky (decentralised, open AT Protocol, you can move your identity and followers between servers). Mastodon (federated, open source, community-run servers, no algorithm, no ads, no venture capital).

Messaging. Signal (end-to-end encrypted, open source, non-profit, no ads, no data harvesting — the gold standard; use it for all campaign organising). Element/Matrix (decentralised, open protocol, self-hostable; if Signal is your phone call, Matrix is your office infrastructure).

Email. Proton Mail (Swiss, end-to-end encrypted, zero-knowledge, free tier, EU data law). Tutanota / Tuta (German, end-to-end encrypted, open source, 100% renewable-powered).

Cloud storage and documents. Nextcloud (open source, self-hostable; many Irish hosts offer it, so your files stay in Ireland on servers you control). CryptPad (encrypted collaborative docs, French research institute, no ads). Disroot (community-run suite — email, cloud, docs, social — donation-funded, Amsterdam).

Code and projects. GitHub (still the largest open-source host, but Microsoft-owned — use it, mind the concentration). Codeberg (non-profit, EU-based, Gitea-powered). Sourcehut (minimal, fast, open source, subscription-funded).

Browsers. Firefox (open source, non-profit Mozilla, strong privacy defaults — the only major browser not built by an advertising company or hardware maker). Brave (Chromium-based, built-in ad and tracker blocking). Tor Browser (for when anonymity matters — slower, but very hard to track).

Maps. OpenStreetMap (the Wikipedia of maps — community-built, open data, no tracking). Organic Maps (offline, OpenStreetMap-based, no tracking, open source).

Creative tools. GIMP (image editing), Inkscape (vector graphics), Kdenlive (video editing), Moho (hand-built animation, no generative AI and no AI water cost). CapCut is still useful for Reels — but it is owned by ByteDance and subject to Chinese data law, so use it for output, not for storing sensitive material.

Images and media. Creative Commons and Wikimedia Commons (millions of openly licensed, attributed images, audio and video). Unsplash and Pexels (free high-quality photos). Before generating an AI image that costs server water and energy, check here first — the human work already exists. Use it and credit it.

News and journalism. The same logic applies to who tells you what is happening, not only to what tools you use to find out. A newsroom funded by advertisers answers, ultimately, to advertisers; a state broadcaster answers to a government that can shape its budget. Reader-owned journalism answers to its readers, structurally, not just in mission-statement language. Switzerland's Republik is a working example rather than a theory: founded 2018 as a reader-funded cooperative (a Genossenschaft), no advertising, funded by member subscriptions, and — matching the transparency this booklet asks of every institution it examines — Republik publishes its own internal governance in public: how it handles confidential sources and whistleblowers, its own policy on AI use, even a report on the gender balance of its own writers. It broke the Palantir–Swiss Army story documented elsewhere in this booklet, survived a direct legal challenge from the company over it, and won. Waterford Whispers News is Ireland's own working example of independent media with real bite, in a different register — satirical, print-and-online, answering to neither an advertiser nor a broadcaster's caution. Neither model is perfect or a full substitute for a well-funded national press. Both prove the ownership structure of the newsroom is not a fixed fact of life — it is a choice, and it is one every country keeps making by default rather than on purpose.

A caution on sovereign AI, from someone who supports it. The instinct to build our own AI and infrastructure rather than depend entirely on two foreign superpowers is sound — Yoshua Bengio makes exactly this case, arguing that Europe and smaller democracies need a "third path" so the profits of automation flow back through their own tax base rather than out of the country entirely. But he adds a warning worth heeding: a sovereign model that is not actually competitive is wasted money. He cites Spain's own first national model, built partly to translate between Catalan and Basque, which cost roughly €150 million and performs worse than freely available commercial tools — and, because it is not competitive, is not used by the companies or citizens it was built for. The lesson is not "don't build sovereign capacity." It is "build it in genuine partnership, at genuine scale, aimed at genuine competitiveness — or don't spend the money at all." Symbolic sovereignty that nobody uses protects no one.

The measurement demand — what we are actually asking for

Behind all of these alternatives is one economic principle: you cannot improve what you do not measure, and you cannot measure what is deliberately hidden. The campaign’s demand for mandatory water disclosure, mandatory energy disclosure and mandatory waste-heat reporting is not a technical ask. It is an economic one — a demand that the true cost of the digital economy appear in the ledger, not just the receipts.

Ireland’s GNI* — the modified measure created specifically to strip out distortions from multinational activity — shows the real Irish economy is significantly smaller than headline GDP suggests. The data centres inflate the GDP numbers. The water they consume appears nowhere. The thermal discharge appears nowhere. The grid capacity they absorb — preventing housing and indigenous industry from connecting — appears as a cost nowhere. An economy that cannot measure its own externalities cannot manage them. An internet owned by five companies cannot serve the public interest of seven billion people. Both are the same problem: concentration without accountability, growth without measurement of cost. The nitrogen fixer’s job is to make the hidden costs visible — one AIE request, one switched default, one citizen-science water test at a time.

The richest poor country: the figure on the poster, and the one in your pocket

Two numbers for the same country. Ireland appears, in the world tables, as one of the richest countries on Earth — GDP per capita of roughly $113,000 in 2024, second in the EU only to Luxembourg and more than double the United Kingdom’s. It is a figure that flatters, and it is very nearly fiction. Ireland’s GDP is inflated by multinationals booking global profits here: intellectual-property depreciation, the retained earnings of redomiciled companies, and the fact that around nine in ten of the world’s leased commercial aircraft are owned in Ireland for tax reasons. The distortion is so large that Ireland’s own Central Statistics Office built a separate measure — Modified Gross National Income, GNI — specifically to strip it out. The gap it reveals is not small: in the CSO’s restatement, headline GDP ran at 162% of GNI, meaning the real domestic economy is roughly a third smaller than the poster. Put per head, in the comparable terms the EU uses (where the EU average is 100): in 2023 Ireland’s GDP per capita stood at *213; its GNI per capita stood at 122. Same country, same year. One number says “more than twice as rich as the average European.” The honest one says “comfortably above average, and no more.” Even the ESRI now uses GNI*, noting plainly that GDP-based measures “can be misleading for Ireland.”

And then what actually reaches the household. Above-average is not nothing — but it is a long way from the headline, and the gap widens again by the time national wealth reaches a kitchen table. Ireland carries some of the highest housing costs and among the highest household electricity prices in the European Union — and a data-centre sector that, as Part 3 sets out, has been estimated to have added around €715 million to household electricity bills since 2015. A country can sit near the top of the GDP tables and still leave its people with relatively little once rent, energy and childcare are paid. That is the precise signature of an extraction economy: the wealth is real, but it is measured where it lands for the multinational, not where it is felt by the resident.

Why this is the whole argument in miniature. This is the KPMG report’s “876,000 jobs” trick, told at national scale. The headline is built from the part that flatters; the costs are kept off the page — the water, the grid, the foregone housing, the rivers. GDP measures what the data centres add to the poster; GNI*, and the state of the Blackwater, and the figure left in an account at the end of the month, measure what is actually here. So when someone tells you Ireland is among the richest countries in the world and can therefore afford to keep giving its water and grid away, they are quoting the figure on the poster. Ask them, gently, for the one in people’s pockets.

The backbone and the build-out: SMEs and data centres, side by side

Put the two economies in one table and the asymmetry stops being abstract. This is the same shape as the GNI* point above and the nitrogen ledger in Part 3 — the indigenous, place-based majority doing the real work and carrying the real costs, while the headline numbers and the policy accommodation attach to the model that books its value here and feels its consequences elsewhere.

Indigenous SMEs Data centres / FDI multinationals
Share of enterprises 99.8% of all active businesses a tiny fraction
Share of jobs ~two-thirds of all employment; Irish-owned firms ~75% of jobs a minority of jobs
Typical pay lowest median weekly earnings (~€654); ~45% earn under €600/week highest (~€907 in non-EU multinationals)
Share of turnover / GVA the minority the majority (foreign-owned ~65%)
Grid & energy wait in the connection queue; pay among the highest energy costs in the EU 22% of all metered electricity (2024), rising toward ~30%; accommodated, moratorium lifted
The training levy pay the 1% NTF levy in full —
Regulatory treatment rates, nitrogen budgets, full scrutiny grandfather clause, confidentiality clause, fast-track

The point is not resentment. The multinationals are not the villains of a melodrama, and the jobs they bring are real.
