The point is to notice which Ireland the rules are actually built around — and to ask why the economy’s own backbone is treated as its afterthought, taxed and levied and scrutinised to fund a build-out it was never consulted about.

Sources: SMEs 99.8% of active enterprises and ~66.9% of persons employed; Irish-owned enterprises ~74.9% of employments; median weekly earnings €654.41 (Irish-owned) vs €906.74 (non-EU multinational), with ~44.5% of Irish-owned employments under €600/week; foreign-owned share of turnover and GVA — Central Statistics Office, Business in Ireland and Employment & Earnings by Nationality of Ownership (2023–2025). Data-centre electricity share 22% of all metered electricity in 2024 (more than all households combined); large energy users 31%; projected toward ~30% by the early 2030s — CSO (10 June 2025) and EirGrid. Energy costs among the highest in the EU — Eurostat (verify current ranking before quoting a precise figure). Regulatory-treatment items as sourced in Parts 1, 3 and 4F.

Sources: Ireland GDP per capita ~$113,000 (2024) and EU/world ranking — IMF / worlddata.info; Briefings for Britain (noting second in EU after Luxembourg). GDP = 162% of GNI and the construction of GNI (IP depreciation, redomiciled-company earnings, aircraft leasing) — Central Statistics Office restatement; “Modified gross national income,” CSO/Eurostat. GDP-per-capita 213 vs GNI-per-capita 122 in PPS, 2023 (EU average = 100) — CSO, “Measuring Ireland’s Progress 2023.” ESRI on GNI as the more reliable living-standards measure — ESRI / Shared Island research. €715m household electricity cost — Friends of the Earth Ireland, as cited in Part 3. Household electricity prices among the highest in the EU — Eurostat (verify current ranking before quoting a precise figure). This section deliberately does not reproduce the precise per-household “disposable income after essentials” tables that appear in AI-generated material, which could not be traced to a primary source; the GDP-vs-GNI contrast and the cost-of-living direction are what the verified data support.

The warehouse, not the workshop: hosting the cost, missing the dividend

Here is the cruellest turn in the whole story. Ireland has made itself the European warehouse of American Big Tech — the largest concentration of US hyperscaler data centres on the continent, drawing 22% of the national grid to serve, overwhelmingly, the commercial cloud and AI operations of a handful of US corporations. We carry the cost: the grid strain, the water, the emissions, the housing and indigenous industry pushed back in the connection queue. And now Europe is trying to build something different — and Ireland is being passed over for it.

The dividend is being built elsewhere. In February 2025 the European Commission launched InvestAI, a plan to mobilise €200 billion for artificial intelligence, including a €20 billion fund for up to five “AI gigafactories” — vast public-interest compute facilities, each with around 100,000 advanced processors, explicitly framed as a sovereignty project: to give European researchers, start-ups and public bodies their own frontier-AI capacity and cut Europe’s dependence on American suppliers. That is the part where the lasting value lives — not the warehouse that runs someone else’s models, but the workshop that builds and owns the next generation. Nineteen “AI Factories” have been selected across the Union — in France, Germany, Spain, the Netherlands, Poland, Austria and more. Ireland’s share was a single “AI Factory Antenna”: a satellite connection point, not a factory. And the gigafactories themselves are being steered, on the Commission’s own siting logic, toward member states with cheap, low-carbon, uncongested energy — the Nordics, Iberia, Austria — which is precisely what a grid already saturated by US-hyperscaler load is not.

Read the two facts together and the position is stark. We took the extraction and are being left off the sovereignty. We host the infrastructure that serves them, and our own grid is too captured — too full of their load, too dependent on gas, too dear — to host the infrastructure that would serve us. The “richest poor country” returns here in its sharpest form: a state wealthy enough on paper to be Europe’s data capital, yet positioned to draw almost none of the public, sovereign value from the very thing it has given its water and power to build. To be exact on the clean-hands line: the verified facts are that Ireland received only an antenna while full Factories went elsewhere, that gigafactory siting favours cheaper low-carbon grids, and that Ireland’s grid is heavily committed to existing data-centre demand. The conclusion drawn from them — that capture has cost us the dividend — is the campaign’s interpretation, offered as an argument, not as a claim that Brussels formally shut Ireland out. The remedy is the one this booklet keeps naming: free the grid, price the externalities, and insist that any compute built on Irish ground return public value to the Irish public — research and public-service access, not merely commercial rates.

Sources: InvestAI (€200bn mobilisation; €20bn for four-to-five AI gigafactories of ~100,000 processors each), launched by the European Commission at the Paris AI Action Summit, 11 February 2025; the AI Continent Action Plan; Council Regulation (EU) 2026/150 amending the EuroHPC Joint Undertaking regulation (EU contribution up to 17% of CAPEX, participating states to at least match) — European Commission (digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu) and EuroHPC JU. The roster of AI Factories (France, Germany, Spain, the Netherlands, Poland, Austria and others) and the 13 second-tier “AI Factory Antennas” including Ireland — European Commission, “AI Factories” (2025–2026). Gigafactory siting favouring low-cost, low-carbon locations, with only Sweden and Finland approaching US/Chinese power prices — CEPS analysis, as reported. US versus European share of significant AI models, and US/China control of AI-specialised data centres — Stanford AI Index and Oxford research cited by CEPS. Ireland’s 22% data-centre electricity share (2024) — CSO, as in Part 3. The “capture cost us the dividend” reading is interpretation, labelled as such.

Make waste-heat reuse non-negotiable

Denmark and Finland already require or enable it — Meta’s Odense facility heats 11,000 homes; Finnish operators warm the equivalent of 250,000 people. A data centre is a giant heater pretending its heat is rubbish. In a country with cold winters and a housing crisis, throwing that heat into a harbour instead of into homes is the waste — not the by-product. The prerequisite: mandatory waste-heat reuse and a published Water Usage Effectiveness figure as an absolute condition of planning — for every new data centre, no exceptions, no grandfathering.

Part 12 — What Was Taken, and What ‘Free’ Really Costs

The models did not invent knowledge. They were trained on ours.

What this chapter is about. This chapter is about the largest uncredited borrowing in history: the human knowledge, art and writing the AI models were trained on, taken without asking and sold back as a service. It asks what ‘free’ really costs, and what we are owed.

“We are like dwarfs standing on the shoulders of giants.” — Bernard of Chartres

The largest uncredited borrowing in history

Modern AI was built by training on the collected writing, art, music, journalism, code, research and conversation of millions of people — largely without consent, acknowledgement or payment. The intelligence in these systems is, in large part, humanity’s own cumulative work, gathered up and resold back to us. Naming that plainly is not anti-technology; it is simply accurate. A tool built on the commons owes something to the commons.

This is why the campaign can use these tools while criticising them — and say so openly. The point is not to pretend we stand outside the system. It is to insist the system give back: consent, credit, compensation, and the right to opt out of training.

The Sanders precedent — 50% for the people

In June 2026, US Senator Bernie Sanders argued exactly this: that AI was built on the creative work of millions — writers, artists, musicians, journalists, teachers, scientists and ordinary citizens — fed into the models, in his words, “without permission, without acknowledgement, without compensation,” and that because the value is drawn from humanity’s collective output, the wealth should be shared collectively. AI, he argued, is a public resource — like minerals or oil taken from public land — that a small number of companies have enclosed as private property.

On 18 June 2026 he turned the principle into a bill: the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act. It would levy a one-time 50% tax — paid in stock — on the largest AI firms (those with revenue above $200 million), depositing the shares into a public sovereign wealth fund on the model of Norway’s oil fund or Alaska’s. Sanders estimates the fund at around $7 trillion; it would carry voting rights and board seats, letting the public block harmful decisions, and pay an annual dividend he puts at more than $1,000 for every person in the country. Crucially for this campaign, the bill reaches beyond the AI labs to data centres, compute infrastructure and advanced robotics — and alongside it Sanders has called for a nationwide moratorium on AI data centres, citing their energy and environmental harm. Senator Elizabeth Warren published a parallel call to tax AI days earlier. Whatever one makes of the exact mechanism — and it faces steep legislative and constitutional odds — the principle is the one this booklet has argued from the start: when a public resource generates private wealth, the public should share in it, and own it.

This is not a fringe idea carried in from the margins. It is the model set out in Part 4E and Part 18 of this booklet — public and diaspora ownership of the strategic commons rather than its quiet transfer to private funds — now sitting, in another country, as an actual Senate bill. The argument has moved.

Practical asks that follow: the right to opt out of AI training; reward and recognition for those who do declare and consent; and treating data and creative work as resources owed compensation, not free raw material.

The mechanism underneath why this concentrates wealth is worth stating plainly, because a Turing Award winner has stated it plainly. Yoshua Bengio — one of the researchers most often called a "godfather" of modern AI, and no fringe voice — laid out the economics directly in a July 2026 interview: once an AI can perform a task a human used to do for a tenth of the price, the market value of that human's labour collapses toward the AI's price. The worker either loses the job or accepts far less, while the returns flow to whoever owns the machines and the capital behind them. Bengio situates this within a longer trend — since the 1980s, the share of productivity gains going to capital rather than to labour has steadily risen — and warns that AI could sharply accelerate it, with the added twist that the profits concentrate not just in a few companies but in a few countries. His conclusion is the same one underneath this entire booklet: if the wealth generated by automation flows entirely to the owners of the systems and the nations that host them, ordinary people and smaller economies face a genuine fiscal squeeze — fewer jobs, less tax revenue, more people needing support, all at once. This is not an argument against the technology. It is an argument for making sure the public retains an ownership stake in it, which is exactly what the Sanders bill and this booklet's own Part 4E propose.

The Newsom conversation — "decisions, not conditions"

On 15 May 2026, Governor Gavin Newsom sat down with Tristan Harris and Aza Raskin of the Center for Humane Technology — the pair behind The Social Dilemma and the newer documentary The AI Doc — for an hour and a half that produced more concrete policy mechanism than most parliamentary committees manage in a year. Worth listening to in full; worth stealing from more than once.

Harris and Raskin's starting point is a warning drawn from their own history: social media's damage came from one unregulated incentive — maximise attention — and every downstream harm (loneliness, disinformation, the sexualisation of teenagers) traced back to that single root. They argue AI's equivalent incentive isn't subscription revenue, which can't justify the capital being spent, but the replacement of human labour outright — a goal OpenAI's own early mission language stated plainly before it was softened for public consumption.

Universal Basic Capital, not Universal Basic Income. Harris is blunt that cash payments alone are "an extremely disappointing answer" to job displacement — people don't just lose income when a machine takes their work, they lose the thing UBI can't refund. His and Raskin's alternative, echoed independently by Newsom under the same name, is ownership: citizens holding an actual equity stake in AI-driven productivity gains, on the Norwegian model, rather than a cheque that arrives after the fact.

The condition that makes or breaks it. The sharpest point in the conversation is the one this booklet has been making from a different angle throughout: a sovereign fund is not automatically a public good. Norway, with an engaged, well-informed citizenry holding its fund to account, built a healthy, durable structure. Venezuela and Libya, sitting on comparable windfalls without that oversight, did not. Newsom's proposed answer for California — a citizen-deliberation structure he calls "Engage California" — is explicitly built on that distinction: the fund is only as good as the public's power to direct it. Public ownership without public oversight is just capture with better branding.

Other mechanisms raised, credited to their actual originators:

  • Reid Hoffman's proposal (LinkedIn co-founder, early PayPal): tax companies by their ratio of employees to revenue, to directly disincentivise the "solo unicorn" — a company built to generate enormous revenue with almost no one on the payroll.
  • Reed Hastings' "trigger-point laws" (from the former Netflix CEO): pre-commit policy responses to specific unemployment thresholds — what happens automatically at 10%, what happens at 20% — decided in calm before the crisis, not negotiated in a panic during it.
  • The Hangzhou ruling. In April 2026, the Hangzhou Intermediate People's Court found that a fintech firm had unlawfully dismissed an employee, "Zhou," after his role was automated — ruling that adopting AI is a business decision, and the costs of that decision cannot be shifted onto the worker by way of dismissal or a punitive demotion. Every level of appeal — arbitration, trial court, and the intermediate court — agreed. Newsom floated this directly as a model California could adapt.
  • Employment insurance, not unemployment insurance. The Dutch system, cited in the conversation, replaces roughly 90% of wages during a transition — keeping someone employed and afloat, rather than cutting them loose and topping them up afterward.
  • Real-time labour monitoring. Newsom's critique of the US WARN Act is that it only fires after the jobs are already gone; he wants a system that signals disruption as it happens.

The throughline for this campaign. Strip away the American specifics and the argument is the same one Part 4E and Part 18 already make about Ireland's own strategic commons: ownership without an engaged public to hold it accountable is not a safeguard, it's a delegation of the safeguard to whoever already has the most power in the room. Norway didn't get it right because it found oil. It got it right because Norwegians were watching.

Sources: "Can Artificial Intelligence Be Controlled? With Tristan Harris & Aza Raskin," This is Gavin Newsom, iHeartPodcasts, 15 May 2026 (direct transcript). Hangzhou ruling: Hangzhou Intermediate People's Court statement, late April 2026, as reported by NPR, Bloomberg, the South China Morning Post, and Fortune (May 2026).

The primary school pipeline — Picker Pals, Food Dudes, and who's actually funding the good news

Before you get to data centres, there is a smaller, quieter version of the same pattern already running through Irish primary schools — and it is worth naming precisely because it is so wholesome-looking. Two programmes, different mechanisms, same underlying move: public money and children's participation doing reputational and commercial work for private companies, at one remove from anyone who could object.

Picker Pals and the Circular Economy Fund. Picker Pals — the litter-picking programme run by the environmental NGO VOICE Ireland in partnership with over 20 local authorities — carries roughly €380,000 in state backing from the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment's Circular Economy Fund. That fund is capitalised entirely by levies on landfill use and single-use plastics — polluter-pays money, in principle. In practice, once it is routed through a school programme, it becomes the platform on which corporate sponsors — BMW Ireland, AIB, Sky, Intel, Smurfit Westrock, Deloitte, ESB and others — hang their own sustainability credentials, at a fraction of what the equivalent PR spend would otherwise cost them. Intel's backing is concentrated in schools around its Leixlip plant; a company whose manufacturing draws heavily on water and industrial permitting is, at minimal cost, building the kind of visible local goodwill that tends to matter when the next planning application lands. On the child-data question specifically: Picker Pals does not appear to run any AI or automated data-gathering layer, and photos of children are kept behind parental consent — this is a genuine point in the programme's favour, not a hidden risk.

Food Dudes is a different animal. Run by Bord Bia and co-funded by the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine and the EU School Fruit and Vegetables Scheme, Food Dudes delivers upward of three million portions of fruit and veg to classrooms a year. Two things are worth separating out here: where the money and food actually come from, and what happens to the data the programme generates.

The supply chain. Despite the programme's local, seasonal framing, Bord Bia's own tender records show the bulk of physical distribution runs through a small number of large operators. Glanmore Foods — Ireland's largest school-meals supplier — was acquired by Compass Group plc, the London-listed catering multinational, in May 2023, for an undisclosed sum, cleared by the Competition and Consumer Protection Commission that July. On the produce side, Keelings supplies domestically grown seasonal crops, while imported items (bananas, citrus) move through the wider Dole plc supply chain — Dole plc itself being a Dublin-headquartered, NYSE-listed company formed in 2021 from the merger of the Irish firm Total Produce with the US's Dole Food Company. None of this is secret or illegal. It is simply not the picture of small, local growers that the programme's branding suggests — logistics at this scale require industrial partners, and Bord Bia's own eTenders confirm that's who wins them.

The data. This is the part that actually matters for this booklet's argument. Ireland's participation in the EU School Fruit and Vegetables Scheme is governed by Regulation (EU) 2016/791, a genuine, verifiable piece of EU law, which requires member states to carry out continuing, granular monitoring and evaluation of the scheme as a condition of receiving EU funding. Schools participate under their Department of Education roll number, and consumption data is tracked at that level. Whether that constitutes fully anonymised data or something short of it — pseudonymised data, cross-referenceable in principle against a school's DEIS (deprivation) status — is a real and specific question, but not one this booklet can answer without a lawyer's read of Bord Bia's actual data-processing agreements; Bord Bia's own general privacy notice states it does not carry out automated profiling, which cuts against the more alarming version of this claim. The honest position is: the EU regulation compelling this level of monitoring is real and sits oddly next to any assumption that school-level nutrition data is simple, disposable, anonymous statistics — and it deserves an actual FOI/AIE request to Bord Bia, not an assumption either way.

The research finding worth sitting with. Bord Bia's own long-term evaluation, run with UCD and Bangor University, found that the programme's effect decays sharply — vegetable consumption peaks during the 16-day reward-based intervention phase and drops back close to baseline within a few years once the free food and prizes stop. The programme's own researchers attribute this to parental purchasing habits at home not changing, not to any harm from the method. Whether the reward-based "operant conditioning" structure — sweets, stickers, and toys for tasting a vegetable — does something less benign to a child's relationship with food than the topline stats capture is a fair question to ask, but it is a psychological argument, not a data-sovereignty one, and this booklet keeps those two registers separate.

Why this belongs next to the data centre chapters. The mechanism is the same one running through Parts 1–4 of this book at a larger scale: a public good (children's health, children's environmental education) is used as the platform on which private companies acquire reputation, market intelligence, or regulatory goodwill, financed substantially by public money, under EU or state frameworks that were not written with this specific extraction in mind. It is a smaller stage, and nobody's water table is at risk — but it is the same architecture, running quietly in nearly every primary school in the country, and it is worth an AIE request or two before it is dismissed as too small to matter.

Sources: VOICE Ireland / Picker Pals sponsor listings (pickerpalsworld.org, voiceireland.org); Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment Circular Economy Fund. Bord Bia / Food Dudes programme materials (fooddudes.ie); Regulation (EU) 2016/791 of the European Parliament and Council, 11 May 2016 (EUR-Lex, verified). Compass Group plc acquisition of Glanmore Foods: Arthur Cox LLP, Irish Examiner, RTÉ, Competition and Consumer Protection Commission case M/23/023 (May–July 2023, undisclosed sum — note this corrects an earlier AI-generated claim of a "€54 million acquisition" and associated legal action, which could not be verified and is not included here). Dole plc corporate history: Irish Times, Business Wire, Dole plc company records.

Externalities — the costs kept off the books

An externality is a cost a company creates but does not pay — it is pushed onto someone else, or onto the shared environment. The water drawn from a stressed harbour, the heat dumped instead of reused, the grid capacity taken before homes can connect, the carbon, the e-waste: these are the real costs of “free” and “cheap” digital services. Free was never free. Someone, somewhere — usually a community with the least power — pays.

What gets measured gets managed. What is hidden cannot be governed. Disclosure is not a nice-to-have — it is the precondition for every other reform.

An Bata Scóir: The Tally Stick, the Hedge School, and the New Empire

Before they came for the water, they came for the words. Ireland has been handed this exact bargain before, and it knows where it leads — and it knows, too, how it was once refused.

What this chapter is about. The historical heart of the book. It tells how Ireland was offered this exact bargain before — the tally stick that shamed a language out of children’s mouths — and how it was refused, by the hedge schools and by the Aisling poets who hid the forbidden dream inside a love song. It is the proof, in our own ground, that the taking can be refused.

“Munavar in the dark… the thing they tried to pave over kept growing underneath.” — (this book)

The first taking: erasure by force

Begin in the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries, with the Penal Laws — a series of statutes enacted to secure the Protestant Ascendancy after the Williamite War. Among them, the Education Act of 1695 — “An Act to restrain Foreign Education” — barred Catholics from sending their children abroad to be educated, while Catholic schools at home were forbidden and the property of teaching orders confiscated. The logic was nakedly stated for its age: control what a people may learn, and you control how they think. This was empire with its face uncovered — erasure by open law. It must be said honestly that the Penal Laws did not target the Irish language by name, and their effect on the language was gradual and tangled with much else; what they did was criminalise the Catholic and Gaelic world in which that language lived and was passed on. Enforcement was uneven and eased through the 1770s before the laws were dismantled. But the intent — to make a people convert, or stay ignorant, in their own country — was plain.

The refusal: the hedge schools

And here is the part of the story we are rarely taught, the part this whole booklet is built on. The people taught the forbidden thing anyway. When Catholic and Gaelic schooling was outlawed, learning went underground — into barns, into back rooms, behind hedgerows with a child posted on the lane to watch for soldiers. These were the hedge schools, na scoileanna sceiche. They taught reading and writing, sometimes in Irish and sometimes in English, and astonishingly often Latin, Greek, mathematics and Irish history besides. Historians agree they were educating something on the order of four hundred thousand children by the 1820s. They testified to one thing above all: the plain, stubborn hunger of ordinary Irish people to see their children learn, a hunger no statute could legislate away. This is the nitrogen fixer made literal — life working quietly beneath a field that had been paved over, impossible to fully kill, and enriching the ground for whatever came after. When the powerful tell you resistance is futile, remember the ditch with the book passed hand to hand.

The other refusal: the dream that could not be outlawed

There was a second refusal happening alongside the hedge schools, quieter still — because it disguised itself as a love song.

If you cannot say a thing aloud, you learn to sing it sideways. Under the same Penal Laws, to speak openly of a free Ireland, or to mourn what had been taken, was dangerous. So the poets of Munster did something extraordinary: they hid the forbidden truth inside a dream. They called it the Aisling — the vision-poem. In it, the poet, alone on a hillside or by a river, meets a woman of unearthly beauty who weeps for the suffering of the land, names the power that has despoiled her, and foretells a deliverance still to come. Then she vanishes, and the poet wakes, carrying the vision onward.

The genius of the form was its deniability. The woman was a vision, not a manifesto. She wept; she did not incite. The oppressor was named in metaphor, never directly. The poems were spoken at fireside gatherings among a few trusted people, passed mouth to mouth, never written where they could be used as evidence — political speech disguised as love poetry, the memory of a stolen sovereignty kept alive in the body of a beautiful woman who was also Ireland herself. The poets of that tradition — Ó Súilleabháin, Ó Rathaille, Merriman — wrote some of the most beautiful work in the Irish language out of the ruins of the world the Penal Laws had dismantled. The woman they wrote about was the world they had lost, and the promise that it was not gone, only waiting.

There is something at the root of this booklet in that image. A Spéirbhean, a stór — O sky-woman, my treasure. The land was always imagined as a woman; the woman was always the land. To despoil one was to despoil the other. That is not a quaint old metaphor — it is the exact instinct this booklet keeps returning to: that what is done to the soil and the water and the living ground is done to us, because we are not separate from it. The Aisling poets knew, three hundred years ago and with the boot on their throats, what we are still struggling to say plainly now — that a people and their land are one body, and to let the land be drained is to let yourself be drained.

Notice what the hedge school and the Aisling share, because it is the whole lesson. Neither tried to out-muscle the empire; the power was absurdly one-sided. They won — and the knowledge and the longing both crossed the generation intact — by refusing the empire’s terms. The scholar refused to let knowledge be confiscated by carrying it where no soldier could reach: inside a human head, on foot, house to house. The poet refused to let truth be silenced by hiding it where no censor could convict it: inside a dream, in metaphor, by the fire. Quietly, patiently, in numbers too dispersed to stamp out — a green thing growing back through every crack faster than it could be pulled. The nitrogen fixer, again, in two forms at once.

The language in the age of the machine — and the salmon of knowledge

There is a third movement in this story of the language, and it is unfolding right now, in our own decade — and it does not fit the simple shape of the rest of this book, which is exactly why it has to be told honestly.

The State is now trying to use artificial intelligence to save Irish, not to take it. Led by Údarás na Gaeltachta, the ArdIntleacht na Gaeilge project — the name folds “AI” inside the Irish for “high intelligence” — aims to build Irish-to-Irish AI speech and translation services across every dialect, so that Gaeilge is not left behind in a digital world built almost entirely in English. Around €4 million has gone to Dublin City University’s Gaois group (2026–2029) to develop the underlying digital resources — the folklore archive Dúchas, the placenames database Logainm, the national corpus, the dialect and biography collections — with a further €900,000 to the ADAPT centre’s eSTÓR project. The stated fear driving it is real and sharp: the “digital extinction” of Irish, the prospect that voice assistants and AI models trained only on the big languages would leave Irish speakers, as one official put it, “cut off in a digital world that excludes Gaeilge.” The ambition is for Irish to be a first mover, a model for minority languages everywhere.

Be fair, because fairness is the point here: this is, on its face, one of the good actors. It is the opposite of extraction — the State and Irish institutions trying to turn the machine toward protecting a living inheritance rather than drinking it dry. This booklet does not oppose it, and it should be credited plainly. If you only knew how to say “AI bad, always,” this case would catch you out, and rightly.

But sit with it a moment longer, because underneath the good intention lies the one question this entire book keeps asking, and it asks it here in the most intimate form of all. To build Irish-language AI, the language itself must be digitised and fed into a model: the recorded voices of native speakers in every dialect, the folklore, the placenames, the whole corpus — a commons built up over fifteen centuries, gathered into a dataset. And so the question is not whether the project is well-meant. It is who ends up owning the model, and the data poured into it. If the result lives in public, Irish hands, it is a genuine act of cultural sovereignty. But if the language’s digital infrastructure comes to run on, or depend upon, the same handful of foreign hyperscale platforms whose appetite the rest of this book documents — the systems designed to “function with any AI product on the market” — then something quieter has happened. The language survives, but only inside someone else’s machine, on someone else’s terms. That is the tally stick rebuilt in software: not the words beaten out of a child, but the words preserved — and the preserving owned elsewhere. The most intimate commons of all, handed into infrastructure a community does not control.

This is where an older Irish image cuts deeper than any policy note. In the myth, the bradán feasa — the salmon of knowledge — swam in a pool ringed by nine hazel trees, ate the nuts of wisdom as they fell, and so held all the knowledge of the world in its body. The boy Fionn, sent to cook it, burned his thumb on its flesh and put the thumb to his mouth, and the knowledge passed to him. Notice what the old story knew: that the knowledge lived in the water, in a creature of the Irish rivers, and that it passed to a person by accident and care, not by capture. Set that beside the machines now being built to “hold all knowledge” — trained on everything ever written, cooled by the rivers, sold back to us as a service — and the contrast writes itself, without a single exaggerated claim. The salmon did not have to drain the well to be wise. It was the well’s wisdom, kept whole. The question Ireland faces with its own language, and with the waters that have always been where our knowledge lived, is whether we keep the salmon — knowledge held in the commons, in the water, in the people — or hand it to a machine that must consume the river to run.

A clean-hands note, because the connection above is a resemblance, not a mechanism: the Irish-language AI project is software, and there is no evidence it is building water-hungry data centres or draining any river. The salmon stands here as an image and a question about sovereignty and ownership — who holds the knowledge, and on whose terms — not as a claim that a translation tool empties an aquifer. The data centres in this book do that work; the language project raises a different and quieter question, and the two should not be welded. The point is the older and larger one: that knowledge, like water and like a language, is a commons — and the whole art is keeping it one.

The second taking: erasure by complicity

By the nineteenth century the mechanism changed costume. With the National School system founded in 1831 — English-only, and free, and genuinely wanted by parents desperate to give their children a future — open force gave way to something quieter and far more durable. The bata scóir: a tally stick hung on a string around a child’s neck, a notch cut for each word of Irish spoken, punishment following the count. The chilling part is who wielded it. As the historian Fintan O’Toole puts it, the stick was “introduced by teachers and parents, not by law or British government.” In some folklore accounts the surveillance reached into the home — a mother marking the stick, a father administering the slap on his return, the stick passed from child to child so that one sibling lay awake hoping to catch another speaking Irish and pass on the beating. Whether or not every such detail held everywhere, the shape is unmistakable, and it is the shape at the heart of this entire document: the most effective control is not imposed from outside by a visible oppressor. It is routed through the people it is done to, until they administer it to themselves and call it love, or sense, or getting on. Nor was this uniquely Irish — the Welsh had the “Welsh Not,” the French regions the symbole, Japan the dialect card. Many empires, one mechanism. (Honesty requires adding that the language’s collapse owed more to the Famine and mass emigration than to any stick; the bata scóir did not kill Irish so much as shame it.)

The gravest erasure: Bessborough, and the ground not yet searched

Some places hold the whole of this in one piece of ground. Bessborough, in Blackrock in Cork city, is one of them — and it belongs here, in the reckoning with what was taken, because it is the gravest Irish instance of erasure by complicity still unfolding as this is written.

Between 1922 and 1998 it was run as a mother and baby institution by the Sisters of the Sacred Hearts of Jesus and Mary. In 2021 the Mother and Baby Homes Commission of Investigation reported 923 child deaths relating to Bessborough. Burial records exist for only 64 of them. The Commission concluded it was “highly likely” that some of the remaining children were buried on the institution’s grounds. Nearly nineteen thousand women and children passed through over those seventy-six years, the majority of mothers separated from their children through forced adoption. The ground has never been fully searched.

In February 2026, Cork City Council granted planning permission to the developer Estuary View Enterprises for 140 apartments across three blocks on the site, subject to seventy conditions — including that a forensic archaeologist and an osteoarchaeologist monitor the works for evidence of burials. The Bessborough Mother and Baby Home Support Group, and Labour councillor Peter Horgan, appealed to An Coimisiún Pleanála.

Let the survivors speak, because it is their ground. Carmel Cantwell, whose infant brother William died in the care of Bessborough in 1960, told the 2026 commemoration that hundreds of children remain unaccounted for and that no exhaustive search of the site has ever been conducted. “We have spoken to witnesses who saw burials take place,” she said. “We know children were laid to rest here, but there is no single, identified burial place.” And then the line that needs no commentary: “The land itself holds the truth. These buildings, these fields — they hold the stories, the secrets, and the remains of our family members.”

The original estate was 210 acres; 150 have already been built over, for housing, a hospital, offices and a retail park. “Was it too much to ask,” she said, “that the last 60 acres surrounding the buildings be preserved as a park of remembrance for the 923 babies that died, the 31 women, and the nearly 19,000 women and children that went through Bessborough?” Human-rights academics of the Clann Project — Dr Maeve O’Rourke, Dr Claire McGettrick and Dr Máiréad Enright — wrote to An Coimisiún Pleanála that the State “is aware of a situation of mass disappearance,” that the Commission did not use “all reasonable means” to search for the disappeared, and that the State’s human-rights obligations “cannot be displaced or delegated by way of conditions on a grant of planning permission to a private property developer.” The historian Catherine Corless, whose work uncovered the Tuam burials, said simply: “They can’t build on that site until they find the babies.”

The decision came on 9 July 2026, and it went against the majority of survivors. An Coimisiún Pleanála substantially upheld Cork City Council's grant — permission stands for 106 of the 140 apartments (75% of the scheme), with one block of 34 units (Block C) refused because it would adversely affect the historic landscape and the setting of Bessborough House, a protected structure — not on the burial question. The commission stated there was "no evidence" of unrecorded burials in the specific area where development is proceeding, a finding TD Liam Quaide called "utterly indefensible" given that no comprehensive survey of the site has ever been carried out. Conditions attached require forensic archaeological monitoring during construction and a liaison officer to engage with survivor groups. Survivors and advocates called it, in RTÉ's own words, "very disappointing." The Labour Party responded that there is still time for Fine Gael and Fianna Fáil "to right this wrong," and stated plainly that a Compulsory Purchase Order of the site remains possible, urging government "to act without delay to ensure that the Bessborough site is recognised and preserved as a site of conscience." Worth stating fairly: survivors are not unanimous. The larger Bessborough Mother and Baby Home Support Group opposes any further development. A smaller group, the Cork Survivors and Supporters Alliance, welcomed this specific decision, believing the one-acre area they consider the actual burial ground — near the folly, marked on a 1950s Ordnance Survey map — falls outside what's being built on. Both positions come from survivors of the same institution, and this booklet records both rather than flattening them into one voice.

This needs not one word of the campaign’s own argument added to it. It is its own argument, and it is the tally stick’s logic at its most absolute: the powerless dead, erased once by an institution that called its cruelty care, and at risk of being erased a second time — quietly, lawfully, under a grant of permission — by a society that would rather build over the question than answer it. A people’s measure is what it does with its most powerless dead. The survivors ask only for what should never have needed asking: that the ground be searched before it is built upon, and that the children be found.

What can still be done — the decision is not the end of the road.

Judicial review. An Coimisiún Pleanála's decision can be challenged in the High Court, but the window is strict and short: applications for leave must be made within eight weeks of the decision date under Section 50 of the Planning and Development Act 2000 — for this decision, that deadline falls in early September 2026. Because the development required an Environmental Impact Assessment, any judicial review that includes even one ground relating to the EIA or Habitats Directives qualifies for Aarhus costs protection under Section 50B — meaning an unsuccessful applicant is shielded from paying the developer's costs, a protection the Irish Supreme Court confirmed applies broadly in Heather Hill v An Bord Pleanála. This is not a do-it-yourself action like the AIE or FOI templates elsewhere in this book — it requires a solicitor, and a "letter before action" should go to the planning authority as the first step, ideally within days, not weeks, of a decision.

The Compulsory Purchase Order. This is now the clearest live lever, and it is not untested — there is direct precedent on this exact land. In the 1970s, the State already compulsorily purchased 140 acres of the original 200-acre Bessborough estate; that land is now industrial parks and part of the N40. Cork City councillors have already passed a unanimous motion calling on council officials to work with central government toward a new CPO of the remaining 60 undeveloped acres — separate from, and not foreclosed by, the planning permission itself. A CPO would let the State acquire the land compulsorily for preservation as a site of conscience, and it sits entirely within government's power to initiate now. The pressure point is political, not legal, and it is sharper than usual here because a government figure has already conceded the point publicly: Taoiseach Micheál Martin said, before the decision was announced, that "the local authority should have purchased the site compulsorily a number of years ago" and that government would "determine how to respond" once the appeal was decided. Social Democrats leader Holly Cairns has already used his own words against him: "That has happened now, so the Taoiseach must clarify... is the Government going to compulsorily purchase this site?" Contact your TD and the Taoiseach's own office directly, quote his words back to him, and ask for a date.

A second, separate legal avenue: the inquest. The Attorney General holds the power to direct a coronial inquest at any time, and this is now live, not theoretical — the south Cork coroner has recently agreed to reconsider a request for an inquest into the 1960 death of a specific named infant connected to Bessborough. An inquest is a different instrument entirely from a judicial review or a CPO: it compels an actual legal inquiry into a cause of death, with its own investigative powers, independent of the planning process altogether. Families of any child who died at Bessborough — or any mother and baby institution — can request one; it does not require the CPO or the planning fight to succeed first, and does not depend on the developer's own goodwill or the outcome of any appeal.

Completing the search that was offered and never finished. It is worth being precise here rather than reaching for the first technology that sounds thorough. Ground-penetrating radar — the tool most people assume is the obvious fix — was assessed for this specific ground by geophysics specialist John Nicholls of Target Geophysics, on the developer's own instruction, and ruled unsuitable: the site was too overgrown, carried extensive dumps of waste material, and showed decades of prior disturbance visible in aerial photographs. That is a genuine, named technical judgement, not neglect. What happened instead, across two separate campaigns, was licensed test trenching. In December 2019, eight trenches were dug under archaeologist Niamh Daly's osteoarchaeological supervision; no human bone or tooth enamel was found in those eight. A later, planned round of ten trenches was never completed — the National Monuments Service suspended the licence before the work finished. Both rounds together sampled only a fraction of the site, which is the normal limitation of trenching as a method: it typically examines 2–5% of a site's surface, not a full survey. The honest ask is not "bring in better equipment." It is: complete the specific search the developer's own expert already recommended, and that the developer itself already offered to carry out. Forensic archaeologist Aidan Harte — who led site investigations at Tuam and Sean Ross Abbey for the Mother and Baby Homes Commission of Investigation, and serves on the Independent Commission for the Location of Victims' Remains, the body that recovers Ireland's Disappeared — identified two specific priority areas at Bessborough for excavation to forensic standard: 88 by 25 metres, and 31 by 25 metres, both chosen for their proximity to the only confirmed burial ground on the site, the enclosed cemetery around the landmark folly. The developer's own representatives told the 2021 planning hearing they would carry out exactly this examination before any construction began. It has not happened. Cork city councillor Kieran McCarthy has separately, and repeatedly, called at council level for a proper geophysical survey of the grounds — a real local political ally already on record. Complementary, non-destructive methods suited to disturbed ground — electrical resistivity tomography, magnetometry (a different technique from GPR, less affected by surface clutter), and formal collection of survivor witness testimony about burial locations — can support Harte's excavation without needing GPR to do work it was already found unfit for on this ground.

Sign and share. The corrected, working petition: change.org/p/stop-future-development-on-remaining-bessborough-grounds.

Sources: Mother and Baby Homes Commission of Investigation (2021): 923 child deaths, 64 burial records, “highly likely” some buried on the grounds. Cork City Council grant to Estuary View Enterprises, February 2026, 70 conditions; appeals to An Coimisiún Pleanála by the Bessborough Mother and Baby Home Support Group and Cllr Peter Horgan, decision issued 9 July 2026 upholding 106 of 140 units, Block C refused on protected-structure/landscape grounds (RTÉ, Irish Times, Irish Examiner, 9 July 2026). Geophysical assessment ruling out GPR and licensed test-trench suspension: Irish Times planning hearing report (2021). Aidan Harte forensic archaeology testimony identifying priority excavation zones: Irish Times (2021). Clann Project academics’ letter (RTÉ, March 2026). Carmel Cantwell and 2026 commemoration (Irish Examiner, Irish Times, 28 June 2026); Catherine Corless (The Echo, May 2026). 1970s CPO of 140 acres of the original estate; Cork City Council's 2026 CPO motion; Taoiseach Micheál Martin's remarks and Holly Cairns TD's response; Cork Survivors and Supporters Alliance's differing position on the folly-adjacent acre — Irish Examiner, "If you want to object to building over Bessborough, you have one week" (April 2026) and Irish Examiner/EchoLive reporting on the 9 July 2026 decision. South Cork coroner's agreement to reconsider a 1960 inquest request and the Attorney General's inquest-direction power — Irish Examiner (April 2026). Judicial review time limits and Aarhus costs protection: Citizens Information, An Coimisiún Pleanála judicial review notice, Heather Hill v An Bord Pleanála (Supreme Court). The development is residential; no data-centre or data-infrastructure element is present or implied.

The same offer, a new empire

Now set the three movements side by side — force, refusal, complicity — and look at the present. The bargain is being offered again, and the through-line is not a coincidence of history but a mechanism that simply changes its clothes. The empire making the offer today wears no crown and flies no flag; it is a transnational power of platforms, data and capital — the same hyperscalers and asset managers named throughout this booklet. The new tongue you must adopt or be left behind is no longer English over Irish; it is the platform over the public square, the algorithm over the attention, the proprietary model over your own judgement, the data economy over the commons. The promise is word-for-word the old one: this is the world that is coming, the old ways are sentiment, to refuse is nostalgia, rational people in impossible circumstances comply. And it works the old way too — through our own complicity. We tick “I accept.” We hand over the words. We let the State write the terms into law on the lobby’s behalf, and then enforce them on us. No conspiracy is needed; none ever was. To be clear, this is an argument about a recurring mechanism, not a claim that any one nation sits plotting the erasure of Irish identity. The point is sharper than that: the structure of taking is the same whether the prize is a language, a religion, a harbour or a stream of data, and whether the taker is a crown or a corporation.

There is a precise word for the behaviour underneath all of this, and it is worth using carefully. To take from a host while giving nothing back to its survival, to weaken the very thing you feed on, and to call the arrangement a partnership — that is the behaviour of a parasite, not a partner. But be exact about what that word does and does not mean here. It is not a label for any nation, any faith, any ethnicity, or any group of people. To use it that way is itself the old dehumanising move — the one that has prefaced atrocity again and again — and this booklet refuses it absolutely. It is a label for a structure: a set of behaviours that can wear almost any costume and shows up at every scale of human life.

The same extractive pattern can run through a marriage, a family, a workplace, a spiritual community, a religion, a cult, a corporation, a hegemony, a plantation, an empire or a government. And here is the fair and crucial point: none of those forms is the pattern. A marriage can be a true partnership or a cage. A faith can liberate or it can control. A government can serve its people or farm them. A corporation can build or it can strip. The label attaches to the behaviour, never to the name on the door or the identity of those inside. What marks the parasitic version, at any scale, is always the same signature — it extracts; it hides the extraction behind a mask of virtue, care or “progress”; it makes the host complicit in its own depletion; and it punishes the refusal to comply. Learn to read that signature and you stop being fooled by the costume, whichever one it is wearing.

This is exactly why the mitochondrion in Part 20 matters as its mirror image. Two different organisms, once strangers and even threats to each other, became one and fed each other — difference held in genuine symbiosis, each made stronger by the other. The parasite is that same story’s shadow: difference exploited instead of integrated, one party thriving precisely as the other is hollowed out. Both are real arrangements found everywhere in nature and in human affairs; the only question that matters is which one you are actually inside, beneath whatever it calls itself.

Which lets us answer, plainly, the question the bata scóir forces on us. Were the rules of the time correct? No. That a rule was on the books and enforced never once made it right — and the people who refused it were right to refuse, as the hedge school proves beyond argument. And it does not finally matter whether the extraction is handed to us by a foreign crown or by our own elected representatives acting as vehicles for a power standing behind them. A captured native government and a colonial administration run the identical programme. It was never really about the country, the flag or the creed. It is, and always was, about the same entitled, deceitful, extractive mechanism wearing the mask of the smart economy and the inevitable future — and about whether, this time, we recognise it clearly enough to refuse it.

The release valve: how the system turns host against host

An extractive system has one permanent vulnerability: if enough people trace their hardship back to its real cause, they will move against the structure itself. So it needs a release valve — somewhere for legitimate anger to go that is not the structure. The oldest and most reliable valve is the scapegoat: take a real, deeply felt grievance and redirect it onto a visible, less powerful group that did not cause it and cannot answer back. The grievance stays genuine. Only the aim is moved.

Take the housing crisis, the rawest grievance in Ireland today. The anger behind it is wholly legitimate — people cannot find homes. But watch where that anger gets steered. “It’s the immigrants” is the release valve. It is far easier to point at the newest and most visible arrivals than at the harder, structural causes: a decade of under-building, planning and infrastructure bottlenecks, land hoarded as a financial asset, and — directly relevant to this booklet — grid and water capacity consumed at scale by data centres, so that the next home, like the next factory, waits behind the server hall in the queue for power. Migration is a legitimate part of any honest housing conversation; the point here is not that it can never be discussed. The point is the mechanism — a structural failure repackaged as the fault of the powerless, precisely because that protects the structure. Blame the newest plant in the field for the exhausted soil, and no one asks who stripped the nutrients.

This is why this booklet steers deliberately clear of the politics of the scapegoat, of any wing. Figures who channel public pain toward immigrants — or toward any group defined by who they are rather than by what they do — are, whether they know it or not, performing a service for the very system they often claim to oppose. They are the release valve. They keep the anger busy and aimed downward, at other hosts, rather than outward at the structure. Many of them are themselves frightened, misled people, and the rule from earlier holds exactly here: be fierce at the mechanism, never at the people caught inside it, on any side. The scapegoat narrative is not an alternative to the extractive system. It is one of the system’s immune defences.

The antidote is the one this whole booklet describes. A monoculture survives by keeping everything divided and competing for the last of the nutrients; an ecosystem survives by holding difference together so the whole field is fed — the mitochondrion, not the parasite. A murmuration cannot be turned against itself. The discipline, then, is to keep tracing every grievance back to its actual cause and to refuse, each time, the invitation to turn on another host. The housing shortage, the water stress, the grid bottleneck and the scapegoat story are not four separate problems. They are one structure — and one diversion protecting it.

But Ireland, of all places, holds both halves of this story. It knows the tally stick — and it knows the hedge school. It knows what it is to have the words shamed out of your mouth, and it knows that the thing they tried to pave over kept growing underneath, and is spoken again today by children whose great-great-grandparents wore the stick. That is not a reason for despair. It is the proof, written into our own ground, that the second taking can be refused exactly as the first one was: quietly, stubbornly, together, underneath. We have done it before. We are the nitrogen fixers. We can do it again.

Part 13 — KAIROS: The Educational Framework

Kairos: the Greek word for the decisive moment. A way to teach people to read whole systems, not isolated parts.

What this chapter is about. This chapter offers an educational framework — a way of teaching the next generation to recognise the pattern and read the map, so it is never handed to them undrawn. KAIROS is the right moment seized.

“Tell me and I forget. Teach me and I remember. Involve me and I learn.” — commonly attributed to Benjamin Franklin

KAIROS (καιρός) is the ancient Greek word for the right, critical or opportune moment — the instant when action must be taken, distinct from ordinary clock time. It is the educational thread of The Ripple Rebellion: a framework for helping young people recognise the moment, think critically, and act.

What KAIROS teaches

Not what to think — how to think, and when to stand firm. The aim is to close the gap between what a school says it values and what it actually equips students to do:

  • Connect the dots: the cobalt in the device to the child who mined it; the data centre in Little Island to the fish kill on the Blackwater; the surveillance bill to the rights of the student in the classroom.

  • Read systems, not just headlines: who decides, who benefits, who pays, and who was never asked.

  • Define and defend boundaries: knowing when something is not right, and that declining to go along with it is a legitimate, powerful act.

  • Use tools wisely: understanding the real cost of “free”, and choosing accordingly.

  • Recognise the moment: that there are times when ordinary rules ask too little of us, and the right response is to act.

The thing KAIROS has to work against: schooling that trains curiosity out of children

Before this framework can teach a child to connect the dots, read a system, or hold a boundary, it has to reckon honestly with what conventional schooling already does to the raw material it's working with — and the research on this, taken together, is more damning than any single anecdote could be.

Curiosity itself measurably declines under conventional schooling, and the scale of the drop is documented, not assumed. Children arrive at school asking their parents an average of 100 questions a day. Inside the classroom, that rate falls to fewer than one question every two hours. Developmental psychologist Susan Engel, who studies curiosity in classrooms at Williams College, documented a fifth-grade classroom where two full hours passed without a single student asking a question — and recorded a teacher responding to a visibly curious child with: "I can't answer questions right now. Now it's time for learning." The institution meant to cultivate the mind had, in that moment, treated curiosity itself as the interruption.

The neuroscience explains why that decline matters more than it might first appear. A 2014 study by Gruber, Gelman and Ranganath, published in Neuron, found that a state of genuine curiosity activates the brain's dopaminergic reward circuits and strengthens the hippocampal connections that consolidate long-term memory — meaning curiosity isn't a pleasant accompaniment to learning, it is the mechanism by which durable learning happens at all. Fear operates on a competing pathway: Amy Arnsten's research (Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 2009) found that the stress response triggered by fear of being wrong or of public correction directly impairs prefrontal cortex function — the exact region responsible for the critical thinking this framework is trying to build.

And the specific claim this booklet does not make, deliberately, is worth stating plainly. A widely circulated story claims a "NASA study" found 98% of five-year-olds test as creative geniuses, dropping to 2% by adulthood. It is repeated constantly online, including in material that reached this booklet during drafting — and on close inspection it does not hold up: the percentages shift between retellings, no traceable primary study can be located, and there is no documented methodology for how 200,000 adults were supposedly tested with no independent record of it happening. It has the shape of a real finding and the substance of an urban legend, and it is excluded here for exactly that reason.

The real, verifiable version of the same point exists, and it is more precisely dated than the myth. Psychologist Kyung Hee Kim analysed nearly 300,000 actual Torrance Tests of Creative Thinking — the standard, peer-reviewed instrument for measuring creativity — collected across several decades. Creativity scores declined steadily beginning in 1990, the same period in which standardised testing became the organising principle of American schooling. By 2008, more than 85% of children tested scored lower on creative elaboration than the average child tested in 1984, with the sharpest decline in the youngest children — the group schools most loudly claim to be nurturing. A system producing that outcome consistently, across decades and populations, is not suffering a design flaw. It is producing its design.

There is a working alternative, not just a critique. Sudbury Valley School in Massachusetts has operated since 1968 without mandatory classes, standardised tests, or a prescribed curriculum, governed directly by its students through one-person-one-vote democracy from age four upward. A study of its graduates by Peter Gray and David Chanoff, published in the American Journal of Education (1986), found alumni gained university admission without difficulty when they chose to pursue it — roughly 90% did — pursued varied careers, and consistently reported a lasting passion for learning. Gallup's own longitudinal polling of American students shows engagement collapsing from 74% in fifth grade to 33% by high school, in every district measured. That collapse is not inevitable. It is what happens, reliably, when a system rewards compliance and calls it education — and KAIROS exists, in part, to hand a young person back the questions that system trained them to stop asking.

And here is the contrast this booklet cannot look away from: the children this happens to grow up, and the country runs on what's left of them. A population trained for twelve years to stop raising its hand, to treat "we don't have time for that" as a reasonable answer, to associate disagreement with the risk of being sent to the office — does not, on turning eighteen, spontaneously acquire the habit of questioning a KPMG report, a self-appointed umpire, or a minister's unsourced figure. Stanley Milgram — already cited elsewhere in this booklet for the 65% of ordinary people who would administer what they believed were lethal shocks to a stranger on a stranger's instruction — gave this exact posture a clinical name: the agentic state, the point at which a person hands their judgement over to whoever is positioned as the authority in the room and stops feeling responsible for what happens next. A school system engineered, however unintentionally, to produce exactly that posture is not a separate problem from the one this booklet spends hundreds of pages documenting. It is the production line for it. Ireland's own habit of deference — to multinational capital, to whichever larger power currently sets the terms, to the report commissioned by the very industry it assesses — does not begin in a boardroom or a Dáil chamber. On the evidence gathered here, it begins in a classroom, with a child who asked a real question and was told, gently, that now was not the time. A country that wants fewer lapdogs and more citizens capable of saying the ball was out has to start further back than the ballot box. It has to start with whether a five-year-old is still allowed to ask why.

Connection and community at the centre

People do not turn to screens, to algorithms, to AI companions because those things are genuinely fulfilling. They turn to them in the absence of something — real connection, belonging, a community that sees them. Loneliness is the soil that the most extractive technologies grow in.

Loneliness isn't just an emotional cost — it's a measurable physiological one. Chronic isolation is associated with a 51% increased risk of heart disease and a 32% increased risk of stroke, driven by the same mechanism any prolonged stress uses: a nervous system held in alert for too long. People with fewer than three types of social connection in their lives are over four times more likely to catch a common cold on exposure than people with strong social networks. And using genetic variation to approximate a natural experiment — a method called Mendelian randomization — researchers have found social connection doesn't just correlate with lower depression risk. It likely causes it. Community isn't the soft alternative to the extraction model. It's protective, in the same register as clean water and a stable grid.

Source: Social Creatures / The Creature Times research summary, socialcreatures.org.

The most powerful counter to a future of screen-mediated isolation is not a better gadget. It is each other. A young person rooted in a real community has far less need of an artificial substitute for connection, and far more resilience against the systems that profit from its absence.

“Every individual matters. Every individual has a role to play. Every individual makes a difference.” — Jane Goodall

Part 14 — When People Took Agency, and It Worked

History is not only made by governments. It is made by people who declined to look away — and were often called difficult for it.

What this chapter is about. A chapter of evidence for hope: documented cases, here and abroad, where ordinary people took agency against an extractive system and won. It exists to answer the voice that says resistance is futile.

“The people united will never be defeated.” — movement slogan

Vicky Phelan

Diagnosed with cervical cancer, Phelan discovered that an incorrect smear result had been withheld from her for years. In 2018 she refused to sign a non-disclosure agreement as part of her settlement — the single decision that broke the CervicalCheck story open. Her refusal to be silenced revealed that hundreds of other women had been affected, forced resignations, and triggered a national reckoning. The NDA she refused is the same instrument now used to keep data-centre water figures from the public. One person’s refusal to stay quiet changed what an entire country was allowed to know.

Catherine Corless

A local historian in Tuam, working largely alone and paying for death certificates herself, she assembled the evidence that there were 796 children with no burial record. She was doubted and dismissed for years. Her work led to a State commission of investigation. No institution asked her to do it. She did it because it was true and it mattered.

Shell to Sea

In Rossport, Co. Mayo, local residents — including the “Rossport Five”, jailed in 2005 — challenged the routing of a high-pressure gas pipeline and the terms on which a public resource was handed to a private consortium. It showed that ordinary communities can make the powerful answer for their decisions.

Roger Casement and the Congo

In 1903, Roger Casement travelled into the interior of the Congo Free State — then the private property of King Leopold II, run as a rubber-extraction regime built on forced labour and mass death. His 1904 report laid the atrocities out in plain, documented detail. With E. D. Morel he founded the Congo Reform Association, and the pressure that followed helped force Leopold to relinquish his private holding.

“In those lonely Congo forests where I found Leopold,” he wrote, “I found also myself, the incorrigible Irishman” — realising that Ireland too was a colony, and that the core injustice was the same: the way the conquerors had taken the land.

That is the pattern-recognition at the heart of this whole booklet: extraction wears different costumes — rubber, land, water, data, grid capacity — but the logic repeats. Casement saw it across a century and a continent. The task now is to see it on our own doorstep.

Part 15 — The Moment: Schools, Tipping Point, Murmuration

Collective action has a threshold. Below it, little moves. Above it, everything does.

What this chapter is about. This chapter is about how change actually tips — the murmuration, the moment a scattered public becomes a single turning flock. It is the theory of how small, aligned actions reach a threshold and become unstoppable.

The idea that a small, committed minority can move an entire system has real, peer-reviewed research behind it — and it deserves to be cited honestly, caveats included. Harvard political scientist Erica Chenoweth, working with Maria Stephan, built a dataset of over 300 violent and nonviolent campaigns across the twentieth century and found nonviolent campaigns succeeded roughly twice as often as violent ones (Why Civil Resistance Works, Columbia University Press, 2011). From that data came the widely quoted "3.5% rule": no campaign in the dataset that reached active, sustained participation from 3.5% of a population failed to achieve its aims. It is a genuinely striking finding, and it is why the figure is quoted so often by movements far beyond the ones Chenoweth originally studied.

It is also worth using precisely rather than as a slogan, because Chenoweth's own later work insists on that. Her 2020 discussion paper, pointedly titled "Questions, Answers, and Some Cautionary Updates Regarding the 3.5% Rule," is explicit that the figure was derived from a narrow category of campaigns — maximalist movements seeking to remove an incumbent government or win territorial independence — and that only a small fraction of even that narrow category, 17 of 169 nonviolent campaigns studied, ever reached the 3.5% threshold at all. A campaign for a specific policy change, like this one, is not the kind of campaign the rule was built to predict, and Chenoweth says so herself. What transfers cleanly regardless is the underlying mechanism, not the specific number: sustained, visible, broad-based participation imposes real economic, social and political costs on the people being asked to hold a position, and it is that cost — not a magic percentage — that moves outcomes. This chapter borrows the mechanism. It does not borrow the number as a promise.

“A small body of determined spirits fired by an unquenchable faith in their mission can alter the course of history.” — attributed to Mahatma Gandhi

Schools: act before the summer holidays

The school year ends within weeks. That makes this urgent. Once students and teachers scatter for the summer, the momentum a class, a year group or a whole school can generate is lost until autumn — by which point more connections are energised and more deadlines have passed. Before term ends, take one concrete action as a school community:

  • Write the class letter to your TD demanding a permanent Citizens’ Assembly and mandatory water disclosure.

  • Run one lesson mapping your nearest data centre and its planning conditions.

  • Pass a student-council or staff motion supporting the campaign’s core asks.

  • Invite a local environmental group in for a single assembly before the holidays.

The tipping point

Social research consistently finds that change does not need everyone — it needs a committed minority. Once a relatively small, determined fraction of a group holds firmly to a new position, it can flip the prevailing norm of the whole. You are not trying to convince the entire country. You are trying to reach the threshold where the position becomes common sense and the silence breaks. Every letter, every submission, every switched default moves the count toward that line.

The murmuration

A murmuration of starlings has no leader. No single bird directs the whole. Each responds to the few nearest it, and from those countless local choices a vast, coherent shape moves across the sky — turning, defending, becoming something no individual bird could be alone.

That is the model: not a command structure, not a single hero, but many people each acting with the few around them, until the whole moves together. Pattern-recognisers, nitrogen fixers, ordinary citizens — each doing the next small thing in concert. That is how a rebellion of ripples becomes a tide.

The nitrogen fixers are the majority

Here is the thing the extraction model depends on you not knowing: the nitrogen fixers are not a minority. They are the majority. The people doing the invisible work — filing the request, reading the planning file, testing the stream, writing the submission, turning up to the meeting nobody else attended — are everywhere. They feel scattered and small. But a monoculture depends entirely on the quiet work happening underground. The moment enough of the unseen majority recognise what they are and act together, the whole structure has to change. Not because any one of them is a hero — but because that is simply how soil, and societies, are fed.

Why hope is not optional — it is the mechanism

There is a reason the tipping-point research matters so much, and it is not just arithmetic. The studies on how committed minorities flip social norms also show what stops the tipping point being reached: not opposition, but resignation. The belief that nothing can change is the single most reliable guarantee that nothing will. Despair is not a neutral mood. It is the load-bearing wall of every extractive system — the thing that keeps the unseen majority convinced they are alone and powerless, and therefore quiet.

Hope, then, is not naive. It is strategic. It is the precondition for the threshold being crossed at all. To act as though change is possible is not to ignore the odds — it is to be the variable that changes them. Every person who refuses despair makes the next person’s refusal a little more plausible. That is how the count moves. That is how silence breaks. Hope is the nitrogen the soil needs most.

There is no “can’t” — boundaries from micro to macro

Two women, four centuries apart, understood something this whole campaign rests on: that the word “can’t” is very often a choice the powerful want you to make on their behalf.

In 1593, Gráinne Mhaol — Granuaile, Grace O’Malley, the pirate queen of Mayo — sailed to London and walked into the court of Elizabeth I to secure the release of her captured son and the return of her lands. She was a Gaelic woman before the most powerful monarch in the known world, who could have had her executed on a word. She did not bend the knee. She refused to recognise Elizabeth as Queen of Ireland, because in her own understanding she was a queen in her own right. The two reportedly spoke in Latin — the neutral tongue, the language of equals. She went as a sovereign meeting a sovereign, not a supplicant begging a ruler. And she secured her son’s release. She asserted the boundary at the highest level imaginable, and the boundary held.

The women who moved the boundary

Grace O’Malley is not an exception; she is the start of a line. It would be easy to file Granuaile under legend — a colourful one-off. She was not. She is the earliest Irish entry in a long, documented record of women who took, or were handed, authority and used it to shift a boundary that powerful men had called fixed. Constance Markievicz, sentenced to death for her part in 1916 and reprieved, became in 1918 the first woman ever elected to the Westminster parliament — and then, refusing that seat, Minister for Labour in the First Dáil, making her one of the first women in the world, and the first in Europe, to hold a cabinet office. A condemned revolutionary became a government minister, and the ceiling she passed through had been declared solid the day before.

And the change kept coming through women in office. The pattern is not confined to the founding drama. Mary Robinson, as a barrister and senator, spent two decades using the law to dismantle restrictions most of her colleagues treated as permanent — challenging the bans on contraception, the marriage bar that forced women out of work, the exclusion of women from juries, and the criminalisation of gay men — before becoming, in 1990, Ireland’s first woman President, and later UN High Commissioner for Human Rights. Mary McAleese followed her into the Áras and spent fourteen years building bridges across a border others preferred to leave walled. And the deepest social shift of the recent era was carried, in large part, by women: the long, patient organising behind the 2018 referendum did not descend from the top but rose from rooms full of ordinary women who decided a settled thing could be unsettled. None of this is sentiment, and none of these women was a saint — they were political, contested, sometimes wrong. The point is sturdier than sainthood. Again and again, the person who moved the immovable boundary was a woman who had been told, often by people quite certain they were being realistic, that it could not be moved. The Ripple Rebellion intends to be the next sentence in that paragraph.

Helen Keller, deaf and blind from before she could speak, was expected by every convention of her age to live a small, managed, silent life. The word “can’t” was the entire frame placed around her. She refused it — learned to read, to write, to speak, graduated from college, and spent her life as a writer and campaigner for the rights of disabled people, workers and women. Not by pretending the barriers were not real, but by refusing to accept that they were the end of the sentence.

What both women understood is the through-line of this entire booklet: it is by asserting the boundary at every level — the personal, the local, the national — that we change how we are treated at every level. The micro and the macro are the same act. The person who declines to accept “that is just how it is” in their own life is practising the exact muscle needed to decline it for a harbour, a river, a country. Boundaries are not selfish. They are how dignity is defended — and dignity defended at one level teaches its defence at every other. You do not bend the knee to a grandfather clause. You do not accept “can’t” about your own water, your own data, your own commons. You speak as an equal, in the neutral language of rights, and you hold the line.

Gráinne Mhaol did not say “can’t” to a queen who could have killed her. Helen Keller did not say “can’t” to a world that had already written her off. The nitrogen fixers do not say “can’t” to a system built to make them feel they must.

The murmuration, one more time

So picture it again — the starlings, the thousands of small decisions, no leader, no single hero, the great shape turning across the evening sky. That is not a metaphor for something distant. It is happening already, every time someone files an AIE request they were told was pointless, tests a stream nobody asked them to test, writes the letter, turns up to the meeting, refuses the easy “can’t.”

You are not waiting for the murmuration to begin. You are already a bird in it.
