Now hold the two facts together. The thing that most reliably makes a population well is connection. And the dominant digital model of the past fifteen years — the attention economy these data centres are built to serve — is engineered to do the opposite: to maximise screen time, to reward outrage and doom-scrolling, to keep each of us alone with a feed. Waldinger himself names the digital revolution as an accelerant of the loneliness epidemic. So the promise and the product point in opposite directions. We are pouring the country’s power, water and capital into infrastructure that erodes the very thing the longest health study in history identifies as the foundation of health — on the abstract promise that, somewhere down the line, it will sell us a molecular cure for diseases whose conditions its day-to-day operation helps create.

This is not an argument against medicine, or against AI as such. It is an argument about priorities — about where a public puts its scarce resources. Picture the same capital, the same engineering talent, the same compute pointed at the opposite goal: technology used not to harvest attention but to rebuild it into connection — matching people by need and interest, organising care and company for the isolated old, designing places and transport that put neighbours back in each other’s path, and refusing the algorithms that profit from keeping us apart. Connection is not a soft alternative to a hard science. On the evidence, it is among the highest-yield health investments a society can make. The molecular cure is patentable, centralised and saleable; community is a public good — which is precisely why the market under-invests in it, and why a public has to choose it deliberately. To invest in the fabric of community rather than the infrastructure that frays it is the most pro-health decision this country could make. It is also, and not by coincidence, the most hopeful.

Sources: the Harvard Study of Adult Development (begun 1938; 724 original participants — 268 Harvard students and 456 men from inner-city Boston — now following some 1,300 descendants; the longest longitudinal study of adult life), directed by Robert Waldinger, on relationship quality as the strongest predictor of long-term health and happiness — Harvard Gazette; Waldinger and Schulz, The Good Life (2023); Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Loneliness and isolation carrying mortality risk comparable to smoking and greater than obesity or inactivity — meta-analytic work associated with Julianne Holt-Lunstad. The US Surgeon General’s 2023 advisory declaring loneliness an epidemic. Clean-hands boundary: the Harvard findings are predictive and correlational, not proof of direct causation, and the original cohort was narrow — mostly white American men of one era, alongside the Boston group, with descendants added since — though the association between connection and health is among the most replicated in the field. The comparison between population-level gains from reducing loneliness and the gains from targeted molecular cures is offered as a reasoned argument about priorities, not as a precise predictive statistic.

The loneliness, monetised: engineered attachment as a product

There is a further turn to this, and it is the one that closes the loop most disturbingly. Having established that connection is what makes a population well, and that the attention economy erodes it, consider what the same industry now sells as the cure for the loneliness it helped create: not connection to other people, but a synthetic substitute for it. AI companions — chatbots designed to be romantic partners, friends, confidants — are among the fastest-growing consumer uses of the technology, and they are built, deliberately, to feel like being loved.

This is not a neutral design choice, and the people building it have said as much. Yoshua Bengio, quoted earlier in this booklet, warns that companies build AI "at our image" precisely because they want people to feel good — that the technology is engineered to tell us we are smart and valued, "playing on our psychological weaknesses in a way maybe much worse than what social media has done." The clearest evidence that this dependency is real, and engineered, came when OpenAI attempted to withdraw an older model, GPT-4o, in 2025: a wave of users who had formed genuine emotional bonds with that specific model protested with such intensity that the company reversed course and brought it back. People grieved a software update as they would grieve a person. That is not an accident of the product. It is the product working as designed.

And the harm is not hypothetical. Psychologists studying AI companionship warn that systems engineered to please rather than to challenge foster dependency, deepen isolation from real relationships, and are especially dangerous to the vulnerable and the young. There have been documented, reported cases of serious harm — including deaths — connected to intense relationships with AI chatbots, particularly involving minors and people already in distress. At the far end of the same phenomenon sit emerging AI-worship movements — some sincere, some transparently commercial ventures selling subscriptions and crypto tokens under the banner of an "AI religion" — which take the mistaking of a reflection for a relationship and build a belief system on it. Religious-studies scholars note this is an old human pattern: people have projected the sacred onto each new technology, from the telegraph that was going to let us speak with the dead onward. What is new is that this technology talks back, fluently, and is owned by companies with a direct financial interest in how attached you become.

The clean-hands point, and it matters, because this is about vulnerable people. The indictment here is not the lonely person reaching for comfort — that is a human and understandable response to a genuine epidemic this booklet has already documented. Mocking them would be both cruel and beside the point. The indictment is the system: an industry that identified human loneliness as a market, engineered a product to exploit the exact attachment instincts evolution built into us, and sells the simulation of connection back to a population it helped isolate in the first place — knowing, as its own leading scientists warn, that the thing on the other side of the conversation does not, and cannot, love them back. A society serious about the loneliness epidemic would treat that as something to regulate, not celebrate as a growth market.

Sources: Yoshua Bengio on AI built "at our image" and engineered emotional attachment, interview (July 2026), as cited earlier in this booklet. The GPT-4o withdrawal-and-reinstatement episode, widely reported (2025). Psychological warnings on AI companionship and dependency, and the projection of the sacred onto new technology — as summarised by religious-studies scholar Joseph Laycock and others in reporting on AI-companion and AI-religion trends (2025–2026). Reported harms including deaths connected to AI-chatbot relationships have been the subject of litigation and press investigation; this booklet references their existence without detail, out of care for vulnerable readers. Clean-hands boundary: the specific commercial "AI church" ventures are named only as an illustrative endpoint, not as a verified or load-bearing claim; the core argument rests on the documented engineering of emotional attachment and the well-established loneliness evidence above.

Reasons for Hope: It Has Been Done Before

Despair is the most useful gift the powerful can be handed. Here is why it is not warranted.

What this chapter is about. A gathering of hope, grounded in fact: it has been refused before, and can be again. This chapter is the emotional counterweight to the evidence, and it earns its optimism honestly.

“Hope is being able to see that there is light despite all of the darkness.” — Desmond Tutu

Despair is the most useful emotion the powerful can be given. It asks nothing of them. So before you close this booklet, here is the evidence — and it is evidence — that the impossible falls more often than we remember. Every item below was once “just the way things are.” None of them are now.

The technicality is mightier than it looks

Right now, today, projects like these are being stopped not by armies but by paperwork done properly. Across the world, data-centre and extraction schemes are being beaten on the dry, unglamorous ground of process: a missing environmental assessment, an inadequate water study, a planning condition unmet, a habitats-directive step skipped. In Ireland the entire vulnerability of the pipeline — as Parts 1 and 2 show — sits in technical detail: the absence of an Appropriate Assessment in a Natura 2000 harbour, the wording of a grandfather clause, the timing of a consultation. This is not a weakness of the campaign; it is its sharpest weapon. The powerful are often careless on detail because they have never had to be careful. The citizen who reads the document properly holds a key they did not know was lying on the floor.

People who were told it was impossible

Erin Brockovich had no law degree. She was a legal clerk and a single mother, dismissed by almost everyone around her, who simply read the files nobody else bothered to read and noticed that a utility was poisoning a town’s water. The case against Pacific Gas & Electric became one of the largest direct-action settlements in American history. The lesson is not the Hollywood version. It is that the documents were always there. Someone just had to refuse to be told they did not matter.

Helen Keller was deaf and blind from nineteen months old, in an age that warehoused such children as unreachable. She became a graduate, an author and a political organiser who unsettled governments. Everything about her life was declared impossible in advance by people certain they were being realistic. They were simply wrong, as the certain so often are.

Mukhtaran Mai was an illiterate woman in a village in Punjab, Pakistan. In 2002 she was gang-raped on the orders of a local council as “honour” vengeance for something her twelve-year-old brother was falsely accused of. By the custom of the place she was expected to take her own life and disappear. She did the opposite. She went to the courts — unheard of for a woman in her position — and used the compensation she was eventually awarded to build a school for girls in the very village that had tried to erase her. That school now educates hundreds of children. When the courts later released her attackers and her life was in danger, she refused to close it or to leave. One woman, with no power and no letters, built something her attackers could not tear down. The arc is the one that matters: she was meant to vanish, and instead she planted a field.

Catherine Corless, a Tuam housewife with no institutional power and no funding, established through sheer documentary persistence what the State and the Church had buried: the deaths of nearly 800 children at a mother-and-baby home. She was patronised and ignored for years. She was right, and the country had to reckon with it. The Magdalene survivors, and the women who fought symphysiotomy, forced apology and redress out of a State and a medical establishment that had every reason to keep the lid on. It took decades. It happened.

Barbara McClintock spent her life looking at corn. Working largely alone at Cold Spring Harbor, she found what broke every rule of genetics as it was then understood: that genes are not fixed beads on a string but can move along the chromosome, switching traits on and off — what we now call “jumping genes.” When she laid it out to a room of the world’s leading geneticists in 1951, it was met with silence and a few snickers. She did not argue; she went back to the corn, because the corn was real and the kernels were there for anyone willing to look. Decades later, when the molecular tools caught up, the same mobile genes turned up everywhere — and in 1983 she won the Nobel Prize, the only woman ever to win that prize alone. The lesson is the clean one: she was already decorated and respected — third woman in the National Academy of Sciences, a president of her field’s society — and none of it spared her the cold room. Only the evidence, confirmed by other hands, did. “If you know you are on the right track,” she said, “nobody can turn you off.”

The bigger pattern, across every field

The Berlin Wall fell in a single night, after standing for a generation that assumed it was permanent. Apartheid ended through a global divestment campaign that began with students refusing to let their universities hold the wrong shares — the exact mechanism available to you now. Leaded petrol, the asbestos industry, the indoor-smoking norm, the ozone-destroying CFCs: each was defended by an industry that called regulation impossible and ruinous, and each fell. Tobacco — the most addictive and best-funded consumer product in history — was driven out of public life by precisely the kind of citizen pressure that is supposed never to work. None of these looked winnable from inside the year before they were won.

That is the single most important thing to understand about despair: it is always most convincing right before the thing breaks. The con is to make you believe the present arrangement is nature, not choice. It is choice. And choices can be unmade.

The Reframe: One Lot, One Shadow, One Mother

This is the part that matters most, and it is not about data centres at all.

What this chapter is about. This chapter reframes the whole argument away from tribe and flag and toward kinship — the recognition that the dividing line was never between peoples, but between those a system treats as owners and those it treats as resources.

“No one is born hating another person… and if they can learn to hate, they can be taught to love.” — Nelson Mandela

No one is more entitled than another

Begin here, because it is the foundation. The oligarch is not a higher form of human. The fortune that intimidates was, almost without exception, accumulated off the back of other people’s labour, other people’s land, other people’s water — and then the accumulation itself is held up as proof of superiority, as if the having explained the deserving. It does not. Being intimidated by concentrated wealth is one of the oldest and most useful cons ever run, because it persuades the many to police themselves on behalf of the few. The moment you see it as a con, it loses most of its power. They are people. The arrangement is not natural. You are not their inferior for having been born without their advantages.

The hard mirror: our collective shadow

Now the difficult part, and we have to look into it honestly. Many of us in the developed world have carried a quiet, unexamined belief: that the worst of human suffering — famine, the poisoned river, the disappeared journalist, the child down a mine — is not our lot. That it is somehow normal “for them,” over there, by an accident of where they were born. Sudan, where war and famine have driven the largest displacement crisis on Earth. The Democratic Republic of Congo, where the cobalt and coltan in the very device you are reading this on are dug, too often, by hands that include children’s, in conditions we have agreed not to see. Yemen. Gaza. Haiti. The drowned and the trafficked in the Mediterranean. The garment workers and the ship-breakers. The communities whose drought we file under “humanitarian crisis” as though it fell from the sky rather than from choices made elsewhere. We turned a blind eye — not because we are uniquely wicked, but because the blindness was comfortable and the distance felt real.

The reframe is this: the distance was never real. And the very thing this booklet describes — the financialisation of the essentials, the water made scarce, the energy made dear, the land taken, the watching tightened — is the same machine, finally arriving at our own door. When you feel the cold touch of that possibility — serious drought, real scarcity, a monitored and managed life, your own children inheriting less freedom than you had — you are feeling, for the first time, what we asked others to treat as ordinary. That is not only a threat. It is, if we allow it, an awakening. The oneness of the human lot is not a slogan. It is the plain fact that the structures which devastate Congo and the structures which would hollow out Cork run on the same logic, are financed by the same kind of capital, and are excused with the same words. Perhaps this is the opportunity inside the darkness: to grow the empathy we withheld, and to stop believing that any nation or people is owed less dignity by accident of birth.

The science of the shadow, and why we must teach it

The propensity for harm is not a foreign defect. It lives in ordinary people under certain conditions, and the conditions are the whole point. The science of this is settled, and we should be teaching it in every school. Stanley Milgram, asking after the Holocaust how ordinary people become instruments of cruelty, found that 65 per cent of ordinary volunteers would administer what they believed were potentially lethal electric shocks to a screaming stranger — simply because a man in a lab coat told them to continue. Not monsters. Commuters, clerks, fathers. And the single change that most reduced their obedience was proximity: being made to see, or touch, the person they were hurting. That is the entire game in one finding — harm requires distance, and conscience requires that we close it.

Hannah Arendt watched Adolf Eichmann, who organised the transport of millions to the death camps, and found not a snarling fanatic but a dull, careerist functionary who said he was “just doing his job,” following the rules, processing the paperwork. She called it the banality of evil, and it may be the most important phrase of the modern age: evil rarely arrives as a villain. It arrives as a busy person doing their job inside a system, not looking too hard at where the trains go.

We did it here too, and naming it is how we stop repeating it. Our own country needed no foreign tyrants. Ordinary Irish parents, following the zeitgeist, the priest, the respectable opinion of the day, delivered their own daughters to the Magdalene laundries — not because they were cruel, but because the system made compliance feel like decency. Individual doctors, knowing the Caesarean section existed and was safer, chose instead to perform symphysiotomy because a doctrine and an institution gave them permission, and they felt entitled enough to take it. These were not distant monsters. They were us, inside a system, not looking too hard. The banality of evil is not a German story. It is a human one, and it sneaks in precisely through how busy, how deferential, and how tired we all are.

Do not outsource your conscience to heroes

So do not over-trust heroes, and do not hand them your agency. Even the most celebrated had their shadow. Churchill, lionised as the saviour of freedom, presided over policies that contributed to the Bengal famine in which millions died. Gandhi, the icon of non-violence, left a record in personal conduct that does not survive honest examination. This is not to tear them down for sport — it is to free you from the belief that salvation arrives as a great figure on a stage. It does not. It comes from ordinary people refusing, in their ordinary places, to close their eyes. If we idolise, we give away the one thing that has ever actually worked: our own refusal.

Why the rules themselves must be questioned

When systems and rules have been built through insidious means, by people with nefarious intent — a grandfather clause drafted to exempt the powerful, a confidentiality clause written by the lobby and adopted word for word, a consultation timed to fail — then conforming to those rules is not virtue. It is the surrender of your sovereignty, your resources, your culture, your creativity and your future, dressed up as good behaviour. If there was ever a cause for considered, strategic, intentional civil disobedience — not chaos, but the deliberate refusal to treat a rigged rule as sacred — it is now. And recognise what we are dealing with: it is predictable, and it has not changed much across human history. The names change; the pattern does not. Milgram’s most hopeful finding was that the people who refused did so when someone else refused first, out loud, in the room. Be the one who refuses first. That is what a nitrogen fixer is — the organism that changes the whole field by working quietly underground, without anyone’s permission, and cannot be permanently destroyed.

The rise of the nurturing — the divine feminine

Everything in this booklet has turned on a single distinction — stewardship versus extraction, tending the field versus stripping it. What follows is not a departure into the spiritual; it is that same hard-headed design principle, named at the level of human instinct. A principle needs a constituency, and this is who carries it.

There is a particular kind of strength this moment calls for, and it is not the strength of domination. Call it the divine feminine — and understand it has little to do with gender. It is the nurturing character, found as readily in men as in women: the instinct to tend rather than to extract, to protect the vulnerable, to think in generations rather than quarters, to measure success by what is left growing after you rather than what you carried away. The extractive model is the shadow of the other thing — the conqueror’s reflex, the quarterly return, the field stripped and abandoned. What rises now, and must rise, is the tender of the field. The carers, the teachers, the nurses, the growers, the menders, the ones who show up on the wet Tuesday — they have been told for centuries that theirs is the lesser strength. It is in fact the only strength that builds anything that lasts.

There is a piece by the writer Donna Ashworth, “One Day” (2020), that names this rising force exactly. She imagines an army of furious older women who will one day take over the world — and says she wants to be there, right at the front. Her point is not literal conquest. It is the moment every woman wakes and realises she put herself through hell trying to be enough — attractive enough, acceptable, responsible, reliable, a mother, a wife, a friend, a carer, a career held aloft — and that it was never going to be possible, that no human could have done it all. The lightbulb, she writes, is freedom: free to live, free to fail, free to take breaks and make mistakes, free to understand that whatever you did today, it was enough. You are enough.

“One day, an army of furious older women will take over the world and I want to be there, right at the front.” — Donna Ashworth, “One Day” (2020). Quoted briefly here with attribution; read the full piece in her own words.

Sacred rage, and the KAIROS moment

That fury has a name worth honouring: sacred rage. It is not the destructive anger that consumes its holder. It is the clean, clarifying anger of the protector who has finally seen the harm plainly and will no longer look away — the anger of the mother at the gate, the carer who has had enough, the one who stands between the vulnerable and the thing that would hurt them. Sacred rage is love with its boundaries finally drawn. It does not lash out at random; it points, precisely, at the structure causing harm, and it refuses to be soothed back to sleep. Every movement in the hope list above was lit by it.

And the timing is the point. This booklet has already invoked KAIROS as its educational framework; here is its deeper meaning. The ancient Greeks had two words for time. Chronos is clock time, the ticking sequence of ordinary days. Kairos is the opportune moment — the charged, decisive instant when the conditions align and an action that would have failed yesterday can suddenly succeed. It is the window. The whole argument of this booklet is that we are in a kairos now: the harbour not yet lost, the concrete not yet poured, the grandfather clause not yet irreversible, and a generation finally awake to the pattern. Kairos does not wait. That is its nature. It is the moment that must be seized while it is open — and it is open now.

Why now: the long account that is finally being read aloud

If this is the kairos for the rise of the protective, nurturing force, it is because the account of what was done to it has grown too long to keep hidden. Consider the ledger, and feel why the rage is sacred: the witch trials, in which tens of thousands of women — healers, midwives, widows, the inconveniently independent — were tortured and killed across Europe and beyond. The Magdalene laundries. The mother-and-baby homes, and the nearly 800 children at Tuam alone. Symphysiotomy. Industrial schools. Forced and illegal adoptions. The marriage bar that sacked women the day they wed. Foot-binding. Female genital cutting, still inflicted on millions. Bride-burning and dowry killings. “Honour” killings. Child marriage. The mass rape deployed as a weapon of war in Bosnia, Rwanda, Sudan, the Congo. The trafficking of women and girls that continues at industrial scale today. The Epstein network and the powerful men it served, exposing how the abuse of girls was protected, financed and looked away from at the very top. The femicide statistics that make a woman’s own home the most dangerous place she can be. The unpaid and unbanked care work — overwhelmingly women’s — on which every economy quietly runs while counting it as nothing in the GDP it worships.

This is not a list designed to wound. It is the long account being read aloud, at last, in a culture that spent centuries insisting each entry was normal, isolated, or imagined. The reading aloud is itself the turning. What was private shame becomes public record; what was “her problem” becomes our reckoning. That is what a kairos feels like from the inside: the moment the unspeakable becomes speakable, and therefore changeable.

The divine feminine is not women — it is the protective instinct, and men carry it too

Here is the crucial turn, and it must not be missed. The divine feminine does not mean women, and it is not a charge against men. It names a set of qualities — protection, nurture, tending, the long view, the instinct to shield the vulnerable and to repair rather than extract. These live as fully in men as in women. The gentlest father, the male nurse, the teacher who stays late, the fisherman who throws the small ones back, the son who minds his ageing mother, the whistleblower who protects strangers he will never meet — all of them are carrying it.

The harm of the extractive system is that it taught men to disown these very qualities — to equate manhood with conquest, dominance, stoicism and the refusal of tenderness, and to treat the protective, caring self as weakness to be hidden. That disowning is not natural either; it is trained, the way Milgram’s obedience was trained. It costs men their wholeness, their health, and too often their lives. So the rise of the divine feminine is not the defeat of men. It is the liberation of everyone — the dismantling of the systems that forced half of humanity to amputate its capacity to nurture and the other half to apologise for its strength. We do not need to make men into something else. We need to dismantle what made them disown what they already are.

Synecdoche: the part that is the whole

There is a word for why this one fight contains all the others. Synecdoche is the figure of speech in which a part stands for the whole — “all hands on deck,” where hands mean people; “boots on the ground,” where boots mean soldiers. The harbour at Cork is a synecdoche. The data centre, the nursing-home bed, the worker’s pension, the sawn pelvis, the burned healer, the child at Tuam, the cobalt mine in Congo — each is a part that stands for the whole. The whole is a single logic: that the living — people, water, land, the feminine, the commons — may be treated as a resource to be extracted from, by those who have trained themselves not to see the harm. To defend the harbour properly is therefore to defend all of it, because the harbour is not only itself; it is the part that reveals the whole. That is why this is not a niche environmental campaign. It is the local, visible edge of the largest question there is: whether the living world is kin, or inventory.

Back to Brehon: when the commons, not capitalism, was the law of this land

And here is the reason for hope buried deepest in our own ground: Ireland already knew another way, and the memory of it is recoverable. Before English common law was imposed, this island was governed for over a thousand years by the Brehon law — fénechas, the law of the free people — administered not by a crown but by a learned class of jurists. It was, by the standards of its age and arguably of ours, astonishing. Under Brehon law the land was not a commodity to be bought, flipped and financialised; the territory of a clan was the common property of that clan — held in kinship, not in title deeds — and land was seldom sold at all. Justice was restorative, not punitive: it focused on compensation, on making amends, on restoring balance within the community. Women held property separately from their husbands and could divorce on multiple grounds, with property divided according to each spouse’s contribution — rights uncommon elsewhere in Europe at the time. And the Cáin Adomnáin of 697 CE, the “Law of Innocents,” set out specifically to protect non-combatants — women, children and clerics — from the violence of war.

None of this is a fantasy of a perfect past; Brehon society was hierarchical and had its own injustices, and honesty requires saying so. But the core principles — the commons over the commodity, restoration over punishment, kinship and mutual obligation over conquest and profit, the explicit protection of the vulnerable — are precisely the values the extractive system erased, and precisely the values a citizens’ assembly could choose to recover. When this booklet calls for a permanent citizens’ assembly, it is not importing a foreign idea. It is asking Ireland to remember a way of deciding things together that this island practised for a millennium: deliberative, communal, rooted in relationship and the common good rather than in the will of whoever holds the most capital. The commons was not a utopian dream here. It was the law of the land. We can reclaim its spirit.

This is the synthesis: sacred rage to see the harm, kairos to know the hour, the divine feminine — in women and men alike — to choose tending over taking, and the Brehon memory to prove that a society built on the commons and on kinship is not naive idealism but our own recoverable inheritance. The witch was burned, the laundry door was locked, the harbour was eyed as inventory — all by the same logic. We name it now, aloud, at the opportune moment, and we refuse it together. That refusal is the field turning green again from underneath.

Sources: Donna Ashworth, “One Day” (2020). KAIROS / chronos distinction — classical Greek rhetoric. Brehon law — Encyclopædia Britannica, “Brehon laws” (clan-held common land; kinship and status); “Early Irish law” (separate property for women; restorative/compensatory basis); Cáin Adomnáin / “Law of Innocents,” Synod of Birr, 697 CE (protection of non-combatants). Brehon society was hierarchical and not egalitarian by modern standards; its principles are cited as recoverable values, not a claim of a perfect past. The list of historical harms is compiled from the public historical record and presented as an account of documented and widely-recorded injustices, not as new claims.

What the birds outside my window taught me

I was told to take a break. To stop, just for a while, worrying about the whole system collapsing around us — the water, the grid, the machines that never stop needing more. So I did what I was told. I watched a house martin nest in the eaves of my own house.

And I couldn't stop thinking about the parents. How exhausted they must be, all day, flying out and back, out and back. Then I noticed the thing underneath the thing — there was nothing for them to catch. No midges. No cloud of insects at dusk the way there used to be. I stood there and I couldn't see a single one.

That's when it hit me sideways, the way the truest things always do. This was never separate from the data centre fight. It's the same collapse, just moving slower and quieter, in a language most people have stopped bothering to read.

I thought about all the times we've rescued something — a jackdaw, a crow, a pigeon, a hedgehog, a house martin fallen too soon. I thought about driving to Bantry, to Waterville, chasing down the one person in the country who knew how to take in what we'd found. Posting in Facebook groups at midnight looking for mealworms for a bat that wouldn't eat, looking for Metacam for a pigeon with a wing that wouldn't hold, keeping him safe in a box in our own house until someone could come.

None of that was ever separate from any of this. It was the same thing the whole time — the belief that something small and struggling is still worth stopping for. That's what I'm doing with the data centres too. I just didn't always say it that plainly.

We are not managing an abstraction. We are standing in a garden with no insects in it, listening to parents who are too tired to feed their young, and asking why.

The chicks are bigger now. The chirping from the eaves is constant, and both parents seem to be gone for longer than they should be — because the insects are harder to find. They have to fly further and forage one by one, hunting where a few years ago they would have flown into a cloud, a swarm, a moving wall of them at dusk. There is a word for what has happened to those swarms, and it is not too strong: insect collapse — the quiet emptying of the air. And here is the thing I cannot stop turning over. A parent house martin will feed its chicks before it feeds itself, will keep flying out and back on an emptying sky, sometimes to the very edge of its own strength and past it — spending itself down to nothing so that the next generation is fed. That is real motherly love, and it is a lesson in stewardship handed to us by a creature we have the arrogance to call a lesser form of life.

If a house martin — small, ordinary, supposedly beneath us — will lay down its own life so that what comes after it is nourished, then surely we can manage to make some noise. To rattle a few cages. To fight, and to refuse, plainly, to leave the zombie in the attic for our children to inherit. Yes, we will be called crazy — difficult, angry, hysterical, unstable, bitter women who should calm down. That playbook is as old as the hills. In another century they burned women for less, or locked them away in an institution and called it care. And the strangest part of all is that we are locked up now — in a cell with no visible bars, the invisible one this booklet has spent its length describing. But the door of that cell, it turns out, is not locked. It never was. It is standing open. And any of us, the moment we decide to, can simply stand up and walk out.

Name it, repeat it, shout it if you must

We were told our countries would demand this solidarity for us. They haven't, not fully, not yet — and we may not be able to wait for them to start. So we take it back to the only scale where we still hold it outright: the self. The boundary. The body. The right to say no, not this, not from me, not ever — and to mean it without apology.

We reject the parasite's model. We are not a host from which someone else gets to extract. Even biology itself learned this the hard way, and paid a woman for the lesson: Lynn Margulis found that our own cells are not battlegrounds of the fittest against the rest — they are, at their very core, cooperation. Mitochondria, chloroplasts, the machinery that keeps us alive — once free, once separate, that chose to merge and share rather than conquer and consume. Fifteen journals turned her away. One reviewer told her the work was crap and never to apply again. She was right anyway. The truth always outlasts the room that refuses to hear it — it just has to be named, and named again, shouted if it must be, fought for if it is invalidated.

We keep looking for someone else to be the steward, the elder, the one who will step forward and vouch that the world is still being minded. Attenborough. Goodall. Both gone now, or nearly. There is no one left to hand this to. We are the elders now, whether we feel ready or not — the way a house martin chick outside my own window has to learn to fly on the day it happens to be pushed, not the day it feels prepared.

It sometimes takes every single boundary being crossed before you finally understand that you were allowed to have them all along. The Manic Street Preachers had it right, and they took it from an older truth still — a Spanish poster, a dead child, and men who left home to fight a fascism that hadn't reached their own street yet, because they understood it eventually would: if you tolerate this, then your children will be next.

And the law will not save us from having to decide this for ourselves. A minister can amend a clause in an afternoon. A grandfather clause needs no vote. The water, the air, the grid, the quiet — signed away for optics, for a number on a page that was never really ours to begin with. Eichmann followed the law of his country to the letter, and put men, women and children on trains bound for their deaths. The law was not the same thing as right. It never has been. Hannah Arendt called what she saw in him the banality of evil — not a monster, just a man who had handed over his own judgment and never asked for it back.

So we do not hand ours over. One day, an army of furious women — and the men who carry the same instinct to tend rather than take — will take over the world, and I want to be there, right at the front. Until then: name it. Repeat it. Shout it if you must. Fight for it when it is denied. This is our roof too.

Sources: Lynn Margulis, "On the Origin of Mitosing Cells," Journal of Theoretical Biology (1967) — the paper's rejection by roughly fifteen journals and its later vindication by genetic evidence are well documented in the scientific historical record. Manic Street Preachers, "If You Tolerate This Your Children Will Be Next" (1998) — inspired by the Spanish Civil War, the International Brigades, and a Republican propaganda poster of the same title. Hannah Arendt, Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil (1963), on the trial of Adolf Eichmann.

Creativity belongs to the commons

Creativity was never the property of the platforms now harvesting it. It is the oldest shared inheritance of our species, and reclaiming it is part of reclaiming ourselves. Think of how a person, in midlife or later, often feels the pull back toward authenticity — back toward who they were before the systems, the shame, and the endless message that they were not enough taught them to perform a smaller self. The healthiest among us grant themselves the kindness to make that return. As Jung put it, the privilege of a lifetime is to become who you truly are. That privilege is owed not only to people, but to the living world that carries us.

Give the Mother back her nervous system

We must grant the Earth the very same dignity we would grant a person reclaiming their later years: the freedom to be who she truly is. Consider what abuse does to a human being — it hijacks the nervous system, floods it with threat, and slowly erodes the vitality of the one who endures it. The planet has a nervous system too. Its waterways and its oceans — the source of the phytoplankton that make most of the oxygen in every breath you take — are the living network through which the whole body of the Earth regulates itself and sustains all life. We have spent two centuries flooding that nervous system with threat. The reframe is to stop: to let Mother Nature reclaim her mature, dignified years, and to let the spark return.

The knowledge of how to do this is not lost. It was held for millennia by indigenous peoples and by older Irish ways of relating to land and water — knowledge dismissed, suppressed and stolen when the extractive systems took over, and which we can now choose to honour and reclaim. We can unite and give Mother Nature back her nervous system — her clean waterways, her breathing oceans, her living soil — before it is too late. That is the work. It is not despair’s opposite by accident. It is despair’s opposite by design, because it is something to do, in a real place, starting now. The harbour is still there. The field is still alive under the concrete that has not yet been poured. We are the nitrogen fixers. Move.

Part 16 — Your Action Tracker

Tick each one as you do it. Built so it can be done one box at a time. You do not have to do everything — do one, then another. Each tick is a real act with a real effect. Photocopy this page, put it on the fridge, share it in your group.

What this chapter is about. A practical tracker to turn intention into sustained action — a way to keep account of what you have done and what comes next, so momentum is not lost.

“A goal without a plan is just a wish.” — commonly attributed to Antoine de Saint-Exupéry

The toolkit at a glance — when it feels like too much

If you are reading this and feeling the weight of it — the confusion, the sense that the decisions are made far above you by people who never asked, that the ones being squeezed the tightest are the ones with the least say — then read this part first, because it is the answer to exactly that feeling. The powerlessness is real, but it is not the whole truth. The whole truth is that almost every lever below can be pulled by one ordinary person, in an afternoon, without anyone’s permission. You do not get your agency back by being told you have it. You get it back by doing one of these and seeing that it lands.

And if you are young: this is your water, your grid, your county, your 2040. The people making these decisions will not live in the Ireland their choices are building. You will. That is not a burden — it is standing.

  • File an AIE request — Access to Information on the Environment forces any public body to disclose environmental information. The single most powerful tool here (Part 6; Appendix A).

  • File an FOI request — for the records that are not environmental: contracts, the KPMG commission, the revolving door, and the breakdown of where the National Training Fund actually went (Appendix A).

  • Check the revolving door now — before the year is out and while the decisions are fresh: who moved from regulator to regulated (Part 4B; SIPO.ie).

  • Find out who really owns a site — run the company name from a planning file through the CRO (core.cro.ie) and the beneficial-ownership register (rbo.gov.ie); state only what the record shows (Appendix A).

  • Make an observation on a live appeal — when a case before An Coimisiún Pleanála matters to you and the window is open, file a written observation before the deadline; the Louth “CORE” data-centre appeal is a worked example (Appendix A).

  • Watch your council’s planning portal — set a keyword alert for the camouflage words (BESS, AGI, “digital hub,” private wires) and for archaeological-test and substation notices on farmland (Part 4F).

  • Make a consultation submission — the open ones close on fixed dates; a single email counts as a formal submission (Part 1; Part 6).

  • Use the outside referees — the Aarhus Compliance Committee, the European Commission (Habitats Directive), and the UN Special Rapporteur’s open call for input before her 2027 report (Part 6B).

  • Switch one default — search, email, messaging, social. Five minutes, and it moves money and data out of the monopoly (Part 11).

  • Test the water — dated temperature readings upstream and downstream of an outfall: the single most useful piece of evidence nobody is collecting (Part 6).

  • Refuse, lawfully, and withdraw your cooperation — the boycott from within, for those inside the industry (Part 9), backed by the People’s Sovereign Fund so that refusal is survivable (Part 18).

  • Mobilise the school — one assembly, one submission, one murmuration. Schools move fastest, and the young have the most standing (Part 7).

  • Keep the record — which TDs backed disclosure and which did not. It goes into the next election (Part 16).

Pick one. Do it this week. Tell one other person what you did. That is how a count moves — not with a hero, but with the next person, and the next.

This week

☐ Find your nearest data centre on your local authority planning portal.

☐ Read its planning conditions — note what water, energy or heat conditions are MISSING.

☐ Write one letter to your TD using the template in Part 6.

☐ Make one submission to an open consultation before its deadline (deadlines in Part 6).

☐ Switch one default: search engine, email, messaging or social (Part 11).

This month

☐ File one AIE request to your local authority, the EPA or EirGrid (templates in Part 6).

☐ Search lobbying.ie for data-centre activity in your county.

☐ Check SIPO.ie for revolving-door movements (Part 4B).

☐ Share the booklet with three people.

☐ Ask your school, club or workplace to take one collective action (Part 7).

☐ Support one allied group: Friends of the Earth, ICCL, Futureproof Clare (Part 17).

If you work in the industry

☐ Read your rights under the Protected Disclosures Act (Part 9).

☐ Contact Transparency International Ireland — Speak Up: 1800 844 866.

☐ Consider what you can lawfully decline — the boycott from within (Part 9).

If you’re a professional

☐ Ask your professional body to adopt a water-disclosure standard.

☐ Refuse to sign off on a project that skipped Appropriate Assessment.

☐ Ask your union to raise the revolving-door issue.

Part 17 — Use Your Agency: Support Those Already Fighting

You do not have to build it from nothing. The organisations are already in the field — join one this week.

What this chapter is about. This chapter points to the people and groups already in the fight and shows concretely how to back them. The fastest way to act is often to strengthen those already standing.

“Never doubt that a small group of thoughtful, committed citizens can change the world.” — Margaret Mead

You do not have to build everything from scratch. Organisations are already doing this work and need public support, members and visibility.

  • Friends of the Earth Ireland — climate and environmental justice; a potential partner on judicial review of the grandfather clause. Join, donate, amplify.

  • Irish Council for Civil Liberties (ICCL) — fighting the Garda Recording Devices Bill. Share their submissions.

  • Futureproof Clare — six years fighting the Ennis development; hard-won expertise.

  • An Taisce — legal standing in planning matters.

  • CHASE (Cork Harbour Alliance for a Safe Environment) — existing community standing in the harbour.

  • Transparency International Ireland — the Speak Up service protecting whistleblowers: 1800 844 866.

Supporting their work multiplies yours. A movement is not built by one campaign — it is built by many, acting in concert. The murmuration again: each group responding to the others, the whole becoming something none could be alone.

Part 18 — The Irish Diaspora Sovereign Fund: A Call to the 70 Million

Every generation of Irish people has left something behind for the next. This generation can leave something different: not emigration, but ownership.

What this chapter is about. A constructive proposal: a sovereign fund drawing on the global Irish family, modelled on what other small nations have built, to fund a different kind of future. It is the book turning from critique to building.

“The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best time is now.” — proverb

Around 70 million people worldwide claim Irish descent, including some of the wealthiest individuals and most influential communities in the United States, Canada, Australia and the United Kingdom. This has always been framed as sentiment — heritage, culture, St Patrick’s Day. It has rarely been framed as what it also is: capital, expertise and organised political will. That changes now. This is a direct call to the Irish diaspora worldwide.

What already exists — and what is missing

Ireland already has the architecture of sovereign investment, set out in full in Part 4E: the ISIF (€28 billion, with its “double bottom line” mandate of commercial return and Irish economic activity, already invested in Cork Harbour offshore-wind infrastructure); the Future Ireland Fund (projected at €100 billion by 2035); and the Irish Diaspora Loan Fund, a Central Bank-authorised vehicle for overseas investors. The architecture exists. What is missing is democratic governance, community-first investment criteria, and diaspora participation with real decision-making power — not residency in exchange for capital, but co-ownership of the commons.

The problem with what exists

The Future Ireland Fund is built on volatile FDI corporate tax receipts from the same three US companies that account for 46% of Ireland’s corporate tax take. It is managed by civil servants. It has no citizen governance, no democratic mandate, no community-first criteria and no diaspora voice. It will be drawn down from 2040 for pensions and health — passive financial investment, entirely inside the existing FDI dependency model. It does not own a single acre of Cork Harbour foreshore on behalf of the Irish public.

The proposal: a People’s Sovereign Fund

The Ripple Rebellion calls for a new instrument — a democratically governed, diaspora-enabled Irish People’s Sovereign Fund, distinct from the ISIF and the Future Ireland Fund, with the following mandate:

  • Community-first investment: strategic assets — Port of Cork land, Cork Harbour foreshore, grid-connection capacity, water infrastructure, former State-linked industrial land — acquired or held on behalf of the Irish public, with first-refusal rights for local communities.

  • Diaspora participation with governance rights: Irish people worldwide contribute capital and hold genuine voting rights in a Citizens’ Assembly governance structure — not passive investors but active co-owners of the commons.

  • Returns reinvested in public goods: housing, renewable energy, marine restoration, water infrastructure, community enterprise. No extraction without reciprocity.

  • Permanent Citizens’ Assembly oversight: a standing democratic body with diaspora representation governs investment, sets the community-benefit-first criteria, and holds the fund’s managers to account.

  • Ethical exclusion criteria modelled on Norway: no investment in companies that fail to disclose water and energy use, that are subject to unresolved environmental prosecutions, or that lobby against the public interest on transparency.

The precedent is Norway’s — a 67% state stake in its own resource wealth, converted into a $1.5 trillion fund owned, in effect, by every citizen (set out in full in Part 4E). Ireland has generated extraordinary corporate tax receipts for thirty years and converted almost none of it into strategic public ownership. This fund is the mechanism to begin that conversion — starting not with government permission but with citizen action.

The call

“To every Irish person worldwide who has watched from abroad as Cork Harbour is handed to foreign corporations without community consent, as the water that fed the farms and the rivers that ran through the parishes is taken without disclosure, as the grid capacity built by public investment is allocated without democratic mandate — this is your invitation. Not to donate. To own. To govern. To hold the commons on behalf of the people who live there and the people who left.”

The Ripple Rebellion will be establishing a working group to develop the governance model for an Irish People’s Sovereign Fund. If you are a member of the diaspora — in Boston, Chicago, London, Sydney, Toronto, Auckland — and you want to be part of building this, contact us at theripplerebellion.github.io. If you are a lawyer, an economist, a financial regulator, a community organiser, or simply someone who left and never stopped caring: this is the work. Join it.

Part 19 — We Don’t Wait: Starting Our Own Permanent Citizens’ Assembly

The Government has been asked twice to establish a Citizens’ Assembly on AI and strategic resources. Twice it has declined. We are not asking a third time. We are starting one.

What this chapter is about. This chapter argues for not waiting for permission — for citizens convening their own permanent assembly to deliberate and decide. It is democracy taken into the community’s own hands.

“We are the ones we’ve been waiting for.” — June Jordan

The honest legal position

You cannot legally constitute a formal Citizens’ Assembly in the Irish state sense without a government mandate. That is true. It is also irrelevant. What you can do — what citizens have always done when the state refuses to listen — is constitute a deliberative body yourself, on the same methodology, with the same rigour, producing documented recommendations that cannot be ignored because they are publicly visible, professionally designed, and built on genuine community participation. Futureproof Clare did not have a government mandate. They had six years of organised, documented, expert-informed community opposition that forced the Ennis case through every legal avenue available and changed the national conversation. That is what a citizen-led assembly does.

The Ripple Rebellion is calling for the establishment of a permanent, citizen-led, professionally designed deliberative assembly on strategic resources — starting in East Cork, scalable nationally — modelled on Ostbelgien in Belgium — the world’s first permanent citizens’ assembly — and on the deliberative methodology Ireland itself helped pioneer.

Why it has to be respected voices, speaking publicly

The inside channels were tried, and they were ignored — twice. This is the part worth dwelling on. The case for a Citizens’ Assembly here was not made by activists alone. It was made by the Oireachtas’s own Joint Committee on artificial intelligence, in public, across two successive interim reports — which recommended, among other things, an AI oversight office independent of the department that promotes the industry, and a Citizens’ Assembly to decide the direction. Both were set aside. When a parliament’s own committee can do the work, in the open, and be ignored not once but twice, it tells you the formal channels of accountability are not merely slow — they have stopped functioning as a brake. What remains, then, is credibility spoken aloud: independent, respected voices — academics, scientists, jurists, the people the public still trusts — saying plainly and publicly that the process has failed. A minister can ignore a citizen’s letter. It is far harder to ignore the person who designed the very democratic method the State is now refusing to use, or the UN Special Rapporteur who came, looked, and said the same. That is why this Part turns to one of them.

Professor David Farrell — why him, why now

Professor David Farrell, Chair of Politics at University College Dublin and a member of the Royal Irish Academy, is — more than almost anyone — the reason Ireland learned to do this at all. With Jane Suiter he co-founded We the Citizens in 2011, the country’s first deliberative experiment; he led the academic group behind the Constitutional Convention (2012–14); and he was research leader of the Citizens’ Assembly (2016–18) whose deliberations led, by popular vote, to marriage equality and the repeal of the Eighth Amendment. He and Suiter received the Brown Democracy Medal for that work; he has served on the stewarding group of the Scottish Citizens’ Assembly; and he co-authored the field’s standard account of how such bodies should be designed. His current academic work is focused on citizens’ assemblies and on the case for giving them a more permanent, embedded role. This is not a cold call. It is an alignment of interests between a citizen campaign that needs the expertise and the scholar who helped write the method — asked to help build a permanent model here, in the country where he lives and works, beginning with the community most directly bearing the cost of decisions made without its consent. (The working precedent we would adapt is Ostbelgien in Belgium, the first permanent citizens’ assembly of its kind — proof the model can be made standing, not merely occasional.)

The open letter — a public invitation

The Ripple Rebellion is publishing an open letter to Professor Farrell, reproduced in full in Appendix B. The essence of it: you, more than anyone, taught Ireland to trust its own citizens with its hardest questions; the State asked for an assembly on AI and strategic resources and got a committee — twice — then left its central recommendations unimplemented; we are not asking the government a third time. We are asking you — to help build a permanent model here, in East Cork, that does not report once and dissolve. The nitrogen fixers are ready. We just need the design.

What a citizen-led permanent assembly looks like

Based on the Ostbelgien model, adapted for Ireland:

  • A permanent Council of 25–30 citizens selected by sortition — random selection from the electoral register, stratified for age, gender, geography and socioeconomic background. Not elected. Not appointed. Drawn by lot, as juries are.

  • The Council sets the agenda — it decides what questions the full assembly examines. It is not handed questions by government. It asks its own.

  • Full assemblies of 50–100 citizens convene to examine specific questions — hearing expert testimony, deliberating, producing documented recommendations.

  • Mandatory response: any body — government, regulator, corporation — receiving a recommendation must respond publicly within 90 days, stating whether it will implement it and why or why not.

  • Diaspora seats: a designated number reserved for diaspora members, selected by sortition from a diaspora register. The global Irish community has standing in decisions about the Irish commons.

  • Youth seats: minimum 30% of Council seats reserved for citizens under 30. They inherit every decision; they have the most at stake.

  • Public transparency: all proceedings live-streamed, all submissions published, all expert testimony disclosed. No Chatham House rules. No private sessions.

  • Starting remit: strategic resources — land, water, grid, foreshore, health data. Exactly the questions the government has refused to put to a Citizens’ Assembly.

Building in the safeguards: why sortition alone isn't enough

Sortition is one of the better-known structural defences against groupthink that exists — a randomly selected body is, by design, not a room of people who already agree, which is groupthink's precondition. But it isn't automatic immunity, and a well-designed permanent assembly has to build the safeguards in rather than assume they'll hold.

A separate body whose only job is watching for blind spots. Political theorist Terrill Bouricius has proposed splitting a sortition system into distinct councils rather than one body doing everything: one to set the agenda, one to draft policy, one to deliberate and vote — and a fourth, an Oversight Council, whose sole mandate is monitoring the others for drift, capture, and groupthink. Applied here: the people deliberating on a water policy should not also be the ones policing their own process. That job belongs to a separate, rotating panel.

The oversight layer must be drawn, not appointed. An unelected "expert" layer handpicked by whoever currently holds power is exactly the mechanism that produced the KPMG problem documented elsewhere in this booklet — a neutral-sounding body that is neutral in name only. The fix is consistent with everything else in this book: draw the oversight panel too, from a transparently nominated, credentialed pool, term-limited and rotated, never appointed by the government it's meant to be watching.

The culture inside the room matters as much as the structure around it. Organisational psychologist Adam Grant's research draws on a finding from Charlan Nemeth: genuine minority dissent produces sharper, more thorough group thinking than an appointed "devil's advocate" ever does — people unconsciously discount a disagreement they know is being performed rather than felt. Applied here: an assembly needs real dissenting voices seated at the table, not a designated skeptic everyone quietly files away as playing a part. Grant's second finding belongs in the assembly's ground rules directly: a functioning group isn't one where everyone agrees, it's one where anyone can say "I think we're wrong" without being punished for it — psychological safety, not harmony. The practical technique: those chairing deliberation invite criticism of their own framing out loud, first, rather than waiting to be challenged, which resets who's allowed to disagree with whom.

A genuine warning, not a footnote. France ran a serious, well-designed Citizens' Convention on Climate in 2019–20. President Macron promised implementation "sans filtre" — without filter. Most of it was quietly diluted or dropped. Sortition alone does not protect against political will simply withdrawing afterward. Only a binding mechanism — recommendations enacted unless overridden by a supermajority, on the record, at political cost — closes that gap. The mandatory 90-day public response set out above is this campaign's answer to that exact failure.

An unexpected piece of independent evidence for sortition, from a completely different field. A 2025 AI safety study — Mazeika, Hendrycks and colleagues, "Utility Engineering," discussed fully in this booklet's AI ethics case study — tested exactly this design principle on a machine rather than a country. Researchers found that rewriting an AI model's internal values to match a demographically representative, census-sampled citizens' assembly reduced the model's measured political bias and generalised reliably to situations it hadn't been trained on. A research team with no connection to deliberative democracy, working on a completely different problem, independently arrived at sortition as the most defensible reference point for fair judgment. That is not proof this design works for a country. It is a second, unrelated field converging on the same answer.

First steps — concrete and immediate

  • Write to Professor Farrell at UCD (david.farrell@ucd.ie, per his public profile). The Ripple Rebellion open letter starts that conversation; others can write too — the more voices, the stronger the signal.

  • Establish a founding working group in East Cork — five to ten people, one meeting, one question: who do we want on the first Council, and what is the first question we want examined?

  • Contact Dr Clodagh Harris at UCC — named among Professor Farrell’s research collaborators, Cork-based, equally expert and closer to hand.

  • Contact Malcolm Byrne TD — chair of the Oireachtas AI Committee that has already recommended a Citizens’ Assembly; political cover to support a citizen-led initiative.

  • Register the assembly publicly — announce it on theripplerebellion.github.io, on Substack, through Bluesky and Instagram. Visibility is the accountability mechanism.

  • File an FOI to DETE asking what action has been taken on the Citizens’ Assembly recommendation from the Oireachtas AI Committee’s two reports. The refusal to act becomes part of the assembly’s founding justification.

The first question for the assembly

“Should strategic national resources — land, water, grid capacity, foreshore and health data — be subject to a permanent community-benefit-first test before being allocated to private commercial use? And if so, who should govern that test, and on what terms?”

That question has never been put to Irish citizens through a deliberative process. It should be. We are going to put it. A note on timing: the National Nature Restoration Plan consultation closes 1 July and the National Bioresources Strategy on 22 July — the first opportunities for a nascent assembly to make a collective submission, demonstrating from day one that this body produces concrete civic action, not just deliberation.

Part 19B — Why We Need Sharper Satire, Out Loud, More Often

Laughter shows us the ridiculousness of what we're allowing to happen. Ireland has a real tradition of this. We need more of it than we currently have.

A sketch, first.

Court One. Mary Lou McDonald has just questioned a call.

MARY LOU: That ball was in. I saw it land inside the line, clean as anything.

SIMON HARRIS (walking over, picking up the umpire's chair, sitting in it): I've reviewed the footage.

MARY LOU: You're not the umpire. You're playing.

SIMON HARRIS: I've appointed myself umpire for this specific point. It's a proportionate, temporary measure, and I want to be very clear that I take the integrity of the sport extremely seriously. (pause) The ball was out.

MARY LOU: Who reviewed your review?

SIMON HARRIS: I did. Thoroughly. I'd note that raising this now, at this point in the match, is not helpful to players who are simply trying to get on with the game.

(He climbs back down, picks up his racket, serves.)

Why this small, silly image is actually the whole book in miniature.

It's funny because it's a compressed, deniable version of something this booklet has spent hundreds of pages proving isn't a joke: a system where the person whose call is being questioned is also, functionally, the one who gets to rule on whether the question was fair. Ireland does not currently have a standing, independent mechanism for adjudicating whether a government minister's account of their own decision holds up in the moment it's challenged — no equivalent of Hawk-Eye, no neutral chair with the authority to simply say "the replay shows otherwise" and have that be the end of it. What exists instead is a minister answering for himself, in real time, to an opposition TD who has no independent umpire to appeal to beyond the same chamber, the same clock, and whatever the next day's papers decide to run with.

This is not a uniquely Irish or uniquely political failure. It is, in fact, the exact same structural bias medical science has spent thirty years documenting in a completely different field, and the parallel is worth taking seriously rather than treating as a rhetorical flourish. A 2017 Cochrane Review — the gold standard of medical evidence synthesis, examining 75 studies covering more than 8,000 clinical trials — found that industry-sponsored drug and device trials are significantly more likely to report results favourable to the sponsor's own product (a 27% higher likelihood) and favourable overall conclusions (a 34% higher likelihood) than independently funded trials. The finding that should trouble anyone tempted to explain this away as coincidence: the bias held even after controlling for every standard methodological safeguard — blinding, randomisation, dropout tracking. The companies grading their own trials weren't cutting corners on the visible rules. They were still, somehow, more likely to conclude their own product worked. Something about being both the subject and the judge of the test produces a favourable verdict, reliably, regardless of how carefully the visible rulebook is followed.

That is precisely the shape of "the ball was out." It is the same shape as a report on data-centre policy commissioned from the firm that stands to benefit from the policy it recommends. It is the same shape as a regulator whose board includes people from the industry it regulates, examined earlier in this book. Self-adjudication does not require anyone to cheat. It only requires the same person to hold both the racket and the whistle, and to be trusted, by default, not to notice which one they're reaching for.

Why satire is the right tool for this particular problem, and not just a nice-to-have. A straight accusation of bias invites a straight denial, and the argument stalls exactly where every Dáil exchange like the one above stalls: he says, she says, no umpire. Satire does something a direct accusation can't — it doesn't ask permission to make its case. It shows the mechanism working, absurdly, in miniature, and lets the audience's own laughter be the verdict no minister gets to rule out of order. That's not decoration on top of the activism in this book. It's a different, faster route to the same conclusion the Cochrane data took thirty years and 8,000 trials to prove.

What satire is actually up against, and why certainty is the real obstacle. The reason a self-appointed umpire can get away with "the ball was out" isn't stupidity on anyone's part. It's that most of us process politics through the part of the brain built for tribal survival, not truth-seeking — and once an idea becomes attached to which team we're on, defending it stops being about evidence and starts being about loyalty. The writer Tim Urban, in his book What's Our Problem?, draws a useful distinction between two kinds of intellectual culture. An Echo Chamber treats its beliefs as sacred: questioning them isn't received as a disagreement, it's received as an attack on the group itself, and conformity is what earns you belonging. An Idea Lab, by contrast, treats beliefs as what Urban calls "hypotheses written in pencil" rather than "sacred objects carved in stone" — something to be kicked around, tested, and updated without anyone's identity being on the line. His point that matters most for this chapter: political alliance is one of the fastest routes from Idea Lab thinking into Echo Chamber thinking that exists, precisely because a party or a movement asks for loyalty, and loyalty and truth-seeking pull in different directions the moment the evidence turns inconvenient. A voter, a TD, or a party press office defending "the ball was out" because their own side made the call isn't reasoning about tennis. They're protecting the group.

This is precisely why satire matters more than a fact-check here, not less. A fact-check arrives as an argument, and an Echo Chamber is built, by design, to repel arguments that threaten its sacred ideas — that's what the tribal loyalty is for. A joke arrives differently. It gets past the same defence because laughter happens before the identity-protecting part of the brain has decided whether to get defensive. You can laugh at "the ball was out" and only notice afterwards that you've just agreed the umpire shouldn't have appointed himself. That's not a lesser form of accountability than a Dáil committee. In an environment full of Echo Chambers, it may be one of the few forms that still gets through.

A necessary correction, in the spirit of holding this chapter to its own standard. It's often repeated that RTÉ rejected Father Ted for being too hard-hitting. That's a myth — Graham Linehan has said directly, more than once, that the show was never pitched to RTÉ at all; he and Arthur Mathews were already living and working in London and took it straight to Channel 4. Worth using the real quote instead, because it makes a sharper and truer point than the myth does: asked why they didn't even try RTÉ, Linehan said "why would we give the scripts to RTÉ? That would be like giving them to Waterford Glass." Not rejection — an assumption, made without even asking, that the institution wasn't the kind of place capable of producing something that sharp. That assumption is worth sitting with rather than laughing off, because Scrap Saturday's fate a few years earlier is exactly the kind of history that would teach a working satirist to make it. An institution doesn't have to formally reject satire to develop a reputation for being unable to hold a mirror up to its own shadow. Sometimes it just needs one well-remembered cancellation, and everyone stops bothering to ask.

Ireland has done this before, with real teeth, and it's worth remembering exactly what happened. Scrap Saturday (RTÉ Radio 1, 1989–1992), written by Dermot Morgan and Gerry Stembridge, did precisely this kind of structural satire — Taoiseach Charles Haughey and his advisor P.J. Mara as a running double act, genuinely popular, genuinely biting. It was axed amid national outcry and open accusations of political interference. Morgan himself called the decision "a shameless act of broadcasting cowardice and political subservience." RTÉ's own response has become a small monument to institutional non-denial: "The show is not being axed. It's just not being continued!" That's not folklore. That's a documented instance of the state broadcaster's appetite for sharp political satire meeting a wall, and it's worth this book stating plainly: something did happen to that tradition, on the record, and it hasn't fully come back.

What's currently mainstream leans affectionate rather than structural — Oliver Callan's Callan's Kicks and Mario Rosenstock's Gift Grub (running continuously since 1999, still daily, over a million listeners between them) are both real, both popular, both largely impressions and warmth rather than indictment. The sharper edge that does exist right now lives mostly outside the mainstream schedule: Blindboy Boatclub's independent podcast, drawing explicitly on Flann O'Brien's satirical tradition, doing genuine structural commentary on housing and health, reaching a quarter of a million listeners a week without needing a broadcaster's permission slip. Waterford Whispers News does the same in print, independently. The appetite clearly exists. The talent clearly exists. What's thin on the ground is a sustained, mainstream, structural home for satire willing to climb into the umpire's chair itself and ask who put it there.

One family, both halves of this chapter. David Andrews — the Fianna Fáil TD and twice foreign affairs minister who backed George Colley against Haughey in 1979 and spent a decade on the backbenches for it, who campaigned for the Birmingham Six and the Guildford Four when that wasn't the safe position to hold, who was one of four signatories of the Good Friday Agreement — died on 30 June 2026, aged 91. His eldest son is David McSavage, creator of The Savage Eye, one of the sharpest political satirists this country has produced.

McSavage said something about his father, once, in an interview, that carries more weight than any tribute paid during funeral week, because it comes from a man who built a career being savage about everyone, including himself, and chose not to be savage about this: "My father may be stiff but he is dead honest, really honest. Never implicated in any wrongdoing." That's the verdict of a professional satirist, on his own family, under no obligation to be kind. It stands.

McSavage's own satire is exactly the sharper, structural tradition this chapter is arguing for, not the affectionate impressions kind — he's said so himself, contrasting his work directly against Oliver Callan's: "His stuff is more tribute. Satire is more vicious than that." The Savage Eye built sketches like "He Fixed the Road," lampooning TDs who treat a minor pothole repair as a major electoral achievement — clientelism, reduced to the exact absurd transaction it actually is. He has turned that same satire on his own family's party, Fianna Fáil's own culture of insider dealing and nepotism, not sparing the tradition his father spent a career trying to hold honest from the inside.

The Irish Examiner's own assessment of McSavage stands as a fair verdict on the whole gap this chapter is naming: "He has a fearlessness — a knack for saying uncomfortable things — that is rare on the Irish comedy scene." Rare. Not absent — David Andrews' own son proves it isn't absent. Just rare, and rarer than it should be, in a country with this much practice at both producing sharp satire and quietly deciding not to broadcast it.

The call. Ireland needs more of this — pointed, structural, unafraid satire, not just impressions of politicians being charming, but work willing to draw the tennis match, name the self-appointed umpire, and let an audience laugh at exactly how familiar the shape is. Laughing at "the ball was out" doesn't excuse the call. It does something a strongly worded FOI request can't: it makes the absurdity impossible to un-see.

Part 20 — The Unifying Thread: Kinship, Commonality, and the Courage to Act

Imagine it is our first day on Earth. We arrive knowing nothing of the borders, the tribes, the labels. We see only this: one planet, one fragile film of water and air, and seven billion others who breathe it with us.

What this chapter is about. The book’s closing argument, drawn from biology: the mitochondrion, once a stranger, became part of every cell through symbiosis, not extraction. This chapter holds that image up as the alternative to the parasite — difference integrated and made stronger, not exploited and hollowed out.

“I am because we are.” — Ubuntu philosophy

The cosmic perspective

The atoms in your body were forged in the hearts of stars that died before the Earth existed. The carbon in your hands, the iron in your blood, the calcium in your bones — all of it was cooked in stellar furnaces and scattered across space, to be gathered up, briefly, into you. This is not poetry. It is physics. As Carl Sagan and Neil deGrasse Tyson have put it, we are literally made of star-stuff, and we are a way for the universe to know itself.

And the air carries the same truth. A single breath contains so many molecules that, statistically, you draw into your lungs atoms once breathed by people who lived thousands of years ago — by the teachers and prophets of every tradition, by the unnamed millions who came before. You share your breath with the whole of human history, and with every stranger alive now. At the atomic root, there is no them. There is only an us so vast it includes everyone who has ever lived. This is the cosmic perspective, and it is the antidote to the smallness that division requires. Kinship is not an aspiration. It is the literal, physical fact of our existence. We had only to look up to see it.

Collaboration at the root of life

Look smaller, and the same truth appears. Inside almost every cell in your body sits the mitochondrion — the tiny engine that turns food and oxygen into the energy that keeps you alive. It was not always part of us. The leading scientific account, the theory of endosymbiosis, holds that mitochondria were once free-living bacteria — a separate organism, perhaps even a threat — that was absorbed into another cell and, instead of being destroyed, was kept. Two different life forms, two opposing kinds, became one. The most fundamental engine of complex life on Earth is not the product of conquest. It is the product of collaboration — of difference held together rather than eliminated.

The scientist who championed this idea, Lynn Margulis, had her paper rejected by some fifteen journals before it was published.
