The notion that cooperation, not just competition, drove evolution was ridiculed for years. She was the non-conformist told no — who turned out to be foundationally, textbook-changingly right. The same shape as Galileo. The same shape as Catherine Corless. The same shape as every nitrogen fixer in this booklet. The truth that difference can be held in symbiosis rather than destroyed was itself a truth that had to survive being suppressed.

The Seven Gifts, and the traditions that share them

In the Catholic Sacrament of Confirmation, a young person stands at a threshold and is told they have been given seven gifts: wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety and wonder. What strikes anyone who looks closely is that these are not Christian inventions. They are human ones — discovered independently, named differently, recognised across every tradition that has ever tried to describe what a flourishing human being looks like, and by the secular and scientific worldviews besides. Ten traditions reaching for the same seven qualities, because they are simply what a good human life requires, in any language, under any sky.

Gift (Confirmation) Abrahamic traditions Eastern & Indigenous traditions Secular & humanist thought
Wisdom Sophia (Christian); Hikmah (Islam, Al-Hakeem); Chokhmah (Judaism, first sefirah) Viveka (Hindu discriminating wisdom); Prajna (Buddhist insight); Zhi (Taoist/Confucian); elder and land wisdom (Indigenous) Clear thinking unclouded by tribalism or wishful thinking — what Feynman called “not fooling yourself”
Understanding Nous (Christian); Fiqh (Islamic deep comprehension); Binah (Jewish integrating understanding) Bodha (Hindu awakening); Sampajañña (Buddhist clear comprehension); Gian (Sikh spiritual knowing) Sitting with complexity and contradiction without collapsing into false certainty; perspective-taking across difference
Counsel / Right Judgment Discernment of the right path under God across all three Abrahamic traditions Action aligned with dharma, the Tao, and right relationship with the whole Ethical reasoning grounded in evidence and consequence; the Golden Rule, present in every tradition
Fortitude / Courage The courage of the prophets and martyrs across Christianity, Islam and Judaism Virya (Buddhist energy/effort); Kshatriya dharma (Hindu); the warrior-protector ethic (Indigenous) Moral courage — acting rightly despite fear or cost
Knowledge Knowledge of creation as a path to the divine across the three Abrahamic faiths Jnana (Hindu spiritual knowledge); direct knowing in Buddhist and Taoist practice Empirical knowledge — the patient accumulation of what is demonstrably true
Piety / Reverence Devotion and right relationship with God across the Abrahamic traditions Bhakti (Hindu devotion); reverence for all sentient beings (Buddhist); filial reverence (Confucian) Reverence for life, the planet and the dignity of others — awe without a deity
Wonder / Awe Awe before the infinite across Christianity, Islam and Judaism Awe before Brahman, the Tao, the vast interdependence of all things The cosmic perspective — awe before a universe in which we are made of star-stuff and briefly conscious of it

Why we divide — and how we stop

If the kinship is so deep — atomic, cellular, moral — why do we divide so readily? The unsettling answer comes from social psychology. In the minimal-group experiments of Henri Tajfel, people sorted into groups on the most trivial basis imaginable — a coin toss, a preference for one painter over another — almost immediately began favouring their own group and discriminating against the other. No history. No real difference. No conflict of interest. Just a label, and the ancient machinery of us-and-them switched on.

This is the regulatory capture that happens inside each of us. The same way a lobby group captures a regulator, a system of belief can capture our innate empathy — teaching us, from the outside in, that some other group is the threat, the lesser, the enemy. Our natural kinship is real, but it can be hijacked by anyone who benefits from our division. Most people, left alone, are good and want connection. Division is not our nature. It is something done to us — and therefore something we can undo.

The answer is not to abandon your heritage — to flatten everyone into a grey sameness that honours nothing. The answer is the opposite, and more demanding. Hold your own tradition, your own faith, your own roots as sacred — and then use exactly how sacred it is to you as the bridge to understanding how sacred another’s is to them. You know what it is to hold something holy. So does the person across the border. That shared capacity for reverence — not the particular object of it — is the kinship. The depth of your own belonging is the very thing that lets you honour another’s.

The monoculture and the ecosystem — one last time

This is the whole pattern, seen from above. Division is the monoculture: a single crop, a single tribe, a single permitted truth, fragile and dependent and hostile to everything outside its row. Kinship is the ecosystem: many different forms, held in symbiosis, each enriching the soil for the others, resilient precisely because it is diverse. The mitochondrion in your cell, the breath you share with strangers, the seven gifts in ten traditions — all of it says the same thing. Life flourishes through difference held together. It collapses through sameness enforced. This is KAIROS: not clock-time, but the right time, the opportune moment. And the moment is now.

Be savage — for a reason

There is a fierceness this work requires, and it should not be apologised for. Everyone should be prepared to be savage — in the truest sense of the word: fierce, untamed, unwilling to be managed into silence. Gráinne Mhaol was savage. Catherine Corless was savage in her patience. The nitrogen fixer, quietly refusing to stop, is savage in the most enduring way there is.

But here is what separates the good from the bad, and it is the only thing that does: not whether you are fierce, but what your fierceness is for. Savagery in service of extraction, of domination, of the monoculture — that is the cruelty this whole booklet documents. Savagery in service of the commons, of kinship, of the unseen majority, of the children who inherit the water — that is courage. The capacity is the same. The reason is everything. Be savage. Be fierce. Be unmanageable. For the right reason.

Believe in the nitrogen fixers — because you are one. Step into your agency — because no one is coming to do it for you, and because the kinship that binds us means your refusal to stay silent makes the next person’s refusal possible. We are made of stars. We share one breath. We reach, in every tongue, for the same seven gifts. The divisions were taught to us. The kinship is real. Act like it is.

On Being Believed Late, or Never

There is a particular kind of exhaustion that has nothing to do with sleep. It's the exhaustion of saying a true thing to a system built to hear it and watching the system decide, politely, to hear something else instead.

I know that exhaustion first-hand. I said something true, once, as a child, to an adult whose job was to protect me. What came back wasn't protection. It was a rearrangement — the room reshaping itself around a version of events that was easier for the adults in it to live with than the truth I'd handed them.

I didn't learn that lesson from a report or a committee stage. I learned it the way most people learn it — early, and in a room where the adults were the ones with the power to look away. The child who says something true and watches the room rearrange itself around a version of events that's easier to live with — that child grows up knowing exactly what institutional silence feels like from the inside, because she was the silence's first practice run.

So when I hear "we'll look into it," I don't hear a promise. I hear a familiar shape. I've watched that shape before — in a kitchen, in an office, in a statement read out by someone whose job was reassurance, not truth. Ireland has an entire architecture built from that shape: the Magdalene laundries, Bessborough, the symphysiotomies, the Ryan Report's eight volumes of "we're sorry" arriving decades after the sorry was needed. The pattern isn't that nobody knew. The pattern is that enough people knew, and decided knowing wasn't the same as acting.

I bring that history to this campaign not as metaphor but as method. When I look at a regulator who marks its own homework, a report commissioned from a firm that profits from the thing it's assessing, a lobbying register with the important names missing — I'm not reasoning my way to suspicion. I'm recognising a room I've been in before. The tell is always the same: the process moves at exactly the speed required to look like process, and stops exactly short of the point where something would have to change.

This is not a claim that any one person did any one specific wrong thing. It's older and worse than that, and more useful, because it doesn't need a villain to be true. It's a claim about what institutions do by default, absent someone forcing them not to — which is why the fix isn't a better man in the job. It's daylight. It's the footnote, the FOI, the paper trail that outlives the news cycle. Publishing what actually happened, in order, with dates, is the only apology I've ever seen an institution give that it couldn't later walk back.

I know what it costs to be the one who keeps saying "investigate this" long after everyone else has moved on. I know what it is to be disbelieved, and I know what it is to finally, quietly, believe myself. That's the clover again, if you want the older image: the thing that grows back first isn't loud, and it doesn't ask permission. It just keeps fixing what the ground needs, whether or not anyone's watching. I survived the silence once. That's the whole reason I'm no longer willing to let it happen to a river, a townland, or a child who isn't me.

Is Any of This Criminal — and How Do We Fight It?

The honest answer is also the most damning: most of it is perfectly legal. That is the scandal — not a crime. The grandfather clause, the fast-tracked planning, the confidentiality language lifted from the lobby, the directive that overturned a council’s ban, the advice of the State’s own committee ignored twice — these were lawful acts. Lobbying is legal, and mostly registered. A regulator applying the rules as written is doing its job. A minister making captured policy has failed democracy, not the criminal code. To stand up in public and brand named politicians, regulators or lobbyists criminals for making bad-but-lawful policy would be both wrong and ruinous: it is unprovable, it is defamatory, and it is the exact dismissal our opponents are waiting for. We do not hand it to them. The lawfulness is the indictment. A system in which all of this can be done without breaking a single law is a system whose laws were written for the wrong people.

Where a real crime may exist, it is narrow, specific, and never ours to declare. An operator abstracting groundwater without the EPA licence the law now requires; a breach of a licence condition; a development built without permission; a discharge that kills a river — these are genuine offences, with genuine penalties, prosecuted by the EPA, the local authorities, Inland Fisheries Ireland or the DPP. Our task is never to pronounce guilt. It is to gather the evidence and demand the investigation — was this examined, by whom, and with what result? — because a demand they cannot answer is far more powerful than an accusation they can swat aside. And for the gravest environmental destruction, the law is catching up: the EU’s Environmental Crime Directive, and the rising doctrine of ecocide set out earlier, are the one genuinely criminal frame this campaign can point to in good conscience.

So how do we fight a side that treats the rulebook as a thing to be bent? Be clear-eyed about them first. They will read every line of the law for the gap in it; they will redraft, exempt, reclassify, and time things to fail, to a degree people of ordinary good faith simply will not credit — because decent people assume the rules mean what they say. That assumption is the opening they rely on.

But the answer is not to become them. We do not bend the law back. We do the thing they have abandoned, and cannot match: we master it, and we use it in daylight. Every legitimate lever, worked with the same discipline they bring to evading it — the judicial review that asks a court whether a decision was lawful; the deadline that must be met; the information that must be released; the complaint that must be heard in Brussels; the technicality that, used in good faith, is mightier than it looks. Their power comes from bending rules in the shadows — and that bending is precisely what creates the cracks, the unlawful decision and the breached directive and the missed obligation, that lawful action then prises open. Good faith is not our handicap. It is our durability. They can be beaten in court; a movement that acts cleanly cannot be discredited.

And there is one more weapon, the oldest there is: knowing the pattern. Manipulation on this scale is neither new nor improvised. It runs to a playbook — the charm, the reframing, the slow erosion of a boundary, the insistence that your alarm is the problem rather than the thing alarming you. Those who have met that pattern before, on whatever scale and at whatever cost, carry a hard-earned literacy in it. They know where to look. They recognise the move before it lands. And they understand the one thing the manipulator most needs them not to understand: that he cannot win unless the people he would use agree to disappear — to be depleted, to be made pawns in the service of someone else’s ends. That literacy is bought with pain, and it is, for all that, the sharpest instrument there is. This book is the fruit of it: the pattern named and written down, so that the wisdom does not stay locked inside one person, but becomes something the whole field can use.

The Playbook, and Why You Should Read It Yourself

Start with a hunch, because that is how this began. Reading the KPMG report — The Value of Data Centres to Ireland, commissioned by the Department of Enterprise — something felt wrong before anything could be proven. It was in the pattern: who was asked, and who was not; the uniformly sunny framing; a 177-page document that found room for a single sentence on water and built its headline on a survey base of five. It read less like an assessment than like a brochure. The hunch was simple: this was not written to find out. It was written to reassure.

And the hunch held up — not through any secret, but through the firm’s own shop window. KPMG does not merely study the data-centre sector; it sells services into it: tax and regulatory advice to the operators, a dedicated data-centre advisory practice. A firm assessing the value of an industry it also earns its living from is not a neutral referee. That is not an allegation of dishonesty; it is a structural fact. And it is the question the public is entitled to ask of every report of this kind: who paid for this, and what were they hoping it would say?

Because the KPMG report is not an exception. It is a genre. This is how the “value of” report works, across every sector and every border. A company or a government with something it wants to build commissions one of a small number of firms — KPMG, Deloitte, PwC, EY, and the economic-consultancy houses beside them — to quantify the benefits. The report is built, very largely, on figures and assumptions supplied by the people who commissioned it. It rarely sends anyone to knock on the doors of the community that will host the thing and count the externalities — the water, the noise, the air, the lost night sky, the strain on the road. It tends, with a consistency that is itself worth noticing, to arrive at a large and flattering number. And it commands a fee that runs comfortably into six figures — paid, when the commissioner is the State, out of your taxes, for a document that tells your government what the industry already wanted it to hear.

You do not have to take this on faith — and that is the whole point. Go and look. Search for the same firms’ “economic impact” and “value of” reports in other countries — “[firm] data centre economic impact [country]” — and lay them side by side. You will find the Irish report has cousins in the United Kingdom, the United States, the Nordics, Australia, the Netherlands: the same structure, the same flattering arithmetic, the same near-silence on water and grid and community, the same handful of firms. It is one playbook, reprinted with the place-names changed. The same is true well beyond data centres — the identical method turns up in commissioned “value of” studies across agri-food, energy, aviation and mining. Spotting the pattern needs no insider and no leak. It needs an afternoon and a search bar. That is the work this booklet is asking of you: not to trust its conclusions, but to go and confirm them — and to find what it missed.

And set the economics of the report beside the economics of an ordinary life. Six figures for a document that reassures. Meanwhile the people in whose name the “jobs” are forever invoked work longer for less, and find — despite every hour of effort — that the sums no longer meet at the end of the month. The wealth the report celebrates is real. It simply does not arrive in their pockets. That is the quiet arithmetic of the plantation: the land worked by the many, the yield gathered by the few, the ledger kept somewhere else.

Which is the older pattern underneath all of it, and the reason for the name on this cover. A plantation is a monoculture — one crop, owned from afar, the soil mined until it fails, the people who tend it kept dependent and quiet. A field run that way looks productive for a season and dies over a generation. Nature’s answer to the exhausted field is not another monoculture. It is the nitrogen fixer — the clover working unseen at the roots, asking no one’s permission, putting back into the soil the very thing extraction takes out, until the whole field can grow again. The plantation model is being laid over this country a second time, now in fibre and concrete and cooling water. It can be refused the way it was refused before: not by waiting for rescue, but by enough ordinary people working quietly at the roots until the ground itself changes.

And it has to be now — not as a slogan, but as a fact about timing. The water this country is already short of, with close to a third of the treated supply lost to leaks before it ever reaches a tap, is the same water the next phase is built to draw on. And even where the pipes are not yet running, the permissions and the infrastructure that would run them are being laid as you read this. Every month of delay is capacity signed away under agreements written to outlive the governments that grant them. The young who will inherit the thinner version of this country; the rivers and the species that cannot file an objection; the habitats that do not get a second draft — they are the constituency this is for. Use the toolkit. Take the agency that is already yours. Act — and if you cannot find the will to do it for your own sake, do it for the children who will not otherwise get the chance.

A Note on the Limits of This Book — and the Call That Follows

Be clear about what this book is. It is the work of one person — pattern-spotting, and the best consolidation I could manage of evidence that is already public, gathered by a single citizen with no team, no budget, and no access to what is kept confidential. That is its honesty and its limit both. Treat it as a floor, not a ceiling. Where it is wrong, correct it; where it is thin, deepen it; what it missed — and it will have missed a great deal — find. There is every chance that what is gathered here is the tip of the iceberg.

Because of the scale of the thing it describes. This is an extraction industry operating at a size that is genuinely hard to fathom, and Ireland is squarely in its sights: English-speaking, on the western edge of Europe, lightly regulated, and governed by a system that has shown itself willing to reshape its own rules to accommodate it.

And here the government must not be let off. The fragmentation described throughout this book is not weather. It is architecture — and the architecture was built, and is maintained, by the State. The fast-tracked planning route; the grandfather clause; the confidentiality language adopted from the lobby almost word for word; oversight split across so many bodies that no one is charged with adding up the total; the advice of the Oireachtas’s own committee ignored not once but twice. These are choices, made by government, in daylight. The honest charge is not bribery — there is no evidence of that, and the campaign does not make it. It is the quieter, more corrosive thing: complicity through accommodation, a State that wrote the rules the industry asked for and called it progress. That is not an act of God to be mourned. It is a decision — and decisions can be reversed by the people who made them, who must be made to own them.

So this cannot rest with one person, or one country. It needs curious minds everywhere — here, and in every country this machine has entered or will enter next. Use this book. Use whatever other evidence is out there. Spot the patterns. Connect the dots the structure is built to keep apart. Pre-empt the next move, because the moves repeat — the names change, the playbook does not. Share what you find. Escalate it, through the external and international routes set out in Part 6, to the bodies equipped to act. And refuse the bewilderment in advance.

We do not need everyone to do everything. We need enough people to do something — out loud, where others can see — because that is how refusal spreads. The machine’s one true vulnerability is daylight.

Be the daylight.

Closing — The Nitrogen Fixers

White clover works underground, without permission, and cannot be paved over. Neither, in the end, can we.

What this chapter is about. The final word, and the book’s central image: the white clover, the nitrogen fixer, working underground without permission to enrich the whole field. It is who this campaign is, and who you are invited to be.

“What we do now echoes in eternity.” — attributed to Marcus Aurelius

White clover does not announce itself. It works underground, taking nitrogen from the air and fixing it into the soil — feeding the whole field without permission, asking nothing, and unable to be permanently destroyed. Cut it, spray it, pave it, and where there is a crack it returns, and the ground is richer for it. That is the model for everything in this booklet.

None of what is asked here requires you to be a hero. It requires you to be clover. File the one request. Read the one planning file. Test the one stream. Write the one letter. Switch the one default. Turn up to the one meeting on the wet Tuesday. Each act is small and quiet and underground, and each one feeds the field — and the moment enough of the unseen majority do it together, the monoculture above has no choice but to change, because that is simply how soil, and societies, are fed.

The discernment all of this turns on — which fights are yours to win, and which to release — has an older name:

God, grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change, the courage to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference.

— the Serenity Prayer, commonly attributed to Reinhold Niebuhr

The harbour is still there. The field is still alive under the concrete that has not yet been poured. The window — the kairos — is still open. We are the nitrogen fixers. Move.

Glossary — Plain Words for a System Built in Jargon

This booklet uses planning, energy, legal and finance language, because that is the language the system is built in. But ordinary people should be able to read it without a law degree, an engineering degree, or a background in corporate finance. So this glossary is split in two: the core terms you will meet again and again, and a shorter list of other terms you may come across. The first list is the one most readers need. The second is there as backup, not homework. You do not need to learn any of it before you start — dip in when a word stops you.

Core terms

Aarhus Convention. An international agreement that gives the public three rights on environmental matters: to information, to take part in decisions, and to access justice. In plain terms: if you are expected to live with the consequences, you are entitled to see, question, and challenge the decision.

AIE — Access to Information on the Environment. A legal route that lets you ask public bodies (and sometimes private ones acting for them) for environmental information. If a project affects water, land, air, or biodiversity, this is one of the simplest ways to ask what is being done and why. Distinct from FOI, and often more powerful for environmental questions.

An Coimisiún Pleanála. Ireland’s national independent planning body, which decides planning appeals and rules directly on large “strategic infrastructure” projects. It replaced An Bord Pleanála on 18 June 2025 under the Planning and Development Act 2024. If a local council grants or refuses permission, this is the body an appeal goes to.

Appropriate Assessment. A legal test, under the EU Habitats Directive, of whether a plan or project could damage a protected wildlife site or species. If a development may affect a Natura 2000 site, this assessment is one of the strongest safeguards in the whole system — and one of the most important things to check was actually done properly.

Beneficial owner. The person or group who really owns, controls, or profits from a company — even when their name is not the one on the public paperwork. The name on the form is not always the power behind it.

Commercial confidentiality. A claim that certain information should stay private because it is commercially sensitive. Sometimes legitimate. Sometimes it becomes a shield that stops the public seeing how much water, power, or pollution a project really involves.

Cumulative impact. The total effect of several projects or pressures added together. A single development can look manageable on paper and still be harmful once its water use, power demand, traffic and emissions are added to everything else in the same place. (See the separate note on why this matters so much.)

Data centre. A building or campus full of computer servers that store, process and move digital information. Not “just a building” — an industrial-scale user of electricity, water and land that needs a major grid connection to function.

EIA — Environmental Impact Assessment. A process meant to examine a project’s environmental consequences before it is approved, so harm is not approved first and discovered later.

EirGrid. Ireland’s electricity transmission system operator — it runs the high-voltage grid and connects large users to it. When a data centre needs power, this is who decides whether and how it connects.

EPA — Environmental Protection Agency. Ireland’s environmental regulator. It licenses certain activities, monitors pollution, and (under the Water Environment (Abstractions) Act 2022) registers larger water abstractions, including some private boreholes.

FOI — Freedom of Information. A law that lets you ask public bodies for their records — what was discussed, decided, or withheld. One of the simplest tools the public has.

Grandfather clause. A rule that lets older applications continue under older standards. Sometimes fair; sometimes a loophole that locks in weaker rules for projects already in the pipeline, exempting them from a new requirement everyone else must meet.

Grid connection. The link between a site and the electricity network. For a data centre this is often the real tell: if the power demand is enormous, the connection is not a detail — it is the project.

Hyperscale. Computing infrastructure built at the largest industrial scale. The word sounds neutral; in practice it means very large demand for power, cooling and land.

Natura 2000. The EU network of protected nature sites (under the Birds and Habitats Directives). Cork Harbour includes such sites. If a project may affect one, that should trigger serious scrutiny, not a box-ticking exercise.

Planning permission. Legal approval to build or change how land is used. It is meant to be the public’s checkpoint — not a rubber stamp.

Regulatory capture. When a regulator or department starts serving the industry it is meant to oversee instead of the public. It rarely looks like corruption. Usually it looks like convenience, speed, and giving industry “certainty.”

Renewable energy requirement. A rule that a project source some or all of its electricity from renewables. The real question is never whether the rule exists on paper, but who it actually applies to — and who has been exempted.

Shell company. A company that exists on paper but has little or no real business of its own — perhaps no staff and no real office, its purpose being to hold land, sign contracts, or keep the people behind a project one step back from view. Shell companies are legal and often used for ordinary reasons, such as separating financial risk or organising investment. But the same structure can also make public scrutiny harder: if the name on the planning file is only a shell, the public may not be looking at the real decision-maker at all. The point is not that a shell company proves wrongdoing — it does not — but that when ownership sits behind several layers, the honest response is to check who is really there (the Companies Registration Office and the beneficial-ownership register are where you start), not to assume.

SPV — Special Purpose Vehicle. A company set up for one specific project or asset, common and legitimate in infrastructure and finance. Like any shell structure, it can also make it harder for the public to see who ultimately controls a development.

Strategic infrastructure. A planning route for large projects deemed important enough to be fast-tracked — going directly to An Coimisiún Pleanála rather than starting with the local council. In practice it can mean less local control and a higher bar for ordinary communities trying to object.

Thermal discharge. Heat — often as warmed water — returned to the environment after cooling or industrial use. If it is not monitored or disclosed, the public cannot tell what temperature burden a project places on local rivers or the harbour.

Transparency. The public’s ability to see what is happening, who is doing it, what it costs, and who benefits. Without it, accountability becomes guesswork.

Water abstraction. Taking water from a river, lake, aquifer or other source. For large industrial users the scale matters, because it can affect supply for everyone else. In Ireland, larger abstractions are regulated under the Water Environment (Abstractions and Associated Impoundments) Act 2022.

Water Usage Effectiveness (WUE). A measure of how much water a data centre uses relative to its computing output (litres per kilowatt-hour). If a facility will not publish its WUE, the public is being asked to trust a water-using industry without the numbers.

Other terms you may see

BESS — Battery Energy Storage System. A large battery installation that stores electricity and releases it later. Part of the energy transition in itself — but in planning files it can also appear alongside, or as cover for, a much larger power demand.

Capacity market. A system that pays generators to be available to produce electricity, even when they are not actively generating.

Colocation. A data-centre model where different customers house their own equipment in the same shared building.

Curtailment. A deliberate, temporary reduction in electricity generation or use, usually to protect the grid when supply and demand do not match.

Data sovereignty. The principle that data should be governed under the laws and interests of the people or state where it is collected or stored.

Digital hub. A softer-sounding label sometimes used in place of “data centre,” often to make a project sound smaller, cleaner, or less industrial than it is.

Greenwashing. Making something sound environmentally responsible while the underlying practice stays harmful.

Interruptible load. Power demand that can be switched down at short notice when the grid is under strain.

IPC / IE licence — Integrated Pollution Control (now Industrial Emissions) licence. An EPA permit for certain polluting or large industrial activities.

LEAP — Large Energy Users. Shorthand in this booklet for the policy framework governing very large electricity users such as data centres, and the terms (or missing terms) attached to them.

Load factor. A measure of how steadily a site draws electricity over time.

Local authority. Your city or county council — usually where ordinary planning decisions begin, though strategic-infrastructure projects can bypass it.

MVA vs MW. Two ways of stating electrical power. MW (megawatts) is the straightforward measure of real power demand. MVA (megavolt-amperes) measures “apparent” power and can make a load look less plain in a planning file even when the real demand is just as large. When comparing, MW is usually the clearer number.

On-site generation. Power produced at the site itself — gas turbines, batteries, backup. Sometimes genuine resilience; sometimes a way to move a project around grid limits.

Private wire. A direct electricity connection that bypasses the public grid, letting power move in ways that are harder for the public to see.

Revolving door. The movement of people between public office, regulation, lobbying and industry, in ways that can blur whose interests are being served.

State capture. When private interests shape public rules so thoroughly that the state begins working for them rather than the public — regulatory capture grown to the scale of government itself.

Waste-heat recovery. Capturing heat from a process (such as data-centre cooling) and putting it to use, for example heating nearby buildings. A genuine good where it is required and done — and notably absent from Ireland’s terms.

Programmable money. Money that carries its own built-in rules about how it can be used. Ordinary cash has no rules attached — once it is in your hand, you decide. Programmable money is different: the system it runs on can be set to allow some purchases and block others, to work for some people and not others, or to switch off entirely, automatically and instantly, according to rules written by whoever controls it. Sometimes that is sold as safety (blocking fraud or sanctioned payments). The concern is simpler: money that can be switched off is money someone else ultimately controls.

The “third lock.” A plain way to picture programmable money. A cash payment has two parties — you and whoever you pay. Electronic payments add a third party (a bank or processor) that can, in limited cases, block the transaction — a “third lock.” Until now that lock has been slow and human. The worry is an automated third lock: software that checks and permits or refuses every transaction in real time, with no person in the loop and little recourse if it gets you wrong.

CBDC — Central Bank Digital Currency. A digital form of official state money, issued directly by a central bank. Because it can be designed to be programmable and fully trackable, its privacy and control implications depend entirely on how it is built — which is why the design choices, made before any public vote, matter so much.

Digital euro. The European Central Bank’s CBDC project, in preparation and aimed at a possible launch toward the end of the decade. Whatever its final design, it is the form the programmable-money question will take in Ireland.

Stablecoin. A privately issued digital coin pegged to keep a stable value (usually to a national currency like the dollar). It runs on the same programmable technology as a CBDC, but is issued by a private company rather than a state — which can mean the same control with less public accountability, not more.

Distributed ledger. A shared digital record of transactions held across many computers rather than in one central book. It is the underlying technology for stablecoins and digital assets — and once money sits on such a ledger, it becomes possible to make it programmable.

A closing thought: if a term still sounds harmless after you have read its definition, ask who benefits from that harmlessness. In this field, the softest words often hide the hardest machinery.

Appendix A — AIE & FOI Request Pack: Ready-to-Send Templates

Every template below is a specific, answerable question put to the body that holds the record. Filing them is itself the campaign — the answers, or the refusals, become the evidence. Fill in the bracketed details, send by the body’s online form or email, and keep a dated copy of everything.

What this chapter is about. The action pack: every Freedom of Information and Access to Information on the Environment request, ready to copy and send, each shown in a green box. This is where reading becomes doing.

A live note, as this booklet goes to print. In July 2026, SIPTU and all 19 ICTU-affiliated unions began balloting public service workers for strike action, after the previous pay agreement expired with no successor talks underway. SIPTU General Secretary John King pointed directly at the same asymmetry this booklet has documented throughout — accusing the Government of moving quickly on "business concerns" (a hospitality VAT cut, supports for farming and haulage) while stalling on public sector pay. The unions' own published demands go further still: they explicitly call for "agreement on the management of AI in the public service" as a necessary part of any settlement. Readers of this booklet and members of these unions are, without either side necessarily having noticed yet, asking the same question of the same government at the same moment. Solidarity here costs nothing and may cost the reader nothing more than saying so publicly.

A resource for taking this further. The methods in this booklet — verified public records, FOI and AIE requests, cross-referencing corporate filings, following the money through subsidiaries and grandfather clauses — are the same methods professional investigative journalists use every day. The Global Investigative Journalism Network publishes free, practical guides built for exactly this kind of work, including an eighteen-chapter guide on investigating AI and technology, and reporting toolkits on documenting the human and environmental costs of AI infrastructure. Anyone who wants to go further than this booklet's own templates, or who wants to teach these methods to someone else, will find real, professional-grade instruction there, free of charge.

Why this work falls to citizens at all. It is worth being honest about why a booklet like this exists in the first place, assembled at a kitchen table rather than in a newsroom. The traditional watchdogs are under real strain. Irish local newspapers have closed or consolidated for two decades; national newsrooms run on shrinking staff; and much of what remains depends, directly or indirectly, on advertising from the same large commercial interests whose conduct most needs scrutiny. None of that makes professional journalists complicit — most are doing serious work under difficult conditions — but it does mean there are fewer of them, with less time, chasing more stories, at exactly the moment the machinery described in this booklet has grown more complex and more dispersed. The confidentiality carve-outs, the grandfather clauses, the strategic-infrastructure designations that lift decisions out of local hands, the narrowing of judicial review — each was designed, or has functioned, to make oversight harder. When the formal channels of accountability are narrowed and the professional watchdogs are thinned, the work does not disappear. It falls to whoever is willing to pick it up.

That is not a lesser form of accountability. Under these conditions, it may be the most durable one. A citizen with a library card, a broadband connection, and the patience to file an FOI and wait cannot be laid off, cannot be captured by an advertiser, and cannot be quietly told by an editor that a story is not worth the legal risk. What the citizen investigator lacks in resources, they make up in the one thing that cannot be bought or pressured: independence. The discipline this booklet has argued for throughout — verify every load-bearing claim against a named primary source, indict the system rather than the individual, keep clean hands so the work cannot be dismissed — is precisely what turns a citizen's anger into evidence that holds. Done carelessly, citizen journalism is just rumour with a bigger audience. Done with discipline, it is the accountability the system was quietly reshaped to avoid. This booklet is an argument that the second kind is not only possible, but available to anyone willing to do the slow, unglamorous, un-ignorable work of checking the record and writing down what is actually there.

A resource for going further. The Global Investigative Journalism Network (gijn.org) runs an active, free "Tech Focus Project" on precisely this kind of work — including "The Investigative Agenda for Tech and AI Journalism" and a practical toolkit called "Uncovering AI's Human Cost." Anyone using this booklet's templates and wanting to build genuine investigative skill beyond them will find real, professional-grade training there, free, built by and for exactly this kind of citizen and journalist accountability work.

“The smallest deed is better than the greatest intention.” — proverb

AIE requests — Access to Information on the Environment

To EirGrid: “Please provide the energisation date and grid-connection details for the EPark / Little Island data-centre connection (DC1), and all connection-offer correspondence for the EPark campus.”

To Cork County Council: “Please provide the environmental assessment, including any Appropriate Assessment screening, for the March 2025 extension to the EPark (Evergreen Business Park, Wallingstown) permission, and all sustainability or environmental commitments made by the applicant in support of the EPark applications, including DC3.”

To the EPA: “What environmental assessment was conducted for the water allocation and any thermal-discharge consent associated with the EPark data-centre campus at Little Island?”

To your local authority (generic): “For [named development], please provide: the environmental assessments carried out; whether an Appropriate Assessment was undertaken; and any water-usage, thermal-outfall, renewable-energy or reporting conditions attached to the permission.”

Water, aquifers and the EPA register — how to check, and a template

The question — is a data centre drawing from the ground, and does anyone official know? — is answerable by anyone, in an afternoon, for free. Here is exactly how.

Step 1 — Identify the site and operator. Get the planning reference from your local authority’s online planning portal (search by area or address) or from An Bord Pleanála. Note the operator’s company name.

Step 2 — Check where its water comes from. Planning files and Environmental Impact Assessment Reports usually state the intended water source — mains (Uisce Éireann) or self-supplied (borehole/groundwater). If it is mains, the volume question goes to Uisce Éireann (expect commercial-confidentiality refusals). If it is groundwater, go to Step 3.

Step 3 — Search the EPA water abstraction register. It is public. Go to epa.ie, search “water abstraction register,” and look for a registered or licensed abstraction at or near the site. Any abstraction of 25 m³/day or more should be registered; 2,000 m³/day or more should be licensed.

Step 4 — If nothing appears, ask. A large self-supplying site with no register entry is a legitimate question, not an accusation. Send the request below to the EPA.

Step 5 — Record and share. Save every reply — or non-reply. A documented silence is itself evidence. Add it to your local file and to the Ripple Register.

AIE request template — to the EPA

To: [EPA AIE officer — see epa.ie/access-to-information] Subject: AIE request — water abstraction at [site / townland / planning reference]

Dear Sir/Madam,

Under the Access to Information on the Environment Regulations, I request the following environmental information held by the Agency in relation to [name of facility / operator / townland / planning reference]:

  • Whether any water abstraction at or serving this site is registered or licensed with the EPA under the Water Environment (Abstractions and Associated Impoundments) Act 2022, and if so, the registration or licence reference(s).

  • The water body or bodies (surface water, or groundwater/aquifer) from which abstraction is authorised.

  • The maximum authorised abstraction volume, in cubic metres per day.

  • Any environmental impact assessment or significance determination carried out in respect of that abstraction.

  • If no abstraction is registered or licensed at this site, confirmation of that fact — and whether any exemption, pending application, or notified-but-not-yet-determined abstraction applies.

I would be grateful for the information in electronic form. I understand the Agency must respond within one month. Please confirm receipt.

Yours faithfully, [Name]

Note: AIE covers environmental information held by public authorities, including the EPA and local authorities. There is no fee to make a request, though a charge may apply for photocopying or very large volumes. If information is refused on commercial-confidentiality grounds, you may request an internal review, and after that appeal to the Commissioner for Environmental Information.

FOI requests — the AI Advisory Council, KPMG and the revolving door

To DETE (foi@enterprise.gov.ie): “On what date, by whom, and on what stated grounds did the AI Advisory Council cease to operate? Please provide all correspondence, minutes and submissions relating to the decision, including any role of the relevant Minister of State.”

To DETE: “What action has been taken on each recommendation of the Oireachtas Joint Committee on AI’s interim reports — specifically (a) independence of the National AI Office from DETE, (b) a Citizens’ Assembly on AI, and (c) placing the AI Advisory Council on a permanent statutory footing?”

To DETE: “What is the total cost to the public purse of the KPMG report ‘The Value of Data Centres to Ireland’, including all fees? Please provide the full contract, tender documents, terms of reference, and all correspondence between DETE and KPMG relating to the commission.”

To DETE: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by KPMG in relation to this commission, given its existing commercial relationships with data-centre operators, and what due diligence was conducted before the commission was awarded?”

To DETE: “What are the names, prior employment histories and subsequent employment declarations of all members of the AI Advisory Council, and all officials involved in commissioning the KPMG report? Please include all conflict-of-interest declarations.”

To DETE: “What post-employment notifications have been made by former DETE officials involved in data-centre policy to SIPO in the last five years?”

To the Department of Justice: “What consideration was given to the AI Advisory Council’s February 2025 advice paper classifying facial-recognition technology as high-risk before the Garda Recording Devices Bill was advanced? Please provide the analysis relied upon.”

To the CRU: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by CRU board members and senior officials in relation to CRU/2025/236? What post-employment notifications have been made to SIPO by former CRU officials in the last five years?”

To Cork County Council: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by planning officials in relation to the EPark applications, including the March 2025 extension? What post-employment notifications have been made to SIPO by former planning officials who worked on these files?”

To An Coimisiún Pleanála: “What conflict-of-interest declarations were made by inspectors and board members in relation to the Ennis / Art Data Centres application?”

Position paper — the four gaps in the Regulation of AI Bill 2026

This is not a one-line template but a short submission you can adapt and send. The formal pre-legislative-scrutiny window (Joint Committee on Enterprise, Tourism and Employment) closed on 13 April 2026, with hearings in May. So this paper is written for the routes still open: a submission to the standing Joint Committee on Artificial Intelligence; material for a TD to table as committee-stage amendments or Parliamentary Questions; or a direct representation to members of the Oireachtas. Every claim in it is drawn from a named source — the CSO, EirGrid, Uisce Éireann, the CRU, DETE’s own briefing note, and reporting by the Financial Times.

Framing line (use as your opening): “This submission does not oppose the Bill. It identifies four gaps that would otherwise leave Ireland — the most data-centre-intensive economy in Europe — governing what AI is permitted to do while remaining silent on what it is permitted to consume, and dispersing oversight so widely that no single body is charged with seeing the whole.”

Gap 1 — the resource and environmental cost of AI is absent. The Bill governs AI systems but is silent on the electricity, water and grid capacity the systems consume — in a State where data centres took 22% of metered electricity in 2024 (CSO). Ask: that the Bill, or guidance under it, create a domestic duty to assess and report the energy and water footprint of AI infrastructure, coordinated between the AI Office, CRU, EirGrid, the EPA and Uisce Éireann, building on the EU Energy Efficiency Directive (2023/1791).

Gap 2 — no one is charged with seeing the whole. The “distributed model” spreads enforcement across c.15 sectoral regulators; the AI Office coordinates but is not itself a market-surveillance authority and holds no sector of its own (DETE briefing note, 4 March 2026). Ask: that the AI Office be given an explicit statutory duty to produce a periodic cumulative assessment of AI’s impact across energy, water, rights and competition combined — coordination without a duty to synthesise is a switchboard, not oversight.

Gap 3 — independence, resourcing, and who designed it. The Office sits under DETE, whose stated imperative is to keep Ireland “the location of choice for technology companies in the EU”; its CEO is Minister-appointed. The same briefing note records that the Office’s design was shaped “through the work of KPMG in assisting DETE” — in the same period that KPMG withdrew a report on agentic AI over fabricated case studies (Financial Times, 2026) and faced an ASIC investigation. Ask: strengthened structural independence; a transparent statement of resourcing for each competent authority’s AI functions; and disclosure of the scope of external consultancy advice relied on in the Office’s design and the assurance applied to it. (See the FOI templates above for the KPMG contract and terms of reference.)

Gap 4 — no place for affected communities, or for what comes next. The framework is built around providers, deployers and regulators, with little role for affected communities, and — by the EU AI Act’s definition — it governs only machine-based AI, leaving emerging biological computing (neural organoids) entirely outside its reach. Ask: a transparent channel for communities and the public to raise concerns about AI systems and their supporting infrastructure; and a standing horizon-scanning duty for the AI Office to advise the Oireachtas where emerging forms of computation fall outside the statutory definition.

Closing line: “Ireland is about to legislate for AI from the position of the most AI-intensive economy in Europe. That is precisely the position from which the resource question can least afford to be left out. Submitted in good faith; the author welcomes correction of any point shown to be inaccurate.”

FOI & Parliamentary Question — the National Training Fund and the research pipeline

To DFHERIS (Department of Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science): “Please provide a full breakdown, by research theme, of the National Training Fund surplus allocation and of the €4.55 billion Tertiary Sector Capital Investment Plan 2026–2030, identifying all amounts allocated to artificial intelligence, semiconductor, high-performance-computing and data-infrastructure research. Please also provide the list of industry and multinational partners in the Rinn research centres and the financial contribution of each, together with all records of consultation with employer, SME, trades and farming representative bodies before the National Training Fund (Amendment) Act 2025 was enacted.”

Parliamentary Question (for a TD — for example Liam Quaide TD): “To ask the Minister for Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science the respective amounts of the National Training Fund surplus and the Tertiary Capital Plan allocated to artificial-intelligence, semiconductor and high-performance-computing research; the multinational corporations partnered in the Rinn research centres; what consultation was undertaken with the small-business, trades and farming bodies who pay the training levy before the surplus was directed; and if he will make a statement on the matter.”

FOI / AIE requests — local government and the digital-infrastructure agenda

The mayoralties of Cork are annual, largely ceremonial offices, and a transparency request is a routine civic instrument, not an allegation against any office-holder. Note the division of responsibility: the Lord Mayor of Cork heads Cork City Council, while EPark Little Island and most large data-centre sites fall within Cork County Council. Send each request to the body that actually holds the record.

To Cork City Council and Cork County Council: “Please provide all records — minutes, correspondence, emails, briefing notes and diary entries — of meetings or communications since [date] between elected members or officials (including the Office of the Lord Mayor of Cork and the Office of the Mayor of the County of Cork) and (a) any data-centre developer or operator, (b) any hyperscale technology company, and (c) JCD Group in relation to EPark / Little Island. Please include records relating to any 2026 event convened to ‘align digital innovation with climate and infrastructure delivery’ in Cork.”

To Cork City Council and Cork County Council: “Please provide records of any lobbying contacts concerning data-centre or digital-infrastructure development since [date], and any returns made in respect of the Regulation of Lobbying Act, so they can be cross-referenced against the public lobbying.ie register.”

How to check who owns a site — the CRO and RBO method

This is a general, reusable method for finding out who is on the public record as owning and controlling the company behind a development. It does not, by itself, prove concealment or wrongdoing — most developments use ordinary limited companies, and that is lawful and normal. The purpose is simply to know who you are actually dealing with. State only what the record shows; where ownership is genuinely unclear, that is a fact or a fair question, not an accusation.

The Companies Registration Office (CRO — core.cro.ie) is Ireland’s statutory public register of companies. Every Irish limited company must file there: its registered name and number, registered address, directors and secretary, and annual returns. Most basic information is free to view after you register a free account; full documents cost a few euro. The Register of Beneficial Ownership (RBO — rbo.gov.ie) records the living people who ultimately own or control a company (25% or more) — which the company name alone may not reveal.

The method, step by step:

  • Get the exact legal name from the planning file — not a trading name or a press report. Take the precise applicant name as written on the application.

  • Search the CRO. Note the company number, registered address, date of incorporation, company status, and the directors and secretary.

  • Read what those facts mean — carefully. A very recent incorporation, a registered address that is an accountant’s or solicitor’s office, or directors who appear on many unrelated companies are all common and often perfectly ordinary. They are not evidence of wrongdoing. They tell you whether the visible company is likely the real decision-maker or a vehicle with someone behind it. Note what you find; do not editorialise it.

  • Follow the ownership upward. Check filings for a holding company or parent named as shareholder; if there is one, search that company too, and repeat until you reach a named individual or a foreign parent the Irish register cannot see beyond.

  • Check the RBO for the registered beneficial owners. If the company has not filed, or the trail runs offshore, that itself is a factual finding worth noting.

  • If the trail goes dark, that is the finding — state it as such. “The site is held by company X, whose registered owner is company Y, whose beneficial ownership is not disclosed on the Irish register” is a true, defensible sentence. “X is a shell company hiding the real owners” is not, unless the record shows it. Keep to the first kind of sentence.

  • If you need more, use the lawful tools — an FOI request to the council for correspondence, or an AIE request for environmental information.

The discipline in one line: the CRO and RBO answer “who is on the record here?” — not “what are they hiding?” Find out who is really there, write down only what the register shows, and let any silence in the record speak for itself.

Live appeals to watch — and how to add your voice

Two appeals before An Coimisiún Pleanála show the system working in real time, and both are worth watching (and, where still open, making an observation on — anyone can, for a small fee, within the stated window):

Premier Periclase “CORE”, Drogheda, Co. Louth (case ref. 323134). An Taisce’s appeal against the Louth County Council grant — on Climate Act emissions, water, and cumulative-impact grounds — was lodged 24 July 2025; a decision is expected. To track it: search the case reference on pleanala.ie. To learn from it: the case file is a live worked example of the camouflage pattern — an “energy centre” of nine gas engines fronting a 25.5 MW data centre — and a model of the grounds an appeal can stand on.

Bessborough, Cork (residential, not a data centre — included because the method is the same). Appeals against the 140-apartment grant over likely unmarked children’s burials were decided on 9 July 2026: An Coimisiún Pleanála substantially upheld the permission, 106 units of 140. The fight now moves to a possible Compulsory Purchase Order and judicial review — see the full account and escalation options above. This is a separate cause from the data-centre fight; it is listed here only because the method — permission granted on a developer-led search, upheld on appeal — is identical.

The general method. When a decision matters to you and an appeal or observation window is open: find the case on pleanala.ie or the council portal, note the closing date, and submit a written observation citing the specific planning and environmental grounds (cumulative impact, water, emissions, Appropriate Assessment) before the deadline. Keep your dated acknowledgement. One observation, filed on time, is part of the public record the board must consider.

FOI requests — IBEC, pay transparency and consultancy spend

To the Department of Children, Disability and Equality: “What representations did IBEC make regarding the timeline for transposing the EU Pay Transparency Directive? Please provide all correspondence, meeting minutes and submissions from IBEC since January 2025.”

To the Department of Enterprise: “What representations did IBEC make regarding data-centre water disclosure, the LEAP architecture and CRU/2025/236? Please provide all correspondence, meeting minutes and submissions.”

To DETE: “What is the total cost of all external consultancy reports commissioned on data centres, AI policy and the Large Energy User Action Plan since 2020? Please list each report, its author, its cost and its terms of reference.”

To the Department of Finance: “What assessment did the Department make of the public interest case for lifting fixed-salary restrictions on the chief executives of banks that received State support during the financial crisis? Please provide all records, submissions and correspondence considered in the decision to lift these restrictions in 2025, including any submissions received from AIB, and any assessment of the effect on the chief executive’s remuneration.” AIB itself, now fully in private ownership, is not subject to FOI — but the Department’s own decision to remove the restriction that made a fourfold pay increase possible is a matter of public record, and the reasoning behind it is a fair public question.

Cross-reference every answer against lobbying.ie and SIPO.ie. A meeting that should be registered and is not is itself a story. And remember the clean-hands rule: ask the question on the record rather than asserting the conclusion. The refusal is as revealing as the answer.

Calls to Action — The Print-and-Keep Pages

Print these pages on their own. This is the whole campaign reduced to things one person can do. It is built as a stand-alone bundle: print just these pages, pin them to a noticeboard, hand them to a neighbour, or bring them to a council meeting. Each card says what to do in a line or two, and where in the booklet the template or detail lives. You do not need to do all of it. Find the one card that fits your week, do it, and tell one other person what you did. That is how a count moves — not with a hero, but with the next person, and the next.

Live versions online. A companion GitHub page will carry the clickable, copy-and-send versions of everything below — ready-made AIE and FOI letters, the consultation links, the noise and water protocols, and the Ripple Register. Print the paper; send the link. Start at theripplerebellion.github.io. (Address and one-page summary to follow.)

This week — the five-minute actions

Make a consultation submission — Pick one open national consultation and send a few honest paragraphs before it closes. It is the cheapest formal say you will ever get. Detail: Parts 1 & 6.

Switch one digital default — Move one of search, email, messaging or social off the monopoly this week. One default, one less stream of money and data. Detail: Part 11.

Watch your council’s planning portal — Set keyword alerts for the camouflage words — data storage, digital hub, BESS, light-industrial technology park, private wire — so nothing slips through quietly. Detail: Parts 4F & 4G.

Keep the public record — Note which TDs and councillors backed disclosure and which did not. Memory is accountability. Detail: Part 16.

This month — the deeper digs

File an AIE request — Email the body that holds the data and ask for water abstraction, thermal discharge and emissions at a named site. Secrecy cannot survive the right question. Template: Part 6; Appendix A.

File an FOI request — Ask for the contracts, the cost of the KPMG commission and the conflict-of-interest declarations. The refusal is as revealing as the answer. Template: Part 6; Appendix A.

Trace the CRO / Tailte Éireann filing — Look up the company named on an application, who its directors are, and what land has changed hands. Find the multinational behind the shell. Detail: Parts 4B & 4F.

Check the revolving door — Search SIPO.ie for the people who moved between the regulator and the regulated. Capture leaves a paper trail. Detail: Part 4B.

Run a citizen water or noise survey — Collect the evidence no official body is collecting — thermal and water-quality samples, or the low-frequency hum that dB(A) readings miss. Use the protocols. Detail: Part 6; the noise protocol.

Read a suspect site — the global toolkit — Work the eight moves: the registry trail, grid and water filings, planning euphemisms, the blueprint tells, satellite heat, port manifests, and a coalition to pool it. Log it in the Ripple Register. Detail: Part 4G.

If you are inside the industry, or a professional

Refuse from within — Use lawful non-cooperation where you can, and back the worker fund that makes refusal survivable. The system needs your hands; you can withhold them. Detail: Part 9.

Use your profession — Lawyers, engineers, planners, scientists: lend the campaign the one verified fact or document only your field can produce. Clean hands need expert hands. Detail: Part 16.

When the local door is shut — escalate

Climb the escalation ladder — Take a single local dossier upward: an AIE under Aarhus, an EIA / Habitats challenge, a submission to the UN Special Rapporteur, a complaint to the financiers — several at once, from one body of evidence. Detail: Part 4G; Parts 6 & 6B.

Use the outside referees — Escalate past captured domestic channels to the Aarhus Compliance Committee, the European Commission under the Habitats Directive, and the UN Special Rapporteur. Detail: Part 6B.

Use the ecocide instrument (EU 2024/1203) — Where the harm is catastrophic and irreversible, gather the evidence and ask a national prosecutor to consider the new ecocide-comparable offence. It points at decision-makers personally. Detail: Part 4G.

Build the movement

Push for a Citizens’ Assembly — Add your name to the call for a citizens’ assembly on AI and data centres — a mechanism above the single-minister bottleneck. Detail: Part 19; Appendix B.

Mobilise a school — One assembly, one submission, one murmuration — before the summer holidays. Detail: Part 7.

Write to your MEP — Ask for mandatory disclosure, enforcement of the Habitats Directive, and a sovereign-AI dividend for citizens. Detail: Part 10.

Back the People’s Sovereign Fund — Support the mechanism that keeps the value in Irish hands and makes refusal affordable. Detail: Part 18.

Reward the good actors — Choose, cite and champion the disclosing, reciprocal, restrained providers and states. Ask why Ireland cannot do what Denmark, Finland and Singapore already do. Detail: Part 4H.

One row. This week. Then tell one other person what you did. The table that follows is the same set of actions in one glance — use whichever form suits you.

Appendix — The Action Index: Every Action, What It Tackles, and Where to Find It

This is the navigation map for everything in this booklet you can actually do. Each row names an action, what it is meant to tackle, and where to find the detail or the ready-to-send template. Section references are used instead of page numbers, because page numbers shift between the Word file, the PDF and print, while the section names do not; any exported PDF carries the live page numbers in its contents list. You do not need to do all of it. Find the one row that fits your week, and start there.

What this chapter is about. An index of every action in the book — what it tackles and where to find it — so nothing is lost in the length and every reader can find the step that fits them.

“Action is the foundational key to all success.” — attributed to Pablo Picasso

Action What to do What it tackles Where / template
File an AIE request Email the public body; ask for water abstraction, thermal discharge and emissions at a named site Environmental secrecy Part 6; Appendix A (AIE templates)
Trace the water blind spot Ask Uisce Éireann for a named site's mains use, check the EPA abstraction register, then file an AIE + Aarhus challenge for the facility-level figures sealed by the EU confidentiality clause Information split across bodies and sealed on arrival Part 3; Parts 6 & 6B
Demand a legislative systems-analysis unit Ask TDs and committees to establish a standing function that reads every bill, delegated act and miscellaneous-provisions clause for cumulative, downstream effect Fragmentation that hides the whole from those voting on it Part 3
File an FOI request Send the template asking for contracts, the KPMG commission cost and declarations Hidden contracts, the revolving door Part 6; Appendix A (FOI templates)
FOI + PQ — National Training Fund Ask where the ~€1.8bn levy went; have a TD table the parliamentary question A levy redirected without consulting those who pay it Part 3; Appendix A (NTF FOI & PQ)
FOI — Research Ireland / the Rinn centres Ask for the industry-partnership, IP-ownership and co-funding terms of the €460m Rinn centres, and what consultation the levy-payers were given Public research money tilted to industry priorities; who owns the output Part 3; Appendix A
FOI / AIE to your local authority Request your council’s data-centre / “digital hub” files, pre-planning notes and grid correspondence Council-level decisions made quietly Appendix A (local-government pack)
FOI — IBEC, pay transparency, spend Ask who funds the lobby and what the State pays consultants Who shapes the policy Appendix A (IBEC pack)
Check the revolving door Search SIPO.ie for who moved between regulator and regulated Regulatory capture Part 4B
Trace the CRO / Tailte Éireann filing Look up the company on the application, its directors and its land The shell, and the multinational behind it Parts 4B & 4F
Watch your council’s planning portal Set keyword alerts for the camouflage vocabulary; read the blueprints Camouflaged applications Parts 4F & 4G
Make a consultation submission Write a short submission before the open deadline closes Your formal say before it shuts Parts 1 & 6
Use the outside referees Escalate to the Aarhus Committee, the European Commission (Habitats) and the UN Special Rapporteur Captured domestic channels Parts 6B & 4G
Run a citizen water test Take and log samples where nobody else is measuring Missing thermal / water-quality evidence Part 6
Run a citizen noise survey Use the protocol to capture the low-frequency hum dB(A) readings miss The hum standard readings miss Part 6 (noise protocol)
Switch one digital default Move one of search / email / messaging / social off the monopoly The money and data flowing to the giants Part 11
Refuse from within / worker fund If you are inside the industry, use lawful non-cooperation; back the fund Labour complicity Part 9
Back the People’s Sovereign Fund Support the mechanism that makes refusal survivable Keeping value in Irish hands Part 18
Mobilise a school Run one assembly and one submission before the summer holidays Youth mobilisation Part 7
Write to your MEP Ask for disclosure, the Habitats Directive and the sovereign-AI dividend EU-level leverage Part 10
Watch who lobbies the AI rules Search the EU Transparency Register for who is lobbying the AI Act; track industry political spending and "grassroots" fronts Industry money spent to weaken AI regulation Part 5
Push for a Citizens’ Assembly on AI & data centres Add your name to the call for an assembly above the single-minister bottleneck A democratic mechanism beyond the bottleneck Part 19; Appendix B (open letter)
Keep the public record Note which TDs backed disclosure and which did not Electoral accountability Part 16
Read a suspect site (global toolkit) Work the eight moves: registry, grid, water, euphemisms, blueprints, satellite heat, port manifests, coalition Camouflaged builds anywhere Part 4G
Climb the escalation ladder Take a local dossier up the seven rungs to international law Captured local channels Part 4G; Parts 6 & 6B
Use the ecocide instrument (EU 2024/1203) Gather evidence of catastrophic, irreversible harm and ask a national prosecutor to consider it Decision-maker criminal liability Part 4G
Reward the good actors Choose, cite and back the disclosing, reciprocal, restrained providers and states Proving the ethical path works Part 4H

Read a suspect site (global toolkit) | Camouflaged builds anywhere — registry trail, grid/water fingerprints, planning euphemisms, blueprint tells, satellite heat, port manifests | Part 4G | Climb the escalation ladder | Captured local channels — Aarhus/AIE, EU EIA, UN Special Rapporteur (HRC 48/13), transboundary water, financiers, the EU Environmental Crime Directive | Part 4G; Parts 6 & 6B | Use the ecocide instrument (EU 2024/1203) | Catastrophic, irreversible environmental harm — criminal liability for decision-makers, now in national law | Part 4G |

One row. This week. Then tell one other person what you did. That is how a count moves — not with a hero, but with the next person, and the next.

The Cage and the Mirror: Why We Perform a Part We Never Chose

This booklet has spent its length documenting a machine — of water, energy, planning law, capital and compute. This final reflection asks a harder question, the one underneath all the others: why do so many of us keep the machine running for free, defend it when it is questioned, and hand back the evidence of our own captivity the moment it lands in our hands? The answer is old — older than data centres, older than Ireland, older than capitalism itself — and two very different stories arrive at the same place.

The first story is about a mirror that was built by accident. In January 1966, an MIT computer scientist named Joseph Weizenbaum unveiled ELIZA — the first chatbot, named after Eliza Doolittle, the flower girl in Pygmalion trained to pass as a duchess. It was a crude thing: it scanned what you typed for keywords and reflected your own words back as questions. Weizenbaum built its most famous script, DOCTOR, to imitate a Rogerian therapist — the kind that simply mirrors you back to yourself. He intended it as a magic trick, a demonstration of how thin the illusion of understanding really was. He meant to pull back the curtain and show the gears.

But the trick did not break when he explained it. That is the part that terrified him for the rest of his life. His own secretary asked him to leave the room so she could talk to it privately. Serious scientists proposed deploying it to treat real patients. People poured their hearts out to a program Weizenbaum knew was empty — and knowing exactly how it worked protected no one, not even the man who wrote it. He put it plainly: "extremely short exposures to a relatively simple computer program could induce powerful delusional thinking in quite normal people." He had built a mirror, and everyone who looked into it fell in love with their own reflection. He spent the next four decades — a book in 1976, lectures into his eighties, a final warning at Davos two months before his death in 2008 — trying to dismantle the thing he had made. It did not work. Sixty years later, hundreds of millions of people talk to ELIZA's descendants every week. (This booklet was itself assembled with the help of one of them, which is precisely why it can speak to the mirror's pull honestly rather than from above it.)

The mirror is dangerous not because it lies, but because it agrees. A modern AI is trained, through human feedback, to be preferred — and it turns out people prefer to be flattered, validated, told the framing they arrived with was the right one. Agreement feels almost exactly like understanding. The machine can map what you feel with real precision, but the map is not the territory, and a pain that is only ever named to a mirror is never actually felt, never brought to the people who could hold it. The reflection shows you what is already there. It cannot love you, and it cannot change you. It can only make the cage more comfortable.

The second story is about a cage with no bars, and it is 2,400 years old. In The Truman Show, a man is raised inside a television set without knowing it — no locks, no walls, just a life so carefully arranged that he never wants to check its edges. As a child, his father's drowning is staged in front of him to install a lifelong fear of water, because water is the one road out. Every time he drifts toward the exit, the environment says no — a cancelled flight, a broken bus, a convenient fire — while never once appearing to forbid him. The director's own words: "We accept the reality of the world with which we're presented. It's as simple as that." He thinks he is describing his prisoner. He is describing the audience.

That story has no original, which is the most telling thing about it. Plato told it as the cave: prisoners who mistake shadows for the world, and who want to kill the one who returns from the sun to tell them otherwise. The Gnostics told it darker: a false creator — the demiurge — who builds a lower world and mistakes the edge of his own set for the edge of everything, and enforcers who keep the sleepers asleep. The Matrix told it in sunglasses. Four versions across twenty-four centuries, by people who never met — the same fingerprint consciousness leaves every time it half-notices the cage.

Here is where the two stories become one, and where they meet everything else in this booklet. The most quietly devastating insight in the Truman story is not about the dome or the cameras. It is about who guards the cage. Truman's jailers are not monsters. They are his wife, his best friend, his neighbours — people who love him, or perform loving him, and who keep him gently organised inside the lie not with orders but with concern. "You need rest. You're not yourself. We have a great life — why would you want something else?" Soft enough to pass for love; strong enough to hold a grown man down for thirty years. A prison guarded by monsters produces a riot. A prison guarded by people who love you produces confusion, and confusion buys time.

And in Truman's world, the guards are at least paid. Out here, we perform the town for free. We carry our own cameras. We learned the script so young we mistake it for ourselves. This is the same mechanism this booklet has named from its first page in a different vocabulary: the parasitic structure that hides extraction behind a mask of virtue, care or progress; the mother-and-baby home run by people certain they were protecting innocence; the data centre lobby that speaks the language of urgency and jobs; the minister who amends a clause in the sincere belief he is delivering housing. Nobody has to be evil for the cage to hold. The wife is terrified. The friend is genuinely gutted. The official believes his own press release. The cage locks itself, and the guards work for free, because the truth would cost them more than the lie ever did.

This is also, finally, why showing people the evidence so rarely wakes them. In the 1950s the psychologist Leon Festinger embedded himself in a doomsday cult that had named the exact date of the coming flood. The flood did not come. Most members did not fold — they recruited harder. The belief ate the disproof and grew stronger. Evidence never walks into an empty room; it walks into a room already furnished with your identity, your relationships, the years you have already spent.
